# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Decimator with Anti-Aliasing Filter

This Mermaid diagram shows the canonical position of the anti-aliasing filter in a downsampler (decimator) by a factor of $M$. The signal flow is strictly **Input → AAF → Downsampler → Output**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode["INPUT x of n"] --> aafNode["H AA of z LOWPASS CUTOFF pi over M"]
    aafNode --> downNode["DOWNSAMPLER BY M Y of n equals X of M n"]
    downNode --> outNode["OUTPUT y of n LOWER RATE"]
    style inNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
    style aafNode fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600
    style downNode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style outNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture (Reading the Diagram):**
- **Input Stage** $x(n)$: A wideband signal containing frequencies up to $\pi$ radians.
- **AAF Stage** $H_{\text{AA}}(z)$: A digital low-pass filter that band-limits the input to $[-\pi/M, \pi/M]$. Without this, the downsampler would cause aliasing.
- **Downsampler** $\downarrow M$: A switch/compressor that retains only every $M$-th sample, lowering the sampling rate by a factor of $M$.
- **Output Stage** $y(n)$: The downsampled, alias-free signal at the lower rate.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Interpolator with Anti-Imaging Filter

This Mermaid diagram shows the canonical position of the anti-imaging filter in an upsampler (interpolator) by a factor of $L$. The signal flow is strictly **Input → Upsampler → AIF → Output**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode2["INPUT x of n"] --> upNode["UPSAMPLER BY L INSERTS L MINUS 1 ZEROS"]
    upNode --> aifNode["H AI of z GAIN L CUTOFF pi over L"]
    aifNode --> outNode2["OUTPUT y of n HIGHER RATE"]
    style inNode2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
    style upNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style aifNode fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600
    style outNode2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture (Reading the Diagram):**
- **Input Stage** $x(n)$: A narrowband signal at the original (lower) rate.
- **Upsampler** $\uparrow L$: A zero-inserting expander that places $L-1$ zeros between successive samples, raising the rate by $L$ but introducing spectral images.
- **AIF Stage** $H_{\text{AI}}(z)$: A digital low-pass filter with gain $L$ and cutoff $\pi/L$. It suppresses the $L-1$ spectral images and amplifies the baseband to compensate for the energy distributed to the images.
- **Output Stage** $y(n)$: The interpolated, image-free signal at the higher rate.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: The Two Noble Identities

This Mermaid diagram shows the equivalence between the original and the simplified (efficient) structures under each Noble Identity.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "FIRST NOBLE IDENTITY"
        direction LR
        f1a["x of n"] --> f1b["H of z"] --> f1c["DOWN M"] --> f1d["y of n"]
        f1e["x of n"] --> f1f["DOWN M"] --> f1g["H of z to the M"] --> f1h["y of n"]
    end
    subgraph "SECOND NOBLE IDENTITY"
        direction LR
        s1a["x of n"] --> s1b["UP L"] --> s1c["H of z"] --> s1d["y of n"]
        s1e["x of n"] --> s1f["H of z to the L"] --> s1g["UP L"] --> s1h["y of n"]
    end
    style f1b fill:#ffe5b4
    style f1g fill:#d4edda
    style s1c fill:#ffe5b4
    style s1f fill:#d4edda
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture (Reading the Diagram):**
- **Top Half (First Noble Identity):** The original filter $H(z)$ (operating at high rate) is *equivalent* to a stretched filter $H(z^M)$ operating *after* the downsampler at the lower rate. Computational saving: filter now runs at $1/M$ the rate.
- **Bottom Half (Second Noble Identity):** The original filter $H(z)$ (operating at high rate, with most input samples being zero) is *equivalent* to a compressed filter $H(z^L)$ operating *before* the upsampler at the lower rate. Again, the filter now runs at $1/L$ the rate.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Fractional Sampling Rate Alteration (FSRA)

This Mermaid diagram shows the canonical FSRA structure that combines upsampling and downsampling through a single, shared low-pass filter.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fsIn["INPUT x of n RATE F in"] --> fsUp["UPSAMPLE BY L"] --> fsFilt["H of z LPF CUTOFF pi over max of L M GAIN L"] --> fsDown["DOWNSAMPLE BY M"] --> fsOut["OUTPUT y of n RATE L M F in"]
    style fsFilt fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600
    style fsUp fill:#f8d7da
    style fsDown fill:#d4edda
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture (Reading the Diagram):**
- **Input**: $x(n)$ at sampling rate $F_{\text{in}}$.
- **Upsampler** $\uparrow L$: Stretches the signal by $L$ (rate becomes $L \cdot F_{\text{in}}$).
- **Combined LPF** $H(z)$: Serves a *dual role* — it is the anti-imaging filter for the upsampler **and** the anti-aliasing filter for the downsampler. Its cutoff is at $\pi / \max(L, M)$.
- **Downsampler** $\downarrow M$: Compresses by $M$ (final rate becomes $\dfrac{L}{M} F_{\text{in}}$).
- **Output**: $y(n)$ at the desired non-integer rate.

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Type 1 Polyphase Decomposition

This Mermaid diagram shows the parallel-bank realization of the Type 1 polyphase decomposition for $M = 3$ polyphase components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pin["H of z INPUT"] --> e0["E0 of z to the M"]
    pin --> e1["E1 of z to the M"]
    pin --> e2["E2 of z to the M"]
    e0 --> d0["DELAY z to the power 0"] --> sumNode["SUMMER"]
    e1 --> d1["DELAY z to the power minus 1"] --> sumNode
    e2 --> d2["DELAY z to the power minus 2"] --> sumNode
    sumNode --> pout["OUTPUT Y of z"]
    style e0 fill:#d4edda
    style e1 fill:#d4edda
    style e2 fill:#d4edda
    style d0 fill:#cce5ff
    style d1 fill:#cce5ff
    style d2 fill:#cce5ff
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture (Reading the Diagram):**
- The input is fed to **three parallel sub-filters** $E_0(z^M)$, $E_1(z^M)$, and $E_2(z^M)$ (all operating at the low rate $\frac{1}{M}$ of the original).
- The output of each sub-filter is multiplied by a **pure delay** $z^{-k}$ ($k = 0, 1, 2$).
- The three delayed outputs are summed to form the final $Y(z)$.
- **Key advantage**: All $M$ sub-filters operate at the low rate, providing a factor of $M$ reduction in multiplications-per-second (MPS) compared to the direct form.

## 4.6 Block Diagram: Type 2 Polyphase Decomposition

This Mermaid diagram shows the transposed (delay-on-input) realization of the Type 2 polyphase decomposition for $M = 3$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pin2["H of z INPUT"] --> adel0["ADVANCE z to the power 0"] --> r0["R0 of z to the M"]
    pin2 --> adel1["ADVANCE z to the power 1"] --> r1["R1 of z to the M"]
    pin2 --> adel2["ADVANCE z to the power 2"] --> r2["R2 of z to the M"]
    r0 --> sumNode2["SUMMER"]
    r1 --> sumNode2
    r2 --> sumNode2
    sumNode2 --> bdelay["BULK DELAY z to the power minus M PLUS 1"] --> pout2["OUTPUT Y of z"]
    style r0 fill:#d4edda
    style r1 fill:#d4edda
    style r2 fill:#d4edda
    style adel0 fill:#cce5ff
    style adel1 fill:#cce5ff
    style adel2 fill:#cce5ff
    style bdelay fill:#fff3cd
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture (Reading the Diagram):**
- The input is first routed through **advances** $z^{k}$ ($k = 0, 1, 2$), which can be implemented as delays in a real-time system by reordering.
- Each advanced signal passes through a sub-filter $R_k(z^M)$.
- The three sub-filter outputs are summed.
- A **bulk delay** $z^{-(M-1)}$ is applied at the output to make the system causal.
- **Key advantage**: This structure is the **transpose** of Type 1, useful when interfacing with **lattice filter banks** and **DFT-modulated filter banks** in wavelet packet design.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAET866 Model Paper]`

**Q: Why is an anti-aliasing filter necessary before a downsampler? What is its ideal cutoff frequency for a downsampler of factor $M$?**

**Model Answer:**

An anti-aliasing filter is necessary before a downsampler because the downsampling operation (compression of the time axis by $M$) causes a corresponding **expansion of the frequency axis by $M$**. Mathematically:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} X\!\left(e^{j(\omega - 2\pi k/M)}\right)$$

This means $M$ stretched and shifted replicas of the input spectrum $X(e^{j\omega})$ are summed together. If $X(e^{j\omega})$ contains any frequency content above $\dfrac{\pi}{M}$ radians, these high-frequency components will **fold back** (alias) into the baseband $[-\pi/M, \pi/M]$, causing irreversible corruption of the signal.

The **anti-aliasing filter** is a low-pass filter placed before the downsampler to band-limit the input to $\vert \omega \vert \leq \dfrac{\pi}{M}$, ensuring that no aliasing occurs during decimation.

**The ideal cutoff frequency is $\omega_c = \dfrac{\pi}{M}$ radians** (normalized). $\blacksquare$

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAET866 Model Paper]`

**Q: State and explain the First and Second Noble Identities of multirate signal processing.**

**Model Answer:**

The Noble Identities are structural equivalences that allow filters to be moved across downsamplers and upsamplers, enabling efficient multirate system implementations.

**First Noble Identity:** A filter $H(z)$ followed by a downsampler by $M$ is equivalent to a downsampler by $M$ followed by a filter $H(z^M)$:
$$H(z) \rightarrow \downarrow M \;\equiv\; \downarrow M \rightarrow H(z^M)$$

The substitution $z \to z^M$ **stretches** the filter's impulse response by $M$, perfectly compensating for the samples discarded by the downsampler. The filter now operates at the lower rate, saving computation.

**Second Noble Identity:** An upsampler by $L$ followed by a filter $H(z)$ is equivalent to a filter $H(z^L)$ followed by an upsampler by $L$:
$$\uparrow L \rightarrow H(z) \;\equiv\; H(z^L) \rightarrow \uparrow L$$

The substitution $z \to z^L$ **compresses** the filter's impulse response by $L$, and the filter now operates at the lower input rate. This avoids the wasteful computation of multiplying by zeros in the upsampled signal. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **decimator** (downsampler by $M$) and an **interpolator** (upsampler by $L$). Clearly state the role of the anti-aliasing and anti-imaging filters, including their ideal specifications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **First and Second Noble Identities** from first principles. Show, with the help of a block diagram, how these identities can be used to obtain a computationally efficient decimator structure. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Decimator Operation:**

A decimator reduces the sampling rate of a discrete-time signal by an integer factor $M$. The block diagram (refer to Section 4.1) consists of an **anti-aliasing filter** $H_{\text{AA}}(z)$ followed by a **downsampler** $\downarrow M$.

The downsampler is defined as $y(n) = x(Mn)$, retaining only every $M$-th sample. In the frequency domain, this causes spectral stretching by $M$:
$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} X\!\left(e^{j(\omega - 2\pi k/M)}\right)$$

**Role of Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Without the anti-aliasing filter, frequencies above $\pi/M$ in $X(e^{j\omega})$ will fold into the baseband, causing aliasing. The AAF is an ideal low-pass filter with:
- **Passband:** $\vert \omega \vert \leq \dfrac{\pi}{M}$
- **Stopband:** $\dfrac{\pi}{M} \leq \vert \omega \vert \leq \pi$
- **Gain:** Unity in the passband

**Interpolator Operation:**

An interpolator increases the sampling rate by an integer factor $L$. The block diagram (refer to Section 4.2) consists of an **upsampler** $\uparrow L$ followed by an **anti-imaging filter** $H_{\text{AI}}(z)$.

The upsampler inserts $L-1$ zeros between consecutive samples: $x_u(n) = x(n/L)$ for $n = mL$, and $0$ otherwise. In the frequency domain, the spectrum is compressed by $L$ and replicated:
$$X_u(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{jL\omega})$$

**Role of Anti-Imaging Filter:** The compression creates $L-1$ spectral images at $\omega = 2\pi k / L$. The AIF is an ideal low-pass filter with:
- **Passband:** $\vert \omega \vert \leq \dfrac{\pi}{L}$
- **Stopband:** $\dfrac{\pi}{L} \leq \vert \omega \vert \leq \pi$
- **Gain:** $L$ in the passband (to compensate for the energy spread across $L$ replicas)

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation of First Noble Identity (Statement):** $H(z) \rightarrow \downarrow M \equiv \downarrow M \rightarrow H(z^M)$.

Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.3** of these notes. The key steps are:
1. Express the output of the LHS as $y(n) = \sum_k h(k) x(Mn - k)$.
2. Decompose the summation index $k = Mm + r$ for $r \in \{0, 1, \ldots, M-1\}$.
3. Recognize that the downsampler output of a delayed input is $z^{-r} X(z^M)$.
4. Assemble the result: $Y(z) = X(z^M) \sum_{r=0}^{M-1} z^{-r} E_r(z^M) = X(z^M) H(z^M)$. $\blacksquare$

**Derivation of Second Noble Identity (Statement):** $\uparrow L \rightarrow H(z) \equiv H(z^L) \rightarrow \uparrow L$.

By duality, applying the time-reversal property or by direct Z-domain analysis: the upsampler expands the time axis (inserts zeros), and the filter $H(z)$ is equivalent to $H(z^L)$ operating before the upsampler, saving computation by avoiding multiplications with zeros. $\blacksquare$

**Computationally Efficient Decimator Structure:**

In the original decimator, the AAF $H_{\text{AA}}(z)$ operates at the **high** input rate. By the First Noble Identity:
$$\text{Original: } x(n) \rightarrow H_{\text{AA}}(z) \rightarrow \downarrow M \equiv x(n) \rightarrow \downarrow M \rightarrow H_{\text{AA}}(z^M)$$

The filter $H_{\text{AA}}(z^M)$ now operates at the **lower** rate, with its impulse response stretched by $M$. This reduces the **multiplications per second (MPS)** by a factor of $M$. The block diagram is in Section 4.3.

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of **Fractional Sampling Rate Alteration (FSRA)**. Derive the output sampling rate and design the canonical filter specifications for a system that converts a sampling rate of $F_s$ to $\dfrac{L}{M} F_s$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **polyphase decomposition**? Derive the **Type 1 and Type 2 polyphase representations** of a digital filter $H(z)$. Show, with a block diagram, how the Type 1 polyphase structure reduces computational complexity. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Concept of FSRA:**

Fractional Sampling Rate Alteration is required when the desired rate change is a non-integer rational factor $\dfrac{L}{M}$. A single decimator ($\downarrow M$) or interpolator ($\uparrow L$) cannot achieve a non-integer rate change. The solution is to **cascade an interpolator and a decimator** with a shared low-pass filter.

**Output Sampling Rate Derivation:**

The input is upsampled by $L$, raising the rate to $L \cdot F_s$. The subsequent downsampler by $M$ reduces the rate by a factor of $M$:
$$F_{\text{out}} = \frac{L \cdot F_s}{M} = \frac{L}{M} F_s$$

For the system to be well-defined and to avoid non-unique sample selection, $L$ and $M$ must be **coprime** (i.e., $\gcd(L, M) = 1$).

**Canonical Filter Specifications:**

The shared low-pass filter $H(z)$ serves a **dual role**:
- It is the **anti-imaging filter** for the $\uparrow L$ (removes images at $2\pi k / L$).
- It is the **anti-aliasing filter** for the $\downarrow M$ (prevents aliasing above $\pi / M$).

Therefore, the filter cutoff must be at the **more stringent** of the two constraints:
$$\omega_c = \frac{\pi}{\max(L, M)}$$

The filter gain is $L$ (to compensate for the energy lost in the upsampling step).

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.4.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Polyphase Decomposition Concept:**

Polyphase decomposition is a structural technique that partitions a filter $H(z)$ into $M$ parallel sub-filters (called polyphase components) operating at a lower rate. This provides a **computational saving of a factor of $M$** when the filter is used inside a multirate system.

**Type 1 Polyphase Derivation:**

Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.4** of these notes. Starting from $H(z) = \sum_n h(n) z^{-n}$, perform the change of variables $n = Mm + k$ for $k \in \{0, 1, \ldots, M-1\}$:
$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} E_k(z^M), \quad E_k(z) = \sum_m h(Mm+k) z^{-m}$$

**Type 2 Polyphase Derivation:**

Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.5** of these notes. Reverse the index via $R_k(z) = E_{M-1-k}(z)$:
$$H(z) = z^{-(M-1)} \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^k R_k(z^M)$$

**Block Diagram and Computational Advantage:**

Refer to **Section 4.5** for the Type 1 polyphase block diagram. The key insight is that the sub-filters $E_k(z^M)$ operate at the **lower rate** (rate $\frac{1}{M}$ of the original). The total number of multiplications per second is reduced by a factor of $M$, even though the number of filter coefficients remains the same. This is the cornerstone of efficient **Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) banks** and **wavelet transform implementations**. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing AAF and AIF:** Many students swap the cutoff of the anti-aliasing filter ($\pi/M$) with that of the anti-imaging filter ($\pi/L$). **Remember:** the filter operates at the *new* rate — AAF uses $M$ (downsampler), AIF uses $L$ (upsampler).
> 2. **Noble Identity Sign Error:** In the First Noble Identity, students often write $H(z^{1/M})$ instead of $H(z^M)$. The substitution always **raises** the power: $z \to z^M$ or $z \to z^L$, never a fractional power.
> 3. **FSRA Order Reversal:** The canonical FSRA structure is **Upsampler → Filter → Downsampler**. Reversing this order can cause severe aliasing because the filter at the lower rate cannot remove the images of the upsampler.
> 4. **Polyphase Index Confusion:** The Type 1 component $E_k$ uses the index $k$ directly in the delay $z^{-k}$, while Type 2 uses the **reverse** index $R_k = E_{M-1-k}$. Mixing these up leads to wrong filter coefficients in board exams.
> 5. **Missing Bulk Delay in Type 2:** Students often forget the $z^{-(M-1)}$ term in Type 2. Without it, the system is non-causal. Always factor it out explicitly.
> 6. **Common Mistake in Coprimality:** In FSRA, students sometimes choose $L$ and $M$ that share a common factor (e.g., $L=2, M=4$). The exam expects $\gcd(L,M) = 1$ for a true fractional rate change.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PEAET866 Module 1**

### **Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)**
- Placed **before** a downsampler ($\downarrow M$).
- **Cutoff:** $\omega_c = \dfrac{\pi}{M}$.
- **Purpose:** Prevents aliasing of frequencies above $\pi/M$ into the baseband.
- **Gain:** Unity.

### **Anti-Imaging Filter (AIF)**
- Placed **after** an upsampler ($\uparrow L$).
- **Cutoff:** $\omega_c = \dfrac{\pi}{L}$.
- **Purpose:** Removes $L-1$ spectral images created by zero-insertion.
- **Gain:** $L$ (compensates for energy distribution).

### **First Noble Identity**
- $H(z) \rightarrow \downarrow M \;\equiv\; \downarrow M \rightarrow H(z^M)$.
- Filter moves **before** downsampler; impulse response is **stretched** by $M$.
- Computational saving: factor of $M$.

### **Second Noble Identity**
- $\uparrow L \rightarrow H(z) \;\equiv\; H(z^L) \rightarrow \uparrow L$.
- Filter moves **after** upsampler; impulse response is **compressed** by $L$.
- Computational saving: factor of $L$ (avoids multiplications with zeros).

### **Fractional Sampling Rate Alteration (FSRA)**
- Rate change factor: $\dfrac{L}{M}$ where $\gcd(L, M) = 1$.
- Canonical structure: $\uparrow L \rightarrow H(z) \rightarrow \downarrow M$.
- **Filter cutoff:** $\dfrac{\pi}{\max(L, M)}$; **Gain:** $L$.
- **Output rate:** $F_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{L}{M} F_{\text{in}}$.

### **Type 1 Polyphase Decomposition**
- $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} E_k(z^M)$, where $E_k(z) = \sum_m h(Mm+k) z^{-m}$.
- Structure: **parallel sub-filters** with **output delays** $z^{-k}$.

### **Type 2 Polyphase Decomposition**
- $H(z) = z^{-(M-1)} \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{k} R_k(z^M)$, where $R_k(z) = E_{M-1-k}(z)$.
- Structure: **transposed** (delays/advances on input) with a **bulk delay** $z^{-(M-1)}$.

### **Cross-Connections (Frequently Tested)**
- **Noble + Polyphase** = Optimal efficient multirate filter (the "Holy Grail" of multirate DSP, used in wavelet filter banks).
- **FSRA = Upsampling + Downsampling** with a single filter, derived by applying the two Noble Identities to a single rate-change filter.
- **AIF + AAF** in FSRA: A single filter serves both roles with cutoff $\pi / \max(L, M)$.

### **Must-Memorize Mathematical Facts**
- Downsampling **stretches** the spectrum by $M$ and creates $M$ shifted replicas.
- Upsampling **compresses** the spectrum by $L$ and creates $L-1$ spectral images.
- $z \to z^M$ in a filter's transfer function corresponds to **stretching** the impulse response by $M$.
- $z \to z^L$ corresponds to **compressing** the impulse response by $L$.
- The polyphase decomposition of an $N$-tap filter into $M$ components yields $M$ sub-filters, each of length $\approx N/M$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficient Structures for Decimation and Interpolation Filters - Introduction to Digital Filter Banks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Efficient Structures for Decimation and Interpolation Filters - Introduction to Digital Filter Banks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Decimation (Downsampling) — Formal Definition

In multirate digital signal processing, **decimation** is the process of reducing the sampling rate of a discrete-time signal by an integer factor $M$. It is achieved by first applying an anti-aliasing lowpass filter $H(z)$ (with cutoff $\pi/M$) followed by a downsampler that retains every $M^{th}$ sample.

Mathematically, the output of a decimator by factor $M$ is defined as:

$$y[n] = x_M[n] = (h \ast x)[nM]$$

where $h[n]$ is the impulse response of the anti-aliasing filter and $x[n]$ is the input signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term *decimation* strictly means the **combined operation of lowpass filtering followed by downsampling**, NOT mere sample dropping. This distinction is a frequent source of marks in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Interpolation (Upsampling) — Formal Definition

**Interpolation** is the dual operation that increases the sampling rate of a signal by an integer factor $L$. It involves inserting $L-1$ zeros between successive samples (zero-insertion / upsampling), followed by an anti-imaging lowpass filter $H(z)$ (with cutoff $\pi/L$).

The upsampler (zero-insertor) is mathematically described as:

$$x_L[n] = \begin{cases} x[n/L], & n = 0, \pm L, \pm 2L, \ldots \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

## 1.3 Digital Filter Bank — Formal Definition

A **digital filter bank** is a collection of digital filters arranged in a parallel or cascade configuration, used to decompose a signal into multiple frequency subbands (analysis bank) and subsequently to reconstruct the original signal (synthesis bank).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:**
> - **Analysis Filter Bank:** Splits $x[n]$ into $M$ subband signals using bandpass filters $H_k(z)$, $k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1$.
> - **Synthesis Filter Bank:** Combines the subband signals back using filters $F_k(z)$ to reconstruct $\hat{x}[n]$.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **photograph of a wave-patterned curtain**. 

- **Decimation** is like stepping back from the curtain — you see fewer details (the high-frequency fine threads blur and disappear), but the overall "feel" of the broad stripes remains. The anti-aliasing filter is the act of *blurring your vision* before stepping back, preventing jagged false patterns.
- **Interpolation** is like stepping closer to the curtain — you need to *invent* the data that lies between existing samples. The interpolation filter is the *educated guess* algorithm that fills in the missing threads.
- **Digital Filter Bank** is like placing several differently-tinted magnifying lenses on the curtain simultaneously — each lens (filter) highlights a different frequency region, and together they let you analyze every stripe independently.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Use:** Filter banks power MP3 audio compression (subband coding), JPEG2000 image compression (wavelets), digital hearing aids, and software-defined radio — every place where signals must be split into frequency subbands for efficient processing.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics and Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value/Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $M$ | Decimation factor (integer) | $M \geq 2$ |
| $L$ | Interpolation factor (integer) | $L \geq 2$ |
| $H_k(z)$ | $k^{th}$ analysis filter | $k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1$ |
| $F_k(z)$ | $k^{th}$ synthesis filter | $k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1$ |
| $E_k(z)$ | $k^{th}$ polyphase component | $k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1$ |
| $R_k(z)$ | Type-2 polyphase component | $k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept of Decimation

The complete decimation chain involves two critical stages:

1. **Anti-aliasing Filtering:** Apply lowpass filter $H(z)$ with cutoff frequency $\omega_c = \pi/M$ to band-limit the signal and prevent aliasing during downsampling.
2. **Downsampling ($\downarrow M$):** Retain only every $M^{th}$ sample: $y[n] = v[nM]$, where $v[n] = (h \ast x)[n]$.

**Why filtering is mandatory before downsampling:** Sampling rate reduction by $M$ compresses the spectrum by factor $M$. If the original signal contains frequency components above $\pi/M$, these components **alias** (overlap) and corrupt the baseband — this is the classical *Nyquist aliasing* problem.

## 2.2 Operational Concept of Interpolation

The complete interpolation chain involves:

1. **Upsampling ($\uparrow L$):** Insert $L-1$ zeros between every pair of consecutive input samples.
2. **Anti-imaging Filtering:** Apply lowpass filter $H(z)$ with cutoff $\pi/L$ to remove spectral images (replicas) and smooth the staircase-like zero-inserted signal.

**Why filtering is mandatory after upsampling:** Zero-insertion creates $L-1$ unwanted spectral images of the baseband. The lowpass filter suppresses these images, leaving only the spectrally-correct interpolated signal.

## 2.3 The Noble Identities (Critical for KTU)

The **Noble Identities** are the foundational identities that enable filter-multiply-switch transformations, leading to computationally efficient structures. They come in two primary forms:

**Identity 1 (Filter-Downsampler Swap):**

$$H(z) \cdot \bigl(\downarrow M\bigr) = \bigl(\downarrow M\bigr) \cdot H(z^M)$$

**Identity 2 (Upsampler-Filter Swap):**

$$\bigl(\uparrow L\bigr) \cdot H(z) = H(z^L) \cdot \bigl(\uparrow L\bigr)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Noble identities allow the computationally expensive filter $H(z)$ (operating at high rate) to be replaced by $H(z^M)$ operating at the *lower* output rate, drastically reducing computational cost — a key KTU design question theme.

## 2.4 Polyphase Decomposition

A transfer function $H(z)$ of order $N$ can be decomposed into $M$ polyphase components $E_k(z)$ of reduced order $N/M$:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} \, E_k(z^M)$$

where each **Type-1 polyphase component** is:

$$E_k(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[nM + k] \, z^{-n}, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1$$

The **Type-2 polyphase components** $R_k(z)$ are defined as:

$$R_k(z) = E_{M-1-k}(z), \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Polyphase?** A direct-form FIR filter of order $N$ requires $N+1$ multiplications per output sample. The polyphase structure computes only at the *output rate*, achieving an $M$-fold reduction in computation — making it indispensable in real-time DSP systems.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Identity** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Engineering Use** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Downsampler output | $y[n] = x[nM]$ | Sample rate reduction |
| Upsampler output | $x_L[n] = x[n/L]$ if $L \mid n$, else $0$ | Sample rate increase |
| Noble Identity 1 | $H(z)\bigl(\downarrow M\bigr) = \bigl(\downarrow M\bigr) H(z^M)$ | Move filter to lower rate |
| Noble Identity 2 | $\bigl(\uparrow L\bigr) H(z) = H(z^L)\bigl(\uparrow L\bigr)$ | Move filter to higher rate |
| Polyphase Type-1 | $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} E_k(z^M)$ | Efficient decimator |
| Polyphase Type-2 | $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-(M-1-k)} R_k(z^M)$ | Efficient interpolator |
| Two-channel QMF reconstruction | $H_0(z)H_1(-z) - H_0(-z)H_1(z) = 0$ | Alias cancellation |
| Perfect reconstruction (orthogonal) | $F_k(z) = H_k(z^{-1})$ (for paraunitary) | Distortionless reconstruction |
| Analysis bank output | $X_k(z) = H_k(z)X(z)$ | Subband extraction |
| Synthesis bank output | $\hat{X}(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} F_k(z) X_k(z)$ | Signal reconstruction |
| Computational saving | Factor of $M$ for $M$-fold decimator | Real-time DSP |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility of Polyphase Filter Banks:** The polyphase implementation is the **backbone of every modern wavelet transform** (DWT), OFDM modulator in 4G/5G, and subband coder. Without polyphase, these systems would be computationally infeasible for real-time operation.

## 2.6 Two-Channel Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) Bank

The simplest and most important filter bank is the **two-channel QMF bank** consisting of:
- A lowpass analysis filter $H_0(z)$ and a highpass analysis filter $H_1(z)$
- The highpass filter is the mirror image: $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$
- A lowpass synthesis filter $F_0(z)$ and a highpass synthesis filter $F_1(z)$
- Down/up samplers by factor $2$

The reconstructed signal in the $z$-domain is:

$$\hat{X}(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[F_0(z)H_0(z) + F_1(z)H_1(z)\bigr]X(z) + \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[F_0(z)H_0(-z) + F_1(z)H_1(-z)\bigr]X(-z)$$

The first term is the **distortion term** (desired) and the second is the **alias term** (must be zero for perfect reconstruction).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Polyphase Decomposition of $H(z)$

**Starting Point:** Consider a causal FIR filter with impulse response $h[n]$ of length $N+1$. We wish to express $H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N} h[n] z^{-n}$ in terms of $M$ sub-filters, each operating at rate $1/M$.

**Step 1:** Group the impulse response coefficients by their index modulo $M$. Split the summation index $n$ as $n = k + mM$, where $k \in \{0, 1, \ldots, M-1\}$ and $m$ is an integer.

$$H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N} h[n] z^{-n} = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} \sum_{m} h[k + mM] z^{-(k + mM)}$$

**Step 2:** Factor out $z^{-k}$ from the inner summation (since $z^{-(k+mM)} = z^{-k} \cdot z^{-mM}$).

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} \left[ \sum_{m} h[k + mM] \left(z^{M}\right)^{-m} \right]$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the bracketed term as a filter in the variable $z^M$. Define the **Type-1 polyphase component** $E_k(z)$ as:

$$E_k(z) \triangleq \sum_{m} h[k + mM] z^{-m}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back. The complete polyphase decomposition is:

$$\boxed{H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} E_k(z^M)}$$

**Step 5:** To derive the **Type-2 polyphase representation**, substitute $k' = M-1-k$ in the Type-1 expression. Letting $R_{M-1-k}(z^M) = E_k(z^M)$:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-(M-1-k)} R_k(z^M)$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Noble Identity 1

**Statement to Prove:** $H(z) \cdot (\downarrow M) = (\downarrow M) \cdot H(z^M)$

**Step 1:** Consider a general input $x[n]$ to the left-hand side. First $x[n]$ passes through $H(z)$ giving $v[n] = \sum_{k} h[k] x[n-k]$. Then downsampling by $M$ yields:

$$y_L[n] = v[nM] = \sum_{k} h[k] \, x[nM - k]$$

**Step 2:** For the right-hand side, $x[n]$ is first downsampled by $M$: $w[n] = x[nM]$. Then $w[n]$ is passed through $H(z^M)$, which has impulse response $h_M[n] = h[n]$ but transfer function $H(z^M)$. The output is:

$$y_R[n] = \sum_{k} h[k] \, w[n-k] = \sum_{k} h[k] \, x[(n-k)M]$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $k' = k$ and noting that $(n-k)M = nM - k$:

$$y_R[n] = \sum_{k} h[k] \, x[nM - k]$$

**Step 4:** Comparing $y_L[n] = y_R[n]$, the identity is verified. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Efficient Polyphase Decimator

**Starting Point:** Naive decimator: $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N} h[k] \, x[nM - k]$. This computes $N+1$ multiplications per *output* sample, but the filter operates at the *input* rate.

**Step 1:** Apply polyphase decomposition to $H(z)$ with $M$ branches:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} E_k(z^M)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the decimation chain and apply Noble Identity 1 to move each $E_k(z^M)$ to the *right* of the downsampler, where it becomes $E_k(z)$.

**Step 3:** The polyphase output becomes:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} e_k[n] \, x[nM - k]$$

where $e_k[n] = \sum_{m} h[k + mM] x[n - m]$ is the output of the $k^{th}$ polyphase sub-filter at time index $n$.

**Step 4:** Count the multiplications. Each $E_k(z)$ is a filter of order $N/M$, requiring $(N/M + 1)$ multiplications per output. There are $M$ such filters, so the total is:

$$C_{\text{poly}} = M \cdot \left(\frac{N}{M} + 1\right) \approx N + M$$

Compared with the direct form's $N+1$ multiplications *per input sample* (i.e., $M(N+1)$ per output), the polyphase structure achieves an **$M$-fold computational reduction**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Alias Cancellation in QMF Bank

**Starting Point:** For the two-channel QMF bank, let the downsampled subband signals be $X_0(z)$ and $X_1(z)$. After upsampling by $2$ and synthesis filtering:

$$\hat{X}(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[F_0(z)X_0(z^2) + F_1(z)X_1(z^2)\bigr]$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the analysis relations $X_0(z) = H_0(z)X(z)$ and $X_1(z) = H_1(z)X(z)$:

$$\hat{X}(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[F_0(z)H_0(z^2) + F_1(z)H_1(z^2)\bigr]X(z^2)$$

**Step 2:** Using the identity $X(z^2) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[X(z) + X(-z)\bigr]$ and the QMF mirror property $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$:

$$\hat{X}(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[T_0(z)X(z) + T_1(z)X(-z)\bigr]$$

where $T_0(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[F_0(z)H_0(z) + F_1(z)H_0(-z)\bigr]$ is the **distortion transfer function** and $T_1(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[F_0(z)H_0(-z) + F_1(z)H_0(z)\bigr]$ is the **alias transfer function**.

**Step 3:** For perfect alias cancellation, we require $T_1(z) = 0$ for all $z$. The canonical choice is $F_0(z) = 2H_0(z)$ and $F_1(z) = -2H_0(-z)$, yielding the **orthogonal (power-symmetric) QMF** with $T_1(z) \equiv 0$.

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Polyphase Decomposition for $M=2$

**Given:** $H(z) = 1 + 2z^{-1} + 3z^{-2} + 4z^{-3} + 5z^{-4} + 6z^{-5}$ (length 6).

**Step 1:** Identify coefficients: $h[0]=1, h[1]=2, h[2]=3, h[3]=4, h[4]=5, h[5]=6$.

**Step 2:** Split by even and odd indices for $M=2$:

$$E_0(z) = h[0] + h[2]z^{-1} + h[4]z^{-2} = 1 + 3z^{-1} + 5z^{-2}$$

$$E_1(z) = h[1] + h[3]z^{-1} + h[5]z^{-2} = 2 + 4z^{-1} + 6z^{-2}$$

**Step 3:** Verify: $H(z) = E_0(z^2) + z^{-1}E_1(z^2) = (1 + 3z^{-2} + 5z^{-4}) + z^{-1}(2 + 4z^{-2} + 6z^{-4})$.

$$H(z) = 1 + 2z^{-1} + 3z^{-2} + 4z^{-3} + 5z^{-4} + 6z^{-5} \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Direct-Form Decimator Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input x(n)"] --> B["Lowpass Filter H(z) cutoff pi/M"]
    B --> C["Downsampler ↓M"]
    C --> D["Output y(n) at rate Fs/M"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **naive structure**. The lowpass filter operates at the *input rate* $F_s$, which is computationally wasteful. The polyphase version in §4.3 fixes this.

## 4.2 Direct-Form Interpolator Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input x(n) at rate Fs/L"] --> B["Upsampler ↑L insert zeros"]
    B --> C["Lowpass Filter H(z) cutoff pi/L"]
    C --> D["Output y(n) at rate Fs"]
```

## 4.3 Efficient Polyphase Decimator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input x(n)"] --> B1["Delay z-0"]
    A --> B2["Delay z-1"]
    A --> B3["Delay z-M+1"]
    B1 --> C1["Sub-filter E0(z)"]
    B2 --> C2["Sub-filter E1(z)"]
    B3 --> C3["Sub-filter E(M-1) z"]
    C1 --> D["Adder Sum"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E["Downsampler ↓M"]
    E --> F["Output y(n) at Fs/M"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** All sub-filters $E_k(z)$ operate at the **output (low) rate**, NOT the input rate. This is the source of the $M$-fold computational saving.

## 4.4 Two-Channel QMF Analysis-Synthesis Filter Bank

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input x(n)"] --> B1["H0(z) Lowpass Analysis"]
    A --> B2["H1(z) Highpass Analysis"]
    B1 --> C1["Down ↓2"]
    B2 --> C2["Down ↓2"]
    C1 --> D1["Up ↑2"]
    C2 --> D2["Up ↑2"]
    D1 --> E1["F0(z) Lowpass Synthesis"]
    D2 --> E2["F1(z) Highpass Synthesis"]
    E1 --> F["Adder Combiner"]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G["Output xhat(n) reconstructed"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept Link:** The QMF bank is the **father of the Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)**. Cascading QMF banks recursively (Mallat's algorithm) generates an octave-band wavelet decomposition used in JPEG2000.

## 4.5 Polyphase Decomposition Block Diagram (Type-1)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input x(n)"] --> B1["z-0 branch"]
    A --> B2["z-1 branch"]
    A --> B3["z-(M-1) branch"]
    B1 --> C1["E0(z^M)"]
    B2 --> C2["E1(z^M)"]
    B3 --> C3["E(M-1) z^M"]
    C1 --> D["Adder"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E["Output y(n)"]
```

## 4.6 Computational Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Decimation Task"] --> B{"Filter Order N"}
    B --> C1["Direct Form: N+1 mult per input sample"]
    B --> C2["Polyphase: N/M+1 mult per branch × M branches"]
    C1 --> D1["Total = M(N+1) per output"]
    C2 --> D2["Total = N+M per output"]
    D1 --> E["Computational Ratio M:1 in favor of Polyphase"]
    D2 --> E
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define decimation and interpolation in multirate signal processing. State the role of the anti-aliasing filter in the decimation process.**

**Model Answer:**
Decimation is the process of reducing the sampling rate of a discrete signal by an integer factor $M$ using a lowpass filter (anti-aliasing) followed by a downsampler. Interpolation is the dual process that increases the sampling rate by an integer factor $L$ via an upsampler followed by a lowpass filter (anti-imaging). The anti-aliasing filter, with cutoff $\omega_c = \pi/M$, band-limits the input signal to prevent **aliasing** of frequency components above $\pi/M$ when the sample rate is reduced. **(3 marks)**

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is polyphase decomposition? Why is it preferred over direct-form implementation for decimation?**

**Model Answer:**
Polyphase decomposition splits a transfer function $H(z)$ into $M$ sub-filters $E_k(z)$ such that $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} E_k(z^M)$. It is preferred because the sub-filters operate at the *lower output rate* (not the input rate), yielding an $M$-fold reduction in multiplications per second — critical for real-time DSP systems. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the polyphase decomposition of a FIR filter $H(z)$ with $M$ branches. Show that the polyphase implementation of an $M$-fold decimator reduces computational complexity by factor $M$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and prove the two Noble identities for multirate systems. Illustrate with block diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Polyphase Decomposition:** (See §3.1 for full derivation.)
Starting from $H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N} h[n] z^{-n}$, we substitute $n = k + mM$:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} \underbrace{\sum_{m} h[k+mM](z^M)^{-m}}_{E_k(z^M)}$$

Defining $E_k(z) = \sum_{m} h[k+mM]z^{-m}$ gives $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} E_k(z^M)$. The Type-2 form: $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-(M-1-k)} R_k(z^M)$.

For the $M$-fold decimator, direct form requires $M(N+1)$ multiplications per output sample. The polyphase structure moves each $E_k(z^M)$ to the right of the downsampler (by Noble Identity 1), where it becomes $E_k(z)$ of order $N/M$. Total cost = $M \cdot (N/M + 1) = N + M$ multiplications per output. **Computational saving ratio = $\frac{M(N+1)}{N+M} \approx M$ for large $N$.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Noble Identities:**
- **Identity 1:** $H(z)(\downarrow M) = (\downarrow M) H(z^M)$.
  - *Proof:* LHS: $y_L[n] = \sum_k h[k] x[nM - k]$. RHS: $y_R[n] = \sum_k h[k] x[(n-k)M] = \sum_k h[k] x[nM-k]$. Equal. ✓
- **Identity 2:** $(\uparrow L) H(z) = H(z^L)(\uparrow L)$.
  - *Proof:* LHS: $w[n] = (\uparrow L)x \Rightarrow w[n] = x[n/L]$ for $L \mid n$. Then $y_L[n] = \sum_k h[k] w[n-k] = \sum_k h[k] x[(n-k)/L]$ when $L \mid (n-k)$. RHS: $v[n] = \sum_k h[k] x[n-k]$, then $y_R[n] = v[n/L] = \sum_k h[k] x[(n-k)/L]$. Equal. ✓

Block diagrams show filter-downsampler and upsampler-filter commutativity. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the structure and operation of a two-channel Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) bank. Derive the conditions for perfect reconstruction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the FIR filter $H(z) = 1 + 2z^{-1} + 3z^{-2} + 4z^{-3} + 5z^{-4} + 6z^{-5}$, find the polyphase components for $M=3$ and sketch the efficient decimator structure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Two-Channel QMF Bank:** A two-channel QMF bank consists of an analysis bank $\{H_0(z), H_1(z)\}$ with $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ (mirror property), downsamplers by 2, and a synthesis bank $\{F_0(z), F_1(z)\}$. The reconstructed signal is:

$$\hat{X}(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[F_0(z)H_0(z) + F_1(z)H_1(z)\bigr]X(z) + \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[F_0(z)H_0(-z) + F_1(z)H_1(-z)\bigr]X(-z)$$

**Perfect reconstruction conditions:**
- **Alias cancellation:** $F_0(z)H_0(-z) + F_1(z)H_1(-z) = 0$
- **No distortion:** $F_0(z)H_0(z) + F_1(z)H_1(z) = 2z^{-d}$ (pure delay)

The orthogonal QMF choice $F_0(z) = 2H_0(z)$, $F_1(z) = -2H_1(z)$ satisfies both conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Polyphase for $M=3$:** Given coefficients $h[0]=1, h[1]=2, h[2]=3, h[3]=4, h[4]=5, h[5]=6$.

For $M=3$, the three branches are indexed by $k=0, 1, 2$:

$$E_0(z) = h[0] + h[3]z^{-1} = 1 + 4z^{-1}$$

$$E_1(z) = h[1] + h[4]z^{-1} = 2 + 5z^{-1}$$

$$E_2(z) = h[2] + h[5]z^{-1} = 3 + 6z^{-1}$$

**Verification:** $H(z) = E_0(z^3) + z^{-1}E_1(z^3) + z^{-2}E_2(z^3) = (1+4z^{-3}) + z^{-1}(2+5z^{-3}) + z^{-2}(3+6z^{-3})$

$$= 1 + 2z^{-1} + 3z^{-2} + 4z^{-3} + 5z^{-4} + 6z^{-5} \checkmark$$

**Efficient structure:** The decimator uses three parallel branches $E_k(z)$ with delays $z^{-k}$ for $k=0,1,2$, summed and downsampled by 3. (See §4.3 block diagram.) Each branch requires 2 multiplications per output; total = 6 multiplications per output vs. direct form's 18 multiplications per input (= 54 per output) — a $9\times$ speedup. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing decimation with mere downsampling** — Decimation = lowpass filter + downsampler, NOT just sample-dropping. (-2 marks if filter omitted.)
> 2. **Wrong polyphase index** — Many students write $E_k(z) = \sum h[kM+n]z^{-n}$ (incorrect) instead of $E_k(z) = \sum h[k + mM]z^{-m}$ (correct). The index is $k + mM$, NOT $kM + m$. (-3 marks.)
> 3. **Forgetting the mirror property** $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ in QMF derivations — without this, perfect reconstruction cannot be derived. (-2 marks.)
> 4. **Computing computational complexity incorrectly** — Count multiplications per *output* sample, not per input sample. The polyphase advantage is per output, since the filter runs at the low rate. (-1 mark.)
> 5. **Missing the $z^{-k}$ delay terms** in the polyphase block diagram — these delays are essential for correct time-alignment of the parallel branches. (-1 mark.)
> 6. **Sign errors in alias-cancellation derivation** — Watch the sign of $X(-z)$ in the $T_1$ term carefully; one sign error propagates through the entire reconstruction condition. (-2 marks.)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Decimation** = Anti-aliasing LPF (cutoff $\pi/M$) + Downsampler $\downarrow M$. Output rate = $F_s/M$.
- **Interpolation** = Upsampler $\uparrow L$ (zero-insertion) + Anti-imaging LPF (cutoff $\pi/L$). Output rate = $L \cdot F_s$.
- **Noble Identity 1:** $H(z)(\downarrow M) = (\downarrow M) H(z^M)$ — push filter past downsampler to lower rate.
- **Noble Identity 2:** $(\uparrow L) H(z) = H(z^L)(\uparrow L)$ — pull filter before upsampler to higher rate.
- **Type-1 Polyphase:** $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} E_k(z^M)$ with $E_k(z) = \sum_m h[k+mM]z^{-m}$.
- **Type-2 Polyphase:** $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-(M-1-k)} R_k(z^M)$ with $R_k(z) = E_{M-1-k}(z)$.
- **Polyphase advantage:** Computes at output rate, not input rate — saves factor of $M$ in multiplications.
- **Two-channel QMF:** $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ (mirror); $F_0 = 2H_0$, $F_1 = -2H_1$ for perfect reconstruction.
- **Perfect reconstruction conditions:** Alias term = 0, distortion term = $2z^{-d}$ (pure delay).
- **QMF → DWT link:** Cascading QMF banks with octave-band splitting yields the **Mallat's algorithm** for DWT.
- **QMF applications:** Subband coders (MP3, AAC), wavelet-based image compression (JPEG2000), DTMF detection, OFDM modulators.
- **Standard complexity:** Direct FIR decimator: $M(N+1)$ mults/output; Polyphase: $\approx N+M$ mults/output.
- **Anti-aliasing/anti-imaging filter** is the SAME filter type (ideal lowpass) but serves different purposes in the two operations.
- **Critical formula to memorize:** $\hat{X}(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[T_0(z)X(z) + T_1(z)X(-z)]$ — distortion + alias split.
- **Always verify** the polyphase decomposition by substituting back: $E_0(z^M) + z^{-1}E_1(z^M) + \ldots \stackrel{?}{=} H(z)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficient Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Efficient Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of multirate digital signal processing (DSP) and discrete wavelet transforms (DWT), **Efficient Implementation** refers to the set of algebraic, structural, and architectural techniques used to compute the output of a filter bank or wavelet transform with the **minimum possible number of arithmetic operations (multiplications and additions) per input sample**.

Formally, given a 2-channel analysis–synthesis filter bank with analysis filters $H_0(z)$ and $H_1(z)$ of length $N$, the *naive* direct-form implementation requires $2N$ multiplications and $2(N-1)$ additions per output sample pair. **Efficient implementation** techniques — namely **polyphase decomposition**, **Noble identities**, and **lattice factorizations** — restructure this computation so that the filtering operations are performed at the **lower (downsampled) rate**, yielding a complexity reduction by a factor of **2** (and a further reduction to $O(N)$ instead of $O(N^2)$ in the cascade case).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Efficient Implementation):**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to be able to (a) derive the polyphase components of a given filter, (b) apply the **first** and **second Noble identities** to redraw filter bank structures, (c) implement the 2-channel quadrature mirror filter (QMF) bank in its efficient polyphase form, and (d) understand the **Mallat pyramid algorithm** as the canonical efficient DWT structure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Restaurant Order Aggregator"**
> Imagine a busy restaurant where 1000 raw food items (input samples) arrive at a kitchen every hour. The head chef (the main filter $H_0$) usually has to inspect every single item to decide which dish it becomes — slow and expensive.
>
> **Efficient Implementation is like a smart prep-staff:** before the chef even sees the food, the staff *first separates* the food into two pre-sorted trays — odd-indexed items on Tray A and even-indexed items on Tray B (this is **polyphase decomposition**). Then, instead of feeding all 1000 items through the chef, the staff downsamples (removes duplicates) so only 500 items reach the chef — but they arrive at the *chef's own natural working speed* (this is the **Noble identity**). The chef now does **half the work for the same result**.
>
> For multi-level processing (a 3-course meal), instead of redoing the sorting for every course, the kitchen uses a **pyramid** — each course refines the previous one. This is the **Mallat algorithm**.

## 1.3 Why "Efficient"?

In real-time DSP applications such as **JPEG2000 image compression**, **ECG signal denoising**, **biometric feature extraction**, and **software-defined radio**, signals are sampled at rates of **kHz to MHz**. A naive filter bank implementation would:

| Issue | Impact |
|---|---|
| Perform filtering at the *high* input rate | Wasted MAC operations on samples that will be discarded by downsampling |
| Require long filters for sharp transitions | $O(N^2)$ complexity in cascaded stages |
| Consume excess power | Critical in battery-operated and embedded devices |

The goal of efficient implementation is therefore to **move filtering operations behind the downsamplers** (so they operate at the lowest possible rate) and to **factor** long filters into a cascade of simpler, computationally cheap stages.

## 1.4 Key Block Components of Efficient Implementation

The three pillars of efficient implementation in Module 1 are:

1. **Polyphase Decomposition** — splits any filter $H(z)$ into $M$ sub-filters (its *polyphase components*), each operating on a different phase of the input.
2. **Noble Identities** — algebraic equivalences that allow downsamplers/upsamplers to be commuted past filters.
3. **Lattice Structures** — recursive factorizations of paraunitary filter banks into a cascade of simple **rotation ( Householder-type )** stages.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polyphase Decomposition — The Core Idea

Any FIR filter of order $N-1$ (length $N$) can be written as:

$$H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h[n]\, z^{-n}$$

If we group every $M$-th coefficient together, we obtain the **Type-1 Polyphase Decomposition**:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k}\, E_k(z^M)$$

where the polyphase components are:

$$E_k(z) = \sum_{m} h[mM + k]\, z^{-m}, \qquad k = 0, 1, \dots, M-1$$

For **2-channel filter banks** ($M=2$), this becomes:

$$H_0(z) = E_0(z^2) + z^{-1}\, E_1(z^2)$$
$$H_1(z) = E_0(z^2) - z^{-1}\, E_1(z^2) \quad \text{(for alias cancellation, QMF case)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Polyphase"?** The components $E_k(z)$ contain only those samples that share the *same phase* (residue mod $M$) relative to the downsampler. Each $E_k$ thus "sees" the input at $1/M$ the original rate.

## 2.2 Noble Identities

The two Noble identities are the algebraic backbone of efficient implementation. They tell us *when* we can swap a filter and a downsampler/upsampler without changing the system's input–output behavior.

**First Noble Identity (Downsampler commutes with filter):**

$$\boxed{\;H(z)\,\bigl(\downarrow M\bigr) = \bigl(\downarrow M\bigr)\,H(z^M)\;}$$

**Second Noble Identity (Upsampler commutes with filter):**

$$\boxed{\;\bigl(\uparrow M\bigr)\,H(z) = H(z^M)\,\bigl(\uparrow M\bigr)\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition behind Noble Identities:**
> - A filter $H(z)$ followed by a downsampler only needs to *compute* every $M$-th output. So instead of computing all $M$ outputs, compute only one — but the filter must now "stretch" in time by a factor of $M$. This stretching corresponds to $H(z^M)$.
> - The *reverse* holds for upsamplers: a filter following an upsampler sees only non-zero samples spaced $M$ apart, so its coefficients need only act on the actual (non-zero) inputs — hence $H(z^M)$.

## 2.3 The Polyphase Matrix

The two analysis filters of a 2-channel filter bank can be written as a **polyphase matrix**:

$$\mathbf{H}_p(z) = \begin{bmatrix} E_{00}(z) & E_{01}(z) \\ E_{10}(z) & E_{11}(z) \end{bmatrix}$$

such that:

$$\begin{bmatrix} H_0(z) \\ H_1(z) \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{H}_p(z^2) \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z^{-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

The **efficient implementation** uses the identity:

$$\begin{bmatrix} H_0(z) \\ H_1(z) \end{bmatrix} (\downarrow 2) = \mathbf{H}_p(z) \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z^{-1} \end{bmatrix} (\downarrow 2)$$

i.e., filtering is performed at the **downsampled rate**.

## 2.4 Paraunitary Condition and Lattice Factorization

A polyphase matrix $\mathbf{H}_p(z)$ is **paraunitary** if:

$$\mathbf{H}_p(z)\,\mathbf{H}_p^\dagger(z^{-1}) = c\,\mathbf{I}$$

For real-coefficient orthogonal filter banks, $c=1$. Such a matrix can be factorized as a cascade of **lattice stages**:

$$\mathbf{H}_p(z) = \mathbf{R}_K \cdot \mathbf{\Lambda}(z) \cdot \mathbf{R}_{K-1} \cdots \mathbf{\Lambda}(z) \cdot \mathbf{R}_1 \cdot \mathbf{R}_0$$

where each $\mathbf{R}_i$ is a 2×2 **rotation matrix**:

$$\mathbf{R}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & \sin\theta_i \\ -\sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \end{bmatrix}$$

and $\mathbf{\Lambda}(z) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & z^{-1} \end{bmatrix}$ is the **delay** matrix.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why lattices matter (KTU relevance):** They reduce the *design* problem (find $N$ filter coefficients) to finding $K = N/2$ **rotation angles** $\theta_i$. These are numerically well-conditioned and lead to lossless structures used in JPEG2000 (Cohen–Daubechies–Feauveau 9/7 wavelet).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Concept** | **Equation / Identity** | **Use in Exam** |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Type-1 Polyphase | $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} E_k(z^M)$ | Decompose any $H(z)$ |
| 2 | Polyphase component | $E_k(z) = \sum_{m} h[mM+k] z^{-m}$ | Compute components |
| 3 | First Noble Identity | $H(z) (\downarrow M) = (\downarrow M) H(z^M)$ | Move filter after down-sampler |
| 4 | Second Noble Identity | $(\uparrow M) H(z) = H(z^M) (\uparrow M)$ | Move filter before up-sampler |
| 5 | Polyphase matrix form | $\mathbf{H}(z) = \mathbf{H}_p(z^2) \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z^{-1} \end{bmatrix}$ | Compact representation |
| 6 | Efficient 2-ch. analysis | $\begin{bmatrix} H_0 \\ H_1 \end{bmatrix}(\downarrow 2) = \mathbf{H}_p(z) \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z^{-1} \end{bmatrix}(\downarrow 2)$ | $2\times$ saving |
| 7 | Paraunitary condition | $\mathbf{H}_p(z)\mathbf{H}_p^T(z^{-1}) = \mathbf{I}$ | Lossless / orthogonal FB |
| 8 | Lattice rotation | $\mathbf{R}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & \sin\theta_i \\ -\sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \end{bmatrix}$ | Factor paraunitary matrix |
| 9 | Delay matrix | $\mathbf{\Lambda}(z) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & z^{-1} \end{bmatrix}$ | Stage between rotations |
| 10 | Mallat's algorithm | $a_{j}[n] = \sum_k h_0[k-2n]\, a_{j+1}[k]$ | Iterative DWT |
| 11 | QMF alias-cancel | $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ | Standard QMF pair |
| 12 | Computational saving | Factor of **2** per stage for 2-ch FB | Estimate in exam |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where is this used in industry?**
> - **JPEG2000 Image Compression:** Uses the CDF 9/7 wavelet, whose efficient lattice implementation enables real-time compression of medical and satellite images.
> - **Wearable ECG Monitors (e.g., Apple Watch, Holter monitors):** Multi-resolution analysis with efficient DWT detects QRS complexes at minimum power.
> - **5G / Software-Defined Radio (SDR):** Channelizers based on polyphase DFT filter banks split wideband signals into sub-channels efficiently.
> - **Audio Coding (MP3, AAC, Opus):** Sub-band filtering via polyphase QMF banks reduces the data rate by factor of 32 with perceptual transparency.
> - **Seismic Signal Processing:** Multi-level DWT extracts features at different frequency resolutions, all in real time thanks to efficient Mallat-tree implementations.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Type-1 Polyphase Decomposition

**Starting point:** Any FIR filter $H(z)$ of order $N-1$:

$$H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h[n]\,z^{-n}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute** $n = mM + k$, where $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, M-1\}$ and $m \in \mathbb{Z}$.

When $n$ runs from $0$ to $N-1$, for each fixed $k$, the index $m$ runs over an appropriate range. Thus:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} \sum_{m} h[mM + k]\, z^{-(mM + k)}$$

**Step 2 — Factor** $z^{-(mM+k)} = z^{-k} \cdot (z^M)^{-m}$:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} \left( \sum_{m} h[mM+k]\,(z^M)^{-m} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Define the polyphase component:**

$$\boxed{E_k(z) = \sum_{m} h[mM+k]\, z^{-m}}$$

Therefore:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k}\, E_k(z^M) \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Worked Example (KTU-style):** Given $H(z) = 1 + 2z^{-1} + 3z^{-2} + 4z^{-3} + 5z^{-4} + 6z^{-5}$, find the $M=2$ polyphase components.

*Step A:* Identify $h[0]=1, h[1]=2, h[2]=3, h[3]=4, h[4]=5, h[5]=6$.

*Step B:* For $k=0$ (even indices): $h[0]=1,\; h[2]=3,\; h[4]=5$, so:
$$E_0(z) = 1 + 3z^{-1} + 5z^{-2}$$

*Step C:* For $k=1$ (odd indices): $h[1]=2,\; h[3]=4,\; h[5]=6$, so:
$$E_1(z) = 2 + 4z^{-1} + 6z^{-2}$$

*Step D:* Verify the reconstruction:
$$H(z) = E_0(z^2) + z^{-1}E_1(z^2) = (1 + 3z^{-2} + 5z^{-4}) + z^{-1}(2 + 4z^{-2} + 6z^{-4})$$
$$= 1 + 2z^{-1} + 3z^{-2} + 4z^{-3} + 5z^{-4} + 6z^{-5} \;\checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation: First Noble Identity

**Claim:** $H(z) (\downarrow M) = (\downarrow M) H(z^M)$.

**Step 1 — LHS:** Let $v[n] = (h * x)[n]$ be the filter output, and $y[n] = v[nM]$ be the downsampled output. In the $z$-domain:

$$V(z) = H(z) X(z), \qquad Y(z) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{l=0}^{M-1} V(W_M^l z^{1/M})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** $V(z)$:

$$Y(z) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{l=0}^{M-1} H(W_M^l z^{1/M})\, X(W_M^l z^{1/M})$$

**Step 3 — RHS:** Compute $(\downarrow M) H(z^M) X(z)$. The downsampler applied to $W(z) = H(z^M) X(z)$ gives:

$$Y_{RHS}(z) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{l=0}^{M-1} H\!\left((W_M^l z^{1/M})^M\right) X(W_M^l z^{1/M}) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{l=0}^{M-1} H(W_M^{lM} z)\, X(W_M^l z^{1/M})$$

**Step 4 — Use the identity** $W_M^{lM} = e^{-j2\pi l} = 1$, so $H(W_M^{lM} z) = H(z)$. Thus:

$$Y_{RHS}(z) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{l=0}^{M-1} H(z)\, X(W_M^l z^{1/M}) = H(z) \cdot \frac{1}{M} \sum_{l=0}^{M-1} X(W_M^l z^{1/M})$$

**Step 5 — Compare with LHS:** Both sides reduce to the same expression. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation: Efficient 2-Channel Filter Bank Using Polyphase + Noble Identity

**Step 1 — Start with the standard 2-channel analysis bank:**

$$V_0(z) = H_0(z) X(z), \qquad V_1(z) = H_1(z) X(z)$$
$$Y_0(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[V_0(z^{1/2}) + V_0(-z^{1/2})\bigr], \qquad Y_1(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[V_1(z^{1/2}) + V_1(-z^{1/2})\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the polyphase decomposition** to both $H_0$ and $H_1$:

$$H_0(z) = E_{00}(z^2) + z^{-1} E_{01}(z^2)$$
$$H_1(z) = E_{10}(z^2) + z^{-1} E_{11}(z^2)$$

So:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_0(z) \\ V_1(z) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} E_{00}(z^2) & z^{-1}E_{01}(z^2) \\ E_{10}(z^2) & z^{-1}E_{11}(z^2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X(z) \\ z^{-1} X(z) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} E_{00}(z^2) & E_{01}(z^2) \\ E_{10}(z^2) & E_{11}(z^2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X(z) \\ z^{-1} X(z) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Factor the polyphase matrix:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_0(z) \\ V_1(z) \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{H}_p(z^2) \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z^{-1} \end{bmatrix} X(z)$$

**Step 4 — Now apply the downsampler.** Use the First Noble Identity to *pull* the polyphase filter **behind** the downsampler:

$$Y_0(z) = (\downarrow 2)\, V_0(z) = (\downarrow 2)\, E_{00}(z^2) X(z) = E_{00}(z)\, (\downarrow 2) X(z)$$

**Step 5 — The complete efficient structure** is therefore:

$$\begin{bmatrix} Y_0(z) \\ Y_1(z) \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{H}_p(z) \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z^{-1} \end{bmatrix} (\downarrow 2)\, X(z)$$

**Computational saving:**

| Quantity | Naive | Efficient |
|---|---|---|
| Multiplications per input sample | $N$ per filter $\times 2$ filters $= 2N$ | $N/2$ per polyphase component $\times 4 = 2N$ at half rate $= N$ |
| Effective MACs per output sample | $2N$ | $N$ |
| **Speed-up factor** | 1 | **2×** |

$\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation: Mallat's Pyramid Algorithm (DWT Cascade)

**Setup:** A discrete-time signal $a_{J}[n]$ is split into a *coarse* approximation $a_{J-1}[n]$ and a *detail* $d_{J-1}[n]$ at each level:

$$a_{j-1}[n] = \sum_{k} h_0[k - 2n]\, a_j[k] \qquad \text{(low-pass + downsample)}$$
$$d_{j-1}[n] = \sum_{k} h_1[k - 2n]\, a_j[k] \qquad \text{(high-pass + downsample)}$$

**Step 1 — Express as convolution + decimation:** Let $g_0[n] = h_0[-n]$ (time-reversed low-pass), then:

$$a_{j-1}[n] = (g_0 * a_j)[2n] = \bigl((\downarrow 2)(h_0 * a_j)\bigr)[n]$$

**Step 2 — Apply polyphase form** (efficient):

$$\begin{bmatrix} A_{j-1}(z) \\ D_{j-1}(z) \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{H}_p(z) \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z^{-1} \end{bmatrix} (\downarrow 2)\, A_j(z)$$

**Step 3 — Cascade** $J$ such stages. The output consists of $1$ coarse approximation and $J$ detail sequences, with total sample count:

$$\underbrace{N}_{\text{original}} = \underbrace{N/2^J}_{\text{coarse}} + \underbrace{\sum_{j=0}^{J-1} N/2^{j+1}}_{\text{details}} = N/2^J + N(1 - 1/2^J) = N \;\checkmark$$

**Complexity of full $J$-level DWT:**

| Stage | Samples input | MACs per output | Output length | Total MACs |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N$ | $N$ | $N/2$ | $N^2/2$ |
| 2 | $N/2$ | $N/2$ | $N/4$ | $N^2/4$ |
| ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ |
| Total | — | — | — | $N^2(1 - 2^{-J}) \approx N^2$ |

Compare to direct convolution of a length-$N$ filter across all $J$ levels: $O(J N^2)$. Mallat's algorithm achieves the **same** $O(N^2)$ total — but the *constant* is much smaller, and the structure is **multi-resolution**: each level produces a *new frequency band* (octave-band). $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Derivation: Lattice Factorization of a 2-Channel Paraunitary Filter Bank

**Claim:** Any 2-channel paraunitary polyphase matrix of order $K$ can be written as:

$$\mathbf{H}_p(z) = \mathbf{R}_K \cdot \mathbf{\Lambda}(z) \cdot \mathbf{R}_{K-1} \cdots \mathbf{\Lambda}(z) \cdot \mathbf{R}_0$$

**Step 1 — For $K=1$** (length-2 filters), the polyphase matrix is:

$$\mathbf{H}_p(z) = \begin{bmatrix} h_0[0] & h_0[1] \\ h_1[0] & h_1[1] \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Paraunitarity** requires $\mathbf{H}_p \mathbf{H}_p^T = \mathbf{I}$. This forces:

$$h_0[0]^2 + h_0[1]^2 = 1, \qquad h_1[0] = -h_0[1], \qquad h_1[1] = h_0[0]$$

**Step 3 — Set** $h_0[0] = \cos\theta_0$, $h_0[1] = \sin\theta_0$:

$$\mathbf{H}_p(z) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_0 & \sin\theta_0 \\ -\sin\theta_0 & \cos\theta_0 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{R}_0$$

**Step 4 — For $K=2$** (length-4 filters), factor out one rotation and one delay:

$$\mathbf{H}_p(z) = \mathbf{R}_1 \cdot \mathbf{\Lambda}(z) \cdot \mathbf{R}_0$$

Verify paraunitarity:
$$\mathbf{H}_p(z)\mathbf{H}_p^T(z^{-1}) = \mathbf{R}_1 \mathbf{\Lambda}(z) \mathbf{R}_0 \mathbf{R}_0^T \mathbf{\Lambda}^T(z^{-1}) \mathbf{R}_1^T = \mathbf{R}_1 \mathbf{\Lambda}(z)\mathbf{\Lambda}(z^{-1}) \mathbf{R}_1^T = \mathbf{R}_1 \mathbf{R}_1^T = \mathbf{I} \;\checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Inductively continue** for higher $K$. The angle $\theta_i$ at each stage is found by **matching coefficients** of the original filter with the cascaded lattice form. $\blacksquare$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Operation Count Comparison

Let $N$ = filter length, $M$ = number of channels, $J$ = DWT levels.

| Structure | MACs per input sample | Memory (states) | Numerically Robust? | KTU Exam Favorite? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct 2-ch FB (naive)** | $2N$ | $2(N-1)$ | Moderate | Yes (compare) |
| **Efficient polyphase 2-ch** | $N$ | $N$ | Yes | ⭐ Yes |
| **Polyphase $M$-ch DFT bank** | $N$ | $N$ | High | ⭐ Yes |
| **Mallat DWT (J levels)** | $\approx N$ (per output) | $\sum_j (N/2^j) < 2N$ | Yes | ⭐ Yes |
| **Lattice (paraunitary)** | $N$ | $N$ | Excellent | Yes (theory) |
| **Lifting scheme** | $N/2$ | $N/4$ | Excellent | (Module 2) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Polyphase Decomposition of a Single Filter

The block diagram of the Type-1 polyphase decomposition of $H(z)$ into $M$ sub-filters $E_k(z^M)$, with the original structure on the left and the rearranged (equivalent) form on the right.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x["X(z)"] --> d1["delay z^-1"]
    x --> E0["E_0 z^2"]
    d1 --> E1["E_1 z^2"]
    d1 --> E2["E_2 z^2"]
    d1 --> d3["delay z^-1"]
    d3 --> E3["E_3 z^2"]
    E0 --> s1["sum Σ"]
    E1 --> s1
    E2 --> s1
    E3 --> s1
    s1 --> y["Y(z) = H(z) X(z)"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The signal $X(z)$ is split into $M$ phase-shifted versions (one delay per phase increment). Each phase is filtered by the *low-rate* polyphase component $E_k(z^2)$ (note the argument $z^2$ corresponds to a 2× expansion in time). The results are summed to give the original $Y(z)$.

## 4.2 Efficient 2-Channel Analysis Bank (Polyphase + Noble Identity)

The **canonical efficient structure** that KTU examiners love to draw: the input is *first* delayed and downsampled (using the identity branch), and *then* filtered by the low-rate polyphase matrix $\mathbf{H}_p(z)$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x["X(z)"] --> split{"split"}
    split --> top["direct path"]
    split --> bot["delay z^-1"]
    top --> ds1["(↓ 2)"]
    bot --> ds2["(↓ 2)"]
    ds1 --> H00["E_00 z"]
    ds1 --> H10["E_10 z"]
    bot --> H01["E_01 z"]
    bot --> H11["E_11 z"]
    H00 --> sum0["+"]
    H01 --> sum0
    H10 --> sum1["+"]
    H11 --> sum1
    sum0 --> Y0["Y_0 z  lowpass output"]
    sum1 --> Y1["Y_1 z  highpass output"]
```

> **Examiner's tip:** Note that *all four* polyphase filters $E_{00}, E_{01}, E_{10}, E_{11}$ now operate on the **downsampled** signal. The downsamplers are *pushed to the front*, eliminating redundant computation.

## 4.3 Mallat's Pyramid Algorithm (3-Level DWT)

The recursive multi-resolution decomposition: the approximation at level $j$ feeds into the *next* level, while the detail is kept as output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2["a_2 n  finest level input"] --> L1["H_0  lowpass + downsample"]
    A2 --> H1["H_1  highpass + downsample"]
    L1 --> A1["a_1 n  level 1 approx"]
    L1 --> L2["H_0"]
    L1 --> Lh["H_1"]
    L2 --> A0["a_0 n  level 2 approx"]
    Lh --> D1["d_1 n  level 1 detail"]
    H1 --> D2["d_2 n  level 2 detail"]
```

> **Reading the pyramid:** At each level, the *low-frequency approximation* is recursively decomposed further, while the *high-frequency detail* is retained. The total data volume is preserved (critical & alias-cancelled), and complexity is $O(N)$.

## 4.4 Lattice Structure (Paraunitary Filter Bank, 2 Stages)

A cascade of 2×2 rotation matrices $\mathbf{R}_i$ interleaved with delay matrices $\mathbf{\Lambda}(z)$ — the hardware- and numerically-efficient implementation of a length-4 paraunitary filter bank.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in["X z"] --> R0["R_0  rotation θ_0"]
    R0 --> Lam1["Λ z  = diag 1  z^-1"]
    Lam1 --> R1["R_1  rotation θ_1"]
    R1 --> Lam2["Λ z  = diag 1  z^-1"]
    Lam2 --> R2["R_2  rotation θ_2"]
    R2 --> out0["Y_0 z  analysis output 0"]
    R2 --> out1["Y_1 z  analysis output 1"]
```

> **Engineering significance:** A single rotation stage uses only **2 multiplications and 2 additions** (4 MACs) — independent of filter length. Increasing the filter order only requires *adding* more lattice stages, making the structure **modular** and **pipeline-friendly** for VLSI implementation.

## 4.5 Computational-Flow Block Architecture of the Full Efficient Implementation

For KTU 2024, the *most-asked* high-level diagram is the one that shows the **before / after** of applying polyphase + Noble identity.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NAIVE["NAIVE STRUCTURE  O 2N per sample"]
        N1["X z"] --> N2["H_0 z"]
        N1 --> N3["H_1 z"]
        N2 --> N4["(↓ 2)"]
        N3 --> N5["(↓ 2)"]
        N4 --> N6["Y_0 z"]
        N5 --> N7["Y_1 z"]
    end

    subgraph EFFICIENT["EFFICIENT STRUCTURE  O N per sample"]
        E1["X z"] --> E2["split into phases"]
        E2 --> E3["(↓ 2)  all branches"]
        E3 --> E4["H_p z  polyphase matrix"]
        E4 --> E5["Y_0 z"]
        E4 --> E6["Y_1 z"]
    end
```

> **Take-away:** The naive structure filters *first* (at the high rate) and downsamples *after*. The efficient structure downsamples *first* and filters *after* (at the low rate). The math says they are equivalent (by Noble identity), but the second uses **half the arithmetic**.
