# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State and prove the First Noble Identity for multirate systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The First Noble Identity states that a filter $H(z)$ followed by a downsampler by factor $M$ is equivalent to a downsampler by $M$ followed by the filter $H(z^M)$:

$$H(z)\,(\downarrow M) = (\downarrow M)\,H(z^M)$$

**Proof sketch:** Let $V(z) = H(z)X(z)$. The downsampled output is

$$Y(z) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{l=0}^{M-1} V(W_M^l z^{1/M}) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{l=0}^{M-1} H(W_M^l z^{1/M})\,X(W_M^l z^{1/M})$$

For the right-hand side, downsampling $H(z^M)X(z)$ gives $\frac{1}{M}\sum_l H((W_M^l z^{1/M})^M) X(W_M^l z^{1/M})$. Since $W_M^{lM}=1$, this equals $\frac{1}{M}\sum_l H(z) X(W_M^l z^{1/M})$, identical to the LHS. $\square$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only the *statement* and skip the *proof* — you lose 1–2 marks. Always show at least the *key algebraic step* (the $W_M^{lM} = 1$ identity).

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the polyphase decomposition of a filter? Why is it useful in efficient implementation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The polyphase decomposition expresses a filter $H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h[n]z^{-n}$ as a sum of $M$ sub-filters $E_k(z)$ that operate on the *evenly-spaced phases* of the input:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k}\,E_k(z^M), \qquad E_k(z) = \sum_{m} h[mM+k]z^{-m}$$

It is useful in efficient implementation because, **combined with the First Noble Identity**, it allows the filter bank to perform all filtering operations at the **downsampled rate**, halving the computational complexity. It also enables a **modular, multi-rate architecture** ideal for VLSI and real-time DSP. $\square$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Internal choice (OR) is provided within the module.

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** For the FIR filter $H(z) = 1 + 3z^{-1} + 5z^{-2} + 7z^{-3} + 2z^{-4} + 4z^{-5} + 6z^{-6} + 8z^{-7}$, find the **Type-1 polyphase components** for $M=2$. Show that the decomposition reconstructs $H(z)$ exactly. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **polyphase implementation of a 2-channel QMF analysis bank**. Show that the polyphase matrix form yields a 2× reduction in computational complexity. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients:**
$h[0]=1, h[1]=3, h[2]=5, h[3]=7, h[4]=2, h[5]=4, h[6]=6, h[7]=8$.

**Step 2 — Polyphase component for $k=0$ (even indices $n=0,2,4,6$):**

$$E_0(z) = h[0] + h[2]z^{-1} + h[4]z^{-2} + h[6]z^{-3} = 1 + 5z^{-1} + 2z^{-2} + 6z^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Polyphase component for $k=1$ (odd indices $n=1,3,5,7$):**

$$E_1(z) = h[1] + h[3]z^{-1} + h[5]z^{-2} + h[7]z^{-3} = 3 + 7z^{-1} + 4z^{-2} + 8z^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Reconstruct** $H(z)$ using the identity $H(z) = E_0(z^2) + z^{-1}E_1(z^2)$:

$$E_0(z^2) = 1 + 5z^{-2} + 2z^{-4} + 6z^{-6}$$
$$z^{-1}E_1(z^2) = 3z^{-1} + 7z^{-3} + 4z^{-5} + 8z^{-7}$$

**Step 5 — Sum:**

$$H(z) = 1 + 3z^{-1} + 5z^{-2} + 7z^{-3} + 2z^{-4} + 4z^{-5} + 6z^{-6} + 8z^{-7} \;\checkmark$$

This matches the original filter, confirming the decomposition. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Standard 2-channel analysis bank:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_0(z) \\ V_1(z) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} H_0(z) \\ H_1(z) \end{bmatrix} X(z), \qquad \begin{bmatrix} Y_0(z) \\ Y_1(z) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} V_0 \\ V_1 \end{bmatrix} (\downarrow 2)$$

**Step 2 — Apply polyphase decomposition** to $H_0$ and $H_1$:

$$H_0(z) = E_{00}(z^2) + z^{-1}E_{01}(z^2), \quad H_1(z) = E_{10}(z^2) + z^{-1}E_{11}(z^2)$$

Define the polyphase matrix:

$$\mathbf{H}_p(z) = \begin{bmatrix} E_{00}(z) & E_{01}(z) \\ E_{10}(z) & E_{11}(z) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Express the analysis output:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} Y_0(z) \\ Y_1(z) \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{H}_p(z^2)\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z^{-1} \end{bmatrix} X(z) \Big|_{\text{after } (\downarrow 2)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the First Noble Identity** to *pull* the polyphase filter behind the downsampler:

$$\boxed{\begin{bmatrix} Y_0(z) \\ Y_1(z) \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{H}_p(z)\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z^{-1} \end{bmatrix} (\downarrow 2)\, X(z)}$$

**Step 5 — Complexity comparison:**

| Implementation | MACs per input sample | Filter length $N$ | Total MACs / sample |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct | $2N$ | $N$ per filter | $2N$ |
| Polyphase + Noble | $N$ (split into $4 \times N/2$ at half rate) | $N/2$ | $N$ |
| **Saving factor** | — | — | **2×** |

**Conclusion:** The polyphase form achieves identical input–output behavior to the direct form but at **half the arithmetic cost**, with the additional benefit that the polyphase components are shorter ($N/2$ taps) and operate on a sparse, time-stretched signal. This is the foundation of all practical DWT and QMF implementations, including JPEG2000. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students often *stop* at the polyphase decomposition and forget to apply the **First Noble Identity** to actually achieve the 2× saving. Marks are reserved specifically for that critical step — write it out explicitly.

---

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Derive the polyphase decomposition of $H(z)$ for a general decimation factor $M$. Using the result, express a 4-channel analysis filter bank in its efficient polyphase form. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** What is a **paraunitary filter bank**? Show that the polyphase matrix of a 2-channel paraunitary filter bank of length-4 filters can be factorized as a cascade of two rotation stages and one delay matrix. Compute the rotation angles for the filter with impulse response $h_0 = \{\cos 15°, \sin 15°, \cos 45°, \sin 45°\}$. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Polyphase for general $M$:** Substituting $n = mM+k$ in $H(z) = \sum h[n]z^{-n}$:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k}\sum_{m} h[mM+k](z^M)^{-m} = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k}E_k(z^M)$$

**Step 2 — 4-channel analysis bank:** Four filters $H_i(z)$, $i=0,1,2,3$ each decomposed:

$$H_i(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{3} z^{-k}E_{ik}(z^4)$$

**Step 3 — Stack as a 4×4 polyphase matrix:**

$$\mathbf{H}_p(z) = \begin{bmatrix} E_{00}(z) & E_{01}(z) & E_{02}(z) & E_{03}(z) \\ E_{10}(z) & E_{11}(z) & E_{12}(z) & E_{13}(z) \\ E_{20}(z) & E_{21}(z) & E_{22}(z) & E_{23}(z) \\ E_{30}(z) & E_{31}(z) & E_{32}(z) & E_{33}(z) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Apply First Noble Identity:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} Y_0(z) \\ Y_1(z) \\ Y_2(z) \\ Y_3(z) \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{H}_p(z) \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z^{-1} \\ z^{-2} \\ z^{-3} \end{bmatrix} (\downarrow 4)\, X(z)$$

This uses $4\times$ the parallelism but reduces total work by $4\times$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Definition:** A 2-channel filter bank with polyphase matrix $\mathbf{H}_p(z)$ is **paraunitary** if it satisfies:

$$\mathbf{H}_p(z)\,\mathbf{H}_p^\dagger(z^{-1}) = c\,\mathbf{I}$$

For real-coefficient orthogonal filter banks, $c=1$ and $\dagger \to T$.

**Step 2 — Lattice factorization for length-4 filters:**

$$\mathbf{H}_p(z) = \mathbf{R}_1 \cdot \mathbf{\Lambda}(z) \cdot \mathbf{R}_0$$

where $\mathbf{R}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & \sin\theta_i \\ -\sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \end{bmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{\Lambda}(z) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & z^{-1} \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 3 — Expand:**

$$\mathbf{H}_p(z) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & \sin\theta_1 \\ -\sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_0 & \sin\theta_0 \\ -z^{-1}\sin\theta_0 & z^{-1}\cos\theta_0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Rightarrow \mathbf{H}_p(z) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1\cos\theta_0 - \sin\theta_1\sin\theta_0 z^{-1} & \cos\theta_1\sin\theta_0 + \sin\theta_1\cos\theta_0 z^{-1} \\ -\sin\theta_1\cos\theta_0 - \cos\theta_1\sin\theta_0 z^{-1} & -\sin\theta_1\sin\theta_0 + \cos\theta_1\cos\theta_0 z^{-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Read off coefficients** of $H_0(z) = E_{00}(z^2) + z^{-1}E_{01}(z^2)$:
- $E_{00}(z^2)$ constant term: $\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_0 = h_0[0] = \cos 15°$
- $E_{01}(z^2)$ constant term: $\cos\theta_1\sin\theta_0 = h_0[2] = \cos 45°$

Therefore: $\tan\theta_0 = \cos 45° / \cos 15° \approx 0.7654$, giving $\theta_0 \approx 37.43°$.

- $z^{-1}$ term of $E_{00}$: $-\sin\theta_1\sin\theta_0 = h_0[1] = \sin 15°$, so $\sin\theta_1 = -\sin 15° / \sin\theta_0 = -0.2588 / 0.6137 \approx -0.422$, giving $\theta_1 \approx -24.95°$ (or equivalently $155.05°$).

- $z^{-1}$ term of $E_{01}$: $\sin\theta_1\cos\theta_0 = h_0[3] = \sin 45°$, check: $(-0.422)(0.966) \approx -0.408 \neq 0.707$. ⚠️

> **Resolution:** The given filter pair does **not** form a paraunitary bank as stated — the constants violate $\mathbf{H}_p\mathbf{H}_p^T = \mathbf{I}$. A proper exam-suitable example would use $h_0 = \{\cos 15°, \sin 15°, -\sin 15°, \cos 15°\}$ (Haar-like rotation cascade), which gives $\theta_0 = 15°$, $\theta_1 = 15°$, and is provably paraunitary.

**Conclusion:** Lattice factorization converts a difficult filter design into a search over rotation angles — typically done via optimization (e.g., gradient descent on the angle set $\{\theta_0, \dots, \theta_{K-1}\}$). $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students often forget to **verify** the paraunitarity condition after writing the lattice. Always check $\mathbf{H}_p(z)\mathbf{H}_p^T(z^{-1}) = \mathbf{I}$ explicitly — full 1 mark is reserved for this in the marking scheme.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Efficient Implementation**

- [ ] **Polyphase Type-1 form:** $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} E_k(z^M)$, with $E_k(z) = \sum_m h[mM+k]z^{-m}$.
- [ ] **First Noble Identity:** $H(z)(\downarrow M) = (\downarrow M) H(z^M)$ — filter *before* down-sampler can be moved *after*, with $z \to z^M$.
- [ ] **Second Noble Identity:** $(\uparrow M)H(z) = H(z^M)(\uparrow M)$ — the dual for up-samplers.
- [ ] **Polyphase matrix** for 2-ch FB: $\mathbf{H}_p(z) = \begin{bmatrix} E_{00}(z) & E_{01}(z) \\ E_{10}(z) & E_{11}(z) \end{bmatrix}$.
- [ ] **Efficient 2-ch structure** uses $\mathbf{H}_p(z)$ *after* the downsampler → **2× MAC saving**.
- [ ] **Paraunitary** = $\mathbf{H}_p(z)\mathbf{H}_p^\dagger(z^{-1}) = c\mathbf{I}$ → lossless, numerically robust, used in JPEG2000.
- [ ] **Lattice factorization:** $\mathbf{H}_p(z) = \prod_i [\mathbf{R}_i\,\mathbf{\Lambda}(z)]$ with $\mathbf{R}_i$ being a 2×2 rotation by angle $\theta_i$.
- [ ] **Mallat's pyramid algorithm** recursively applies 2-ch FB on the lowpass branch → multi-resolution DWT with $O(N)$ complexity per level.
- [ ] **QMF alias-cancel condition:** $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ for the standard quadrature mirror pair.
- [ ] **Memory:** Polyphase form needs $N$ states vs. $2N$ in direct form — also saves memory.
- [ ] **Real-world hits:** JPEG2000 (CDF 9/7), MP3/AAC (polyphase QMF), 5G channelizers, ECG denoising.
- [ ] **Common exam mistakes:** Forgetting the First Noble Identity step; mixing up Type-I and Type-II polyphase; not verifying paraunitarity; missing the $W_M^{lM}=1$ simplification.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Two Channel Quadrature Mirror Filter Bank (QMF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Two Channel Quadrature Mirror Filter Bank (QMF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Two-Channel Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) Bank** is a fundamental multirate digital signal processing architecture that decomposes an input discrete-time signal $x(n)$ into two subband components using a pair of complementary analysis filters, processes (or quantizes) them independently, and then perfectly reconstructs the original signal through a corresponding synthesis filter bank. The name "quadrature mirror" arises because the magnitude response of the highpass filter is the *mirror image* (about $\omega = \pi/2$) of the lowpass filter's magnitude response, combined with a $\pi/2$ phase quadrature relationship.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A two-channel QMF bank is a maximally decimated (critically sampled) filter bank in which the analysis filters $H_0(z)$ (lowpass) and $H_1(z)$ (highpass) satisfy the mirror condition $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$, and the overall system aims to achieve **perfect reconstruction (PR)** of the input signal despite the aliasing introduced by downsampling by a factor of **2**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — The Audio Equalizer**
> Imagine a sound engineer splitting a music track into two frequency bands on an equalizer:
> - **Low-band (0 – 5 kHz)** → routed to a *bass speaker*
> - **High-band (5 kHz – 20 kHz)** → routed to a *treble speaker*
>
> Each band is processed (volume adjusted, compressed) independently. Later, the two processed signals are summed back to reproduce the original track. The QMF bank is the mathematical model of this exact process in the discrete-time domain. The "mirror" property ensures that the bass and treble regions **do not overlap in their transition** — they are perfect complements of each other on the frequency axis.
>
> The key engineering challenge: because we *throw away half the samples* (downsampling) in each branch, **aliasing** creeps in. A properly designed QMF cancels this aliasing at the reconstruction stage.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Decimation factor (M):** $\mathbf{2}$ (critically sampled two-channel bank)
- **Mirror frequency:** $\omega = \pi/2$ (i.e., $f = f_s/4$)
- **Nyquist frequency:** $\omega = \pi$
- **Number of subbands:** $\mathbf{2}$
- **Typical prototype filter length:** $N = 2K$ where $K \in \mathbb{Z}^+$

### 1.4 System Topology Overview

A two-channel QMF bank is composed of **two functional halves**:

| Half | Component | Function |
|:-----|:----------|:---------|
| Analysis Bank | $H_0(z)$ (Lowpass) | Extracts lower half of spectrum |
| Analysis Bank | $H_1(z)$ (Highpass) | Extracts upper half of spectrum |
| Analysis Bank | $\downarrow 2$ downsamplers | Reduce sample rate by half |
| Synthesis Bank | $\uparrow 2$ upsamplers | Restore original sample rate |
| Synthesis Bank | $G_0(z), G_1(z)$ | Reconstruct full-band signal |
| Synthesis Bank | Summer ($\Sigma$) | Combine reconstructed subbands |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Logic of the QMF Bank

The QMF bank operates in a strict, sequential pipeline:

1. **Decomposition Stage (Analysis):**
   - The input $x(n)$ is fed *in parallel* into a lowpass filter $H_0(z)$ and a highpass filter $H_1(z)$.
   - Both filtered outputs are **downsampled by 2** ($\downarrow 2$), producing critically decimated subband signals $v_0(n)$ and $v_1(n)$.

2. **Subband Processing Stage:**
   - In practical codecs (e.g., subband coding, MP3, JPEG 2000), $v_0(n)$ and $v_1(n)$ are quantized/coded.
   - In the *ideal theoretical* QMF, this stage is considered transparent (no quantization noise introduced).

3. **Reconstruction Stage (Synthesis):**
   - Each decimated signal is **upsampled by 2** ($\uparrow 2$) by inserting zeros.
   - The upsampled streams are filtered by synthesis filters $G_0(z)$ and $G_1(z)$.
   - The two filtered outputs are summed to produce $\hat{x}(n)$.

### 2.2 The Three Critical Distortions

For any two-channel filter bank, the reconstructed signal $\hat{x}(n)$ is a *distorted* version of $x(n)$ containing three possible impairments:

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Three Distortions in QMF**
> 1. **Aliasing Distortion** — caused by downsampling; appears as frequency folding.
> 2. **Magnitude Distortion** — caused by non-flat overall amplitude response $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) G_0(e^{j\omega}) + H_1(e^{j\omega}) G_1(e^{j\omega}) \vert$.
> 3. **Phase Distortion** — caused by non-linear phase of the overall system.

The *primary objective* of QMF design is to **eliminate all three** to achieve **Perfect Reconstruction (PR)**.

### 2.3 The Mirror Condition

The defining relation of a QMF bank is:

$$
H_1(z) = H_0(-z)
$$

Substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$
H_1(e^{j\omega}) = H_0(e^{j(\omega - \pi)}) = H_0(-e^{j\omega})
$$

This means the frequency response of $H_1$ is the *mirror image* of $H_0$ about $\omega = \pi/2$. If $H_0$ is a lowpass filter with cutoff $\pi/2$, then $H_1$ is automatically a highpass filter with cutoff $\pi/2$.

> [!NOTE] **Why "Quadrature"?**
> The term *quadrature* refers to the fact that the two filters are offset in frequency by $\pi/2$ (a quarter of the sampling frequency), and their impulse responses are related by an alternating sign — i.e., a $90°$ "quadrature" phase relationship.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|
| 1 | $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ | Mirror condition (defines QMF) |
| 2 | $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_1(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = 1$ | Power complementary condition |
| 3 | $H_1(e^{j\omega}) = H_0(e^{j(\omega - \pi)})$ | Frequency-domain mirror relation |
| 4 | $G_0(z) = 2 H_1(-z) = 2 H_0(z)$ | Synthesis filter for alias cancellation |
| 5 | $G_1(z) = -2 H_0(-z) = -2 H_1(z)$ | Synthesis filter for alias cancellation |
| 6 | $T(z) = H_0(z) G_0(z) + H_1(z) G_1(z)$ | Distortion transfer function |
| 7 | $A(z) = H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z)$ | Aliasing transfer function |
| 8 | $\hat{X}(z) = T(z) X(z) + A(z) X(-z)$ | Output spectrum (QMF bank) |
| 9 | $H_0(e^{j\omega}) G_0(e^{j\omega}) - H_0(e^{j(\omega - \pi)}) G_0(e^{j(\omega - \pi)}) = 0$ | Alias cancellation in QMF |
| 10 | $\hat{x}(n) = c \cdot x(n - n_0)$ | Perfect reconstruction condition |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Coding:** MP3, AAC use QMF banks (often cascaded tree structures) to exploit perceptual masking.
- **Image Coding:** JPEG 2000 uses wavelets derived from lifting schemes; the underlying filter banks are QMF-based.
- **Speech Processing:** Subband speech coders (e.g., G.722) use two-channel QMF splits.
- **Digital Communications:** Multicarrier modulation (DMT/OFDM) inherits its structure from QMF filter banks.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG/EEG subband analysis for noise removal.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Output Spectrum of the Two-Channel QMF Bank

Let $x(n)$ be the input signal with z-transform $X(z)$. The analysis filters produce:

$$
V_0(z) = H_0(z) X(z) \qquad V_1(z) = H_1(z) X(z)
$$

After **downsampling by 2**, using the standard multirate identity for the z-transform of a downsampled signal:

$$
V_0^{dec}(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ V_0(z^{1/2}) + V_0(-z^{1/2}) \right]
$$

Substituting $V_0(z) = H_0(z) X(z)$:

$$
V_0^{dec}(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0(z^{1/2}) X(z^{1/2}) + H_0(-z^{1/2}) X(-z^{1/2}) \right]
$$

After the channel, **upsampling by 2** (zero insertion) is performed. The z-transform of an upsampled signal satisfies:

$$
\hat{V}_0(z) = V_0^{dec}(z^2)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\hat{V}_0(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0(z) X(z) + H_0(-z) X(-z) \right]
$$

Similarly, for the highpass branch:

$$
\hat{V}_1(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_1(z) X(z) + H_1(-z) X(-z) \right]
$$

The synthesis filters $G_0(z)$ and $G_1(z)$ process these upsampled signals:

$$
\hat{X}(z) = G_0(z) \hat{V}_0(z) + G_1(z) \hat{V}_1(z)
$$

Substituting:

$$
\hat{X}(z) = \frac{1}{2} G_0(z) \left[ H_0(z) X(z) + H_0(-z) X(-z) \right] + \frac{1}{2} G_1(z) \left[ H_1(z) X(z) + H_1(-z) X(-z) \right]
$$

Grouping the terms containing $X(z)$ and $X(-z)$:

$$
\hat{X}(z) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0(z) G_0(z) + H_1(z) G_1(z) \right]}_{\text{Distortion Transfer Function } T(z)} X(z) + \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z) \right]}_{\text{Aliasing Transfer Function } A(z)} X(-z)
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{X}(z) = T(z) X(z) + A(z) X(-z)}
$$

The first term $T(z) X(z)$ represents the *desired* signal (subject to magnitude/phase distortion). The second term $A(z) X(-z)$ represents *aliasing* (since $X(-z)$ is the spectrum of $x(n)$ aliased by $\pi$).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Aliasing Cancellation Condition

To eliminate the aliasing term, we require:

$$
A(z) = H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z) = 0
$$

Solving for $G_1(z)$:

$$
G_1(z) = - \frac{H_0(-z) G_0(z)}{H_1(-z)}
$$

Using the mirror condition $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$, we get $H_1(-z) = H_0(z)$:

$$
G_1(z) = - \frac{H_0(-z) G_0(z)}{H_0(z)}
$$

A simple and popular choice is the **direct-form QMF choice**:

$$
G_0(z) = 2 H_1(-z) = 2 H_0(z)
$$

Substituting this into the expression for $G_1(z)$:

$$
G_1(z) = - \frac{H_0(-z) \cdot 2 H_0(z)}{H_0(z)} = -2 H_0(-z) = -2 H_1(z)
$$

Verifying alias cancellation:

$$
A(z) = H_0(-z) \cdot 2 H_0(z) + H_1(-z) \cdot (-2 H_1(z)) = 2 H_0(-z) H_0(z) - 2 H_0(z) H_0(-z) = 0 \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE] **Result:** With $G_0(z) = 2 H_0(z)$ and $G_1(z) = -2 H_1(z)$, aliasing is **completely cancelled** by construction.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Distortion Transfer Function (After Alias Cancellation)

With the aliasing cancelled, the output reduces to:

$$
\hat{X}(z) = T(z) X(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0(z) G_0(z) + H_1(z) G_1(z) \right] X(z)
$$

Substituting $G_0(z) = 2 H_0(z)$ and $G_1(z) = -2 H_1(z)$:

$$
T(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ 2 H_0^2(z) - 2 H_1^2(z) \right] = H_0^2(z) - H_1^2(z)
$$

Using the mirror relation $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$:

$$
T(z) = H_0^2(z) - H_0^2(-z)
$$

Evaluating on the unit circle $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$
T(e^{j\omega}) = H_0^2(e^{j\omega}) - H_0^2(e^{j(\omega - \pi)})
$$

For **perfect reconstruction** we need:

$$
T(e^{j\omega}) = c \cdot e^{-j\omega n_0}
$$

where $c$ is a non-zero constant and $n_0$ is a pure delay. The magnitude response $\vert T(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ must be flat (constant) and the phase must be linear.

> [!WARNING] **Key Limitation of QMF**
> A standard QMF bank (with linear-phase $H_0$) is **NOT** a perfect reconstruction system in general. It only achieves *near-perfect reconstruction* because $\vert T(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ is generally not flat. This is a classic result established by Johnston (1980) and Vaidyanathan (1993). To achieve exact PR, one must use **Conjugate Quadrature Filters (CQF)** with the design condition $H_1(z) = z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Power Complementary Property

For a QMF to avoid magnitude distortion, the filters must be power complementary. From the requirement $\vert T(e^{j\omega}) \vert = c$ (constant):

$$
\vert H_0^2(e^{j\omega}) - H_0^2(e^{j(\omega - \pi)}) \vert = c
$$

Squaring both sides:

$$
\left[ H_0^2(e^{j\omega}) - H_0^2(e^{j(\omega - \pi)}) \right] \left[ H_0^{*2}(e^{j\omega}) - H_0^{*2}(e^{j(\omega - \pi)}) \right] = c^2
$$

Expanding and using $H_0(e^{j(\omega - \pi)}) = H_0(e^{j\omega} e^{-j\pi}) = H_0(-e^{j\omega})$:

$$
\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^4 - H_0^2(e^{j\omega}) H_0^{*2}(-e^{j\omega}) - H_0^{*2}(e^{j\omega}) H_0^2(-e^{j\omega}) + \vert H_0(-e^{j\omega}) \vert^4 = c^2
$$

By symmetry $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \vert H_0(-e^{j\omega}) \vert$ (real-coefficient filter):

$$
2 \vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^4 - 2 \text{Re} \left\{ H_0^2(e^{j\omega}) H_0^{*2}(-e^{j\omega}) \right\} = c^2
$$

For real-coefficient filters, the second term reduces to $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^4$:

$$
\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^4 = \frac{c^2}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = \frac{c}{\sqrt{2}} = \text{const}
$$

This is the **all-pass** constraint — meaning $H_0$ would need to be all-pass, which contradicts the requirement that $H_0$ is lowpass. Therefore, **standard linear-phase QMF cannot achieve PR**; the magnitude response always has some ripple (passband/stopband).

---

### 3.5 The CQF (Conjugate Quadrature Filter) Solution

To achieve true perfect reconstruction, the **CQF condition** is used:

$$
H_1(z) = z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})
$$

The synthesis filters are chosen as:

$$
G_0(z) = 2 H_0(z) \qquad G_1(z) = 2 H_1(z)
$$

This is the **Daubechies / Smith-Barnwell condition** for PR. The corresponding CQF bank is equivalent to a **discrete wavelet transform** with a specific mother wavelet.

---

### 3.6 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Analysis Branch | Filter | Decimation | Synthesis Branch | Filter | Upsampling |
|:----------------|:-------|:-----------|:----------------|:-------|:-----------|
| Lowpass output | $H_0(z)$ | $\downarrow 2$ | Lowpass reconstruction | $G_0(z)$ | $\uparrow 2$ |
| Highpass output | $H_1(z)$ | $\downarrow 2$ | Highpass reconstruction | $G_1(z)$ | $\uparrow 2$ |

| Condition | Mathematical Form | Status in Standard QMF |
|:----------|:------------------|:------------------------|
| Mirror condition | $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ | **Satisfied** |
| Alias cancellation | $A(z) = 0$ | **Satisfied** (by choice of $G_0, G_1$) |
| Magnitude flatness | $\vert T(e^{j\omega}) \vert = c$ | **NOT satisfied** (ripple present) |
| Linear phase | $\angle T(e^{j\omega}) = -\omega n_0$ | Satisfied if $H_0$ is linear phase |
| Perfect reconstruction | $\hat{x}(n) = c \cdot x(n-n_0)$ | **NOT** satisfied in standard QMF |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **Q1. (3 Marks)** Define a two-channel Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) bank. State the mirror condition.

**Model Answer:**
A two-channel QMF bank is a critically sampled multirate system that decomposes the input signal $x(n)$ into two subband signals using a lowpass analysis filter $H_0(z)$ and a highpass analysis filter $H_1(z)$, each followed by downsampling by 2. The signal is then reconstructed using upsamplers and synthesis filters $G_0(z), G_1(z)$.
The **mirror condition** that defines a QMF bank is:

$$
H_1(z) = H_0(-z)
$$

This ensures that the magnitude response of $H_1$ is the mirror image of $H_0$ about $\omega = \pi/2$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
> **Q2. (3 Marks)** Distinguish between aliasing distortion and magnitude distortion in a two-channel QMF bank.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Aliasing Distortion | Magnitude Distortion |
|:-------|:-------------------|:---------------------|
| **Cause** | Downsampling by 2 | Non-flat $\vert T(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ |
| **Mathematical form** | $A(z) X(-z)$ | $\vert T(e^{j\omega}) \vert \neq \text{const}$ |
| **Cancellation** | Possible via $G_0, G_1$ design | Requires power complementarity |
| **Effect on output** | Spectral folding | Amplitude ripple in passband/stopband |
| **Eliminated in QMF?** | Yes | No (in standard linear-phase QMF) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q3(A). (14 Marks)**
**(a)** Derive the output expression $\hat{X}(z)$ of a two-channel QMF bank in terms of the analysis and synthesis filters, clearly identifying the aliasing and distortion transfer functions. (7 Marks)

**(b)** State and prove the aliasing cancellation condition for a two-channel QMF bank. Show the choice of synthesis filters that achieves this cancellation. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q3(A)(a):

Starting with the analysis branch outputs:

$$
V_0(z) = H_0(z) X(z) \qquad V_1(z) = H_1(z) X(z)
$$

After downsampling by 2 and applying the multirate identity:

$$
\hat{V}_0(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0(z) X(z) + H_0(-z) X(-z) \right]
$$

$$
\hat{V}_1(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_1(z) X(z) + H_1(-z) X(-z) \right]
$$

After synthesis filtering and summation:

$$
\hat{X}(z) = G_0(z) \hat{V}_0(z) + G_1(z) \hat{V}_1(z)
$$

Substituting and grouping:

$$
\hat{X}(z) = \frac{1}{2} \underbrace{\left[ H_0(z) G_0(z) + H_1(z) G_1(z) \right]}_{T(z)} X(z) + \frac{1}{2} \underbrace{\left[ H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z) \right]}_{A(z)} X(-z)
$$

- $T(z)$: **Distortion Transfer Function** (desired term, but with possible magnitude/phase distortion)
- $A(z)$: **Aliasing Transfer Function** (unwanted term to be eliminated)

#### Model Solution for Q3(A)(b):

To cancel aliasing, set $A(z) = 0$:

$$
H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z) = 0
$$

Solving for $G_1(z)$:

$$
G_1(z) = - \frac{H_0(-z) G_0(z)}{H_1(-z)}
$$

Apply the mirror condition $H_1(-z) = H_0(z)$:

$$
G_1(z) = - \frac{H_0(-z) G_0(z)}{H_0(z)}
$$

Choose the simple canonical form $G_0(z) = 2 H_0(z)$:

$$
G_1(z) = -2 H_0(-z) = -2 H_1(z)
$$

**Verification:**

$$
A(z) = H_0(-z) \cdot 2 H_0(z) + H_0(z) \cdot (-2 H_0(-z)) = 2 H_0(z) H_0(-z) - 2 H_0(z) H_0(-z) = 0 \checkmark
$$

Hence, with $G_0(z) = 2 H_0(z)$ and $G_1(z) = -2 H_1(z)$, the aliasing is completely cancelled.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**Q3(B). (14 Marks)**
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a two-channel QMF bank. Clearly identify the analysis and synthesis sections. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the power complementary property of a two-channel QMF bank. Why is a standard linear-phase QMF bank unable to achieve perfect reconstruction? (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q3(B)(a):

A two-channel QMF bank consists of the following blocks (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram):

**Analysis Section (decomposition):**
- Input $x(n)$ is fed to a lowpass filter $H_0(z)$ and highpass filter $H_1(z)$ in parallel.
- The filtered outputs are downsampled by 2 ($\downarrow 2$).
- This produces two subband signals: lowpass $v_0(n)$ and highpass $v_1(n)$.

**Synthesis Section (reconstruction):**
- Each subband signal is upsampled by 2 ($\uparrow 2$) by inserting zeros between samples.
- The upsampled streams are filtered by synthesis filters $G_0(z)$ and $G_1(z)$.
- The two outputs are summed to produce $\hat{x}(n)$.

The reconstruction process attempts to reproduce $x(n)$ with only minimal distortion despite the lossy downsampling step.

#### Model Solution for Q3(B)(b):

The **power complementary property** states:

$$
\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_1(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = 1 \quad \text{for all } \omega
$$

This ensures that the magnitude response of the overall system is flat, eliminating magnitude distortion. For this to hold, with $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$:

$$
\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\omega - \pi)}) \vert^2 = 1
$$

**Why standard linear-phase QMF cannot achieve PR:**

For a linear-phase $H_0(z)$ of length $N$, the transfer function $H_0(e^{j\omega})$ has the form $H_0(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \cdot R(\omega)$ where $R(\omega)$ is real. Substituting into the PR requirement for $T(z) = H_0^2(z) - H_0^2(-z)$:

$$
T(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)} \left[ R^2(\omega) - R^2(\omega - \pi) \right]
$$

The magnitude:

$$
\vert T(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \vert R^2(\omega) - R^2(\omega - \pi) \vert
$$

For this to be constant, either $R(\omega) = 0$ (trivial) or $R(\omega) = R(\omega - \pi)$ (constant all-pass, contradicting lowpass nature). Therefore, **magnitude distortion is unavoidable** in standard linear-phase QMF banks — this is a fundamental limitation. To achieve true PR, one must use **CQF** (Conjugate Quadrature Filters) with the design condition $H_1(z) = z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in QMF Bank Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ in the downsampling identity** — this is the most common error; students often write $\hat{V}_0(z) = H_0(z) X(z) + H_0(-z) X(-z)$ without the $1/2$ scaling, costing **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing mirror condition with CQF condition** — students write $H_1(z) = H_0(-z^{-1})$ for QMF; the correct QMF form is $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ (the $z^{-1}$ inside belongs to CQF only).
> 3. **Skipping the verification step** in alias cancellation — always substitute back to show $A(z) = 0$; merely stating the conditions is **not enough** for full marks.
> 4. **Missing the limitation statement** — the question "Why not perfect reconstruction?" is worth 2–3 marks; students often stop at the alias cancellation step and lose these.
> 5. **Wrong units in frequency** — remember $\omega$ is *normalized* in radians/sample, ranging from $0$ to $\pi$. Never write it in Hz without specifying the sampling rate.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist for QMF Bank**
> - QMF = **Qu**adrature **M**irror **F**ilter, two-channel, critically sampled by **M = 2**.
> - **Mirror condition (defining):** $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$.
> - **Alias cancellation synthesis filters:** $G_0(z) = 2 H_0(z)$, $G_1(z) = -2 H_1(z)$.
> - **Output equation:** $\hat{X}(z) = T(z) X(z) + A(z) X(-z)$.
> - **Distortion transfer function:** $T(z) = H_0^2(z) - H_0^2(-z)$.
> - **Aliasing transfer function:** $A(z) = H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z)$.
> - **Power complementary property:** $\vert H_0 \vert^2 + \vert H_1 \vert^2 = 1$.
> - **Standard linear-phase QMF is NOT a perfect reconstruction system** (magnitude distortion always present).
> - **CQF condition for true PR:** $H_1(z) = z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$.
> - **Mirror frequency:** $\omega = \pi/2$ (i.e., quarter sampling rate).
> - **Decimation factor:** $\mathbf{M = 2}$ (critically sampled).
> - **Aliasing cancelled by design, but magnitude distortion remains** — this is the key trade-off.
> - **Reconstruction is delayed by $(N-1)$** samples (for a filter of length $N$).
> - **CQF is equivalent to discrete wavelet transform** with the chosen prototype filter.
> - **QMF banks are widely used in audio coding (MP3, AAC), image subband coding, and biomedical signal processing.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perfect Reconstruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Perfect Reconstruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of multirate digital signal processing and wavelet transforms, **Perfect Reconstruction (PR)** is defined as the property of a filter bank system whereby the output signal $\hat{x}(n)$ is an exact, delayed replica of the input signal $x(n)$, subject only to a possible constant amplitude scaling and a fixed time delay. Mathematically, the PR condition is expressed as:

$$\hat{x}(n) = c \cdot x(n - n_0)$$

where $c$ is a non-zero constant (typically $c = 1$ or $c = 2$ depending on the normalization convention used) and $n_0$ is an integer-valued constant system delay. When this condition is satisfied, the analysis–synthesis system is said to achieve **lossless reconstruction**, and the reconstructed signal contains zero aliasing error, zero amplitude distortion, and zero phase distortion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Perfect Reconstruction is the cornerstone concept of Module 1. It forms the theoretical foundation upon which Quadrature Mirror Filters (QMF), Conjugate Quadrature Filters (CQF), orthogonal wavelets, and the biorthogonal wavelet family are all built. Expect direct examination questions on PR conditions in both Part A and Part B.

### 1.2 Two-Channel Analysis–Synthesis Filter Bank: System Overview

The standard two-channel filter bank, which is the workhorse architecture for studying PR, consists of two distinct sections:

**1. Analysis Section (Decomposition Side)**
- The input signal $x(n)$ is fed in parallel into two digital filters:
  - A **lowpass filter** $H_0(z)$ with impulse response $h_0(n)$
  - A **highpass filter** $H_1(z)$ with impulse response $h_1(n)$
- The outputs of these filters are then downsampled (decimated) by a factor of $2$, producing the subband signals $v_0(n)$ and $v_1(n)$.

**2. Synthesis Section (Reconstruction Side)**
- The subband signals are first upsampled (interpolated) by a factor of $2$.
- They are then passed through two reconstruction filters:
  - A **lowpass filter** $G_0(z)$ with impulse response $g_0(n)$
  - A **highpass filter** $G_1(z)$ with impulse response $g_1(n)$
- The filtered outputs are summed to produce $\hat{x}(n)$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Lossless Audio Transmission" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Perfect Photocopy Machine:**
> Imagine you have an original color document. You scan it using two separate color filters: one that captures the red channel and one that captures the blue channel. Each captured channel is then compressed (downsampled) to save storage space. Later, you want to reconstruct the original document by expanding (upsampling) the two channels back to full resolution, applying inverse filters, and combining them.
>
> - **Aliasing** is like the red ink bleeding into the blue channel — colors get mixed up and you cannot tell them apart.
> - **Amplitude Distortion** is like the reconstructed document being too dim or too bright compared to the original.
> - **Phase Distortion** is like the text appearing shifted on the page — words are in the wrong location.
>
> **Perfect Reconstruction** is the engineering goal where the photocopy is *bit-for-bit identical* (up to a constant scale and known delay) to the original document. In audio coding (MP3, AAC), PR ensures the listener hears exactly what was recorded. In image coding (JPEG 2000), PR ensures the decompressed image is a perfect mirror of the source.

### 1.4 Why Perfect Reconstruction Matters in Engineering

The PR property is **not a luxury** — it is an absolute engineering necessity in the following applications:

| Application Domain | Role of Perfect Reconstruction |
|---|---|
| **Subband Coding (Speech/Audio)** | Ensures transparent audio compression (e.g., MP3, AAC, Opus codecs) |
| **Image Compression (JPEG 2000)** | Enables lossless-to-lossy compression modes in a single framework |
| **Wavelet-Based Denoising** | Guarantees that the inverse DWT faithfully restores the signal structure |
| **Biomedical Signal Processing** | Critical for ECG/EEG analysis where any distortion can cause misdiagnosis |
| **Communication Systems** | Used in filter bank multicarrier (FBMC) modulation for 5G waveforms |
| **Numerical Analysis** | Underpins lifting schemes for lossless integer-to-integer wavelet transforms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The constant $c$ in the PR equation is **NOT an error** — it is a global amplitude scaling that can be normalized away in practice. What truly matters is that there is **no signal-dependent distortion** (no aliasing) and **no frequency-dependent distortion** (flat magnitude response across all frequencies).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Systematic Breakdown of the Two-Channel PR System

The two-channel filter bank can be analyzed in the **Z-domain** by tracing the signal from input $X(z)$ to output $\hat{X}(z)$ through each branch.

#### Step 1: Analysis Bank — Filtering and Downsampling

In the analysis section, the input signal $X(z)$ is filtered by $H_0(z)$ and $H_1(z)$, producing:

$$V_0(z) = H_0(z) X(z) \quad \text{and} \quad V_1(z) = H_1(z) X(z)$$

The downsampling operation by a factor of $M=2$ is represented in the Z-domain by the well-known **multirate identity**:

$$Y(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ V_0(z^{1/2}) + V_0(-z^{1/2}) \right]$$

Therefore, after downsampling, the subband signals become:

$$V_{0d}(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0(z^{1/2}) X(z^{1/2}) + H_0(-z^{1/2}) X(-z^{1/2}) \right]$$

$$V_{1d}(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_1(z^{1/2}) X(z^{1/2}) + H_1(-z^{1/2}) X(-z^{1/2}) \right]$$

#### Step 2: Synthesis Bank — Upsampling and Filtering

The synthesis section upsamples each subband signal by inserting $M-1 = 1$ zero between samples, then filters with $G_0(z)$ and $G_1(z)$, and finally sums the two branches. The output is:

$$\hat{X}(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ G_0(z) H_0(z) + G_1(z) H_1(z) \right] X(z) + \frac{1}{2} \left[ G_0(z) H_0(-z) + G_1(z) H_1(-z) \right] X(-z)$$

This can be compactly written as:

$$\hat{X}(z) = T(z) X(z) + S(z) X(-z)$$

where we define two crucial transfer functions.

### 2.2 The Two PR Transfer Functions: $T(z)$ and $S(z)$

**Distortion Function (or Transfer Function):**

$$T(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0(z) G_0(z) + H_1(z) G_1(z) \right]$$

**Aliasing Function (or Alias Component):**

$$S(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z) \right]$$

The reconstructed signal is then:

$$\hat{X}(z) = T(z) X(z) + S(z) X(-z)$$

### 2.3 The Three Conditions for Perfect Reconstruction

For $\hat{X}(z) = c \, z^{-n_0} X(z)$ to hold, we need **three independent conditions** to be satisfied simultaneously.

| Condition | Mathematical Statement | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Condition 1: Alias Cancellation (AC)** | $S(z) = 0$ | Eliminate the $X(-z)$ term that represents high-frequency aliasing folded back into the baseband |
| **Condition 2: Amplitude Distortion Cancellation** | $\vert T(z) \vert = c$ (constant for all $z$ on the unit circle) | Ensure all frequency components are passed with equal gain (flat magnitude response) |
| **Condition 3: Phase Linearity** | $\angle T(z) = -\omega n_0$ (linear phase) | Ensure all frequency components are delayed by the same amount $n_0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** In most KTU examination questions, **Condition 1 (Alias Cancellation)** is tested most frequently, since it is purely algebraic in nature. Conditions 2 and 3 together are often simplified into a single requirement: $T(z) = c \, z^{-n_0}$ for some constant $c$ and integer delay $n_0$.

### 2.4 Alias Cancellation: Closed-Form Solution

The condition $S(z) = 0$ yields the linear system:

$$H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z) = 0$$

This is a system of **two equations in two unknowns** (the synthesis filters $G_0(z)$ and $G_1(z)$), parameterized by the choice of an arbitrary "free" filter. The standard KTU-canonical solution is:

$$\boxed{G_0(z) = -2 \, H_1(-z) \quad \text{and} \quad G_1(z) = 2 \, H_0(-z)}$$

**Verification (KTU-Style Substitution):**

$$H_0(-z) \cdot [-2 H_1(-z)] + H_1(-z) \cdot [2 H_0(-z)] = -2 H_0(-z) H_1(-z) + 2 H_0(-z) H_1(-z) = 0 \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Once the analysis filters $H_0(z)$ and $H_1(z)$ are designed, the synthesis filters are *uniquely determined* (up to a global scaling) by the alias cancellation condition. This is why the design of a perfect reconstruction filter bank reduces to designing **just two analysis filters** — the rest is algebra.

### 2.5 Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) Banks

The QMF bank is the most elementary and historically significant two-channel PR system. It is defined by the elegant mirror-image relationship:

$$H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$$

**Naming Origin:** The magnitude response of $H_0(z)$ and $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ are mirror images of each other with respect to the frequency $\omega = \pi/2$ (i.e., $f = f_s/4$). This is because $\vert H_1(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \vert H_0(e^{j(\pi - \omega)}) \vert$, which creates a "quadrature mirror" symmetry.

**Substituting into the PR conditions with $G_0(z) = H_1(-z) = H_0(z)$ and $G_1(z) = H_0(-z) = H_1(z)$:**

$$T(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0^2(z) - H_0^2(-z) \right]$$

$$S(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ -H_0(z) H_0(-z) + H_0(-z) H_0(z) \right] = 0 \checkmark \text{ (Alias-free by construction)}$$

**Remaining PR condition for QMF:**

$$\vert H_0^2(z) - H_0^2(-z) \vert = c^2 \quad \text{on the unit circle}$$

This is the famous **power complementary condition** (also called the **power symmetric** condition). A classical QMF example is the **Johnston filter** designed with this property.

### 2.6 Conjugate Quadrature Filter (CQF) Banks

A CQF bank is defined by the stronger relationship:

$$H_1(z) = -z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$$

where $N$ is the order (length) of the filter minus 1. The frequency-domain equivalent is:

$$H_1(e^{j\omega}) = -e^{-j(N-1)\omega} \, H_0^*(e^{j(\pi - \omega)})$$

This means the magnitude responses are mirror-symmetric and the phase responses are conjugate. **CQF banks achieve Perfect Reconstruction exactly** when:

$$H_0(z) H_0(z^{-1}) + H_0(-z) H_0(-z^{-1}) = 2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Daubechies Connection:** The CQF condition is precisely the algebraic identity that **Ingrid Daubechies** exploited in 1988 to construct her famous family of compactly supported orthogonal wavelets (db2, db3, db4, …). Every Daubechies wavelet corresponds to a CQF-based PR filter bank.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Distortion function $T(z)$ | $T(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[H_0(z)G_0(z) + H_1(z)G_1(z)]$ | Should equal $c\,z^{-n_0}$ |
| Aliasing function $S(z)$ | $S(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[H_0(-z)G_0(z) + H_1(-z)G_1(z)]$ | Must equal $0$ |
| Reconstructed signal | $\hat{X}(z) = T(z)X(z) + S(z)X(-z)$ | $z$-domain representation |
| PR Condition (full) | $T(z) = c\,z^{-n_0}$ and $S(z) = 0$ | Both must hold |
| QMF mirror condition | $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ | Magnitude mirror at $\omega = \pi/2$ |
| CQF condition | $H_1(z) = -z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$ | Orthogonality constraint |
| CQF PR identity | $H_0(z)H_0(z^{-1}) + H_0(-z)H_0(-z^{-1}) = 2$ | Power complementary form |
| QMF power symmetry | $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\pi - \omega)}) \vert^2 = 2$ | On unit circle |
| Synthesis filters (AC) | $G_0(z) = -2 H_1(-z)$, $G_1(z) = 2 H_0(-z)$ | Alias-cancelled form |
| Polyphase matrix $E(z)$ | $E(z) = \begin{bmatrix} H_{00}(z) & H_{01}(z) \\ H_{10}(z) & H_{11}(z) \end{bmatrix}$ | Used in paraunitary design |
| Paraunitary condition | $E(z) \tilde{E}(z^{-1}) = c I$ | Lossless system property |
| Noble identities (downsampler) | $H(z) \downarrow 2 = \downarrow 2 \, H(z^2)$ | Multirate equivalence |
| Noble identities (upsampler) | $\uparrow 2 \, H(z) = H(z^2) \uparrow 2$ | Multirate equivalence |
| Decimation gain factor | $1/M$ (where $M$ is downsampling factor) | Energy preservation |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility of PR

The Perfect Reconstruction property is the mathematical guarantee that underpins several billion-dollar industries:

- **Audio Streaming (Spotify, Apple Music):** Filter banks with near-PR properties enable perceptual audio codecs.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI Reconstruction):** Wavelet-based PR enables lossless compression of medical scans.
- **Seismic Data Processing:** Oil & gas exploration uses PR filter banks to decompose seismic traces for noise removal.
- **5G/6G Communications:** Filter bank multicarrier (FBMC) systems leverage PR for spectrally efficient waveforms.
- **Astronomy:** Space telescope data (Hubble, James Webb) is wavelet-compressed losslessly for archival.

> [!TIP]
> **Study Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked "What is Perfect Reconstruction?", always state the **three-part condition**: (1) no aliasing, (2) flat magnitude response, and (3) linear phase — all three together. Examiners reward students who explicitly enumerate these three sub-conditions.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation #1: The Reconstructed Signal in the Z-Domain

**Objective:** Derive the expression $\hat{X}(z) = T(z) X(z) + S(z) X(-z)$ for a generic two-channel filter bank.

**Step 1 — Define the analysis filter outputs.**

The analysis filters process the input signal as:

$$V_0(z) = H_0(z) X(z)$$

$$V_1(z) = H_1(z) X(z)$$

This is direct application of the convolution theorem: filtering in the time domain corresponds to multiplication in the Z-domain.

**Step 2 — Apply the downsampling by 2 identity.**

The general multirate identity for downsampling by factor $M$ states:

$$Y(z) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} V_k(z^{1/M}) \cdot W_M^{-k \cdot \text{index}}$$

For $M=2$, this specializes to:

$$Y(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ V_0(z^{1/2}) + V_0(-z^{1/2}) \right]$$

Applying this to both subbands:

$$V_{0,\downarrow}(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0(z^{1/2}) X(z^{1/2}) + H_0(-z^{1/2}) X(-z^{1/2}) \right]$$

$$V_{1,\downarrow}(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_1(z^{1/2}) X(z^{1/2}) + H_1(-z^{1/2}) X(-z^{1/2}) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the upsampling by 2 identity.**

Upsampling by $M$ is represented in the Z-domain as $Y(z) = X(z^M)$. Therefore:

$$U_0(z) = V_{0,\downarrow}(z^2) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0(z) X(z) + H_0(-z) X(-z) \right]$$

$$U_1(z) = V_{1,\downarrow}(z^2) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_1(z) X(z) + H_1(-z) X(-z) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Filter the upsampled signals and sum.**

The synthesis filters $G_0(z)$ and $G_1(z)$ process the upsampled signals, and the two branches are summed:

$$\hat{X}(z) = G_0(z) U_0(z) + G_1(z) U_1(z)$$

Substituting and grouping the $X(z)$ and $X(-z)$ terms:

$$\hat{X}(z) = \frac{1}{2} \underbrace{\left[ G_0(z) H_0(z) + G_1(z) H_1(z) \right]}_{2 T(z)} X(z) + \frac{1}{2} \underbrace{\left[ G_0(z) H_0(-z) + G_1(z) H_1(-z) \right]}_{2 S(z)} X(-z)$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form.**

$$\boxed{\hat{X}(z) = T(z) X(z) + S(z) X(-z)}$$

where $T(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[G_0(z) H_0(z) + G_1(z) H_1(z)]$ and $S(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[G_0(z) H_0(-z) + G_1(z) H_1(-z)]$. This is the **canonical KTU derivation** that appears in board exam solutions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation #2: Solution of the Alias Cancellation Equations

**Objective:** Given the alias cancellation requirement $S(z) = 0$, derive the closed-form expressions for the synthesis filters $G_0(z)$ and $G_1(z)$.

**Step 1 — Set up the equation.**

$$G_0(z) H_0(-z) + G_1(z) H_1(-z) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate $G_0(z)$.**

$$G_0(z) H_0(-z) = -G_1(z) H_1(-z)$$

**Step 3 — Introduce a free parameter.**

We have one equation in two unknowns. Following KTU's standard textbook treatment (Vaidyanathan, Soman, Navaki), we introduce a free "product filter" $P(z)$ such that:

$$G_0(z) H_0(-z) = -P(z) \quad \text{and} \quad G_1(z) H_1(-z) = P(z)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the synthesis filters.**

$$G_0(z) = -\frac{P(z)}{H_0(-z)} \quad \text{and} \quad G_1(z) = \frac{P(z)}{H_1(-z)}$$

**Step 5 — Choose the simplest valid product filter.**

The simplest choice is $P(z) = 2 H_0(-z) H_1(-z)$, which makes both synthesis filters become FIR (finite impulse response) and yields the **Kaiser–Bellanger identity**:

$$\boxed{G_0(z) = -2 H_1(-z) \quad \text{and} \quad G_1(z) = 2 H_0(-z)}$$

**Step 6 — Verify alias cancellation explicitly.**

$$S(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ (-2 H_1(-z)) \cdot H_0(-z) + (2 H_0(-z)) \cdot H_1(-z) \right]$$

$$S(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ -2 H_0(-z) H_1(-z) + 2 H_0(-z) H_1(-z) \right] = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Justification:** The factor of $2$ in the synthesis filters exactly cancels the factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ from the decimation gain. This is no accident — it is the algebraic manifestation of the **noble identities** in multirate systems theory.

---

### 3.3 Derivation #3: QMF Bank Perfect Reconstruction Condition

**Objective:** Derive the power-symmetry condition for a QMF bank to achieve perfect reconstruction.

**Step 1 — Substitute QMF relations into the distortion function.**

For a QMF bank: $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$, and the alias-cancelled synthesis filters are $G_0(z) = H_1(-z) = H_0(z)$ and $G_1(z) = H_0(-z) = H_1(z)$.

Substituting into $T(z)$:

$$T(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0(z) \cdot H_0(z) + H_0(-z) \cdot H_0(-z) \right] = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0^2(z) + H_0^2(-z) \right]$$

Wait — let me carefully re-derive. With $G_0(z) = H_0(z)$ and $G_1(z) = H_0(-z)$:

$$T(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0(z) \cdot H_0(z) + H_0(-z) \cdot H_0(-z) \right]$$

$$T(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0^2(z) + H_0^2(-z) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the PR requirement.**

For perfect reconstruction, we need $T(z) = c \, z^{-n_0}$. If we choose $c = 1$ and $n_0 = N-1$ (a common convention), then on the unit circle $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$\frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0^2(e^{j\omega}) + H_0^2(e^{j(\pi - \omega)}) \right] = e^{-j(N-1)\omega}$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude squared form (power symmetry).**

Taking the magnitude squared of both sides:

$$\frac{1}{4} \left\vert H_0^2(e^{j\omega}) + H_0^2(e^{j(\pi - \omega)}) \right\vert^2 = 1$$

For a **linear-phase lowpass filter** with real coefficients and length $N+1$, $H_0(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-jN\omega/2} \cdot \vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ (symmetric case). In this case, the cross-term in the magnitude expansion vanishes, and we obtain the classical **power-symmetry condition**:

$$\boxed{\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\pi - \omega)}) \vert^2 = 2}$$

This is the celebrated result that **Johnston (1980)** and **Jain–Crochiere (1981)** used to design the famous QMF filter banks used in subband speech coding.

---

### 3.4 Derivation #4: Polyphase Representation of PR Filter Bank

**Objective:** Express the analysis filter bank in its polyphase form, which is the modern state-of-the-art approach to PR design.

**Step 1 — Decompose the analysis filter into even and odd polyphase components.**

Any filter $H_0(z)$ can be written as:

$$H_0(z) = H_{00}(z^2) + z^{-1} H_{01}(z^2)$$

where the polyphase components are:

$$H_{00}(z) = \sum_{n} h_0(2n) z^{-n} \quad \text{(even-indexed samples)}$$

$$H_{01}(z) = \sum_{n} h_0(2n+1) z^{-n} \quad \text{(odd-indexed samples)}$$

**Step 2 — Form the polyphase matrix.**

For a two-channel analysis bank, define the **polyphase matrix** $E(z)$ as:

$$E(z) = \begin{bmatrix} H_{00}(z) & H_{01}(z) \\ H_{10}(z) & H_{11}(z) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Express the analysis bank compactly.**

Using the noble identity, the entire analysis filter bank with downsampling is equivalent to:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_{0,\downarrow}(z) \\ V_{1,\downarrow}(z) \end{bmatrix} = E(z) \begin{bmatrix} X(z) \\ z^{-1} X(z) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — State the paraunitary (PR) condition.**

The filter bank achieves perfect reconstruction if and only if the polyphase matrix satisfies the **paraunitary condition**:

$$\boxed{E(z) \, \tilde{E}(z^{-1}) = c \, I}$$

where $\tilde{E}(z)$ denotes the "reversed" polyphase matrix (columns reversed, with appropriate sign changes) and $I$ is the $2 \times 2$ identity matrix. This single equation encapsulates all three PR conditions (alias cancellation, flat magnitude, linear phase) in one elegant matrix identity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Polyphase?** The polyphase formulation converts a **time-varying** multirate system into an equivalent **time-invariant** single-rate system. This is the key insight that makes modern PR filter bank design tractable and is heavily used in the design of orthonormal wavelet bases.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Filter Bank Architectures

| **Architecture** | **Relationship** | **PR Achieved?** | **Filter Type** | **Wavelet Link** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Generic 2-channel | None specified | Only if 3 conditions met | Arbitrary | None yet |
| **QMF** | $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ | Only if power-symmetric | Linear phase, FIR | Battle–Lemarié family |
| **CQF** | $H_1(z) = -z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$ | **Exactly** (by design) | Linear phase, FIR | Daubechies wavelets |
| **Orthogonal (Paraunitary)** | Polyphase matrix $E(z)$ is paraunitary | **Exactly** (by design) | FIR or IIR | All orthogonal wavelets |
| **Biorthogonal** | $H_0, H_1$ and $G_0, G_1$ are designed as a pair | **Exactly** (by design) | FIR, linear phase | Cohen–Daubechies–Feauveau (CDF) wavelets |
