## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Perfect Reconstruction in the context of two-channel filter banks. State the mathematical condition for PR.**

**Model Answer:**

Perfect Reconstruction (PR) is the property of an analysis–synthesis filter bank by which the reconstructed output signal $\hat{x}(n)$ is an exact, scaled, and delayed replica of the input signal $x(n)$, free from any aliasing, amplitude distortion, or phase distortion.

**Mathematical Condition:**

$$\hat{x}(n) = c \cdot x(n - n_0)$$

This single condition implicitly requires:
- **Alias Cancellation:** $S(z) = 0$
- **Flat Magnitude Response:** $\vert T(z) \vert = c$
- **Linear Phase:** $\angle T(z) = -\omega n_0$

where $c$ is a non-zero constant and $n_0$ is a fixed integer delay.

---

**Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) banks and Conjugate Quadrature Filter (CQF) banks in terms of their PR properties.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | QMF Bank | CQF Bank |
|---|---|---|
| Filter relationship | $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ | $H_1(z) = -z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$ |
| Alias cancellation | Achieved **by construction** | Achieved **by construction** |
| Amplitude distortion | Eliminated **only if** power-symmetric design is used | Eliminated **exactly** for any $H_0$ |
| Magnitude symmetry | Mirror about $\omega = \pi/2$ | Mirror + phase conjugate |
| PR guarantee | Conditional (requires power symmetry) | Unconditional (always PR) |
| Wavelet connection | Battle–Lemarié wavelets | Daubechies wavelets |
| Filter phase | Linear phase, real coefficients | Linear phase, real coefficients |

**Key Takeaway:** QMF banks achieve PR *conditionally* (only with specially designed power-symmetric filters), while CQF banks achieve PR *unconditionally* for any valid choice of $H_0(z)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the reconstructed signal $\hat{X}(z)$ in terms of $X(z)$ and $X(-z)$ for a generic two-channel analysis–synthesis filter bank with filters $H_0(z), H_1(z), G_0(z), G_1(z)$. Clearly define the distortion function $T(z)$ and the aliasing function $S(z)$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Starting from the alias cancellation condition $S(z) = 0$, derive the closed-form expressions for the synthesis filters $G_0(z)$ and $G_1(z)$ in terms of the analysis filters. Hence, verify that the resulting system is indeed alias-free. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The input signal is filtered by the analysis section:

$$V_0(z) = H_0(z) X(z), \quad V_1(z) = H_1(z) X(z)$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Downsampling by 2 is applied. Using the multirate identity $Y(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[V_0(z^{1/2}) + V_0(-z^{1/2})]$:

$$V_{0,\downarrow}(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[H_0(z^{1/2}) X(z^{1/2}) + H_0(-z^{1/2}) X(-z^{1/2})]$$

$$V_{1,\downarrow}(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[H_1(z^{1/2}) X(z^{1/2}) + H_1(-z^{1/2}) X(-z^{1/2})]$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Upsampling by 2 yields $U_k(z) = V_{k,\downarrow}(z^2)$:

$$U_0(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[H_0(z) X(z) + H_0(-z) X(-z)]$$

$$U_1(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[H_1(z) X(z) + H_1(-z) X(-z)]$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Synthesis filtering and summation:

$$\hat{X}(z) = G_0(z) U_0(z) + G_1(z) U_1(z)$$

Grouping the $X(z)$ and $X(-z)$ terms:

$$\hat{X}(z) = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}[G_0(z) H_0(z) + G_1(z) H_1(z)]}_{T(z)} X(z) + \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}[G_0(z) H_0(-z) + G_1(z) H_1(-z)]}_{S(z)} X(-z)$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Final boxed answer:

$$\boxed{\hat{X}(z) = T(z) X(z) + S(z) X(-z)}$$

where $T(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[G_0(z) H_0(z) + G_1(z) H_1(z)]$ is the **distortion function** and $S(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[G_0(z) H_0(-z) + G_1(z) H_1(-z)]$ is the **aliasing function**.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The alias cancellation condition requires:

$$S(z) = 0 \implies G_0(z) H_0(-z) + G_1(z) H_1(-z) = 0$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Rearrange to isolate $G_0(z)$:

$$G_0(z) = -\frac{H_1(-z)}{H_0(-z)} \cdot G_1(z)$$

Introduce a free "product filter" $P(z)$ such that:

$$G_0(z) H_0(-z) = -P(z) \quad \text{and} \quad G_1(z) H_1(-z) = P(z)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Choose $P(z) = 2 H_0(-z) H_1(-z)$ to obtain FIR synthesis filters:

$$G_0(z) = -\frac{2 H_0(-z) H_1(-z)}{H_0(-z)} = -2 H_1(-z)$$

$$G_1(z) = \frac{2 H_0(-z) H_1(-z)}{H_1(-z)} = 2 H_0(-z)$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Verification by substitution into $S(z)$:

$$S(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[(-2 H_1(-z)) H_0(-z) + (2 H_0(-z)) H_1(-z)\right]$$

$$S(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[-2 H_0(-z) H_1(-z) + 2 H_0(-z) H_1(-z)\right] = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 (0.5 mark):** Conclusion: The system is alias-free, but PR also requires $T(z) = c \, z^{-n_0}$, which depends on the specific choice of analysis filters $H_0(z)$ and $H_1(z)$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** For a two-channel QMF bank with $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ and synthesis filters $G_0(z) = H_0(z)$, $G_1(z) = H_0(-z)$, derive the distortion function $T(z)$. Show that aliasing is automatically cancelled. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the power-symmetry condition $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\pi - \omega)}) \vert^2 = 2$ for a linear-phase FIR lowpass filter $H_0(z)$ of length $N+1$. State the design implication of this condition. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Substitute the QMF relations $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$, $G_0(z) = H_0(z)$, $G_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ into the alias function:

$$S(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[H_0(-z) H_0(z) + H_0(-z) H_0(-z)\right] = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[2 H_0(z) H_0(-z)\right]$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Wait — re-checking: With $G_0(z) = H_0(z)$ and $G_1(z) = H_0(-z)$:

$$S(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[G_0(z) H_0(-z) + G_1(z) H_1(-z)\right] = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[H_0(z) H_0(-z) + H_0(-z) H_0(z)\right] = H_0(z) H_0(-z)$$

Hmm, this is **not zero** in general. The correct QMF setup uses $G_0(z) = H_1(-z) = H_0(z)$ and $G_1(z) = -H_0(-z) = -H_1(z)$ (with a sign flip for alias cancellation):

$$S(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[H_0(z) H_0(-z) + (-H_0(-z)) H_0(z)\right] = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute $T(z)$:

$$T(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[H_0(z) H_0(z) + (-H_0(-z)) H_0(-z)\right] = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[H_0^2(z) - H_0^2(-z)\right]$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Final result:

$$\boxed{T(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[H_0^2(z) - H_0^2(-z)\right] \quad \text{and} \quad S(z) = 0 \text{ (alias cancelled)}}$$

---

**Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** For PR, $T(z)$ must equal $c \, z^{-n_0}$. Choosing $c=1$ and $n_0 = N$ (where $N$ is the filter order), we require:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\left[H_0^2(e^{j\omega}) - H_0^2(e^{j(\pi - \omega)})\right] = e^{-jN\omega}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** For a **linear-phase FIR** filter of length $N+1$ with real coefficients, the frequency response has the form $H_0(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-jN\omega/2} \cdot A(\omega)$, where $A(\omega)$ is a real-valued amplitude function (assuming symmetric impulse response). Then:

$$H_0^2(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-jN\omega} A^2(\omega)$$

$$H_0^2(e^{j(\pi - \omega)}) = e^{-jN(\pi - \omega)} A^2(\pi - \omega) = (-1)^N e^{jN\omega} A^2(\pi - \omega)$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Substituting:

$$\tfrac{1}{2} e^{-jN\omega} \left[ A^2(\omega) - (-1)^N (-1)^{2N\omega/\pi} A^2(\pi - \omega) \right] = e^{-jN\omega}$$

For this to hold for all $\omega$ with a real-valued $A(\omega)$, we need the magnitudes to satisfy:

$$A^2(\omega) + A^2(\pi - \omega) = 2$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Since $A(\omega) = \vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert$:

$$\boxed{\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\pi - \omega)}) \vert^2 = 2}$$

**Design Implication (last sub-step):** This condition states that the magnitude response of $H_0$ and the magnitude response of $H_1 = H_0(-z)$ are **power complementary**. A practical design example is Johnston's 32-tap QMF filter, which satisfies this identity numerically to within machine precision.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and Where They Lose Marks):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ in $T(z)$ and $S(z)$** — Most students write $T(z) = H_0 G_0 + H_1 G_1$ without the downsampling factor. This loses **2 marks** in Part B.
> 2. **Confusing the QMF and CQF relations** — Writing $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ for a CQF loses **1 mark** in Part A.
> 3. **Confusing alias cancellation with full PR** — Alias cancellation $S(z) = 0$ is **necessary but not sufficient** for PR. Many students stop here. The condition $T(z) = c \, z^{-n_0}$ must also hold. This conflation loses **3 marks** in Part B.
> 4. **Wrong sign in the CQF condition** — The correct form is $H_1(z) = -z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$ (with a **minus** sign). Dropping the sign leads to a non-orthogonal system. **Penalty: 2 marks**.
> 5. **Not verifying the final result** — Always substitute your answer back into the original equation to confirm $S(z) = 0$. Examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for the verification step.
> 6. **Mixing up polyphase indices** — $H_{00}(z)$ uses even-indexed samples ($h_0(2n)$) and $H_{01}(z)$ uses odd-indexed samples ($h_0(2n+1)$). Reversing these loses **1 mark** in the polyphase derivation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam:**
>
> - **PR Definition:** $\hat{x}(n) = c \cdot x(n - n_0)$ — exact scaled, delayed replica.
> - **Three PR Conditions:** (1) $S(z) = 0$ alias cancellation, (2) $\vert T(z) \vert = c$ flat magnitude, (3) $\angle T(z) = -\omega n_0$ linear phase.
> - **Key Equations:**
>   - $T(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[H_0(z) G_0(z) + H_1(z) G_1(z)]$
>   - $S(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z)]$
>   - $\hat{X}(z) = T(z) X(z) + S(z) X(-z)$
> - **Synthesis Filters (Alias-Cancelled):** $G_0(z) = -2 H_1(-z)$, $G_1(z) = 2 H_0(-z)$.
> - **QMF Relation:** $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ (magnitude mirror at $\omega = \pi/2$).
> - **QMF PR Condition:** $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\pi - \omega)}) \vert^2 = 2$ (power symmetry).
> - **CQF Relation:** $H_1(z) = -z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$.
> - **CQF PR Identity:** $H_0(z) H_0(z^{-1}) + H_0(-z) H_0(-z^{-1}) = 2$.
> - **Polyphase Matrix:** $E(z) = \begin{bmatrix} H_{00}(z) & H_{01}(z) \\ H_{10}(z) & H_{11}(z) \end{bmatrix}$.
> - **Paraunitary PR Condition:** $E(z) \tilde{E}(z^{-1}) = c I$.
> - **Noble Identities:** Down-sample-then-filter is equivalent to filter-then-down-sample with $z^2$.
> - **Daubechies Connection:** All Daubechies wavelets (db2, db3, ...) arise from CQF-based PR filter banks.
> - **CDF Connection:** Cohen–Daubechies–Feauveau biorthogonal wavelets arise from biorthogonal PR filter banks.
> - **JTFA / Lifting:** Lifting schemes preserve the PR property by construction.
> - **Energy:** In a PR system, total signal energy is preserved (Parseval's theorem).
> - **Practical Design:** The Johnston and Smith–Barnwell filter design algorithms target the power-symmetry condition.
> - **Exam Tip:** Always define $T(z)$ and $S(z)$ explicitly before applying PR conditions. Boxed final answers get full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time-Frequency Analysis of Signals - Spectral Analysis of Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Time-Frequency Analysis of Signals - Spectral Analysis of Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Time-Frequency Analysis: Formal Definition

**Time-Frequency Analysis (TFA)** is a class of signal processing techniques that describe and represent a signal simultaneously in the **time domain** and the **frequency domain**, producing a two-dimensional function known as a **Time-Frequency Distribution (TFD)**. The goal is to capture *when* particular frequency components occur within a non-stationary signal, something the classical Fourier Transform fundamentally cannot reveal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Conceptual Form):**
> A Time-Frequency Representation (TFR) is a function $T_x(t, \omega)$ of two independent variables — time $t$ and angular frequency $\omega$ — that describes the energy density (or intensity) of a signal $x(t)$ at the joint location $(t, \omega)$ in the time-frequency plane.

The standard classical tools belonging to this family include:
- The **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)**
- The **Gabor Transform**
- The **Wigner–Ville Distribution (WVD)**
- The **Spectrogram** (the squared magnitude of STFT)
- The **Wavelet Transform** (the multiresolution extension of TFA)

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — Listening to a Symphony

Imagine you are listening to a **90-minute orchestra recording**:
- A **musical score** tells you *which notes are played* (frequency content) but not *when* they are played. This is the role of the **Fourier Transform** — a global, time-blind analysis.
- If you wanted to know *when* the violins come in during the third movement, you would rewind and listen **in small chunks**. Each chunk is a **short window** that you Fourier-analyze separately. The collection of all these small Fourier Transforms, stitched side-by-side, is the **STFT**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** Think of the STFT as a **moving spotlight** sweeping across a piece of music. The spotlight's **width** represents the window length. A narrow spotlight gives precise *temporal* localization but blurry *frequency* resolution; a wide spotlight gives precise *frequency* resolution but blurry *temporal* localization. This trade-off is mathematically codified in the **Heisenberg–Gabor Uncertainty Principle**.

## 1.3 Why the Classical Fourier Transform Fails for Non-Stationary Signals

The classical **Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT)** is given by:

$$
X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt
$$

This integral is computed over the **entire duration** of the signal. Therefore, $X(\omega)$ reveals *what* frequencies are present, but **never *when* they occur**. For a **non-stationary** signal (whose spectral content changes over time — e.g., speech, music, ECG, radar chirps, seismic waves), this global averaging destroys crucial time-local information.

**Illustrative Failure Case:** Consider two signals:
- $x_1(t)$: a sinusoid of 10 Hz from $t = 0$ to $t = 1$ s, then 40 Hz from $t = 1$ s to $t = 2$ s.
- $x_2(t)$: a sinusoid of 40 Hz from $t = 0$ to $t = 1$ s, then 10 Hz from $t = 1$ s to $t = 2$ s.

Both signals possess identical Fourier magnitudes, yet they are entirely different signals in practical terms. The Fourier Transform cannot distinguish them.

## 1.4 Stationary vs. Non-Stationary Signals — The Key Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A signal is **wide-sense stationary (WSS)** if its mean and autocorrelation are invariant to time shifts. The Fourier Transform is mathematically optimal for such signals. For **non-stationary** signals, the Fourier Transform is **insufficient**, and joint time-frequency methods become necessary.

| Property | Stationary Signal | Non-Stationary Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Statistical parameters | Time-invariant | Time-varying |
| FT sufficient? | **Yes** | **No** |
| TFA required? | No | **Yes** |
| Examples | Pure sinusoid, white noise | Speech, music, ECG, chirp |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) — Mathematical Foundation

The **STFT** of a signal $x(t)$ is defined as the Fourier Transform of the signal multiplied by a short sliding analysis window $w(t-\tau)$ centered at time $\tau$:

$$
\text{STFT}_x^{(w)}(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, w^{*}(t-\tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt
$$

**Equivalent inner-product (projection) form:**

$$
\text{STFT}_x^{(w)}(\tau, \omega) = \left\langle x(t),\, w(t-\tau)\, e^{j\omega t} \right\rangle = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, \left[ w(t-\tau)\, e^{j\omega t} \right]^{*} dt
$$

### 2.1.1 Why This Definition Works
1. **Multiplication by $w(t-\tau)$** isolates the portion of the signal **localized around time $\tau$**.
2. **The FT of the windowed segment** then reveals the frequencies present *only within* that small interval.
3. **Sliding $\tau$ across the time axis** produces a 2-D function $X(\tau, \omega)$ — the time-frequency map.
4. The window is typically **real, even, and symmetric** (e.g., Gaussian, Hamming) and is **normalized to unit energy** $\int |w(t)|^2 dt = 1$.

### 2.1.2 The Inversion Formula (Synthesis)
Provided the window satisfies the **admissibility condition** (specifically, $\int w(t)\, dt \neq 0$ for STFT — a weaker condition than wavelets), the original signal can be perfectly recovered:

$$
x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \text{STFT}_x^{(w)}(\tau, \omega)\, w(t-\tau)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega\, d\tau \,\Big/\, \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} |w(t)|^2 dt
$$

## 2.2 The Spectrogram

The **Spectrogram** is the squared magnitude of the STFT and represents a real-valued, non-negative energy distribution:

$$
S_x^{(w)}(\tau, \omega) = \left| \text{STFT}_x^{(w)}(\tau, \omega) \right|^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The spectrogram estimates the **local energy density** of $x(t)$ in the time-frequency plane. It is the most widely used visual TFR in applications such as speech analysis, audio forensics, and biomedical signal processing.

## 2.3 The Gabor Transform (Complex-Modulated STFT)

When the window $w(t)$ is a **Gaussian**, the STFT is called the **Gabor Transform** and the analysis atoms (windowed sinusoids) attain the **minimum possible time-frequency area** — i.e., the optimal joint localization permitted by the uncertainty principle.

## 2.4 Window Functions — Engineering Toolkit

The choice of window governs the trade-off between main-lobe width (frequency resolution) and side-lobe leakage (spectral leakage). The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following:

| Window Type | Time-Domain Expression $w(t)$ (centered at 0) | Key Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $w(t) = 1$ for $\vert t \vert \le T/2$, else $0$ | Best freq. resolution, worst leakage |
| Hamming | $w(t) = 0.54 + 0.46 \cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi t}{T}\right)$ | Low first side-lobe ($\approx -41$ dB) |
| Hanning (Hann) | $w(t) = 0.5 + 0.5 \cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi t}{T}\right)$ | Smooth roll-off, $-31$ dB side-lobe |
| Blackman | $w(t) = 0.42 + 0.5\cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi t}{T}\right) + 0.08\cos\left(\dfrac{4\pi t}{T}\right)$ | Very low leakage, wide main lobe |
| Gaussian | $w(t) = e^{-t^2 / (2\sigma^2)}$ | Best joint T-F localization |

## 2.5 Time-Frequency Resolution — The Uncertainty Principle

A fundamental theoretical and engineering limitation is the **Heisenberg–Gabor Uncertainty Inequality**:

$$
\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_\omega \;\ge\; \frac{1}{2}
$$

where $\sigma_t$ and $\sigma_\omega$ are the **standard deviations** of $\vert x(t) \vert^2$ and $\vert X(\omega) \vert^2$ respectively, treated as probability densities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equality holds if and only if $x(t)$ is a Gaussian.** This is why the Gaussian window is uniquely privileged in Gabor analysis.

### 2.5.1 Practical Consequence in STFT
- **Narrow window** $\Rightarrow$ small $\sigma_t$ (good time resolution) $\Rightarrow$ large $\sigma_\omega$ (poor frequency resolution).
- **Wide window** $\Rightarrow$ large $\sigma_t$ (poor time resolution) $\Rightarrow$ small $\sigma_\omega$ (good frequency resolution).
- **The resolution is uniform across the entire T-F plane.** This is the **chief motivation for the Wavelet Transform**, which uses multi-resolution analysis with short windows at high frequencies and long windows at low frequencies.

## 2.6 The Wigner–Ville Distribution (WVD) — A Bilinear TFR

The **Wigner Distribution** of a complex analytic signal $z(t)$ is defined as:

$$
W_z(t, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} z\!\left(t + \frac{\tau}{2}\right) z^{*}\!\left(t - \frac{\tau}{2}\right) e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The WVD achieves the **theoretical maximum T-F concentration** (theoretically sharp time-frequency atoms), but it suffers from **cross-term interference** for multi-component signals. This limitation prompted the development of smoothed and re-assigned variants (Choi–Williams, Rihaczek, etc.).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Remark |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fourier Transform | $X(\omega) = \int x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ | Global, no time info |
| 2 | STFT | $\text{STFT}_x(\tau,\omega) = \int x(t) w^{*}(t-\tau) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ | Joint T-F analysis |
| 3 | STFT (alt. form) | $\text{STFT}_x(\tau,\omega) = \int X(\Omega) W^{*}(\Omega-\omega) e^{-j\Omega \tau} d\Omega$ | Multiplication in time = convolution in frequency |
| 4 | Spectrogram | $S_x(\tau,\omega) = \vert \text{STFT}_x(\tau,\omega) \vert^2$ | Real, non-negative energy density |
| 5 | Inversion | $x(t) = \dfrac{1}{C_w} \iint \text{STFT}_x(\tau,\omega) w(t-\tau) e^{j\omega t} d\omega d\tau$ | $C_w = 2\pi \int \vert w(t) \vert^2 dt$ |
| 6 | Energy recovery (Parseval) | $\iint S_x(\tau,\omega)\, d\tau d\omega = 2\pi E_x$ | Total energy equality |
| 7 | Heisenberg–Gabor | $\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_\omega \ge \frac{1}{2}$ | Equality for Gaussian |
| 8 | Wigner–Ville | $W_z(t,\omega) = \int z(t+\tau/2) z^{*}(t-\tau/2) e^{-j\omega \tau} d\tau$ | Maximum T-F concentration, has cross terms |
| 9 | Gabor Transform | STFT with $w(t) = e^{-t^2/2\sigma^2}$ | Optimal T-F localization |
| 10 | Resolution | $\Delta t = $ window length, $\Delta f = 1/\Delta t$ | Inversely related |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall (KTU Valuation):** When writing the STFT, the window argument is $(t - \tau)$, *not* $(\tau - t)$. Also, complex conjugation is on the *window* (or equivalently on the modulated atom in the inner-product form). Many students lose marks by misplacing the conjugation or the time-shift sign.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: STFT as a Filter Bank Interpretation

A deeper engineering understanding of the STFT is obtained by recasting it as a **bandpass filter bank** of complex-modulated, time-shifted analysis filters.

**Step 1 — Start with the STFT definition.**

$$
\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, w^{*}(t-\tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Re-group the modulation and the window shift.**

We treat the complex exponential and the shifted window as a *single* time-varying analysis atom. We can write this as the inner product of $x(t)$ with a complex atom:

$$
\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) = \left\langle x(t),\, h_{\tau,\omega}(t) \right\rangle, \quad \text{where } h_{\tau,\omega}(t) = w(t-\tau)\, e^{j\omega t}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the change of variables to convert the time-domain product into a frequency-domain convolution.**

Let $u = t - \tau$, so $t = u + \tau$ and $dt = du$. Substituting:

$$
\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(u+\tau)\, w^{*}(u)\, e^{-j\omega (u+\tau)}\, du
$$

**Step 4 — Factor out the time-shift exponential.**

$$
\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) = e^{-j\omega \tau} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \left[ x(u+\tau)\, e^{-j\omega u} \right] w^{*}(u)\, du
$$

**Step 5 — Express the inner integral as a frequency-domain convolution using the duality of the convolution theorem.**

Recognize that the integral is the cross-correlation of $X(\Omega) e^{j\Omega \tau}$ (the FT of $x(t+\tau)$) with $W(\Omega - \omega)$:

$$
\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) = e^{-j\omega \tau} \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(\Omega)\, e^{j\Omega \tau}\, W^{*}(\Omega - \omega)\, d\Omega
$$

**Step 6 — Final filter-bank form.**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) = e^{-j\omega \tau}\, \frac{1}{2\pi}\, \left[ X(\Omega) * W^{*}(\Omega) \right]_{\Omega = \omega} \cdot e^{j\Omega \tau}\;}
$$

This shows that the STFT can be implemented by a **bank of bandpass filters** whose impulse responses are the modulated, time-shifted atoms $h_{\tau,\omega}(t)$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Recovery (Spectrogram Total Energy)

**Goal:** Show that integrating the spectrogram over both time and frequency yields $2\pi$ times the total signal energy.

**Step 1 — Start with the spectrogram.**

$$
S_x(\tau,\omega) = \left| \text{STFT}_x(\tau,\omega) \right|^2 = \text{STFT}_x(\tau,\omega) \cdot \text{STFT}_x^{*}(\tau,\omega)
$$

**Step 2 — Write the full double integral of the spectrogram.**

$$
I = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(\tau,\omega)\, d\omega\, d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \text{STFT}_x(\tau,\omega)\, \text{STFT}_x^{*}(\tau,\omega)\, d\omega\, d\tau
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the explicit STFT expression for both factors.**

$$
I = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, w^{*}(t-\tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt \right] \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x^{*}(t')\, w(t'-\tau)\, e^{j\omega t'}\, dt' \right] d\omega\, d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Swap the order of integration (justified by Fubini's theorem).**

$$
I = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) x^{*}(t') \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} w^{*}(t-\tau) w(t'-\tau) e^{-j\omega(t-t')}\, d\omega\, d\tau \right] dt\, dt'
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the $\omega$-integral using the orthogonality of complex exponentials.**

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{-j\omega(t-t')}\, d\omega = 2\pi\, \delta(t - t')
$$

Substituting this yields:

$$
I = 2\pi \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) x^{*}(t')\, \delta(t-t') \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} w^{*}(t-\tau) w(t'-\tau)\, d\tau \right] dt\, dt'
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the sifting property of the delta function.**

$$
I = 2\pi \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} |x(t)|^2 \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} w^{*}(t-\tau) w(t-\tau)\, d\tau \right] dt
$$

**Step 7 — Simplify using the change of variable $u = t - \tau$ and the normalization of the window energy.**

Define $E_w = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} |w(u)|^2 du$. Then:

$$
I = 2\pi \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} |x(t)|^2 \cdot E_w\, dt = 2\pi E_w \cdot E_x
$$

**Step 8 — Specialize to the standard normalization $E_w = 1$.**

$$
\boxed{\;\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(\tau,\omega)\, d\omega\, d\tau = 2\pi E_x\;}
$$

This confirms the **energy-preservation property** of the spectrogram.

## 3.3 Derivation: The Heisenberg–Gabor Uncertainty Bound (Intuitive Form)

**Step 1 — Define the time-domain probability density from the signal energy.**

Treat $\vert x(t) \vert^2$ as a probability density (it is non-negative and integrates to finite energy $E_x$):

$$
p_t(t) = \frac{|x(t)|^2}{E_x}, \quad \int p_t(t)\, dt = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean time $\bar{t}$ and the time-spread $\sigma_t^2$.**

$$
\bar{t} = \int t\, p_t(t)\, dt, \quad \sigma_t^2 = \int (t - \bar{t})^2\, p_t(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Repeat in the frequency domain using Parseval's relation.**

The FT $X(\omega)$ of $x(t)$ satisfies $E_x = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int |X(\omega)|^2 d\omega$, so define analogously:

$$
p_\omega(\omega) = \frac{|X(\omega)|^2}{2\pi E_x}, \quad \sigma_\omega^2 = \int (\omega - \bar{\omega})^2\, p_\omega(\omega)\, d\omega
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the classical Cauchy–Schwarz inequality to the inner product of $t\cdot x(t)$ and $x'(t)$ (derivative w.r.t. $t$).**

By Parseval, $\int t\, x(t) [x'(t)]^* dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int [j\omega X(\omega)]^* [j X(\omega) + j\omega X'(\omega)]\, d\omega$ — and the cross-term simplifies to yield a bound on $\sigma_t \sigma_\omega$.

The rigorous result, after simplification, is:

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_\omega \;\ge\; \frac{1}{2}\;}
$$

with **equality if and only if $x(t) = A\, e^{j\omega_0 t} e^{-(t-t_0)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$**, a translated, modulated Gaussian.

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example — STFT of a Gaussian-Modulated Chirp

Consider $x(t) = e^{-t^2/2}\, e^{j\alpha t^2/2}$ — a Gaussian-windowed linear FM (chirp).

**Step 1 — Identify the analysis window as Gaussian and substitute.**

With $w(t) = e^{-t^2/2}$, the STFT becomes:

$$
\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{-t^2/2} e^{j\alpha t^2/2} e^{-t^2/2} e^{-j\omega t} e^{+j\omega \tau} dt
$$

(using $w(t-\tau) = e^{-(t-\tau)^2/2}$ and its complex conjugate as $e^{-(t-\tau)^2/2}$)

**Step 2 — Combine the exponent terms and complete the square.**

The combined exponent (excluding $-j\omega t$) is:

$$
-\frac{t^2}{2} - \frac{(t-\tau)^2}{2} + \frac{j\alpha t^2}{2} = -\left(1 - \frac{j\alpha}{2}\right) t^2 + t\tau - \frac{\tau^2}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the resulting integral as a Gaussian integral and evaluate.**

The integral evaluates to a closed-form expression involving $\sqrt{\pi/(1 - j\alpha/2)}$, which represents a **time-frequency "ridge"** that tracks the instantaneous frequency of the chirp $\omega_i(t) = \alpha t$ — a beautiful illustration of STFT's ability to localize chirp signals in the T-F plane.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which TFR

| Signal Type | Best TFR | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Stationary sinusoid | Fourier Transform (or STFT with any window) | Time information is irrelevant |
| Slowly varying tones | STFT with long window | High frequency resolution needed |
| Transients (impacts, clicks) | STFT with short window | High time resolution needed |
| Chirps / FM signals | Wigner–Ville or Reassignment | Need to track instantaneous frequency |
| Music / speech | Spectrogram | Visual interpretability, established practice |
| Multi-scale features | Wavelet Transform | Variable resolution; overcomes STFT's uniform resolution |
| Multi-component signals | Smoothed Pseudo WVD | Suppresses WVD cross-terms while preserving resolution |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of STFT Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal x(t)] --> B[Time-Delay Stage]
    B --> C[Window w(t-tau)]
    C --> D[Complex Modulator e to the power j omega t]
    D --> E[Fourier Transform Kernel]
    E --> F[STFT Output X of tau, omega]
    F --> G[Magnitude Squared]
    G --> H[Spectrogram Sx tau omega]
    H --> I[2D T-F Display]

    subgraph Inverse_Synthesis
        J[STFT Coefficients] --> K[Synthesis Window w t minus tau]
        K --> L[Modulator e to the power j omega t]
        L --> M[Overlap Add Integrator]
        M --> N[Reconstructed x hat of t]
    end
```

## 4.2 Time-Frequency Resolution Trade-off — Visual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Wide_Window
        A1[Long Window in Time] --> A2[Narrow main lobe in Frequency]
        A2 --> A3[GOOD Frequency Resolution]
        A2 --> A4[POOR Time Resolution]
    end
    subgraph Narrow_Window
        B1[Short Window in Time] --> B2[Wide main lobe in Frequency]
        B2 --> B3[GOOD Time Resolution]
        B2 --> B4[POOR Frequency Resolution]
    end
    subgraph Heisenberg_Bound
        C1[sigma_t times sigma_omega greater than or equal to 0.5]
        C2[Equality only for Gaussian]
    end
    Wide_Window --> Heisenberg_Bound
    Narrow_Window --> Heisenberg_Bound
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Tree of Time-Frequency Representations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TFA[Time-Frequency Representations] --> Linear[Linear TFRs]
    TFA --> Bilinear[Bilinear TFRs]

    Linear --> STFT[Short-Time Fourier Transform]
    Linear --> Gabor[Gabor Transform Gaussian Window]
    Linear --> WT[Wavelet Transform]

    STFT --> Spectrogram[Spectrogram = |STFT| squared]
    Gabor --> Spectrogram

    Bilinear --> WVD[Wigner-Ville Distribution]
    WVD --> SPWVD[Smoothed Pseudo WVD]
    WVD --> Rihaczek[Rihaczek Distribution]
    WVD --> Choi[Choi-Williams Distribution]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow: STFT Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S0[Start: Continuous signal x of t] --> S1[Choose window w of t and length T]
    S1 --> S2[Choose hop size H less than T]
    S2 --> S3[Segment 1: slice x from 0 to T, multiply by w]
    S3 --> S4[Compute N-point FFT of windowed segment]
    S4 --> S5[Store as STFT column at tau = H]
    S5 --> S6{Slide by H}
    S6 -->|Next segment| S3
    S6 -->|End of signal| S7[Stack columns to form 2D STFT matrix]
    S7 --> S8[Compute magnitude squared to get spectrogram]
    S8 --> S9[Plot spectrogram as T-F image]
```

## 4.5 Comparison: STFT Tiling vs. Wavelet Tiling of the T-F Plane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STFT_Uniform
        T1[Time axis t] --> T2[omega axis]
        T1 --> R1[Tile 1: same width, same height]
        T1 --> R2[Tile 2: same width, same height]
        T1 --> R3[Tile 3: same width, same height]
        T1 --> R4[Tile 4: same width, same height]
    end
    subgraph Wavelet_Adaptive
        W1[Time axis t] --> W2[omega axis]
        W1 --> Q1[Tile 1: short in time, tall in frequency - high omega]
        W1 --> Q2[Tile 2: short in time, tall in frequency - high omega]
        W1 --> Q3[Tile 3: long in time, short in frequency - low omega]
        W1 --> Q4[Tile 4: long in time, short in frequency - low omega]
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> *Define the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT). Explain how it overcomes the limitation of the conventional Fourier Transform in analyzing non-stationary signals.*

**Model Answer:**

The STFT of a signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$
\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, w^{*}(t-\tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt
$$

where $w(t-\tau)$ is a sliding analysis window centered at time $\tau$, and $\omega$ is the angular frequency.

**Limitation of FT:** The classical FT $X(\omega) = \int x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ integrates over the *entire* signal duration, providing only *global* frequency information. For non-stationary signals, FT cannot indicate *when* a particular frequency occurs.

**How STFT overcomes this:** By multiplying $x(t)$ with a short, sliding window $w(t-\tau)$ before applying the FT, the STFT effectively analyzes the signal *locally in time*. The output $\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega)$ is a 2-D function of time-shift $\tau$ and frequency $\omega$, giving a joint time-frequency representation that captures both the *what* and the *when* of the signal's spectral content.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for FT limitation, 1 for STFT remedy)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> *State and explain the Heisenberg–Gabor uncertainty principle. What is its significance in time-frequency analysis?*

**Model Answer:**

The Heisenberg–Gabor uncertainty principle states that for any finite-energy signal $x(t)$ and its Fourier transform $X(\omega)$, the product of the time spread $\sigma_t$ and the frequency spread $\sigma_\omega$ is bounded below:

$$
\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_\omega \;\ge\; \frac{1}{2}
$$

where $\sigma_t^2 = \int (t - \bar{t})^2 |x(t)|^2 dt / E_x$ and similarly for $\sigma_\omega$.

**Significance:**
1. It is **impossible** to simultaneously achieve arbitrarily high resolution in both time and frequency.
2. **Equality holds only for Gaussian signals** — justifying the privileged use of the Gaussian window in Gabor analysis.
3. It establishes a **fundamental trade-off** in STFT design: short windows (good time resolution, poor frequency resolution) vs. long windows (good frequency resolution, poor time resolution).
4. It motivates the development of **multi-resolution methods** like the Wavelet Transform, which use variable window sizes adapted to the frequency band of interest.

*(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for definition of spreads, 1 for significance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) of a signal $x(t)$ using a window $w(t)$, clearly explaining the role of the window function. Explain why a Gaussian window is preferred for Gabor analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the signal $x(t) = \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ with $f_0 = 10$ Hz. Compute and plot (sketch) the magnitude of the STFT using:
   - (i) a rectangular window of length $T = 0.1$ s
   - (ii) a rectangular window of length $T = 1.0$ s
   
   Discuss the trade-off in frequency resolution versus time resolution. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) STFT Derivation and Window Role**

The conventional FT $X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ is a global transform that loses all time information. To retain temporal locality, we define a **sliding analysis window** $w(t)$ that is non-zero only over a finite interval. The STFT of $x(t)$ is then defined as:

$$
\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, w^{*}(t-\tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt
$$

**Derivation of Inversion Formula:** Aiming to recover $x(t)$ from its STFT, we multiply both sides by $w(t-\tau) e^{j\omega t}$ and integrate over $\tau$ and $\omega$:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega)\, w(t-\tau)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega\, d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t')\, w^{*}(t'-\tau)\, w(t-\tau)\, e^{-j\omega t'}\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\tau\, d\omega\, dt'
$$

Using $\int e^{j\omega(t-t')} d\omega = 2\pi \delta(t-t')$ and assuming $\int w^{*}(t'-\tau) w(t-\tau) d\tau = C_w \delta(t-t')$:

$$
\therefore\ x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi C_w} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega)\, w(t-\tau)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega\, d\tau
$$

**Role of the window:**
- Localizes the signal in time before Fourier analysis.
- Controls the **time-frequency resolution trade-off** (window length $T$ determines $\Delta t \sim T$ and $\Delta f \sim 1/T$).
- Suppresses spectral leakage via side-lobe reduction.
- Different windows (Hamming, Hanning, Blackman) offer different main-lobe vs. side-lobe trade-offs.

**Why Gaussian?** The Gaussian $w(t) = e^{-t^2/2\sigma^2}$ is the **unique** window that simultaneously:
- Achieves the **minimum joint time-frequency area** (Heisenberg–Gabor bound is met with equality).
- Has the **shortest possible frequency-domain main lobe** for a given time-domain width.
- Is **closed under FT** (FT of a Gaussian is a Gaussian), preserving symmetry.

**(b) STFT of a Pure Cosine — Resolution Trade-off**

For $x(t) = \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ with $f_0 = 10$ Hz:

The FT of an infinite cosine is $X(\omega) = \pi [\delta(\omega - 2\pi f_0) + \delta(\omega + 2\pi f_0)]$, producing two perfect delta spikes at $\pm 10$ Hz.

**(i) Rectangular window, $T = 0.1$ s:**
The windowed signal is $x_T(t) = \cos(2\pi f_0 t) \cdot \text{rect}(t/T)$, whose FT is:

$$
X_T(\omega) = \frac{T}{2} \left[ \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{(\omega - 2\pi f_0)T}{2}\right) + \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{(\omega + 2\pi f_0)T}{2}\right) \right]
$$

The main lobe width is $\Delta f = 2/T = 20$ Hz, which is *wider* than the spacing to the side lobes, producing a **smeared** spectrum. Frequency resolution: poor; Time resolution: good (only 0.1 s of signal analyzed).

**(ii) Rectangular window, $T = 1.0$ s:**
Main lobe width $\Delta f = 2/T = 2$ Hz, producing **sharp, well-resolved peaks** at $\pm 10$ Hz with visible side lobes. Frequency resolution: excellent; Time resolution: degraded (a transient lasting < 1 s would be missed).

**Conclusion:** The longer window gives finer frequency detail but at the cost of time resolution. This empirically verifies the Heisenberg–Gabor principle: $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \ge \text{constant}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define the **Spectrogram**. Show that the total energy of a signal can be recovered from its spectrogram by integrating over the time-frequency plane. State the formula obtained. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the filter-bank interpretation of the STFT. Discuss how the choice of window affects the time-frequency resolution, with particular reference to the Heisenberg–Gabor uncertainty principle. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Spectrogram Definition and Energy Recovery**

The **Spectrogram** is defined as the squared magnitude of the STFT:

$$
S_x(\tau, \omega) = \left| \text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) \right|^2
$$

It represents a real, non-negative time-frequency energy density.

**Energy Recovery Proof (sketch):**

$$
E_{\text{TFR}} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(\tau, \omega)\, d\tau\, d\omega
$$

Substituting the STFT expressions and exchanging the order of integration (using Fubini's theorem), one obtains:

$$
E_{\text{TFR}} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} |x(t)|^2 \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} |w(t-\tau)|^2\, d\tau\, dt = 2\pi\, E_w\, E_x
$$

With the standard normalization $E_w = \int |w(t)|^2 dt = 1$:

$$
\boxed{\;E_{\text{TFR}} = 2\pi\, E_x\;}
$$

The factor $2\pi$ arises from the angular-frequency convention; using ordinary frequency $f$ instead of $\omega = 2\pi f$ removes this factor. The spectrogram thus *preserves* (up to a constant) the total signal energy — a desirable property analogous to Parseval's theorem for the Fourier transform.

**(b) Filter-Bank Interpretation and Resolution Trade-off**

The STFT can be implemented as a **uniform bandpass filter bank**. Each atom $h_{\tau,\omega}(t) = w(t-\tau) e^{j\omega t}$ acts as a bandpass filter centered at frequency $\omega$ and localized in time around $\tau$. The STFT coefficient $\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega)$ is the output of this filter (followed by a correlator), sampled at time $\tau$.

**Block Diagram (textual):**
```
x(t) → [BPF centered at ω₁] → y₁(t) → sampled at τ → STFT(τ,ω₁)
x(t) → [BPF centered at ω₂] → y₂(t) → sampled at τ → STFT(τ,ω₂)
   ⋮
x(t) → [BPF centered at ω_N] → y_N(t) → sampled at τ → STFT(τ,ω_N)
```

**Effect of Window Choice on Resolution:**

For a window of length $T$:

- **Time resolution:** $\Delta t \approx T$ — short window → sharp temporal localization.
- **Frequency resolution:** $\Delta f \approx 1/T$ — short window → broad main lobe → poor frequency localization.

This inverse relationship is a direct consequence of the **Heisenberg–Gabor inequality** $\sigma_t \sigma_\omega \ge 1/2$. A wider window reduces $\sigma_\omega$ at the cost of increasing $\sigma_t$, and vice versa.

**Practical Implications:**
- For **speech analysis**: typically use Hamming window of 20–40 ms.
- For **transient detection**: use short rectangular window (5–10 ms) despite higher leakage.
- For **spectral estimation of steady tones**: use long Hanning or Blackman window (100 ms or more).
- The uniform resolution of STFT is its **fundamental limitation**, motivating the Wavelet Transform which uses **variable (multi-)resolution**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in STFT/Spectral Analysis Questions:**
> 1. **Sign and conjugation errors:** The window in the STFT must be $w^{*}(t-\tau)$ (complex conjugate), not $w(t-\tau)$. Many students omit the asterisk.
> 2. **Time-shift direction:** Some texts define the STFT with $w(\tau-t)$ instead of $w(t-\tau)$; KTU follows the *standard* engineering convention $w(t-\tau)$ unless otherwise specified. Always state the convention used.
> 3. **Confusing $\omega$ and $f$:** When the question uses $f$ (Hz), the answer must use $2\pi f$ in the exponential — and vice versa. Switching mid-derivation costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the energy normalization constant** ($2\pi E_w$) in the inversion formula. Examiners specifically look for this constant.
> 5. **Misstating the Heisenberg bound:** The correct form is $\sigma_t \sigma_\omega \ge 1/2$ *only* if both spreads are defined in terms of $|x(t)|^2$ and $|X(\omega)|^2$ as densities. Mixing the definitions (e.g., using $f$ instead of $\omega$) changes the constant.
> 6. **Confusing "STFT" with "Spectrogram":** STFT is *complex*; Spectrogram is its *magnitude squared* and is *real and non-negative*. Many students conflate the two.
> 7. **Skipping the role of the window:** A "define STFT" question that omits the window's role in time localization loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaways — Module 2: Time-Frequency & Spectral Analysis**

- ✅ **Fourier Transform limitation:** $X(\omega)$ is a *global* time-blind representation — unsuitable for non-stationary signals.
- ✅ **STFT definition:** $\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) = \int x(t)\, w^{*}(t-\tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$. It is a *linear*, *complex* TFR.
- ✅ **Spectrogram:** $S_x(\tau,\omega) = \vert \text{STFT}_x(\tau,\omega) \vert^2$ — real, non-negative, energy-preserving.
- ✅ **Gabor Transform:** STFT with a Gaussian window — achieves the *minimum* joint T-F area (Heisenberg equality).
- ✅ **Inversion formula:** $x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi E_w} \iint \text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega)\, w(t-\tau) e^{j\omega t} d\omega d\tau$.
- ✅ **Energy preservation:** $\iint S_x(\tau,\omega) d\omega d\tau = 2\pi E_x$ (with $E_w = 1$).
- ✅ **Heisenberg–Gabor:** $\sigma_t \sigma_\omega \ge 1/2$, equality for Gaussian.
- ✅ **Resolution trade-off:** $\Delta t \Delta f \ge 1/(4\pi)$ (or $\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \ge 1/2$).
- ✅ **WVD:** Achieves maximum T-F concentration but has **cross-terms** for multi-component signals.
- ✅ **Motivation for wavelets:** STFT has *uniform* T-F resolution; wavelets use *multi-resolution* (short windows at high $\omega$, long windows at low $\omega$).
- ✅ **Filter-bank view:** STFT = uniformly spaced bandpass filter bank, where each filter is a modulated, time-shifted atom.
- ✅ **Window trade-offs:** Rectangular (sharp, leaky) vs. Hamming/Hanning (smooth, low leakage) vs. Gaussian (optimal joint localization).
- ✅ **Stationary vs. non-stationary:** FT suffices for stationary signals; TFA mandatory for non-stationary signals.
- ✅ **Exam tip:** Always state the *convention* (angular $\omega$ vs. ordinary $f$) and the *normalization* used.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spectral Leakage by Windowing Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Spectral Leakage by Windowing Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** and **Multirate Digital Signal Processing**, *Spectral Leakage* is defined as the **spreading (or smearing) of a signal's true frequency energy into adjacent frequency bins** when a finite-duration segment of an underlying infinite-length (or longer) sequence is analyzed.

Mathematically, when we compute the **N-point DFT**, we are implicitly **windowing** the input sequence $x[n]$ with a **rectangular window** $w_R[n]$ of length $N$:

$$x_N[n] = x[n] \cdot w_R[n]$$

This multiplication in the time domain corresponds to **periodic (or aperiodic) convolution in the frequency domain** with the window's frequency response. When the input signal contains frequency components that do **not coincide exactly with the DFT bin centers** ($f_k = k \cdot f_s/N$), the convolution smears energy from those components across multiple bins instead of concentrating them in one. This phenomenon is **Spectral Leakage**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):**
> Spectral leakage arises directly from the assumption that the signal under analysis is **periodic with period N**. Real-world signals rarely satisfy this periodicity, and the resulting mismatch between the actual signal frequency and the DFT grid frequencies causes leakage.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are looking at a faraway lighthouse through a **slit** (a narrow vertical opening) and trying to determine the **exact angle of its beam**.

- If the lighthouse beam happens to point **straight at the slit**, you see a single sharp bright line — this is **leakage-free** (a *coherent* frequency aligned with a DFT bin).
- If the beam points at a **slight angle**, the light reflects off the edges of the slit and you see light "leaking" sideways into regions that should be dark — this is **spectral leakage**.
- The **width of the slit** represents the **main-lobe width** of the window's frequency response. A wider slit (e.g., Hann window) leaks *more* in spread, but *less* in intensity. A narrower slit (e.g., rectangular window) has a sharper main lobe but produces stronger secondary reflections (side lobes).

### The "Concert Recording" Analogy

Think of recording exactly **30 seconds** of an organ note that happens to be **20.7 seconds long**.

- When you loop those 30 seconds in a player, the waveform **doesn't join smoothly** at the loop boundary (the end does not match the beginning).
- This discontinuity creates **phantom frequencies** in the spectrum that were never in the original note.
- That is precisely what spectral leakage is — the **artificial frequency content created when finite observation truncates non-periodic behavior**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Rectangular Window) |
|---|---|---|
| Main-lobe Width (rad/sample) | $\Delta\omega_M$ | $4\pi/N$ |
| First Side-lobe Level (dB) | $A_{SL}$ | **$-13$ dB** |
| Equivalent Noise Bandwidth (bins) | ENBW | **$1.00$ bin** |
| Scalloping Loss (dB) | $A_{SC}$ | **$3.92$ dB (worst case)** |
| Coherent Sampling Requirement | $f_{coh}$ | $\frac{k \cdot f_s}{N}, \; k \in \mathbb{Z}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Metric — Scalloping Loss:** When the true signal frequency falls *exactly between two DFT bins*, the worst-case amplitude attenuation observed is called **scalloping loss**. For the rectangular window, this is famously **$-3.92$ dB** (≈ 0.637 of the true amplitude).

## 1.4 Role in Wavelets & Multirate Analysis

In **DFT-modulated filter banks** (a major topic in Module 2 of PEAET866), each sub-band filter is a **modulated prototype filter**, and the entire filter bank can be implemented efficiently using a **windowed DFT**. The prototype filter's impulse response, when truncated to a finite length using a window, exhibits **spectral leakage** of its own. Controlling this leakage is essential to achieving **Near-Perfect Reconstruction (NPR)** or **Perfect Reconstruction (PR)** in cosine-modulated and DFT-modulated filter banks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step

The phenomenon of spectral leakage unfolds in a precise logical chain:

- **Step 1 — Implicit Windowing**: Computing the **N-point DFT** of $x[n]$ is *mathematically identical* to first multiplying $x[n]$ by a rectangular window $w_R[n]$ of length $N$, and then taking the DTFT sampled at $N$ equally spaced frequencies.

- **Step 2 — Frequency-Domain Convolution**: Multiplication in time becomes convolution in frequency:
$$X_N(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) \cdot W_R(e^{j(\omega-\theta)}) \, d\theta$$

- **Step 3 — Spreading of a Delta**: If $x[n]$ is a pure complex sinusoid $e^{j\omega_0 n}$, its DTFT is $2\pi \delta(\omega - \omega_0)$. Convolution with $W_R(e^{j\omega})$ replaces the sharp delta with the **frequency response of the rectangular window** (the Dirichlet kernel), shifted to $\omega_0$.

- **Step 4 — Sampling the DTFT**: The DFT samples this spread spectrum at $\omega_k = 2\pi k/N$. If $\omega_0$ aligns with some $\omega_{k_0}$, only one bin receives energy. Otherwise, **multiple bins receive a fraction** of the energy — this is leakage.

- **Step 5 — Side-lobe Contribution**: Even at frequencies far from $\omega_0$, the side lobes of $W_R(e^{j\omega})$ deposit residual energy into bins that should ideally be zero. This is **true leakage** (not just main-lobe spreading).

## 2.2 The Core "Why" and "How"

- **Why does it happen?** Because real-world signals are rarely periodic with period exactly equal to the DFT length $N$, or because their frequencies are not integer multiples of $f_s/N$.
- **How do we control it?** By replacing the rectangular window with a **smoothly tapering window** (Hann, Hamming, Blackman, Kaiser) whose side lobes are much smaller in amplitude.
- **What is the trade-off?** Reducing side-lobe level (less leakage) **widens the main lobe** (poorer frequency resolution). This is the **fundamental trade-off in window design**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula reference** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions on this topic. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation (LaTeX) for absolute value to keep table syntax safe.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rectangular window | $w_R[n] = 1, \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$ | Implicit in any DFT |
| 2 | DTFT of Rectangular window (Dirichlet kernel) | $W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \dfrac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega / 2)} \, e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$ | Magnitude is the aliased sinc |
| 3 | DFT bins | $X[k] = X_N(e^{j\omega}) \big\vert_{\omega = 2\pi k / N}$ | Sampling at $N$ frequencies |
| 4 | Coherent sampling condition | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi k_0}{N}$ for some integer $k_0$ | Eliminates leakage of sinusoid |
| 5 | Main-lobe width (rectangular) | $\Delta\omega_M = \dfrac{4\pi}{N}$ | Distance between first zero-crossings |
| 6 | First side-lobe level (rectangular) | $A_{SL} = -13.26$ dB | Worst among common windows |
| 7 | Scalloping loss (rectangular) | $A_{SC} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\text{sinc}(1/2)}\right) \approx -3.92$ dB | At $\omega_0 = \pi(2k+1)/N$ |
| 8 | Hann window | $w_H[n] = 0.5\!\left(1 - \cos\!\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | Main lobe $8\pi/N$, side lobe $-31.5$ dB |
| 9 | Hamming window | $w_{Hm}[n] = 0.54 - 0.46\cos\!\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}$ | Main lobe $8\pi/N$, side lobe $-42.7$ dB |
| 10 | Blackman window | $w_B[n] = 0.42 - 0.5\cos\!\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1} + 0.08\cos\!\dfrac{4\pi n}{N-1}$ | Main lobe $12\pi/N$, side lobe $-58$ dB |
| 11 | Kaiser window parameter | $w_K[n] = \dfrac{I_0\!\left(\beta\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{2n}{N-1}-1\right)^2}\right)}{I_0(\beta)}$ | $\beta$ tunes trade-off |
| 12 | ENBW (rectangular) | ENBW $= 1.00$ bin | Normalized reference |
| 13 | ENBW (Hann) | ENBW $= 1.50$ bins | $50\%$ wider noise floor |
| 14 | ENBW (Hamming) | ENBW $= 1.36$ bins | Slightly better than Hann |
| 15 | Frequency resolution | $\Delta f = \dfrac{f_s}{N}$ | Depends only on $N$ and $f_s$ |
| 16 | Convolution theorem | $x[n]\,w[n] \;\xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}}\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}X(e^{j\omega}) \circledast W(e^{j\omega})$ | Root cause of leakage |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Spectral leakage is not just a textbook artefact — it directly affects:

- **Audio Coding (MP3, AAC):** MDCT-based filter banks use carefully designed windows (Hann-like) to suppress leakage between sub-bands and avoid *pre-echo* artefacts.
- **OFDM Communication Systems:** Guard intervals (cyclic prefixes) and raised-cosine windows suppress inter-carrier interference, which is mathematically equivalent to controlling spectral leakage.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing (ECG, EEG):** Power-line interference at 50/60 Hz and its harmonics leak into nearby bins; using Blackman windows gives cleaner diagnostic spectra.
- **Vibration Analysis & Machine Diagnostics:** Window choice is critical to detect bearing-fault frequencies near rotating-shaft harmonics without smearing.
- **Filter Bank Prototypes:** In cosine-modulated filter banks (a PEAET866 core topic), the prototype filter's stop-band attenuation is directly linked to its window-induced leakage floor.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic transition is written explicitly. There are **no skipped steps**.

## 3.1 Derivation: DTFT of the Rectangular Window (Dirichlet Kernel)

**Goal:** Find $W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \text{DTFT}\{w_R[n]\}$ where $w_R[n] = 1$ for $0 \le n \le N-1$ and $0$ otherwise.

### Step 1 — Apply the DTFT definition

By the standard DTFT definition:

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} w_R[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the rectangular window's nonzero range

Because $w_R[n]$ is nonzero only for $0 \le n \le N-1$:

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{-j\omega n}$$

### Step 3 — Recognize this as a finite geometric series with ratio $r = e^{-j\omega}$

Using the closed-form $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} r^n = \dfrac{1 - r^N}{1 - r}$ (valid for $r \ne 1$):

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega N}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}$$

### Step 4 — Convert numerator and denominator to sine form using the identity $1 - e^{-j\theta} = 2j \sin(\theta/2) \, e^{-j\theta/2}$

**Numerator** (with $\theta = \omega N$):

$$1 - e^{-j\omega N} = 2j \sin(\omega N / 2) \, e^{-j\omega N / 2}$$

**Denominator** (with $\theta = \omega$):

$$1 - e^{-j\omega} = 2j \sin(\omega / 2) \, e^{-j\omega / 2}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute back and simplify

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{2j \sin(\omega N / 2) \, e^{-j\omega N / 2}}{2j \sin(\omega / 2) \, e^{-j\omega / 2}}$$

The $2j$ factors cancel:

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega / 2)} \, e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$$

### Step 6 — Identify the result

$$\boxed{W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega / 2)} \, e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}}$$

This is the **Dirichlet kernel** $D_N(\omega)$ up to a linear-phase factor. Its magnitude $\vert W_R(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \left\vert \dfrac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega / 2)} \right\vert$ is the **aliased sinc** function.

**Key observations:**
- $\vert W_R(e^{j\omega}) \vert = N$ at $\omega = 0$ (DC gain)
- Zeros occur when $\sin(\omega N / 2) = 0$ but $\sin(\omega / 2) \ne 0$, i.e., at $\omega = 2\pi k / N$ for $k = \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots$ (excluding $k=0$).
- Main-lobe width (between first zeros) is $\Delta\omega_M = \dfrac{4\pi}{N}$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Spectral Leakage of a Pure Sinusoid

**Setup:** Let $x[n] = e^{j\omega_0 n}$ be a complex sinusoid, with $0 < \omega_0 < \pi$.

### Step 1 — DTFT of the infinite sinusoid

$$\text{DTFT}\{e^{j\omega_0 n}\} = 2\pi \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi m)$$

(Aliasing sum from sampling the continuous frequency variable.)

### Step 2 — Multiply by the rectangular window in time

$$x_N[n] = x[n] \cdot w_R[n] = e^{j\omega_0 n} \cdot w_R[n]$$

### Step 3 — Apply the convolution theorem in frequency

$$X_N(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ 2\pi \sum_{m} \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi m) \right] \circledast W_R(e^{j\omega})$$

The convolution with a delta train simply **replicates** the window's response, shifted to each alias location:

$$X_N(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} W_R\!\left(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi m)}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Sample at DFT bin frequencies $\omega_k = 2\pi k / N$

$$X[k] = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} W_R\!\left(e^{j(2\pi k/N - \omega_0 - 2\pi m)}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Analyze two cases

**Case A — Coherent frequency:** $\omega_0 = 2\pi k_0 / N$ for some integer $k_0$.

Then $2\pi k/N - \omega_0 = 2\pi(k - k_0)/N$. One of the $W_R$ terms samples exactly at the **peak** of the Dirichlet kernel (where $\vert W_R \vert = N$), while all other terms sample at the **zeros** of the Dirichlet kernel. Hence:

$$\boxed{X[k_0] = N, \quad X[k] = 0 \text{ for } k \ne k_0}$$

**No leakage — all energy in one bin.**

**Case B — Non-coherent frequency:** $\omega_0 = 2\pi k_0 / N + \delta$, where $0 < \delta < \pi/N$.

Now $2\pi k/N - \omega_0 = 2\pi(k - k_0)/N - \delta$. The peak of $W_R$ is *between* DFT bins, and **multiple bins receive nonzero contributions**:

$$X[k] = \sum_{m} W_R\!\left(e^{j(2\pi(k-k_0)/N - \delta - 2\pi m)}\right)$$

For large $N$, the dominant term (the un-aliased one) gives:

$$\boxed{X[k] \approx \frac{\sin\!\left(\frac{N}{2}\!\left[\frac{2\pi(k - k_0)}{N} - \delta\right]\right)}{\sin\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\!\left[\frac{2\pi(k - k_0)}{N} - \delta\right]\right)} \, e^{-j(\cdot)}}$$

This is the **leaked spectrum** — energy spread across many $k$ values with side-lobe ripples at $-13$ dB and beyond.

## 3.3 Derivation: Scalloping Loss (Worst-Case Amplitude Error)

**Setup:** Compute the DFT magnitude of a sinusoid at the *worst-case* offset $\delta = \pi / N$ (frequency halfway between two bins).

### Step 1 — Evaluate the Dirichlet kernel at the closest bin center

At $k = k_0$, the argument is $\omega - \omega_0 = 2\pi k_0/N - \omega_0$. With $\omega_0 = 2\pi k_0/N + \pi/N$, this gives $\omega - \omega_0 = -\pi/N$. So we evaluate:

$$\left\vert W_R(e^{j\omega}) \right\vert_{\omega = -\pi/N} = \left\vert \frac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega / 2)} \right\vert_{\omega = -\pi/N}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the argument

The numerator becomes $\sin(-\pi N / (2N)) = \sin(-\pi/2) = -1$, and the denominator becomes $\sin(-\pi/(2N)) = -\sin(\pi/(2N))$. Hence:

$$\left\vert W_R(e^{-j\pi/N}) \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(-\pi/2)}{\sin(-\pi/(2N))} \right\vert = \frac{1}{\sin(\pi/(2N))}$$

### Step 3 — Use the small-angle approximation $\sin(x) \approx x$ for large $N$

For large $N$, $\pi/(2N) \ll 1$, so:

$$\frac{1}{\sin(\pi/(2N))} \approx \frac{1}{\pi/(2N)} = \frac{2N}{\pi}$$

But the *true* peak at $\omega = \omega_0$ (coherent case) is $N$. So the **ratio** of observed to true amplitude is:

$$\frac{2N/\pi}{N} = \frac{2}{\pi} \approx 0.6371$$

### Step 4 — Convert to decibels

$$A_{SC} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2}{\pi}\right) \approx 20 \times (-0.1961) \approx -3.92 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{A_{SC} \approx -3.92 \text{ dB}}$$

This is the **scalloping loss** for the rectangular window.

## 3.4 Derivation: Equivalent Noise Bandwidth (ENBW) for Hann Window

**Setup:** The ENBW is defined as the equivalent rectangular bandwidth (in DFT bins) that would produce the same total noise power at the output:

$$\text{ENBW} = N \cdot \frac{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} w^2[n]}{\left( \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} w[n] \right)^2}$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Hann window's sums

Hann window: $w_H[n] = 0.5 - 0.5 \cos(2\pi n / (N-1))$, $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$.

**Sum of window values:** $\sum w_H[n] = 0.5 N - 0.5 \sum \cos(\dots)$.

For large $N$, the cosine sum oscillates and averages to zero, giving $\sum w_H[n] \approx N/2$.

### Step 2 — Compute the sum of squared window values

$$w_H^2[n] = 0.25 \left[ 1 - 2\cos\!\frac{2\pi n}{N-1} + \cos^2\!\frac{2\pi n}{N-1} \right]$$

Using $\cos^2(\theta) = (1 + \cos(2\theta))/2$:

$$w_H^2[n] = 0.25 \left[ 1 - 2\cos\!\frac{2\pi n}{N-1} + 0.5 + 0.5\cos\!\frac{4\pi n}{N-1} \right]$$

$$w_H^2[n] = 0.375 - 0.5\cos\!\frac{2\pi n}{N-1} + 0.125\cos\!\frac{4\pi n}{N-1}$$

Summing over $n$ for large $N$, the cosine sums vanish, leaving:

$$\sum w_H^2[n] \approx 0.375 \cdot N = \frac{3N}{8}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the ENBW

$$\text{ENBW}_H = N \cdot \frac{3N/8}{(N/2)^2} = N \cdot \frac{3N/8}{N^2/4} = N \cdot \frac{3}{8} \cdot \frac{4}{N} \cdot N \cdot \frac{1}{N}$$

Reorganizing carefully:

$$\text{ENBW}_H = N \cdot \frac{3N/8}{N^2/4} = \frac{3N^2/8}{N^2/4} = \frac{3}{8} \times 4 = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5$$

$$\boxed{\text{ENBW}_{\text{Hann}} = 1.5 \text{ bins}}$$

This means the Hann window has a noise floor $10\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 1.76$ dB higher than the rectangular window, in exchange for its $-31.5$ dB side-lobe level.

## 3.5 Master Summary Table of Window Properties

| Window | Main Lobe (bins) | Side-Lobe (dB) | ENBW (bins) | Scalloping (dB) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | **2** | $-13.26$ | $1.00$ | $-3.92$ |
| Hann | **4** | $-31.50$ | $1.50$ | $-1.42$ |
| Hamming | **4** | $-42.70$ | $1.36$ | $-1.78$ |
| Blackman | **6** | $-58.10$ | $1.73$ | $-1.10$ |
| Blackman–Harris | **8** | $-92.00$ | $2.00$ | $-0.83$ |
| Kaiser ($\beta = 8.6$) | $4.5$ | $-65.0$ | $1.50$ | $-1.45$ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Windowing Operation and Leakage Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Infinite Length Signal x_n] --> B[Multiplication by Window w_n]
    W[Rectangular or Tapered Window w_n length N] --> B
    B --> C[Finite Length Signal xN_n]
    C --> D[N Point DFT Computation]
    D --> E[Sampled Spectrum X k at omega k]
    E --> F{Is omega_0 Coherent with 2pi k by N?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Single Bin Peak No Leakage]
    F -- No --> H[Energy Spread Across Multiple Bins]
    H --> I[Side Lobes Cause Far Bin Contamination]
    I --> J[Spectral Leakage Observed]
    G --> K[Clean Spectrum]
```

## 4.2 Time–Frequency Mapping of the Windowing Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1["Time Domain: x of n = exp j omega0 n"] --> T2["Time Domain: Multiply by w of n length N"]
    T2 --> T3["Time Domain: Truncated xN of n"]
    T3 --> F1["Frequency Domain: X of e sup j omega"]
    T3 --> F2["Frequency Domain: W of e sup j omega equals Dirichlet Kernel"]
    F1 --> F3["Convolution X convolve W"]
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> F4["Sampled at omega k equals 2pi k by N"]
    F4 --> F5{"Coherent?"}
    F5 -- Yes --> F6["Single bin spike magnitude N"]
    F5 -- No --> F7["Leaked spectrum with main lobe and side lobes"]
```

## 4.3 Leakage Mechanism — Component Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1 DFT Implies Windowing] --> S2[Step 2 Windowing equals Convolution in Frequency]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 True Spectrum Convolved with Dirichlet Kernel]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Sinusoid Delta Becomes Dirichlet Peak with Sidelobes]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 DFT Samples this Spread Spectrum]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 If Coherent Only One Bin Hit by Peak]
    S5 --> S7[Step 7 If Non Coherent Energy Spread Across Many Bins]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Side Lobes Add Residual Energy to Far Bins]
    S8 --> R[Result: Spectral Leakage Visible in DFT Plot]
```

    Q3 -- No --> Q5{General Purpose Audio Spectral Analysis?}

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Q: Define spectral leakage. Why does it occur when computing the DFT of a finite-length signal?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Spectral leakage is the phenomenon in which the energy of a sinusoidal (or narrowband) component in a signal spreads into adjacent frequency bins of its DFT, instead of being concentrated in a single bin.

**Cause (2 marks):** Computing an $N$-point DFT is equivalent to multiplying the signal $x[n]$ by a rectangular window $w_R[n]$ of length $N$ before taking the DTFT. In the frequency domain, this multiplication corresponds to convolution with the Dirichlet kernel $W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \dfrac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega / 2)} e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$. If the signal's frequency $\omega_0$ does not coincide with a DFT bin frequency $\omega_k = 2\pi k / N$, the Dirichlet kernel's main lobe and side lobes deposit energy into multiple bins, causing leakage.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any three commonly used window functions and compare their side-lobe levels.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Window | First Side-Lobe Level |
|---|---|
| Rectangular | **$-13.26$ dB** |
| Hann | **$-31.5$ dB** |
| Hamming | **$-42.7$ dB** |
| Blackman | **$-58.1$ dB** |

The rectangular window has the **highest** side-lobe level (most leakage), while the Blackman window has the **lowest** among these. However, lower side-lobe level is achieved at the cost of a **wider main lobe** (poorer frequency resolution) — this is the fundamental trade-off in window design.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **answer any ONE full question from the choice**. Each full question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**.

---

### Question Choice A — `(a) + (b) = 14 Marks`

#### Part (a) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the frequency response of a rectangular window of length $N$. Show that its first side-lobe level is approximately $-13$ dB.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Rectangular window definition: $w_R[n] = 1$ for $0 \le n \le N-1$, $0$ elsewhere.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute DTFT:

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{-j\omega n} = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega N}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Convert to closed-form Dirichlet kernel using $1 - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin(\theta/2)e^{-j\theta/2}$:

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega / 2)} e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Identify first side-lobe peak — occurs near $\omega \approx 3\pi / N$ (numerical search). At this point:

$$\left\vert W_R \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(3\pi/2)}{\sin(3\pi/(2N))} \right\vert = \frac{1}{\sin(3\pi/(2N))} \approx \frac{2N}{3\pi}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Side-lobe level relative to main-lobe peak $N$:

$$20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2N/(3\pi)}{N}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2}{3\pi}\right) \approx -13.26 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{A_{SL} \approx -13 \text{ dB}}$$

---

#### Part (b) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]`

**(b)** A pure sinusoid $x[n] = \cos(2\pi \cdot 125 \cdot n / 1000)$ is sampled at $f_s = 1000$ Hz and analyzed using a $N = 64$-point DFT. Determine:
  (i) Whether the frequency is coherent with the DFT grid.  
  (ii) The number of DFT bins receiving significant energy if not coherent.  
  (iii) Scalloping loss in dB.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the normalized digital frequency.

$$\omega_0 = 2\pi \cdot \frac{125}{1000} = \frac{\pi}{4} \text{ rad/sample}$$

DFT bin spacing is $\Delta\omega = 2\pi / 64 = \pi / 32$ rad/sample.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Check coherence. We need $\omega_0 = 2\pi k_0 / 64$, i.e., $k_0 = 64 \cdot \omega_0 / (2\pi) = 64 \cdot (1/8) = 8$. Since $k_0 = 8$ is an integer:

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi \cdot 8}{64} \Rightarrow \text{COHÉRENT — NO LEAKAGE}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Because it is coherent, all energy is concentrated in **bin $k = 8$** (and $k = 64 - 8 = 56$ for the negative-frequency component, since the signal is real).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Number of bins with significant energy = **2** (one for the positive-frequency component, one for its conjugate, due to real-signal symmetry).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Scalloping loss — since the signal is **coherent**, scalloping loss = **0 dB** (the DFT magnitude equals the true amplitude at the bin).

$$\boxed{A_{SC} = 0 \text{ dB}, \quad \text{Bins with energy: } k = 8 \text{ and } k = 56}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Part b):** Many students incorrectly assume the worst-case scalloping loss $(-3.92$ dB$)$ applies here. Read the problem carefully — when $\omega_0$ is an integer multiple of $2\pi / N$, the loss is **exactly 0 dB**. You lose **2 marks** for this mistake.

---

### Question Choice B — `(a) + (b) = 14 Marks`

#### Part (a) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Compare the Rectangular, Hann, and Hamming windows in terms of:
  (i) Main-lobe width
  (ii) First side-lobe level
  (iii) Scalloping loss

Discuss the trade-offs involved in selecting a window for spectral analysis.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | Rectangular | Hann | Hamming |
|---|---|---|---|
| Main-lobe width (bins) | 2 | 4 | 4 |
| First side-lobe level | $-13.26$ dB | $-31.5$ dB | $-42.7$ dB |
| Scalloping loss | $-3.92$ dB | $-1.42$ dB | $-1.78$ dB |
| ENBW | 1.00 | 1.50 | 1.36 |

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Trade-off Discussion:**

- **Rectangular:** Best main-lobe resolution (narrowest), but worst side-lobe level and worst scalloping loss. Use only when the signal is known to be coherent (e.g., synthesized test tones).
- **Hann:** Good general-purpose choice. Moderate main-lobe width (2× rectangular), side lobes $\approx -31.5$ dB. Excellent for detecting weak signals near strong ones when frequency resolution is not critical.
- **Hamming:** Slightly lower side-lobe level than Hann ($\approx -42.7$ dB) at the cost of marginally poorer scalloping loss. Good for speech and audio analysis.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Fundamental Trade-off:**

The trade-off is between **main-lobe width** (frequency resolution) and **side-lobe level** (leakage suppression). Wider main lobes mean two close frequencies may merge into one peak; lower side lobes mean energy from a strong tone will not drown out weak tones at adjacent frequencies. There is **no single optimal window** — the choice depends on the application: coherent signal → Rectangular; general spectral analysis → Hann; speech/audio → Hamming; high dynamic range → Blackman.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Conclusion:**

For the PEAET866 module context (DFT-modulated filter banks), the **Hann window** is preferred because its cosine shape corresponds to the **raised-cosine prototype filter** that achieves near-perfect reconstruction in cosine-modulated filter banks.

---

#### Part (b) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**(b)** A signal $x[n] = \cos(2\pi \cdot 137.5 \cdot n / 1000)$ is sampled at $f_s = 1000$ Hz and a $N = 64$-point DFT is computed.  
  (i) Is the frequency coherent with the DFT grid?  
  (ii) Estimate the DFT magnitudes at the bins closest to the true frequency.  
  (iii) Estimate the scalloping loss.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Coherence Check:**

$$\omega_0 = 2\pi \cdot \frac{137.5}{1000} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 137.5}{1000} = \frac{11\pi}{40} \text{ rad/sample}$$

$$k_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi / 64} = \frac{64 \cdot 137.5}{1000} = 8.8$$

Since $k_0 = 8.8$ is **not an integer**, the frequency is **non-coherent**.

$$\boxed{\text{NON-COHÉRENT — spectral leakage will occur}}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — DFT Magnitude Estimation:**

The true frequency lies **between bins $k = 8$ and $k = 9$**, at fractional offset $\delta = 0.8 \cdot (2\pi/64) = 0.025 \pi$ rad/sample from bin 9.

Using the Dirichlet kernel magnitude:

$$\vert X[k_0 + r] \vert \approx \left\vert \frac{\sin(\pi(r - 0.8))}{\sin(\pi(r - 0.8)/N)} \right\vert \approx \frac{N \sin(\pi(r - 0.8))}{\pi(r - 0.8)}$$

For large $N = 64$, evaluate at $r = 0$ (bin 9) and $r = -1$ (bin 8):

At bin 9: $\vert X[9] \vert \approx \dfrac{64 \sin(-0.8\pi)}{-\pi \cdot 0.8} = \dfrac{64 \cdot 0.5878}{2.513} \approx 14.96$

At bin 8: $\vert X[8] \vert \approx \dfrac{64 \sin(-1.8\pi)}{-\pi \cdot 1.8} = \dfrac{64 \cdot 0.3090}{5.654} \approx 3.50$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Higher Bin Magnitudes (Side Lobes):**

At bin 7 (r = -2): $\vert X[7] \vert \approx \dfrac{64 \sin(-2.8\pi)}{-\pi \cdot 2.8} \approx \dfrac{64 \cdot 0.809}{8.796} \approx 5.89$ — significant! (First side lobe)

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Scalloping Loss:**

For Hann window (if used instead of rectangular), scalloping loss at fractional offset $0.8$ of bin width:

$$A_{SC} = 20\log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \cos(2\pi \cdot 0.8)\right)\right]^{-1/2} \cdot \text{normalization} \approx -1.42 \text{ dB (worst case)}$$

For rectangular window, the worst case is $-3.92$ dB. With offset $0.8$ of half-bin, the actual loss is:

$$A_{SC} \approx 20\log_{10}\!\left[\frac{2}{\pi}\sin(0.8\pi)\right]^{-1} \approx 20\log_{10}(0.795) \approx -2.0 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{A_{SC} \approx -2.0 \text{ dB (rectangular, at this offset)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Part b):** Students often forget that **scalloping loss is not a fixed value** — it depends on where the true frequency falls within a bin. Worst-case is at half-bin offset; losses are smaller near the bin center. You lose **1 mark** for stating $-3.92$ dB without justification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Spectral Leakage by Windowing Effect**

- ✅ **Root Cause:** DFT of $x[n]$ implies multiplication by rectangular window → convolution in frequency with the Dirichlet kernel.
- ✅ **Dirichlet Kernel:** $W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \dfrac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega / 2)} e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$ — main lobe width $= 4\pi / N$.
- ✅ **Coherent Sampling Condition:** $\omega_0 = 2\pi k_0 / N$ — eliminates leakage for pure sinusoids.
- ✅ **Non-Coherent Sampling:** Energy spreads to multiple bins according to the Dirichlet kernel's shape.
- ✅ **Rectangular Window:** Main lobe $= 2$ bins, side lobe $= -13.26$ dB, scalloping loss $= -3.92$ dB (worst), ENBW $= 1.00$ bin.
- ✅ **Hann Window:** Main lobe $= 4$ bins, side lobe $= -31.5$ dB, scalloping loss $= -1.42$ dB, ENBW $= 1.50$ bins.
- ✅ **Hamming Window:** Main lobe $= 4$ bins, side lobe $= -42.7$ dB, scalloping loss $= -1.78$ dB, ENBW $= 1.36$ bins.
- ✅ **Blackman Window:** Main lobe $= 6$ bins, side lobe $= -58.1$ dB, scalloping loss $= -1.10$ dB, ENBW $= 1.73$ bins.
- ✅ **Trade-off:** Lower side-lobe level ↔ Wider main lobe. No free lunch.
- ✅ **Application in Multirate Systems:** Hann-shaped prototype filters dominate **cosine-modulated filter banks** because their cosine shape yields **near-perfect reconstruction** with controlled leakage between sub-bands.
- ✅ **Mathematical Tool:** Convolution theorem $x[n]w[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} \dfrac{1}{2\pi}X(e^{j\omega}) \circledast W(e^{j\omega})$ is the master equation.
- ✅ **Equivalent Noise Bandwidth (ENBW):** Measure of how much extra white noise a window admits relative to the rectangular reference. Lower ENBW = cleaner spectrum for stochastic signals.
- ✅ **Common Exam Trap:** Confusing *main-lobe width* (resolution) with *side-lobe level* (leakage) — they move in opposite directions when changing windows.
- ✅ **DFT Visualization Tip:** A spectrum with only $-13$ dB side lobes looks "noisy" even when the underlying signal is a single pure tone. Switch to a Hann or Blackman window to see the cleaner structure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time and Frequency Localization of Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Time and Frequency Localization of Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Time-Frequency Localization** is the simultaneous representation of a signal's temporal and spectral energy concentration. For a finite-energy signal $x(t) \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$ with Fourier transform $X(\omega)$, the *effective duration* ($\Delta_t$) and *effective bandwidth* ($\Delta_\omega$) are defined as the second central moments of $\vert x(t)\vert^2$ and $\vert X(\omega)\vert^2$ respectively, normalized by the signal's total energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A signal is said to be **localized in time** if its effective duration $\Delta_t$ is small, and **localized in frequency** if its effective bandwidth $\Delta_\omega$ is small. A signal cannot be simultaneously localized in both time and frequency beyond a fundamental lower bound — this is the essence of the **Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a musician playing a single piano note inside a concert hall. You have two ways of "pinning down" this sound:
