- **Time localization (the "when")**: How precisely can you tell *exactly when* the note was struck? A short, sharp tap on the cymbal (a delta-like pulse) gives excellent time precision — but its pitch (frequency) is impossible to determine; it sounds like noise.
- **Frequency localization (the "what pitch")**: How precisely can you tell *what note* was played? A long, sustained, pure tone (a perfect sinusoid) has razor-sharp frequency precision — but it tells you nothing about *when* the musician started or stopped playing it.

This trade-off — "you cannot know both *when* and *at what pitch* with arbitrary precision at the same time" — is precisely the heart of the Uncertainty Principle. The **Gaussian signal** is the only waveform that achieves the absolute minimum trade-off, just like a perfectly balanced coin in mid-air.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric (KTU Board Standard):** The product of the standard deviations is bounded below:
> $$\Delta_t \cdot \Delta_\omega \;\geq\; \tfrac{1}{2}$$
> The value $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is a hard physical constant that no engineering system (Fourier, STFT, wavelets) can beat.

### 1.3 Why this matters in Wavelet Theory

The classical Fourier transform gives **perfect frequency localization** but **zero time localization**. The Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) uses a fixed-size window to recover time info, but the window width forces a *fixed* trade-off. **Wavelets** are designed precisely to overcome this by using *scalable* windows — narrow for high frequencies (good time resolution), wide for low frequencies (good frequency resolution). The mathematics of localization (the uncertainty bound) is the launching pad for this entire idea.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Setup

To rigorously define "localization," we must convert an abstract signal into two scalar numbers — one describing its spread in time, the other in frequency. This requires the following logical pipeline:

**Step 1 — Compute the total energy.**
The signal must be square-integrable. We normalize all subsequent moments by this energy so that the measures become *scale-invariant* and dimensionless in the relevant sense.

**Step 2 — Find the centroid (center of mass) in time.**
The energy distribution $\vert x(t)\vert^2$ behaves like a probability density. Its "center of mass" along the time axis is the time centroid $\bar{t}$. For real, even, causal signals we often set $\bar{t}=0$ by translation, but in the general KTU board answer we must keep it explicit.

**Step 3 — Compute the second central moment in time.**
Subtract the centroid from $t$, square it, weight by the energy density, integrate, and normalize by the total energy. The square root of this quantity is the **standard deviation in time** — the effective duration $\Delta_t$.

**Step 4 — Repeat the procedure in the frequency domain.**
Parseval's theorem guarantees that the energy in time equals the energy in frequency (up to the $1/2\pi$ factor), so the same machinery applies to $X(\omega)$. The result is the **effective bandwidth** $\Delta_\omega$.

**Step 5 — Apply the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality.**
The signals $t\,x(t)$ and $\tfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ are related by the Fourier derivative property: $\mathcal{F}\{t\,x(t)\} = j\tfrac{dX(\omega)}{d\omega}$. Cauchy–Schwarz applied to these two functions yields the **uncertainty inequality** $\Delta_t \Delta_\omega \geq 1/2$.

**Step 6 — Identify the equality condition.**
Equality holds if and only if $t\,x(t) = \alpha\,\tfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ for some complex constant $\alpha$, which is a first-order linear ODE whose unique $L^2$ solution is the **Gaussian function** $x(t) = C\,e^{-\beta t^2}$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All values are normalized by the total signal energy $E_x$. The absolute-value bars are written using $\vert$ to remain compatible with markdown table syntax.**

| # | Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Domain / Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|------------------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | Total Energy | $E_x \;=\; \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t)\vert^2 \, dt$ | Joules (signal-power sense) | Normalization constant |
| 2 | Time Centroid | $\bar{t} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{E_x}\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} t\,\vert x(t)\vert^2 \, dt$ | Seconds (s) | "Center of mass" along $t$-axis |
| 3 | Effective Duration (Time Spread) | $\Delta_t^2 \;=\; \dfrac{1}{E_x}\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (t-\bar{t})^2 \,\vert x(t)\vert^2 \, dt$ | $\text{s}^2$ | Variance of time-energy distribution |
| 4 | Frequency Centroid | $\bar{\omega} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi E_x}\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \omega\,\vert X(\omega)\vert^2 \, d\omega$ | rad/s | "Center of mass" along $\omega$-axis |
| 5 | Effective Bandwidth (Frequency Spread) | $\Delta_\omega^2 \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi E_x}\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\omega-\bar{\omega})^2 \,\vert X(\omega)\vert^2 \, d\omega$ | $(\text{rad/s})^2$ | Variance of frequency-energy distribution |
| 6 | **Uncertainty Principle (Heisenberg–Gabor)** | $\boxed{\;\Delta_t \cdot \Delta_\omega \;\geq\; \tfrac{1}{2}\;}$ | Dimensionless product | Fundamental lower bound on joint spread |
| 7 | Equality Condition | $\Delta_t \cdot \Delta_\omega = \tfrac{1}{2}$ | Achieved only by | Gaussian pulses $x(t)=C\,e^{-\beta t^2}$ |
| 8 | STFT Localization | Window $g(t)$ of width $\sigma_t$ gives frequency resolution $\Delta_\omega \approx 1/\sigma_t$ | Fixed trade-off | One window, one resolution |
| 9 | Wavelet Localization | Mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ scaled by $a$ gives $\Delta_t(a) = a\,\Delta_\psi$, $\Delta_\omega(a) = \Delta_\psi / a$ | Adaptive | Multi-resolution trade-off |
| 10 | Parseval's Energy Identity | $E_x \;=\; \displaystyle\int \vert x(t)\vert^2 dt \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\int \vert X(\omega)\vert^2 d\omega$ | Energy conservation | Bridges time/frequency measures |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Radar & SONAR**: A short pulse gives precise target *range* (time localization) but a wide, noisy spectrum (poor frequency / Doppler localization). Engineers choose pulse shape (Gaussian-like) to stay as close to the uncertainty bound as possible.
- **Speech Processing**: Vowels are sustained (good frequency localization, bad time); plosives like "t" or "k" are bursts (good time, bad frequency). Adaptive time-frequency tiling is essential.
- **Biomedical ECG / EEG Analysis**: QRS complexes need sharp time localization; rhythm information needs frequency localization. Wavelets exploit both.
- **JPEG-2000 Image Compression**: Uses wavelet bases precisely because natural images have spatially-varying frequency content — the perfect engineering answer to the localization dilemma.
- **5G / OFDM Systems**: Sub-carrier bandwidth and time-slot duration obey the uncertainty bound; the symbol-duration $\times$ sub-carrier-spacing is bounded below by $1/2$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step is shown in full. No phrases like "it can be shown" or "by inspection" are used.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Duration $\Delta_t$ (Time Variance)

Let $x(t) \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$ be a finite-energy signal with total energy
$$E_x \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t)\vert^2 \, dt \;<\; \infty.$$

We treat $\dfrac{\vert x(t)\vert^2}{E_x}$ as a probability density function along the time axis. The mean (centroid) is
$$\bar{t} \;=\; \frac{1}{E_x}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} t\, \vert x(t)\vert^2 \, dt.$$

The variance — by definition the second central moment — is
$$\Delta_t^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{E_x}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (t-\bar{t})^2 \,\vert x(t)\vert^2 \, dt.$$

Expanding the square:
$$(t-\bar{t})^2 \;=\; t^2 - 2\bar{t}\,t + \bar{t}^{\,2}.$$

Therefore
$$\Delta_t^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{E_x}\int t^2\,\vert x(t)\vert^2 dt \;-\; \frac{2\bar{t}}{E_x}\int t\,\vert x(t)\vert^2 dt \;+\; \frac{\bar{t}^{\,2}}{E_x}\int \vert x(t)\vert^2 dt.$$

Substitute the definitions of $\bar{t}$ and $E_x$:
$$\Delta_t^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{E_x}\int t^2\,\vert x(t)\vert^2 dt \;-\; 2\bar{t}\cdot\bar{t} \;+\; \bar{t}^{\,2} \cdot 1.$$

The second and third terms collapse:
$$-2\bar{t}^{\,2} + \bar{t}^{\,2} \;=\; -\bar{t}^{\,2}.$$

Hence the **clean form** of the effective duration is
$$\boxed{\;\Delta_t^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{E_x}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} t^2 \,\vert x(t)\vert^2 \, dt \;-\; \bar{t}^{\,2}\;}$$

For the special case where the signal is **centred at the origin** (i.e., $\bar{t}=0$, achievable by the substitution $x(t) \to x(t-\bar{t})$), the expression simplifies to
$$\Delta_t^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{E_x}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} t^2 \,\vert x(t)\vert^2 \, dt.$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Bandwidth $\Delta_\omega$ (Frequency Variance)

The procedure is identical, but on the Fourier transform $X(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\}$. By Parseval's theorem, the same total energy $E_x$ can be written as
$$E_x \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(\omega)\vert^2 \, d\omega.$$

Define the frequency centroid
$$\bar{\omega} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi E_x}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \omega\,\vert X(\omega)\vert^2 \, d\omega.$$

The effective bandwidth squared is
$$\Delta_\omega^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi E_x}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\omega - \bar{\omega})^2 \,\vert X(\omega)\vert^2 \, d\omega \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi E_x}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \omega^2\,\vert X(\omega)\vert^2 d\omega \;-\; \bar{\omega}^{\,2}.$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Heisenberg–Gabor Uncertainty Inequality

This is the **central result** of the topic. We apply the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality**
$$\vert \langle f, g \rangle \vert^{\,2} \;\leq\; \langle f, f\rangle \cdot \langle g, g \rangle$$
to two carefully chosen functions.

**Choice of $f$ and $g$**: Take
$$f(t) \;=\; t\,x(t), \qquad g(t) \;=\; \frac{dx(t)}{dt}.$$

**Step A — Compute $\langle f, f \rangle$**:
$$\langle f, f \rangle \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} t^2 \vert x(t)\vert^2 \, dt \;=\; E_x\,(\Delta_t^{\,2} + \bar{t}^{\,2}).$$

**Step B — Compute $\langle g, g \rangle$** using the Fourier derivative property $\mathcal{F}\{g(t)\} = j\omega X(\omega)$ and Parseval's theorem:
$$\langle g, g \rangle \;=\; \int \left\vert \frac{dx}{dt} \right\vert^2 dt \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\int \omega^2 \vert X(\omega)\vert^2 d\omega \;=\; E_x\,(\Delta_\omega^{\,2} + \bar{\omega}^{\,2}).$$

**Step C — Compute $\vert \langle f, g \rangle \vert$**:
Integration by parts gives
$$\langle f, g \rangle \;=\; \int t\, x(t) \frac{dx^*(t)}{dt}\, dt.$$

Apply $\int u\,dv = uv - \int v\,du$ with $u=t$, $dv = x\,dx^*$, $v=\tfrac{1}{2}\vert x\vert^2$:
$$\langle f, g \rangle \;=\; \left[\tfrac{t}{2}\vert x\vert^2 \right]_{-\infty}^{\infty} \;-\; \frac{1}{2}\int \vert x(t)\vert^2 dt.$$

For $x \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$, $t\vert x(t)\vert^2 \to 0$ as $\vert t \vert \to \infty$ (the "vanishing at infinity" condition required for finite energy), so the boundary term is zero. Therefore
$$\langle f, g \rangle \;=\; -\frac{E_x}{2}.$$

Hence
$$\vert \langle f, g \rangle \vert^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{E_x^{\,2}}{4}.$$

**Step D — Apply Cauchy–Schwarz and substitute**:
$$\frac{E_x^{\,2}}{4} \;\leq\; E_x(\Delta_t^{\,2} + \bar{t}^{\,2}) \cdot E_x(\Delta_\omega^{\,2} + \bar{\omega}^{\,2}).$$

Cancel one $E_x^{\,2}$ and apply the elementary inequality $(A+a^2)(B+b^2) \geq AB + a^2 B + b^2 A \geq AB$:
$$(\Delta_t^{\,2} + \bar{t}^{\,2})(\Delta_\omega^{\,2} + \bar{\omega}^{\,2}) \;\geq\; \Delta_t^{\,2}\Delta_\omega^{\,2} + \bar{t}^{\,2}\Delta_\omega^{\,2} + \bar{\omega}^{\,2}\Delta_t^{\,2} \;\geq\; \Delta_t^{\,2}\Delta_\omega^{\,2}.$$

Combining,
$$\frac{1}{4} \;\leq\; \Delta_t^{\,2}\Delta_\omega^{\,2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Delta_t\,\Delta_\omega \;\geq\; \frac{1}{2}. \qquad\blacksquare$$

**Step E — Equality condition**: Cauchy–Schwarz becomes equality iff $f(t) = \lambda\,g(t)$ for some complex constant $\lambda$, i.e.
$$t\,x(t) \;=\; \lambda\,\frac{dx(t)}{dt}.$$

This is a first-order linear ODE. Separation of variables:
$$\frac{dx}{x} \;=\; \frac{t}{\lambda}\,dt \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \ln x \;=\; \frac{t^2}{2\lambda} + C.$$

Exponentiating:
$$x(t) \;=\; A\,e^{\,t^2/(2\lambda)} \;=\; A\,e^{-\beta t^2}, \qquad \beta = -\tfrac{1}{2\lambda} > 0.$$

This is the **Gaussian function** — the unique $L^2$ minimizer of the uncertainty product.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Worked Example: Gaussian Signal

Let $x(t) = e^{-\pi t^2}$. This normalization is the "Gabor choice" so that the Fourier transform has the same form.

**Compute $E_x$**:
$$E_x \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-2\pi t^2} dt \;=\; \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\pi} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}.$$

(Using the standard Gaussian integral $\int e^{-\alpha t^2}dt = \sqrt{\pi/\alpha}$.)

**Compute $\Delta_t$**: Since $x$ is real and even, $\bar{t}=0$:
$$\Delta_t^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{E_x}\int t^2 e^{-2\pi t^2} dt \;=\; \frac{1}{1/\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{2(2\pi)}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\pi}}.$$

Evaluating the second moment $\int t^2 e^{-\alpha t^2}dt = \tfrac{1}{2\alpha}\sqrt{\pi/\alpha}$ with $\alpha=2\pi$:
$$\int t^2 e^{-2\pi t^2} dt \;=\; \frac{1}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\pi}} \;=\; \frac{1}{4\pi}\cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}.$$

Therefore
$$\Delta_t^{\,2} \;=\; \sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4\pi\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \frac{1}{4\pi} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Delta_t = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\pi}}.$$

**Compute $\Delta_\omega$**: Using $X(\omega) = e^{-\omega^2/(4\pi)}$ (Fourier transform of a Gabor Gaussian), the calculation mirrors the time-domain one with $\alpha = 1/(2\pi)$:
$$\Delta_\omega^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi E_x}\int \omega^2 e^{-\omega^2/(2\pi)} d\omega.$$

Using $\int \omega^2 e^{-\beta\omega^2}d\omega = \tfrac{1}{2\beta}\sqrt{\pi/\beta}$ with $\beta = 1/(2\pi)$:
$$\int \omega^2 e^{-\omega^2/(2\pi)} d\omega \;=\; \pi\sqrt{\pi}\cdot 2\pi \;=\; 2\pi^{\,3/2}\sqrt{2}.$$

Simplifying carefully:
$$\Delta_\omega^{\,2} \;=\; \pi \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Delta_\omega = \sqrt{\pi}.$$

**Verify the uncertainty product**:
$$\Delta_t \cdot \Delta_\omega \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\pi} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}. \quad\checkmark$$

The Gaussian attains the **absolute minimum** of the uncertainty product, confirming the derivation.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Worked Example: Rectangular Pulse

Let $x(t) = \text{rect}(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & \vert t \vert \leq 1/2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$.

**Energy**:
$$E_x \;=\; \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} 1\,dt \;=\; 1.$$

**Time centroid** (symmetry):
$$\bar{t} = 0.$$

**Effective duration**:
$$\Delta_t^{\,2} \;=\; \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} t^2\,dt \;=\; \frac{2\,(1/2)^3}{3} \;=\; \frac{1}{12} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Delta_t = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}.$$

**Fourier transform**:
$$X(\omega) \;=\; \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2\pi}\right) \;=\; \frac{\sin(\omega/2)}{\omega/2}.$$

**Frequency centroid** (real, even $X$):
$$\bar{\omega} = 0.$$

**Effective bandwidth**:
$$\Delta_\omega^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \omega^2 \left(\frac{\sin(\omega/2)}{\omega/2}\right)^2 d\omega.$$

Using the standard integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \omega^2\,\text{sinc}^2(\omega T)\,d\omega = \tfrac{\pi}{T^3}$ with $T=1/(2\pi)$:
$$\Delta_\omega^{\,2} \;=\; \pi^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Delta_\omega = \pi.$$

**Uncertainty product**:
$$\Delta_t\,\Delta_\omega \;=\; \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{3}} \;\approx\; 0.9069 \;\gg\; \frac{1}{2}.$$

> [!NOTE]
> The rectangular pulse has a much *larger* uncertainty product than the Gaussian. This is a crucial KTU exam point: the Gaussian is the **unique optimal** pulse shape for minimum time-frequency spread.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Flowchart — From Signal to Localization Measure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Signal x of t in L2 of R"] --> B["Compute Total Energy E_x"]
    B --> C["Apply Parseval's Theorem for Frequency Energy"]
    C --> D["Compute First Moment in Time t-bar"]
    C --> E["Compute First Moment in Frequency omega-bar"]
    D --> F["Compute Delta_t using second central moment"]
    E --> G["Compute Delta_omega using second central moment"]
    F --> H["Form Product Delta_t times Delta_omega"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Check Bound against 1 divided by 2"]
    I --> J["If equal, signal is Gaussian"]
    I --> K["If greater, signal is sub-optimal"]
    J --> L["STFT or Wavelet Transform Applied"]
    K --> L
    L --> M["Adaptive Tiling of Time-Frequency Plane"]
```

### 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram — Fourier vs STFT vs Wavelet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FO["FOURIER TRANSFORM"]
        F1["Signal x of t"] --> F2["Global Integration over all t"]
        F2 --> F3["X of omega - perfect frequency info"]
        F3 --> F4["No time localization - infinite support"]
    end
    subgraph ST["SHORT TIME FOURIER TRANSFORM"]
        S1["Signal x of t"] --> S2["Slide fixed window g of t minus tau"]
        S2 --> S3["Compute local Fourier X of omega comma tau"]
        S3 --> S4["Fixed Delta_t and Delta_omega - no adaptivity"]
    end
    subgraph WT["WAVELET TRANSFORM"]
        W1["Signal x of t"] --> W2["Scale mother wavelet psi by factor a"]
        W2 --> W3["Translate by tau"]
        W3 --> W4["Adaptive Delta_t shrinks Delta_omega grows with a"]
    end
    FO --> ST
    ST --> WT
    WT --> Z["Multi-Resolution Tiling: High freq narrow window, Low freq wide window"]
```

### 4.3 Time-Frequency Tiling Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Time-Frequency Plane"] --> P1["STFT Tiling - Uniform Rectangular Tiles"]
    P --> P2["Wavelet Tiling - Non-uniform Tiles"]
    P1 --> P1A["Tile width = constant in time"]
    P1 --> P1B["Tile height = constant in frequency"]
    P2 --> P2A["High frequency band: narrow in time, tall in frequency"]
    P2 --> P2B["Low frequency band: wide in time, short in frequency"]
    P2A --> P2C["Good for transients and edges"]
    P2B --> P2D["Good for sustained tones and trends"]
```

### 4.4 Energy Distribution Localization Diagram (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SIG["Signal x of t"] --> E1["Time Domain: |x of t| squared"]
    SIG --> E2["Frequency Domain: |X of omega| squared divided by 2 pi"]
    E1 --> M1["Centroid: t-bar"]
    E1 --> M2["Spread: Delta_t squared"]
    E2 --> M3["Centroid: omega-bar"]
    E2 --> M4["Spread: Delta_omega squared"]
    M1 --> CS["Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality Applied"]
    M2 --> CS
    M3 --> CS
    M4 --> CS
    CS --> OUT["Heisenberg-Gabor Bound: Delta_t times Delta_omega greater than or equal 1 divided by 2"]
    CS --> EQ["Equality only for Gaussian x of t = A exp of minus beta t squared"]
    OUT --> APP1["Application: STFT fixed window"]
    OUT --> APP2["Application: Wavelet adaptive scaling"]
    OUT --> APP3["Application: Radar pulse shaping"]
    OUT --> APP4["Application: Biomedical signal analysis"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`
**Define the effective duration ($\Delta_t$) and effective bandwidth ($\Delta_\omega$) of a finite-energy signal. State the Heisenberg uncertainty principle relating them.**

**Model Answer:**

For a finite-energy signal $x(t) \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$ with total energy
$$E_x \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t)\vert^2 \, dt,$$
the **effective duration** is defined as the square root of the second central moment of the time-energy distribution:
$$\Delta_t^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{E_x}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (t - \bar{t})^2 \,\vert x(t)\vert^2 \, dt, \qquad \bar{t} = \frac{1}{E_x}\int t\,\vert x(t)\vert^2\,dt.$$

Similarly, the **effective bandwidth** is
$$\Delta_\omega^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi E_x}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\omega - \bar{\omega})^2 \,\vert X(\omega)\vert^2 \, d\omega, \qquad \bar{\omega} = \frac{1}{2\pi E_x}\int \omega\,\vert X(\omega)\vert^2\,d\omega.$$

The **Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle** states:
$$\Delta_t \cdot \Delta_\omega \;\geq\; \frac{1}{2}.$$
This imposes a fundamental lower bound on the joint time-frequency spread. *Self-study note: derivation uses the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality on the functions $t x(t)$ and $dx(t)/dt$.*

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`
**State two examples of signals, one with perfect time localization and one with perfect frequency localization. Justify briefly.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Perfect time localization**: The Dirac delta $x(t) = \delta(t - t_0)$. Its energy is concentrated at the single instant $t_0$, so $\Delta_t \to 0$. However, its Fourier transform is the constant $X(\omega) = e^{-j\omega t_0}$, so the energy is uniformly spread over all frequencies, i.e. $\Delta_\omega \to \infty$. The product remains bounded by the uncertainty inequality.

2. **Perfect frequency localization**: The complex sinusoid $x(t) = A\,e^{j\omega_0 t}$. Its Fourier transform is $X(\omega) = 2\pi A\,\delta(\omega - \omega_0)$, so $\Delta_\omega = 0$. However, the signal is non-decaying and persists for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, giving $\Delta_t \to \infty$.

This illustrates the central trade-off: a signal cannot be simultaneously localized in both domains.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls):**
> - **Do not** confuse effective duration $\Delta_t$ (standard deviation in time) with the *support* of the signal (the interval where it is non-zero). The support gives an upper bound, not the exact $\Delta_t$.
> - **Do not** drop the $\tfrac{1}{2\pi}$ factor in Parseval's theorem or in the frequency-moment definitions — it is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> - **Do not** claim the Gaussian *minimizes* the product $\Delta_t \Delta_\omega^2$ or any other variant — it uniquely *minimizes* $\Delta_t \Delta_\omega$ with equality to $1/2$.
> - **Always** verify that your signal belongs to $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ before applying the uncertainty principle. Step functions and unbounded signals are excluded.
> - When the question asks for the "uncertainty product," explicitly *evaluate* it numerically, do not just write the formula.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** Define the effective duration $\Delta_t$ and effective bandwidth $\Delta_\omega$ of a finite-energy signal. Derive the **Heisenberg-Gabor uncertainty inequality** $\Delta_t \Delta_\omega \geq \tfrac{1}{2}$ using the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality. **     (7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the Gaussian signal $x(t) = e^{-\pi t^2}$:
       (i) Compute its total energy $E_x$. **     (2 Marks)**
       (ii) Compute $\Delta_t$ and $\Delta_\omega$. **     (4 Marks)**
       (iii) Verify that the uncertainty product attains the lower bound. **     (1 Mark)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation** [7 Marks — full derivation as in §3.3 above]

1. Define $E_x$, $\bar{t}$, $\Delta_t^{\,2}$, $\bar{\omega}$, $\Delta_\omega^{\,2}$.
2. Apply Cauchy-Schwarz to $f(t) = t x(t)$ and $g(t) = dx(t)/dt$.
3. Compute $\langle f,f\rangle = E_x(\Delta_t^{\,2} + \bar{t}^{\,2})$.
4. Compute $\langle g,g\rangle = E_x(\Delta_\omega^{\,2} + \bar{\omega}^{\,2})$.
5. Compute $\langle f,g\rangle = -E_x/2$ via integration by parts and the vanishing-at-infinity property.
6. Combine: $\frac{1}{4} \leq (\Delta_t^{\,2} + \bar{t}^{\,2})(\Delta_\omega^{\,2} + \bar{\omega}^{\,2}) \geq \Delta_t^{\,2}\Delta_\omega^{\,2}$.
7. Conclude $\Delta_t \Delta_\omega \geq 1/2$.

**(b) Worked Computation** [7 Marks]

**(i)** Total energy:
$$E_x \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-2\pi t^2} dt \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\pi}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}. \quad\text{(2 Marks)}$$

**(ii)** Since $x(t)$ is real and even, $\bar{t}=0$. The second moment:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} t^2 e^{-2\pi t^2}dt \;=\; \frac{1}{2(2\pi)}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\pi}} \;=\; \frac{1}{4\pi\sqrt{2}}.$$

Therefore
$$\Delta_t^{\,2} \;=\; \sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4\pi\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \frac{1}{4\pi} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Delta_t = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\pi}}. \quad\text{(2 Marks)}$$

The Fourier transform is $X(\omega) = e^{-\omega^2/(4\pi)}$, which is also Gaussian. A symmetric calculation in the frequency domain (with $\bar{\omega}=0$) gives
$$\Delta_\omega^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi E_x}\int \omega^2 e^{-\omega^2/(2\pi)}d\omega \;=\; \pi \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Delta_\omega = \sqrt{\pi}. \quad\text{(2 Marks)}$$

**(iii)** Product:
$$\Delta_t \cdot \Delta_\omega \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\pi} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}. \quad\checkmark \quad\text{(1 Mark)}$$

The Gaussian attains the absolute minimum of the uncertainty product.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain with proper definitions why the classical Fourier transform is *not* suitable for analyzing non-stationary signals. Discuss the limitations of the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) in this context. **     (7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the rectangular pulse $x(t) = \text{rect}(t)$ of unit width centred at the origin:
       (i) Compute $E_x$, $\Delta_t$, and $\Delta_\omega$. **     (5 Marks)**
       (ii) Compute the uncertainty product and compare it with the Gaussian's value of $\tfrac{1}{2}$. **     (2 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Fourier vs STFT for Non-Stationary Signals** [7 Marks]

A **non-stationary signal** is one whose frequency content varies with time (e.g., speech, music, ECG, chirp radar signals). The classical Fourier transform
$$X(\omega) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{-j\omega t}\,dt$$
integrates over *all* time and therefore reveals *which* frequencies are present in the signal but *not when* they occur. A delta impulse at $t=0$ and a delta at $t=10$ yield identical Fourier magnitudes — time information is completely lost. **This makes the Fourier transform unsuitable for time-localized analysis of non-stationary signals.**

The **STFT** addresses this by windowing:
$$\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) \;=\; \int x(t)\,g^*(t - \tau)\,e^{-j\omega t}\,dt,$$
where $g$ is a fixed window. The result is a 2-D time-frequency representation. However, the STFT has a **fundamental limitation**: once the window $g$ is chosen, its duration $\sigma_t$ and bandwidth $\sigma_\omega$ are *fixed* for all $(\tau, \omega)$. By the uncertainty principle, $\sigma_t \sigma_\omega \geq 1/2$ — the same trade-off applies to every analysis cell. Therefore:

- A **narrow window** gives good time resolution but poor frequency resolution.
- A **wide window** gives good frequency resolution but poor time resolution.

There is no way to adapt the resolution to the signal's local character. This is the precise motivation for the **Wavelet Transform**, which scales the analysis window inversely with frequency.

**(b) Rectangular Pulse Analysis** [7 Marks]

**(i)** With $x(t) = 1$ for $\vert t \vert \leq 1/2$ and zero otherwise:
- $E_x = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} dt = 1$. **(1 Mark)**
- $\bar{t} = 0$ (symmetry). $\Delta_t^{\,2} = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} t^2 dt = \tfrac{1}{12}$, hence $\Delta_t = \tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}$. **(2 Marks)**
- Fourier transform: $X(\omega) = \tfrac{\sin(\omega/2)}{\omega/2}$. **(implicit, 0 explicit mark here, used in next step)**
- $\Delta_\omega^{\,2} = \pi^2$, hence $\Delta_\omega = \pi$. **(2 Marks)**

**(ii)** Uncertainty product:
$$\Delta_t \cdot \Delta_\omega \;=\; \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{3}} \;\approx\; 0.9069 \;>\; \frac{1}{2}. \quad\text{(2 Marks)}$$

The rectangular pulse's uncertainty product is approximately **1.81 times** the Gaussian minimum. Hence it is a poor joint-localization pulse. The Gaussian is provably optimal.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU exam prep):**

- [ ] **Energy normalization**: Every moment in the localization formulas is divided by $E_x = \int \vert x(t)\vert^2 dt$. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
- [ ] **Time centroid** $\bar{t}$ is computed as $\frac{1}{E_x}\int t\,\vert x\vert^2\,dt$; **bandwidth centroid** $\bar{\omega}$ uses $\frac{1}{2\pi E_x}\int \omega\,\vert X\vert^2\,d\omega$ — the $2\pi$ factor is **mandatory** and commonly missed.
- [ ] **Standard deviation** is the **square root** of the variance: $\Delta_t = \sqrt{\Delta_t^{\,2}}$.
- [ ] **Parseval's Theorem** $E_x = \int \vert x\vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int \vert X\vert^2 d\omega$ links time-domain and frequency-domain energies.
- [ ] **The core inequality**: $\Delta_t \cdot \Delta_\omega \geq \tfrac{1}{2}$. The value $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is the absolute minimum — no signal can beat it.
- [ ] **Cauchy-Schwarz is the proof engine**: applied to $f(t) = t\,x(t)$ and $g(t) = x'(t)$ using the Fourier derivative property $\mathcal{F}\{x'\} = j\omega X$.
- [ ] **Gaussian is the unique equality case**: $x(t) = A\,e^{-\beta t^2}$, $\beta>0$. No other $L^2$ signal achieves $\Delta_t \Delta_\omega = 1/2$.
- [ ] **Rectangular pulse is sub-optimal**: $\Delta_t \Delta_\omega = \pi/(2\sqrt{3}) \approx 0.907$ — remember this numerical value.
- [ ] **Delta function**: $\Delta_t = 0$, $\Delta_\omega = \infty$ (perfect time, no frequency localization).
- [ ] **Sinusoid**: $\Delta_t = \infty$, $\Delta_\omega = 0$ (perfect frequency, no time localization).
- [ ] **Fourier Transform** gives *perfect frequency* but *zero time* localization.
- [ ] **STFT** uses a *fixed* window — uniform tiling of the time-frequency plane.
- [ ] **Wavelet Transform** uses *adaptive* scaling — non-uniform tiling (narrow at high frequency, wide at low frequency).
- [ ] **Lipschitz condition / vanishing moments** of the mother wavelet ensure good time localization of wavelet coefficients.
- [ ] **Admissibility condition** $\int \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega)\vert^2}{\vert \omega \vert} d\omega < \infty$ links frequency localization back to wavelet design.
- [ ] **The uncertainty principle is a *physical* limitation**, not an algorithmic one — it cannot be circumvented by any transform.
- [ ] **Standard KTU Board Keywords** to use verbatim: *effective duration, effective bandwidth, time-frequency localization, uncertainty principle, Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, Parseval's theorem, Gaussian pulse, Heisenberg-Gabor bound.*
- [ ] **For multi-resolution analysis (Module 3 preview)**: the time-frequency tiles of a wavelet basis have constant *area* (set by the uncertainty bound) but *variable aspect ratio* — tall tiles for transients, wide tiles for sustained tones.

> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** When a 14-mark question asks for the *derivation* of the uncertainty principle, examiners award 2 marks for the correct *statement* of the result, 3 marks for the *Cauchy-Schwarz setup*, 1 mark for the *Parseval/derivative step*, and 1 mark for the *final algebraic combination*. Memorize this 7-step logical flow.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Uncertainty Principle and Its Implications - Short Time Fourier Transform - Continuous Time and Discrete Time STFT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Uncertainty Principle and Its Implications - Short Time Fourier Transform - Continuous Time and Discrete Time STFT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Uncertainty Principle (Heisenberg–Gabor Inequality)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
The **Uncertainty Principle**, in the context of signal analysis, states that a finite-energy signal $x(t)$ and its Fourier Transform $X(\omega)$ cannot both be arbitrarily concentrated (localized) in time and frequency simultaneously. The product of the effective time-duration $\Delta t$ and the effective frequency-spread $\Delta \omega$ is bounded below by a universal constant.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board High-Yield Fact:** The constant $\frac{1}{2}$ on the right-hand side is achieved **only** when the signal (and window) is a **Gaussian function** $e^{-at^2}$. This is the minimum possible area in the time-frequency plane and is central to both the Gabor logon and the choice of the Gaussian window in STFT.

**Physical Meaning of the Variables:**
- $\Delta t$ — the **standard deviation** (RMS spread) of $\vert x(t) \vert^2$ around its mean time.
- $\Delta \omega$ — the **standard deviation** (RMS spread) of $\vert X(\omega) \vert^2$ around its mean frequency.
- These quantities are often called the **effective duration** and **effective bandwidth**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Window Trade-off"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine you are listening to a song. To catch a *specific* note (say, the guitarist's solo), you can either:
> 1. **Zoom in very tightly on time** — you know *exactly when* the solo plays, but you cannot tell *which exact pitch* it is (poor frequency resolution).
> 2. **Zoom out to the whole orchestra** — you know *precisely the pitch*, but not *when* it occurred.
>
> The **Uncertainty Principle** says: **you can never have infinite precision in BOTH time and frequency at once**. The product of your "time-zoom" precision and "frequency-zoom" precision is fixed.

This trade-off is the fundamental reason the **Short Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** was invented — it concedes frequency resolution in exchange for time localization, by sliding a finite-length window across the signal.

---

## 1.3 Short Time Fourier Transform (STFT) — Formal Definition

**Continuous-Time STFT (CT-STFT):**

$$X_{STFT}(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, w^{*}(t - \tau) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

where:
- $x(t)$ is the input continuous-time signal.
- $w(t)$ is a **window function** (typically real, even, finite-energy).
- $\tau$ is the **time-shift** parameter (window center).
- $\omega$ is the **angular frequency** parameter.
- $w^{*}(t-\tau)$ denotes complex conjugate of the time-reversed, time-shifted window.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the CT-STFT integral, the windowing operation $x(t) \cdot w(t-\tau)$ extracts a *localized slice* of the signal centered at $t = \tau$. The subsequent multiplication by $e^{-j\omega t}$ computes the **local spectrum** of that slice.

**Discrete-Time STFT (DT-STFT):**

$$X(m, k) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \, w^{*}(n - m) \, e^{-j\omega_k n}$$

For uniform sampling in frequency (with $N$-point DFT of each windowed frame):

$$X(m, k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n + m) \, w(n) \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

where:
- $m$ is the **discrete time-index** (frame number).
- $k$ is the **discrete frequency-index** (bin number).
- $w(n)$ is the discrete window of length $N$.
- $\omega_k = \frac{2\pi k}{N}$ is the sampled angular frequency.

---

## 1.4 Why STFT? The Direct Answer to Uncertainty

| Tool | Time Resolution | Frequency Resolution | Best For |
|------|----------------|---------------------|----------|
| Fourier Transform | None (global) | Infinite | Stationary signals (single note) |
| STFT | Finite (window-controlled) | Finite (window-controlled) | Slowly varying / quasi-stationary signals |
| Wavelet Transform | Multi-scale (good at high freq) | Multi-scale (good at low freq) | Non-stationary, transient signals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The STFT is the *first practical compromise* engineers invented** to overcome the Fourier Transform's inability to handle non-stationary signals — *and* it obeys the Uncertainty Principle in every analysis frame.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Intuition — Step-by-Step Logic

The STFT is constructed in **three conceptual layers**:

1. **Windowing:** Multiply $x(t)$ by $w(t-\tau)$ to isolate a finite-duration piece of the signal around time $\tau$.
   - **Why?** Because the Fourier Transform assumes the signal repeats forever. To make a non-stationary signal "look stationary" locally, we chop it into small pieces.
2. **Spectral Analysis:** Compute the Fourier Transform of the windowed piece. This gives the local frequency content *around* time $\tau$.
3. **Sliding:** Vary $\tau$ continuously (or in steps of $m$ in DT case) to map how the spectrum evolves over time.

> [!NOTE]
> The output $X_{STFT}(\tau, \omega)$ is a **2-D complex function** — a "spectrogram" when its magnitude $\vert X_{STFT}(\tau, \omega) \vert^2$ is plotted against $\tau$ and $\omega$.

---

## 2.2 The Heisenberg Box (Tile) — Visualization of Uncertainty

Each STFT coefficient is associated with a **rectangle (tile)** in the time-frequency plane:
- **Width in time** = effective duration of window $w(t)$, i.e., $\Delta t$.
- **Height in frequency** = effective bandwidth of $W(\omega)$, i.e., $\Delta \omega$.
- **Area of the tile** = $\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$ (constant!).

**Consequence:** You can choose the *shape* of the tile (narrow-wide, or wide-narrow), but you **cannot change its area**. This is the geometric meaning of the Uncertainty Principle.

---

## 2.3 Inversion Formula (Reconstruction)

For the STFT to be useful, the original signal must be recoverable from its STFT (with overlap conditions on the window).

**Continuous-Time Inversion (Gabor's Formula):**

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X_{STFT}(\tau, \omega) \, w(t - \tau) \, e^{j\omega t} \, d\tau \, d\omega \cdot \frac{1}{E_w}$$

where $E_w = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert w(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ is the energy of the window.

**Discrete-Time Inversion (Filterbank Sum):**

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(m, k) \, w(n - m) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Inversion requires the window to satisfy the **Partition of Unity** (or overlap-add) condition:
> $$\sum_{m} \vert w(n - m) \vert^2 = \text{constant (e.g., 1)}$$
> If this fails, reconstruction will have **amplitude ripples** — a guaranteed 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — at least 3 of these 7 equations appear in every KTU ESE question on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Uncertainty Principle (continuous) | $\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$ | Dimensionless (rad·sec) |
| 2 | Uncertainty Principle (discrete) | $\Delta n \cdot \Delta k \geq \frac{N}{4\pi}$ | $N$ = window length |
| 3 | CT-STFT | $X(\tau, \omega) = \int x(t) w^*(t-\tau) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ | Energy density in t-ω |
| 4 | DT-STFT | $X(m, k) = \sum_n x(n) w^*(n-m) e^{-j\omega_k n}$ | 2D indexed by $(m, k)$ |
| 5 | Inversion (CT) | $x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi E_w}\iint X(\tau, \omega) w(t-\tau) e^{j\omega t} d\tau d\omega$ | Requires $E_w \neq 0$ |
| 6 | Inversion (DT) | $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_m \sum_k X(m, k) w(n-m) e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$ | Filterbank summation |
| 7 | Time-Frequency Tile Area | $\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega = \text{const} \geq \frac{1}{2}$ | Equality for Gaussian |

> **Symbols Recap:** $\tau$ (time shift), $\omega$ (angular freq), $m$ (frame index), $k$ (bin index), $w(t)$ (window), $W(\omega)$ (its FT), $E_w$ (window energy).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Speech Processing:** STFT is the mathematical foundation of the **spectrogram**, used in voice recognition, gender identification, and phoneme segmentation.
- **Audio Coding (MP3, AAC):** STFT-based filterbanks (MDCT) decompose audio into 1024 frequency bands for psychoacoustic compression.
- **Radar & Sonar:** STFT detects time-varying Doppler shifts in moving targets.
- **Biomedical Signals (ECG, EEG):** STFT reveals transient cardiac abnormalities or epileptic spikes.
- **Vibration Analysis:** Identifying bearing faults in rotating machinery by tracking time-frequency ridges.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Uncertainty Principle (Heisenberg–Gabor Inequality)

**Goal:** Prove $\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$.

### Step 1 — Define the Effective Time Duration $\Delta t$

The mean time (centroid) of $\vert x(t) \vert^2$ is:

$$t_0 = \frac{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} t \, \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt}{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt}$$

The effective time-spread is the standard deviation around $t_0$:

$$\Delta t = \sqrt{ \frac{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (t - t_0)^2 \, \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt}{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt} }$$

**Logic:** $(t - t_0)^2$ measures squared deviation; the square root makes it dimensionally a "time" quantity. Normalization by total energy makes it scale-independent.

### Step 2 — Define the Effective Bandwidth $\Delta \omega$

Similarly, the mean frequency:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \omega \, \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega}{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega}$$

And:

$$\Delta \omega = \sqrt{ \frac{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\omega - \omega_0)^2 \, \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega}{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega} }$$

**Logic:** Same construction applied in the frequency domain using the energy spectral density.

### Step 3 — Apply the Cauchy–Schwarz Inequality

Consider the inner product $\langle u, v \rangle = \int u^*(t) v(t) \, dt$. By the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality**:

$$\vert \langle u, v \rangle \vert^2 \leq \langle u, u \rangle \cdot \langle v, v \rangle$$

Choose:

$$u(t) = (t - t_0) \, x(t), \qquad v(t) = \frac{dx(t)}{dt}$$

Then:

$$\langle u, v \rangle = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (t - t_0) \, x(t) \cdot \frac{dx^*(t)}{dt} \, dt$$

Compute:

$$\langle u, u \rangle = \int (t - t_0)^2 \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

$$\langle v, v \rangle = \int \left\vert \frac{dx(t)}{dt} \right\vert^2 \, dt$$

**Logic:** $\langle v, v \rangle$ is the "energy of the derivative" — a measure of signal variability (bandwidth).

### Step 4 — Apply Parseval's Theorem to the Derivative

By Parseval's theorem:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left\vert \frac{dx(t)}{dt} \right\vert^2 \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \omega^2 \, \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega$$

(The Fourier Transform of $\frac{dx}{dt}$ is $j\omega X(\omega)$.)

**Logic:** This connects the time-domain derivative to frequency content — high-frequency content = high derivative energy.

### Step 5 — Simplify $\langle u, v \rangle$ via Integration by Parts

$$\int (t - t_0) x(t) \frac{dx^*}{dt} dt = \left[ (t - t_0) \frac{\vert x(t) \vert^2}{2} \right]_{-\infty}^{\infty} - \int \frac{\vert x(t) \vert^2}{2} dt$$

The boundary term vanishes (since $\vert x(t) \vert^2 \to 0$ as $t \to \pm\infty$ for finite-energy signals). The remaining integral gives $-\frac{1}{2} E_x$, where $E_x = \int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$.

**Therefore:**

$$\vert \langle u, v \rangle \vert^2 = \frac{E_x^2}{4}$$

### Step 6 — Assemble Cauchy–Schwarz

$$\frac{E_x^2}{4} \leq \langle u, u \rangle \cdot \langle v, v \rangle$$

Substitute and divide by $E_x^2$ (assuming $E_x \neq 0$):

$$\frac{1}{4} \leq \frac{\langle u, u \rangle}{E_x} \cdot \frac{\langle v, v \rangle}{E_x} = (\Delta t)^2 \cdot \frac{\int \omega^2 \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 d\omega}{E_x}$$

Using Parseval $E_x = \int \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 d\omega / 2\pi$:

$$(\Delta t)^2 \cdot (\Delta \omega)^2 \geq \frac{1}{4}$$

Taking the square root:

$$\boxed{\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equality Condition:** The bound is tight *iff* the Cauchy–Schwarz equality condition holds, which requires $u(t) = c \cdot v(t)$ for some complex constant $c$. This leads to the differential equation:
> $$(t - t_0) x(t) = c \frac{dx(t)}{dt}$$
> whose unique finite-energy solution is a **Gaussian** $x(t) = A e^{-a(t-t_0)^2}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Continuous-Time STFT Inversion

**Goal:** Recover $x(t)$ from $X_{STFT}(\tau, \omega)$.

### Step 1 — Start from the STFT Definition

$$X(\tau, \omega) = \int x(t) w^*(t - \tau) e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

### Step 2 — Multiply Both Sides by $w(t - \tau) e^{j\omega t}$ and Integrate

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(\tau, \omega) w(t - \tau) e^{j\omega t} d\tau d\omega = \int \int x(t') w^*(t'-\tau) e^{-j\omega t'} w(t-\tau) e^{j\omega t} d\omega d\tau dt'$$

### Step 3 — Separate the $\omega$ Integral

The $\omega$ integral gives:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j\omega(t - t')} d\omega = 2\pi \, \delta(t - t')$$

(where $\delta$ is the Dirac delta).

### Step 4 — The Expression Reduces to

$$2\pi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t') \delta(t - t') \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} w^*(t' - \tau) w(t - \tau) d\tau \right] dt'$$

### Step 5 — Recognize the Inner Integral as the Window Autocorrelation

Define:

$$R_{ww}(\tau) = \int w^*(t' - \tau) w(t - \tau) d\tau$$

This becomes:

$$2\pi \, R_{ww}(t) \cdot x(t)$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $x(t)$

If $R_{ww}(0) = E_w = \int \vert w(t) \vert^2 dt$ (which holds for a properly normalized window evaluated at zero lag):

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi \, E_w} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(\tau, \omega) w(t - \tau) e^{j\omega t} d\tau d\omega$$

**Logic:** Inversion is a *two-fold integral* — a frequency-inverse Fourier transform (recovering the windowed piece) plus a time-inverse Fourier transform (reassembling the pieces). The factor $1/E_w$ is the energy normalization needed for perfect reconstruction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — DT-STFT and Its Connection to the Filter Bank

### Step 1 — Frequency Discretization

Sample $\omega$ uniformly at $N$ points: $\omega_k = 2\pi k / N$, $k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$.

$$X(m, k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) w(n - m) e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$$

### Step 2 — Recognize the DFT Structure

If we define the windowed frame as $x_w(n; m) = x(n) w(n - m)$, then for fixed $m$:

$$X(m, k) = \text{DFT}_k \{ x_w(n; m) \}$$

**Logic:** Each frame is a $N$-point DFT of one windowed slice of the signal.

### Step 3 — Reconstruction (Filterbank View)

The inverse DFT of $X(m, k)$ with respect to $k$ recovers $x(n) w(n - m)$:

$$\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(m, k) e^{j 2\pi k n / N} = x(n) w(n - m)$$

Summing over all frames $m$:

$$\sum_{m} x(n) w(n - m) = x(n) \sum_{m} w(n - m)$$

**Logic:** This is the **overlap-add** of all windowed frames. For perfect reconstruction, we need:

$$\sum_{m} w(n - m) = 1 \quad \forall n \quad \text{(or } \sum_m \vert w(n-m) \vert^2 = 1 \text{ with conjugate inversion)}$$

### Step 4 — Final Reconstruction

$$x(n) = \frac{\sum_{m} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k} X(m,k) w(n-m) e^{j 2\pi k n / N}}{\sum_{m} w(n - m)}$$

For the **weighted overlap-add (WOLA)** scheme with normalized windows, the denominator is unity, and we obtain the clean form:

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(m, k) w(n - m) e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** This is mathematically equivalent to a **uniform DFT filter bank**. Each bin $k$ corresponds to a bandpass filter centered at $\omega_k = 2\pi k / N$, and the bank decomposes the signal into $N$ frequency sub-bands.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Resolution Trade-off — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A rectangular window of length $L = 32$ samples is used in a DT-STFT with sampling rate $F_s = 8$ kHz.

**Find:** (a) Time resolution, (b) Frequency resolution, (c) Heisenberg tile area.

### Solution

**(a) Time Resolution (Duration of Window):**

$$\Delta t = \frac{L}{F_s} = \frac{32}{8000} = 4 \text{ ms}$$

**Logic:** Time resolution is *one window length* in seconds. Shorter window → better time resolution (can catch quicker transients).

**(b) Frequency Resolution (Bandwidth per Bin):**

$$\Delta \omega = \frac{2\pi}{L} \cdot F_s = \frac{2\pi F_s}{L} = \frac{2\pi \times 8000}{32} = 500\pi \, \text{rad/sec}$$

In Hz:

$$\Delta f = \frac{F_s}{L} = \frac{8000}{32} = 250 \text{ Hz}$$

**Logic:** The DFT has $L$ bins spanning $[0, F_s]$, so each bin's width is $F_s / L$. Wider window → narrower bins → finer frequency resolution.

**(c) Heisenberg Tile Area (Normalized to Discrete Form):**

In the discrete form $\Delta n \cdot \Delta k \geq \frac{N}{4\pi}$:

$$\Delta n \cdot \Delta k = 32 \times 1 = 32$$

Compare to the bound:

$$\frac{N}{4\pi} = \frac{32}{4\pi} \approx 2.546$$

Since $32 \gg 2.546$, the bound is *vastly satisfied* — meaning a rectangular window is **far from optimal**. Using a Gaussian window would approach equality.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The STFT Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal x t] --> B[Apply Time Shift by tau]
    W[Window Function w t] --> B
    B --> C[Multiply x t times w t minus tau]
    C --> D[Complex Modulation e to power minus j omega t]
    D --> E[Integrate over t minus infinity to infinity]
    E --> F[STFT Coefficient X tau omega]
    F --> G[Slide tau and Repeat]
    G --> H[2D Time Frequency Map Spectrogram]
    H --> I{Magnitude Squared}
    I --> J[Spectrogram Display]
```

**Interpretation:** Each cycle through the diagram produces one *row* of the STFT (a single time slice). Repeating for all $\tau$ fills the 2-D time-frequency plane.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Uncertainty Principle Trade-off (Resolution Slider)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Short Window] -->|Narrow Delta t| B1[Good Time Resolution]
    B1 --> C1[Poor Frequency Resolution]
    A1 --> D1[Wide Delta omega]
    A2[Long Window] -->|Wide Delta t| B2[Poor Time Resolution]
    B2 --> C2[Good Frequency Resolution]
    A2 --> D2[Narrow Delta omega]
    style A1 fill:#FFD700
    style A2 fill:#87CEEB
    style C1 fill:#FF6B6B
    style C2 fill:#FF6B6B
    style B1 fill:#90EE90
    style B2 fill:#90EE90
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram shows the **resolution slider** concept. You can never have both green boxes (good resolution) at the same time — only one of two opposing paths is possible.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Heisenberg Box (Time-Frequency Tiling)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Time Frequency Plane] --> A[Tile 1: Short Window]
    T --> B[Tile 2: Medium Window]
    T --> C[Tile 3: Long Window]
    A --> A1[Width small in time]
    A --> A2[Height large in frequency]
    B --> B1[Width medium in time]
    B --> B2[Height medium in frequency]
    C --> C1[Width large in time]
    C --> C2[Height small in frequency]
    A1 --> R[Area CONSTANT equals 1 by 2]
    A2 --> R
    B1 --> R
    B2 --> R
    C1 --> R
    C2 --> R
    style R fill:#FFB6C1
```

**Interpretation:** Regardless of window choice, the **area** of each tile is invariant (≥ 1/2). This is the geometric essence of the Uncertainty Principle.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — DT-STFT Filter Bank Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Input x n] --> D1[Delay Line Buffer]
    D1 --> M1[Windowed Frame m]
    D1 --> M2[Windowed Frame m plus 1]
    D1 --> M3[Windowed Frame m plus 2]
    M1 --> DFT1[N point DFT bin 0]
    M1 --> DFT2[N point DFT bin 1]
    M1 --> DFT3[N point DFT bin N minus 1]
    M2 --> IDFT1[Inverse DFT Bank]
    M3 --> IDFT2[Inverse DFT Bank]
    DFT1 --> SUM[Overlap Add Summation]
    DFT2 --> SUM
    DFT3 --> SUM
    IDFT1 --> SUM
    IDFT2 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Reconstructed x n]
```

**Interpretation:** This block diagram shows STFT as a **sliding DFT filter bank**. The delay line feeds successive frames into parallel DFT channels, and reconstruction is achieved via inverse DFT and overlap-add.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Comparison: FT vs STFT vs WT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SIG[Non Stationary Signal] --> Q{Contain Transients?}
    Q -->|No| FT[Fourier Transform]
    Q -->|Yes, Slowly Varying| STFT[Short Time Fourier Transform]
    Q -->|Yes, Sharp Transients| WT[Wavelet Transform]
    FT --> F1[1D Spectrum global]
    STFT --> S1[2D Spectrogram time freq]
    WT --> W1[2D Scalogram time scale]
    S1 --> LIMIT1[Fixed Resolution Trade off]
    W1 --> LIMIT2[Multi Resolution Adaptive]
    style FT fill:#E0E0E0
    style STFT fill:#FFE4B5
    style WT fill:#98FB98
```

**Interpretation:** STFT occupies the *middle ground* — it sacrifices some resolution adaptability for computational simplicity. Wavelets (next module) solve this by using variable-scale windows.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State and explain the Heisenberg–Gabor Uncertainty Principle in signal analysis. What is the significance of the constant $\frac{1}{2}$ in the inequality?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Expected 50–60 words):**

The Heisenberg–Gabor Uncertainty Principle states that a finite-energy signal $x(t)$ and its Fourier Transform $X(\omega)$ cannot both be simultaneously concentrated in time and frequency. Mathematically,

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$$

The constant $\frac{1}{2}$ represents the **minimum possible area** of the time-frequency tile, and this lower bound is **achieved only when the signal is a Gaussian function** $x(t) = e^{-at^2}$. (3 marks — definition: 1, formula: 1, Gaussian equality: 1)

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the Short Time Fourier Transform of a continuous-time signal. How does the choice of window length affect time and frequency resolution?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The CT-STFT of $x(t)$ with respect to window $w(t)$ is:

$$X_{STFT}(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, w^*(t - \tau) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

**Effect of window length:**
- **Short window** (small $L$): good **time resolution** (can localize transients) but **poor frequency resolution** (wide main lobe of $W(\omega)$).
- **Long window** (large $L$): good **frequency resolution** (narrow main lobe) but **poor time resolution**.
This trade-off is a direct consequence of the **Uncertainty Principle**. (1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark trade-off)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not write the STFT formula without defining the variables** — losing 1 mark is common.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ in inversion** is a classic 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing the discrete-time STFT with the DTFT** of the windowed signal will cost full marks.
> 4. **Skipping the equality condition of the Uncertainty Principle** (Gaussian) is a frequent oversight.
> 5. **Numerical problems:** Always show **units** (Hz, sec, rad/sec) — KTU examiners explicitly check.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — Subdivision Choice A

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Module 2 Internal Choice — Option A)]**

**(a)** State and prove the **Heisenberg–Gabor Uncertainty Principle** for a finite-energy continuous-time signal $x(t)$. Discuss the **equality condition** and identify the function that achieves the bound. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define the **Continuous-Time Short Time Fourier Transform**. Starting from the definition, derive the **inversion formula** for reconstructing $x(t)$ from its STFT. State and justify the **partition of unity condition** required on the window for perfect reconstruction. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution B-A

**(a) Proof of the Uncertainty Principle (7 Marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):**
For a finite-energy signal $x(t)$ with Fourier Transform $X(\omega)$:

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$$

where

$$\Delta t^2 = \frac{\int (t - t_0)^2 \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt}{\int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt}, \quad \Delta \omega^2 = \frac{\int (\omega - \omega_0)^2 \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 d\omega}{\int \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 d\omega}$$

**Proof (4 marks):** Apply the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality** to the inner product:

$$\langle u, v \rangle = \int (t - t_0) x(t) \frac{dx^*(t)}{dt} dt$$

with $u(t) = (t - t_0) x(t)$ and $v(t) = \frac{dx(t)}{dt}$. After integration by parts (boundary term vanishes for finite-energy signals), we obtain:

$$\vert \langle u, v \rangle \vert^2 = \frac{1}{4} \left( \int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt \right)^2$$

Cauchy–Schwarz gives:

$$\frac{E_x^2}{4} \leq \int (t - t_0)^2 \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt \cdot \int \left\vert \frac{dx}{dt} \right\vert^2 dt$$

By Parseval's theorem, $\int \vert \dot{x} \vert^2 dt = \int \omega^2 \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 d\omega$. Substituting and simplifying:

$$(\Delta t)^2 (\Delta \omega)^2 \geq \frac{1}{4} \implies \Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$$

**Equality Condition (2 marks):** Equality in Cauchy–Schwarz requires $u(t) = c \, v(t)$, i.e.,

$$(t - t_0) x(t) = c \frac{dx(t)}{dt}$$

This ODE has the **unique finite-energy solution**:

$$x(t) = A \, e^{-(t - t_0)^2 / (2c)}$$

which is a **Gaussian function**. Hence, **only Gaussian signals achieve the minimum tile area of $\frac{1}{2}$**.

---

**(b) CT-STFT Definition and Inversion (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**

$$X_{STFT}(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, w^*(t - \tau) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

where $w(t)$ is the window function, $\tau$ is the time-shift, and $\omega$ is the angular frequency.

**Derivation of Inversion (3 marks):** Multiply both sides by $w(t - \tau) e^{j\omega t}$ and integrate over $\tau$ and $\omega$:

$$\iint X_{STFT}(\tau, \omega) w(t - \tau) e^{j\omega t} d\tau d\omega = \iint x(t') w^*(t' - \tau) e^{-j\omega t'} w(t - \tau) e^{j\omega t} d\tau d\omega dt'$$

Using the Fourier identity $\int e^{j\omega(t - t')} d\omega = 2\pi \delta(t - t')$ and recognizing the inner integral as the window autocorrelation:

$$2\pi \int x(t') \delta(t - t') R_{ww}(t) dt' = 2\pi \, R_{ww}(0) \, x(t)$$

If $R_{ww}(0) = E_w = \int \vert w(t) \vert^2 dt$:

$$\boxed{x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi E_w} \iint X_{STFT}(\tau, \omega) w(t - \tau) e^{j\omega t} d\tau d\omega}$$

**Partition of Unity (2 marks):** For perfect reconstruction in the **discrete-time case** with hop size $R$:

$$\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert w(n - mR) \vert^2 = \text{constant} \quad \forall n$$

This condition ensures that when all windowed frames are summed back, no time-instant is over- or under-weighted. Violations produce **amplitude modulation artifacts** (tremolo effect) in the reconstructed signal.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Subdivision Choice B (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Module 2 Internal Choice — Option B)]**

**(a)** Derive the **Discrete-Time Short Time Fourier Transform (DT-STFT)** from first principles, explaining the role of **windowing, time-shift parameter $m$, and frequency-bin parameter $k$**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a DT-STFT system with sampling rate $F_s = 16$ kHz, window length $N = 64$, and hop size $R = 16$, compute the **(i) time resolution, (ii) frequency resolution, (iii) Heisenberg tile area**, and **(iv) the number of overlapping frames per second**. Comment on the **trade-off** between time and frequency resolution. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution B-B

**(a) Derivation of DT-STFT (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Discretize the signal (1 mark):** Sample $x(t)$ at $t = nT$ to get $x(n)$ where $T = 1/F_s$.

**Step 2 — Discretize the window (1 mark):** $w(t) \to w(n)$ of length $N$ (non-zero for $0 \leq n \leq N-1$).

**Step 3 — Discretize the time shift (1 mark):** $\tau = mR$, where $m \in \mathbb{Z}$ is the frame index and $R$ is the hop size (typically $R \leq N$).

**Step 4 — Discretize the frequency (2 marks):** For an $N$-point DFT, sample $\omega$ at $\omega_k = 2\pi k / N$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, N - 1$.

**Step 5 — Assemble the formula (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{X(m, k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, w^*(n - mR) \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}}$$

**Interpretation:** For each frame index $m$, compute the $N$-point DFT of the windowed signal slice $\{x(n) w(n - mR)\}_{n=0}^{N-1}$. The output is a 2-D complex array indexed by $(m, k)$ — time on one axis, frequency on the other.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $F_s = 16000$ Hz, $N = 64$, $R = 16$.

**(i) Time Resolution (1.5 marks):**

$$\Delta t = \frac{N}{F_s} = \frac{64}{16000} = 4 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Frequency Resolution (1.5 marks):**

$$\Delta f = \frac{F_s}{N} = \frac{16000}{64} = 250 \text{ Hz}$$

Or in rad/sec: $\Delta \omega = 2\pi \times 250 = 500\pi \approx 1570.8$ rad/s.

**(iii) Heisenberg Tile Area (Discrete Form) (2 marks):**

The discrete uncertainty bound is $\Delta n \cdot \Delta k \geq \frac{N}{4\pi}$:

$$\frac{N}{4\pi} = \frac{64}{4\pi} \approx 5.09$$

Computed area: $\Delta n \cdot \Delta k = 64 \times 1 = 64$. This is *far above* the bound, so a **rectangular window is suboptimal**. A **Gaussian window** would approach equality.

**(iv) Number of Overlapping Frames per Second (2 marks):**

$$\text{Frames/second} = \frac{F_s}{R} = \frac{16000}{16} = 1000 \text{ frames/s}$$

Each frame overlaps the next by $N - R = 48$ samples (75% overlap).

**Comment on Trade-off (Bonus discussion):** With $N = 64$, the system achieves $\Delta f = 250$ Hz — sufficient for **speech formant analysis**. Doubling $N$ to 128 would halve the frequency resolution to 125 Hz but double the time resolution to 8 ms, **sacrificing transient detection**. This is the classic STFT dilemma, motivating the **wavelet transform** (Module 3) with its adaptive resolution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin these 10 high-yield points to memory for the KTU ESE:**

- [x] **Uncertainty Principle formula:** $\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$ — *equality for Gaussian only.*
- [x] **CT-STFT:** $X(\tau, \omega) = \int x(t) w^*(t - \tau) e^{-j\omega t} dt$
- [x] **DT-STFT:** $X(m, k) = \sum_n x(n) w^*(n - m) e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$
- [x] **Inversion formula** has the $\frac{1}{2\pi E_w}$ factor — *never omit it.*
- [x] **Window length trade-off:** short → good time, poor frequency; long → good frequency, poor time.
- [x] **Heisenberg Box area is constant** — you choose the *shape* (resolution), not the *area*.
- [x] **Gaussian window** is the **only** optimal choice (joint localization).
- [x] **Discrete uncertainty bound:** $\Delta n \cdot \Delta k \geq \frac{N}{4\pi}$.
- [x] **Hop size $R$** determines frame overlap; for $R = N/4$, you get 75% overlap.
- [x] **STFT is the precursor to Wavelets** — it is *not* multi-resolution; wavelets (next module) fix that.

> **Closing Note:** The STFT, despite its fixed-resolution limitation, remains the workhorse of audio, speech, and vibration analysis in industry. Mastering its math here sets the foundation for the adaptive wavelet transform in Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filter Bank Implementation of STFT - Continuous Wavelet Transform - Concept of Wavelets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Filter Bank Implementation of STFT - Continuous Wavelet Transform - Concept of Wavelets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Filter Bank Realization of the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)

### Formal KTU Definition
The **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is rigorously defined as a windowed Fourier integral where the signal is multiplied by a sliding analysis window $g(t)$ and then subjected to a Fourier transform. Mathematically,

$$
X_{\text{STFT}}(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, g^{*}(t-\tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt
$$

From a **filter bank perspective**, the STFT is interpreted as a parallel bank of complex bandpass filters whose center frequencies are uniformly spaced, and each filter output is demodulated to baseband. The complex modulation theorem states that shifting the spectrum of $g(t)$ to $\omega = \omega_k$ is equivalent to creating a bandpass filter centered at $\omega_k$. Thus, the STFT block at frequency $\omega_k$ is identical to the output of a bandpass filter $H_k(\omega) = G(\omega - \omega_k)$ evaluated at the sliding instant $\tau$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** The filter bank view is the *bridge* that connects time-frequency analysis (STFT) to multirate DSP (filter banks). Examiners frequently ask to derive the equivalence between the STFT and a uniformly spaced DFT-modulated filter bank.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **musical equalizer with 32 sliders**, where each slider is a bandpass filter tuned to a specific pitch band. As a song plays, every slider measures the *instantaneous energy* in its own frequency band. The collection of all 32 slider readings at a given time $\tau$ is exactly the **STFT** of the audio. The sliding window $g(t-\tau)$ is the "ear" that captures a short chunk, and the bank of filters is the "equalizer" that splits the chunk into frequency bands. A **filter bank implementation** simply builds that equalizer using actual hardware (analog) or digital filters.

### Key Symbols and Constants
- **$\tau$** : Translation parameter (time location of analysis), measured in **seconds**.
- **$\omega$** : Angular frequency variable, measured in **radians/second**.
- **$g(t)$** : Analysis window (typically real, even, normalized, e.g., Gaussian, Hamming).
- **$\psi(t)$** : Mother wavelet (used later for CWT).
- **$s$** : Scale parameter (dimensionless, $s > 0$).

---

## 1.2 Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) — The Core Concept

### Formal KTU Definition
The **Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)** of a finite-energy signal $x(t) \in L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$ with respect to a *mother wavelet* $\psi(t)$ is defined as the inner product of $x(t)$ with translated and dilated (scaled) versions of $\psi(t)$:

$$
\text{CWT}_{x}(\tau, s) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{s}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-\tau}{s}\right) dt
$$

where $\tau \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **translation** (time-shift) parameter and $s \in \mathbb{R}^{+}$ is the **scale** parameter. The factor $1/\sqrt{s}$ ensures **energy normalization** across all scales.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $1/\sqrt{s}$?** When we dilate a function $\psi(t)$ by a factor $s$, the energy in the time domain scales as $\sqrt{s}$. Dividing by $\sqrt{s}$ ensures that all scaled wavelets have the **same unit energy**, so the CWT coefficients are directly comparable across scales.

### The Admissibility Condition
A function $\psi(t)$ qualifies as a *mother wavelet* if and only if it satisfies the **admissibility condition**:

$$
C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{+\infty} \frac{\vert \hat{\psi}(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\omega}\, d\omega \;<\; \infty
$$

This integral is finite **if and only if** $\hat{\psi}(0) = 0$, i.e., the mother wavelet has **zero mean** (zero DC component). Equivalently,

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \psi(t)\, dt = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero-mean is the defining hallmark of a wavelet.** Unlike a Fourier sine wave (which has non-zero mean over a half-cycle), every wavelet must oscillate and "average to zero." This is why wavelets are "small waves" — little ripples that come and go.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of the CWT as a **microscope with adjustable zoom**:
- The **scale $s$** is the *zoom knob*. Small $s$ = high zoom (looks at fine, high-frequency details); large $s$ = low zoom (looks at coarse, low-frequency structures).
- The **translation $\tau$** is the *slide mechanism* that moves the microscope left and right across the signal.
- The **mother wavelet $\psi(t)$** is the *lens shape* — different lenses (Morlet, Mexican Hat, Daubechies) reveal different features.
This multi-resolution behavior is impossible in the STFT, which uses a *fixed-size window* — a microscope with a single, unchangeable zoom level.

---

## 1.3 The Concept of Wavelets — Why Wavelets?

### Definition
A **wavelet** is a small, oscillatory, finite-energy waveform $\psi(t)$ that is well-localized in both **time** and **frequency** and has a bandpass-like spectrum (zero mean). A *family of wavelets* is generated from the mother wavelet by continuous **translation** and **dilation**:

$$
\psi_{\tau,s}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{s}}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-\tau}{s}\right), \quad s > 0,\; \tau \in \mathbb{R}
$$

### Conceptual Analogy — Why Wavelets Instead of Fourier?
- **Fourier Transform:** Tells you *what frequencies exist* in the entire signal, but says *nothing* about *when* they occur. A composer writing a symphony writes one giant chord: "C, D, E, F, G, A, B, C" — but no timing information.
- **STFT:** Tells you *what frequencies exist at a given time*, but uses a *fixed-size window*. It is like a piano roll where every note has the same duration — you cannot zoom in on a fast trill.
- **Wavelet Transform:** Tells you *what frequencies exist at what times with what resolution*. It is the **adaptive piano roll** — short notes for fast passages (small scale), long notes for sustained chords (large scale). This is called **multi-resolution analysis (MRA)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle Connection:** No transform can simultaneously localize a signal to an arbitrarily small interval in *both* time and frequency. The product of time-spread $\sigma_{t}$ and frequency-spread $\sigma_{\omega}$ is bounded below by $\sigma_{t}\,\sigma_{\omega} \ge \frac{1}{2}$. The STFT has a *fixed* $(\sigma_{t}, \sigma_{\omega})$ rectangle; wavelets trade time-resolution for frequency-resolution (or vice versa) across scales, achieving an *adaptive tiling* of the time-frequency plane.

### Real-World Applications (Engineering Utility)
- **Biomedical ECG/EEG analysis:** Detects transient QRS complexes that STFT would smear.
- **Seismic data processing:** Decomposes earthquake signals into frequency bands at different depths.
- **Image compression (JPEG 2000):** Uses discrete wavelets for sub-band coding.
- **Audio denoising:** Thresholding wavelet coefficients removes noise.
- **Communication systems:** Wavelet-based OFDM and CDMA.

---

## 1.4 Summary of the Three Core Ideas in This Module

| Concept | Core Variable | Resolution Trade-off | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **STFT** | $X_{\text{STFT}}(\tau, \omega)$ | Fixed $\Delta t$ & $\Delta f$ | Stationary signals |
| **Filter Bank** | $H_k(\omega) = G(\omega - \omega_k)$ | Uniform frequency partition | Sub-band processing |
| **CWT** | $\text{CWT}_{x}(\tau, s)$ | Adaptive: $\Delta t \propto s$, $\Delta f \propto 1/s$ | Non-stationary, transient signals |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive: Filter Bank Implementation of the STFT

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. **Start with the STFT definition.** Multiply $x(t)$ by a shifted window $g^{*}(t-\tau)$ to extract a local segment.
2. **Apply modulation.** Multiplying by $e^{-j\omega_k t}$ shifts the segment's spectrum to baseband.
3. **Apply the lowpass filter $g(t)$.** The windowed-Fourier-multiplied signal, when passed through $g$, produces the STFT coefficient.
4. **Reinterpret as a bandpass filter.** Using the modulation theorem of the Fourier transform,

$$
\mathcal{F}\{x(t)\,e^{-j\omega_k t}\} \star G(\omega) = X(\omega - \omega_k)\,G(\omega)
$$

5. **Recognize the bandpass structure.** The right-hand side is exactly the output of a bandpass filter with impulse response $h_k(t) = g(t)\,e^{j\omega_k t}$ and frequency response $H_k(\omega) = G(\omega - \omega_k)$.
6. **Form a uniform filter bank.** For $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$, the $k$-th filter is centered at $\omega_k = \omega_0 + k\,(2\pi/N)$, producing a uniform DFT-modulated filter bank.
7. **The STFT is the simultaneous output of this filter bank evaluated at time $\tau$.**

### The DFT-Modulated Filter Bank
Define the prototype lowpass filter $g(t)$ with real, even impulse response. The complex bandpass filters are:

$$
h_k(t) = g(t)\, e^{j\omega_k t}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1
$$

The output of the $k$-th filter is:

$$
y_k(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, h_k^{*}(t-\tau)\, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, g(t-\tau)\, e^{-j\omega_k t}\, dt
$$

This is precisely the STFT coefficient at frequency $\omega_k$ and time $\tau$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Trick:** To show the equivalence in an exam, the student must start from $y_k(\tau)$ as the convolution of $x(t)$ with $h_k(-t)$ and then substitute $h_k(t) = g(t)e^{j\omega_k t}$ to obtain the STFT integral. This three-line derivation is worth **7 marks** in a typical KTU 14-mark question.

### Engineering Utility
- **Spectral analysis** of audio, vibration, and biomedical signals.
- **Sub-band coding** in audio compression (MP3 uses a similar filter bank).
- **Channelization** in software-defined radio.
- **Noise reduction** by zeroing coefficients in sub-bands dominated by noise.

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive: Continuous Wavelet Transform Mechanics

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. **Choose a mother wavelet $\psi(t)$** that satisfies the admissibility condition.
2. **For each scale $s > 0$**, construct a dilated (stretched or compressed) copy: $\psi_s(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{s}}\psi(t/s)$.
3. **For each translation $\tau$**, slide the dilated wavelet along the signal: $\psi_{\tau,s}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{s}}\psi\!\left(\frac{t-\tau}{s}\right)$.
4. **Compute the inner product** (correlation) of $x(t)$ with $\psi_{\tau,s}(t)$. This yields the CWT coefficient $\text{CWT}_x(\tau, s)$.
5. **Repeat** for all $s$ and $\tau$ to build the time-scale representation.
6. **Reconstruct** $x(t)$ via the inverse CWT:

$$
x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{0}^{+\infty} \!\!\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \text{CWT}_x(\tau, s)\, \psi_{\tau,s}(t)\, \frac{d\tau\, ds}{s^{2}}
$$

### Properties of the CWT
- **Linearity:** $\text{CWT}_{ax_1 + bx_2} = a\,\text{CWT}_{x_1} + b\,\text{CWT}_{x_2}$.
- **Translation invariance:** If $x(t) \to x(t-t_0)$, then $\text{CWT}_x(\tau,s) \to \text{CWT}_x(\tau - t_0, s)$.
- **Scaling property:** If $x(t) \to x(\alpha t)$, then $\text{CWT}_x(\tau, s) \to \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}\,\text{CWT}_x(\alpha\tau, \alpha s)$.
- **Energy conservation (Moyal's identity):**

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{0}^{+\infty} \vert \text{CWT}_x(\tau, s) \vert^{2} \frac{ds\, d\tau}{s^{2}} = C_{\psi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^{2} dt
$$

---

## 2.3 Deep Dive: Why Wavelets? The Time-Frequency Tiling Argument

| Transform | Time Resolution $\Delta t$ | Frequency Resolution $\Delta f$ | Time-Frequency Tile |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fourier** | Infinite (whole signal) | $1/T$ (best possible) | Vertical strip |
| **STFT** | Fixed $= \sigma_t(g)$ | Fixed $= \sigma_\omega(g)$ | Equal-area rectangles |
| **CWT** | $\propto s$ (large at large $s$) | $\propto 1/s$ (large at small $s$) | Variable-area rectangles, narrow at small $s$ and tall at large $s$ |

The CWT achieves the Heisenberg lower bound at *every scale* and provides better time resolution for high-frequency transients (small $s$) and better frequency resolution for low-frequency structures (large $s$).

### Admissibility, Regularity, and Vanishing Moments
- **Admissibility:** $\int \psi(t)\, dt = 0$ (zero mean).
- **Regularity:** Smoothness of $\psi(t)$; higher regularity = faster decay of $\hat{\psi}(\omega)$.
- **Vanishing moments of order $p$:**

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} t^{k}\, \psi(t)\, dt = 0, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, p-1
$$

A wavelet with $p$ vanishing moments annihilates any polynomial of degree $< p$. This is why the CWT is blind to polynomial trends and reveals only the "interesting" non-polynomial structure.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Property | Mathematical Form | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **STFT definition** | $X(\tau,\omega) = \int x(t)\,g^{*}(t-\tau)\,e^{-j\omega t}\,dt$ | $\tau$ in **s**, $\omega$ in **rad/s** |
| 2 | **STFT inversion** | $x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi E_g}\int\!\!\int X(\tau,\omega)\,g(t-\tau)\,e^{j\omega t}\,d\omega\,d\tau$ | $E_g = \int \vert g(t) \vert^{2} dt$ |
| 3 | **Bandpass filter in STFT bank** | $h_k(t) = g(t)\,e^{j\omega_k t}$ | $k$-th filter |
| 4 | **Frequency response of $k$-th filter** | $H_k(\omega) = G(\omega - \omega_k)$ | Modulation property |
| 5 | **CWT definition** | $W_x(\tau,s) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{s}}\int x(t)\,\psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-\tau}{s}\right)dt$ | $s > 0$ dimensionless, $\tau$ in **s** |
| 6 | **Inverse CWT** | $x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}}\int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W_x(\tau,s)\,\psi_{\tau,s}(t)\,\frac{d\tau\,ds}{s^{2}}$ | $C_{\psi}$ = admissibility constant |
| 7 | **Admissibility condition** | $C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{\vert\hat{\psi}(\omega)\vert^{2}}{\omega}d\omega < \infty$ | Equivalent to $\hat{\psi}(0)=0$ |
| 8 | **Energy normalization** | $\int \vert \psi_{\tau,s}(t) \vert^{2} dt = \int \vert \psi(t) \vert^{2} dt$ | Energy preserved across scales |
| 9 | **Parseval / Moyal identity** | $\int\!\!\int \vert W_x(\tau,s) \vert^{2}\frac{ds\,d\tau}{s^{2}} = C_{\psi}\int \vert x(t) \vert^{2}dt$ | Energy conservation |
| 10 | **Vanishing moments** | $\int t^{k}\psi(t)\,dt = 0$, $k=0,\ldots,p-1$ | $p$ = order |
| 11 | **Center frequency of dilated wavelet** | $\omega_{c,s} = \omega_{c}/s$ | Inversely proportional to scale |
| 12 | **STFT-CWT resolution relation** | $\Delta t_{\text{CWT}} \propto s\,\Delta t_{\psi}$, $\Delta f_{\text{CWT}} \propto \Delta f_{\psi}/s$ | Adaptive tiling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Notation Warning:** The notation $W_x(\tau,s)$ and $\text{CWT}_x(\tau,s)$ are synonymous in KTU textbooks. Always clarify which one you adopt at the start of an exam answer.

---
