<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
**Define the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$. State the admissibility condition for a mother wavelet.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The CWT of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ with respect to a mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ is defined as:
> $$CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$
> where $a$ is the scale parameter ($a \neq 0$) and $b$ is the translation parameter.
>
> **Admissibility condition:**
> $$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\omega} d\omega < \infty$$
> This condition ensures that the CWT is invertible and the original signal $x(t)$ can be reconstructed from the wavelet coefficients.

*(1 mark for CWT definition, 1 mark for parameters, 1 mark for admissibility condition)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
**Compare the CWT and the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) in terms of resolution and their suitability for non-stationary signals.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> | Aspect | STFT | CWT |
> |---|---|---|
> | Window | Fixed length | Variable (adaptive) length |
> | Resolution | Uniform time-frequency resolution | Multi-resolution: high time resolution at small scales, high frequency resolution at large scales |
> | Parameters | Frequency $\omega$, shift $\tau$ | Scale $a$, shift $b$ |
> | Non-stationary signals | Limited suitability | Highly suitable |
> | Basis function | Sinusoid inside window | Scaled and shifted mother wavelet |
>
> CWT is **preferred for non-stationary signals** because its adaptive windowing allows it to capture both fast transients and slow trends, whereas STFT uses a fixed window that limits its multi-scale capability.

*(1.5 marks for comparison table, 1.5 marks for conclusion)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the admissibility condition for a mother wavelet starting from the inverse CWT formula. Discuss the physical significance of this condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a signal $x(t) = \delta(t - t_0)$ (an impulse located at $t_0$), compute the CWT using an arbitrary mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ satisfying the admissibility condition. Interpret the result geometrically. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the Admissibility Condition (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 — Forward CWT definition (1 mark):**
> $$CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

> **Step 2 — Inverse CWT formula (1 mark):**
> $$x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \iint CWT_x(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}}$$

> **Step 3 — Substitute forward into inverse, swap integration order (1 mark):**
> $$x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \left[ \iint \frac{1}{\vert a \vert}\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{\tau-b}{a}\right) \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}} \right] d\tau$$

> **Step 4 — Recognize the autocorrelation structure (1 mark):**
> The bracketed integral is the **autocorrelation of $\psi$** integrated over $a$ and $b$. For perfect reconstruction, this must equal $C_{\psi} \delta(t-\tau)$.

> **Step 5 — Use Fourier transform / Wiener-Khinchin theorem (1.5 marks):**
> Applying Parseval and the identity $\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\cos(\omega s)}{s} ds = -\ln\vert \omega \vert$, we obtain:
> $$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\omega} d\omega < \infty$$

> **Step 6 — Physical significance (1.5 marks):**
> - Ensures $C_{\psi}$ is finite and positive, so the inverse exists.
> - Requires $\Psi(0) = 0$, i.e., $\int \psi(t) dt = 0$, meaning the mother wavelet has **no DC component** and is therefore **oscillatory** (a "small wave").
> - This is precisely what allows wavelets to detect both amplitude and polarity changes in signals.

---

**(b) CWT of an Impulse $x(t) = \delta(t - t_0)$ (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 — Apply the CWT definition (1 mark):**
> $$CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\tau - t_0)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{\tau-b}{a}\right) d\tau$$

> **Step 2 — Use sifting property of delta (1.5 marks):**
> $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\tau - t_0)\, f(\tau)\, d\tau = f(t_0)$$
> Therefore:
> $$CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t_0 - b}{a}\right)$$

> **Step 3 — Rewrite the result (1.5 marks):**
> $$CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{b - t_0}{a}\right) = \psi_{a,b}^{*}(t_0)$$

> **Step 4 — Analyze the result (1.5 marks):**
> - The CWT of an impulse is **simply a scaled and shifted replica of the mother wavelet** itself.
> - At the impulse location $b = t_0$, $CWT_x(a, t_0) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \psi^{*}(0)$, which is a maximum (assuming $\psi(0) \neq 0$).
> - For $b \neq t_0$, the coefficient decreases as the daughter wavelet is shifted away from the impulse.

> **Step 5 — Geometric interpretation (1.5 marks):**
> The scalogram of an impulse exhibits a **"ridge"** in the $(a,b)$ plane centered at $b = t_0$, with amplitude $\propto 1/\sqrt{\vert a \vert}$. This confirms the **time-localization property** of CWT: an impulse produces a sharp peak exactly at its location, and this is the basis for **transient detection** in engineering.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of multi-resolution analysis in CWT. Derive the relationship between the scale parameter $a$ and the equivalent pseudo-frequency of the CWT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a signal $x(t) = \cos(2\pi f_1 t)$ for $0 < t < T_1$ and $x(t) = \cos(2\pi f_2 t)$ for $T_1 < t < T_1 + T_2$, with $f_1 < f_2$. Discuss qualitatively what the CWT scalogram would look like, and explain how the choice of mother wavelet affects the resolution. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Multi-resolution Analysis and Pseudo-frequency (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 — Concept of Multi-resolution (2 marks):**
> Multi-resolution analysis (MRA) means that the CWT analyzes the signal at **different resolutions** simultaneously:
> - At **small scales** ($a \ll 1$), the daughter wavelet is **compressed in time** — good for detecting fast, high-frequency transients.
> - At **large scales** ($a \gg 1$), the daughter wavelet is **stretched in time** — good for capturing slow, low-frequency trends.
> - This mimics the human auditory system (and visual cortex) which processes information at multiple scales.

> **Step 2 — Time-frequency localization (2 marks):**
> The daughter wavelet $\psi_{a,b}(t)$ has:
> - **Time spread** $\sigma_t \propto \vert a \vert \cdot \sigma_{\psi}$
> - **Frequency spread** $\sigma_f \propto \sigma_{\psi}/\vert a \vert$
> - The product $\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_f = \sigma_{\psi}^{2}$ is **constant** (consistent with the uncertainty principle).

> **Step 3 — Derive pseudo-frequency (3 marks):**
> Let $f_c$ be the **center frequency** of the mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ (i.e., the peak of $\vert \Psi(f) \vert$).
> When we apply the scale $a$, time is dilated by $a$, so frequencies compress by $1/a$:
> $$f_{\text{equiv}} = \frac{f_c}{a}$$
> 
> **Derivation:** A scaled wavelet $\psi(t/a)$ has Fourier transform $a\, \Psi(a\omega)$. If $\Psi(\omega)$ peaks at $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c$, then $a\,\Psi(a\omega)$ peaks at $\omega = \omega_c/a$, giving $f_{\text{peak}} = f_c/a$.

> **Step 4 — Conclusion:** Therefore, small $a$ ↔ high pseudo-frequency, large $a$ ↔ low pseudo-frequency. This is why a logarithmic scale range $\{2^{1}, 2^{2}, \dots, 2^{N}\}$ is commonly used in practice.

---

**(b) CWT of a Two-Tone Signal (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 — Analyze the signal (1.5 marks):**
> The signal is a **piecewise stationary cosine** — a non-stationary signal with two distinct frequency components: $f_1$ (low) in $[0, T_1]$ and $f_2$ (high) in $[T_1, T_1 + T_2]$.

> **Step 2 — Expected scalogram structure (2.5 marks):**
> The CWT scalogram will show **two horizontal ridges** at different vertical positions:
> - A ridge at $a_1 = f_c / f_1$ (low frequency, large scale) for $b \in [0, T_1]$.
> - A ridge at $a_2 = f_c / f_2$ (high frequency, small scale) for $b \in [T_1, T_1 + T_2]$.
> - A transition region near $b = T_1$ where the ridges "switch."

> **Step 3 — Effect of mother wavelet (3 marks):**
> - **Morlet wavelet (complex, narrow band):** Best for sinusoidal components. Produces **sharp ridges** with excellent frequency resolution. Preferred for tonal signals.
> - **Mexican Hat (real, second derivative of Gaussian):** Good for detecting **peaks and valleys**; produces clear local extrema at zero crossings. Less precise in frequency.
> - **Haar (real, discontinuous):** Best for **abrupt discontinuities** and step changes (e.g., the transition at $T_1$). However, its frequency resolution is poor.
> - **General trade-off:** Narrow-band wavelets (Morlet) → better frequency resolution, poorer time resolution. Wide-band wavelets (Haar) → better time resolution, poorer frequency resolution.

> **Conclusion (KTU-style):** The choice of mother wavelet must match the signal's nature. For tone detection, use Morlet; for edge detection, use Haar; for cusp detection, use Mexican Hat.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> 1. **Sign error in delta integration:** Many students forget the **sifting property** and try to integrate the delta directly. Always use $\int f(\tau)\delta(\tau - t_0) d\tau = f(t_0)$.
> 2. **Admissibility condition limits:** Students often write $C_{\psi} < \infty$ without showing the integrand $\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2} / \omega$. The full expression is **mandatory** for full marks.
> 3. **Confusing scale with frequency:** Remember — **small scale $a$ = high frequency** and **large scale $a$ = low frequency**. Several KTU toppers lose 2 marks by writing this backwards.
> 4. **Units of $b$ vs $t$:** $b$ is **time** (in seconds), and $a$ is **dimensionless** (a pure number). Do not assign units to $a$.
> 5. **Morlet admissibility:** The Morlet wavelet is only **approximately** admissible for finite $\omega_0$. Always add a small correction term $e^{-\omega_0^{2}/2}$ for strict admissibility — this is a common interview/follow-up question.
> 6. **CWT vs DWT:** KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is the CWT not used for compression?"* Answer: **redundancy** — continuous $a, b$ generate infinite coefficients per signal point.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (CWT for Signal Analysis)**

- **CWT Definition:** $CWT_x(a,b) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int x(t) \psi^{*}\!\left(\dfrac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$
- **Parameters:** $a$ = scale (dimensionless), $b$ = translation (seconds).
- **Admissibility:** $C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\omega} d\omega < \infty$
- **Zero mean** of $\psi$ is a **necessary consequence** of admissibility.
- **Inverse CWT:** $x(t) = \dfrac{1}{C_{\psi}} \iint CWT_x(a,b) \psi_{a,b}(t) \dfrac{da\, db}{a^{2}}$
- **Pseudo-frequency:** $f_{\text{equiv}} = f_c / a$ — small $a$ = high $f$, large $a$ = low $f$.
- **Multi-resolution property:** Adaptive $\Delta t$ and $\Delta f$ — Heisenberg principle still holds.
- **STFT vs CWT:** STFT = fixed window, uniform resolution. CWT = adaptive window, variable resolution.
- **Redundancy:** CWT is **highly redundant** because $a, b$ are continuous.
- **Energy preservation:** $\int \vert x(t) \vert^{2} dt = \dfrac{1}{C_{\psi}} \iint \vert CWT_x(a,b) \vert^{2} \dfrac{da\, db}{a^{2}}$
- **Properties:** Linearity, translation invariance, scaling covariance.
- **Common wavelets:** Morlet (complex tonal), Mexican Hat (real cusp), Haar (real step), Daubechies (real, compact support).
- **Use cases:** ECG, EEG, speech, seismic, chirp detection, denoising, edge detection.
- **CWT of impulse** $\delta(t-t_0)$ gives $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \psi^{*}\!\left(\dfrac{t_0 - b}{a}\right)$ — proves time-localization.
- **CWT of sinusoid** $\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ produces a **ridge** in the scalogram at scale $a = f_c / f_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw the **scalogram** when explaining CWT in long-answer questions. Examiners reward visualizations generously. Also, always quote the admissibility condition **with the $\omega$ in the denominator** — leaving it out loses 1 full mark.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Condition of Admissibility and Its Implications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Condition of Admissibility and Its Implications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Admissibility Condition

In wavelet analysis, a function $\psi(t) \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$ is called an **admissible wavelet** (or mother wavelet) if and only if it satisfies the **Admissibility Condition**, mathematically expressed as:

$$C_\psi = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\vert \omega \vert} \, d\omega \;<\; \infty$$

where $\Psi(\omega)$ denotes the **Fourier Transform** of the candidate function $\psi(t)$, defined by:

$$\Psi(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

The finite positive constant $C_\psi$ is called the **Admissibility Constant** of the wavelet $\psi(t)$. It is a scalar that depends purely on the shape of the wavelet and is independent of the input signal being analyzed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The admissibility condition is *not* an optional property — it is the **mathematical gatekeeper** that determines whether a function can be legitimately used as a mother wavelet for the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) and its inverse.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Balanced Tuning Fork" Idea

Imagine you are holding a **tuning fork** that you strike and then slowly move away from your ear. For the sound to "cancel itself out" perfectly when integrated over all time (which is what perfect reconstruction demands), the fork must:

1. **Oscillate equally above and below** its resting position (i.e., its average value over all time must be zero).
2. **Decay smoothly to zero** as time progresses, so that its total energy is finite and concentrated around a specific frequency band.

The admissibility condition is the *mathematical fingerprint* of these two physical requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** A wavelet is "admissible" if its frequency spectrum does not contain a DC (zero-frequency) component, AND it dies out quickly enough at the tails. Both of these together guarantee that the wavelet can be used to perfectly reconstruct any signal it analyzes.

## 1.3 The Two Implicit Requirements Hidden Inside One Equation

Although the admissibility condition is a single integral inequality, it **encodes two simultaneous physical constraints**:

- **Constraint A — Zero Mean (DC-Free Spectrum):** The numerator $\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2$ forces $\Psi(0) = 0$, meaning the wavelet has no constant component.
- **Constraint B — Finite Energy Localization:** The denominator $\vert \omega \vert$ combined with square-integrability forces the wavelet's spectrum to decay fast enough so that the integral converges.

Both must hold **jointly** — meeting only one is insufficient.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

The admissibility condition can be broken down into a clear sequence of logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Start with the CWT Inversion Formula:**
The continuous wavelet transform $W_f(a, b)$ of a signal $f(t)$ is defined as:

$$W_f(a, b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t) \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

Its inverse (reconstruction formula) is:

$$f(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{0}^{\infty} W_f(a, b) \, \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) \frac{da \, db}{a^2}$$

- **Step 2 — Demanding Well-Posedness of Inversion:**
For the inverse to exist for *every* finite-energy signal $f(t)$, the constant $C_\psi$ in the denominator must be **strictly positive and finite**. This necessity gives birth to the admissibility inequality.

- **Step 3 — The Zero-Mean Consequence:**
Since the integrand has $\vert \omega \vert$ in the denominator, near $\omega = 0$ the expression behaves like $\frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\vert \omega \vert}$. For this to be integrable at the origin, $\Psi(\omega)$ must vanish *faster* than $\sqrt{\vert \omega \vert}$. The simplest (and universally required) outcome is:

$$\Psi(0) = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t) \, dt = 0$$

- **Step 4 — Finite Energy (Square Integrability):**
$\psi(t)$ must belong to $L^2(\mathbb{R})$, meaning:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \psi(t) \vert^2 \, dt \;<\; \infty$$

This is the standard "admissible energy" requirement, which ensures the wavelet is localized in both time and frequency (uncertainty principle compatible).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|--------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Admissibility Condition | $C_\psi = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\vert \omega \vert} d\omega < \infty$ | Gatekeeper for valid wavelets |
| 2 | Admissibility Constant | $C_\psi > 0$ (finite) | Normalization for inverse CWT |
| 3 | Zero Mean Condition | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t) \, dt = 0$ | Wavelet must oscillate (no DC) |
| 4 | Fourier Domain Equivalent | $\Psi(0) = 0$ | Spectral null at zero frequency |
| 5 | Finite Energy | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \psi(t) \vert^2 dt < \infty$ | Time-domain localization |
| 6 | CWT Inversion Formula | $f(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \iint W_f(a,b) \, \psi_{a,b}(t) \frac{da\,db}{a^2}$ | Requires admissibility |
| 7 | Parseval's Identity Tie-In | $C_\psi = 2\pi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\vert \omega \vert} d\omega$ | Frequency-domain reconstruction weight |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Note:** In the KTU valuation key, students are penalized for writing $\Psi(\omega = 0) = 0$ instead of the strict integral form $\int \psi(t) dt = 0$. **Always state both forms** in your answer to secure full marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The admissibility condition is not a purely abstract mathematical curiosity. Its implications are felt directly in:

- **Biomedical Signal Processing (ECG / EEG):** Admissible wavelets like the Morlet wavelet are used because their zero-mean nature cancels out slow DC drift in electrode recordings.
- **Seismology:** The admissibility condition guarantees that transient seismic events can be perfectly reconstructed from their wavelet scalograms.
- **Image Compression (JPEG 2000):** The Cohen–Daubechies–Feauveau (CDF) 9/7 wavelet used in lossy compression satisfies admissibility up to numerical tolerance, ensuring invertibility of the discrete wavelet transform pipeline.
- **Fault Detection in Rotating Machinery:** Vibration bursts are analyzed using admissible wavelets so that the mean of the wavelet acts as a high-pass filter, removing steady-state machine noise.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proving $\Psi(0) = 0$ from the Admissibility Condition

We start from the admissibility condition:

$$C_\psi = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\vert \omega \vert} d\omega < \infty$$

**Step 1:** Consider the behavior of the integrand as $\omega \to 0$.

As $\omega \to 0$, the denominator $\vert \omega \vert$ tends to $0$. For the integral to remain finite (not blow up to $\infty$), the numerator $\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2$ must vanish **at least as fast** as $\vert \omega \vert$ near the origin.

**Step 2:** Take the limit $\omega \to 0$ of the integrand.

$$\lim_{\omega \to 0} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\vert \omega \vert} < \infty$$

This requires:

$$\lim_{\omega \to 0} \vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2 = 0$$

**Step 3:** Take the square root of both sides.

Since $\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2$ is non-negative, we conclude:

$$\lim_{\omega \to 0} \vert \Psi(\omega) \vert = 0$$

Equivalently:

$$\Psi(0) = 0$$

**Step 4:** Translate back to the time domain.

By the inverse Fourier transform evaluated at $\omega = 0$:

$$\Psi(0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t) \, e^{-j(0)t} dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t) \, dt$$

Therefore:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t) \, dt = 0$$

This is the **zero-mean condition** — a necessary consequence of the admissibility condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The key insight is that the $\frac{1}{\vert \omega \vert}$ factor in the admissibility integrand acts like a mathematical "spotlight" that highlights the low-frequency region. Any non-zero DC component would cause the integral to diverge to infinity, violating the condition.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Origin of $C_\psi$ from the CWT Inversion Formula

We start with the standard **reconstruction (synthesis) equation** for the continuous wavelet transform:

$$f(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_{0}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W_f(a, b) \, \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) \frac{da \, db}{a^2}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the definition of $W_f(a, b)$ into the right-hand side.

$$f(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_{0}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(\tau) \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{\tau - b}{a}\right) d\tau \right] \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) \frac{da \, db}{a^2}$$

**Step 2:** Combine the inner $a$-dependent prefactors.

The product of the two $\frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}$ terms yields $\frac{1}{a}$, and combined with the outer $\frac{1}{a^2}$:

$$f(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_{0}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(\tau) \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{\tau - b}{a}\right) \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) \, d\tau \, \frac{db \, da}{a^3}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the change of variables $b' = \frac{\tau - b}{a}$ (and consequently $db = -a \, db'$, but the bounds handle the sign).

After substitution, the inner $b$-integral becomes a convolution in $b'$. The remaining integral collapses into:

$$f(t) = \frac{f(t)}{C_\psi} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a^2} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi^{*}(u) \, \psi(u) \, a \, du \right] da$$

Wait — this naive cancellation is what *requires* the rigorous derivation using Parseval's theorem. Let us redo this properly.

**Step 4 — Rigorous Route via Parseval's Theorem:**

Switch to the frequency domain using Parseval's identity:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(\tau) \, g^{*}(\tau) \, d\tau = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F(\omega) \, G^{*}(\omega) \, d\omega$$

The inner double integral in $b$ and $\tau$ becomes, after Fourier manipulation, a single integral over $\omega$:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{\tau - b}{a}\right) \psi\!\left(\frac{t - b}{a}\right) db = a \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \Psi(a\omega) \vert^2 e^{j\omega(t-\tau)} d\omega$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back and integrate over $a$.

$$f(t) = \frac{f(t)}{C_\psi} \int_{0}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \Psi(a\omega) \vert^2 e^{j\omega(t-\tau)} d\omega \, \frac{da}{a}$$

**Step 6:** Change variable $\xi = a\omega$, so $d\xi = \omega \, da$ (treating $\omega$ as fixed for the inner integral):

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(a\omega) \vert^2}{a} da = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\xi) \vert^2}{\xi} d\xi$$

**Step 7:** This integral is exactly the admissibility condition (summing the positive and negative halves):

$$C_\psi = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\vert \omega \vert} d\omega = 2 \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\omega} d\omega$$

**Step 8:** Equating the prefactors for the identity $f(t) = f(t)$ to hold.

$$\frac{C_\psi}{C_\psi} f(t) = f(t) \quad \checkmark$$

This proves that the admissibility condition is **both necessary and sufficient** for the CWT inversion formula to be well-defined for all $f(t) \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Admissibility of the Mexican Hat Wavelet

The Mexican Hat (Ricker) wavelet is defined as:

$$\psi(t) = (1 - t^2) \, e^{-t^2/2}$$

**Step 1:** Compute the Fourier transform.

$$\Psi(\omega) = \sqrt{2\pi} \, \omega^2 \, e^{-\omega^2/2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the admissibility integral.

$$C_\psi = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \sqrt{2\pi} \, \omega^2 \, e^{-\omega^2/2} \vert^2}{\vert \omega \vert} d\omega = 2\pi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\omega^4 \, e^{-\omega^2}}{\vert \omega \vert} d\omega$$

**Step 3:** Simplify using the even symmetry of the integrand.

$$C_\psi = 2\pi \cdot 2 \int_{0}^{\infty} \omega^3 \, e^{-\omega^2} d\omega = 4\pi \int_{0}^{\infty} \omega^3 \, e^{-\omega^2} d\omega$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate using the substitution $u = \omega^2$, $du = 2\omega \, d\omega$.

$$C_\psi = 4\pi \cdot \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\infty} u \, e^{-u} du = 2\pi \cdot \Gamma(2) = 2\pi \cdot 1! = 2\pi$$

**Step 5:** Conclude.

$$C_\psi = 2\pi \;\;<\;\; \infty \quad \checkmark$$

The Mexican Hat wavelet satisfies the admissibility condition with $C_\psi = 2\pi$, and is therefore a valid mother wavelet.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Logical Implications of the Admissibility Condition

The following diagram traces the cascade of consequences that flow from the single admissibility inequality:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Admissibility Condition\n C_psi = integral of |Psi(omega)|^2 / |omega| d(omega) less than infinity"]
    A --> B["Integral converges at omega = 0"]
    A --> C["Integral converges at omega = plus/minus infinity"]
    B --> D["Forces Psi(0) = 0"]
    D --> E["Equivalent to integral of psi(t) dt = 0"]
    E --> F["Zero Mean Condition\n Wavelet must oscillate"]
    C --> G["Forces |Psi(omega)|^2 to decay faster than |omega|"]
    G --> H["Finite Energy in L^2 sense"]
    H --> I["Time-Frequency Localization"]
    F --> J["Joint Consequence\n Valid Mother Wavelet"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["Invertibility of CWT\n Reconstruction Formula Holds"]
    K --> L["Application:\n Signal and Image Analysis"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — CWT Analysis–Synthesis Pipeline (Admissibility-Gated)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Analysis["ANALYSIS STAGE"]
        S["Input Signal f(t)"] --> CWT["CWT Module\n W_f(a,b) = inner product with psi"]
        CWT --> SC["Scalogram\n Time-Frequency Map"]
    end

    subgraph Gate["ADMISSIBILITY GATE"]
        ADM["Check: C_psi less than infinity\n and greater than 0"]
        ADM --> DEC1{"Satisfied?"}
        DEC1 -- Yes --> PROCEED["Proceed to Synthesis"]
        DEC1 -- No  --> REJECT["Reject Function\n Not a Valid Wavelet"]
    end

    SC --> ADM

    subgraph Synthesis["SYNTHESIS STAGE"]
        PROCEED --> INV["Inverse CWT\n Division by C_psi"]
        INV --> RECON["Reconstructed f-hat(t)"]
        RECON --> ERR["Compare with f(t)\n Error Norm"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Verifying Admissibility of a Candidate Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Candidate Function psi(t)"] --> Q1{"Square Integrable?\n integral of |psi(t)|^2 dt less than infinity"}
    Q1 -- No --> R1["Reject:\n Not in L^2 of R"]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2["Compute Fourier Transform Psi(omega)"]
    Q2 --> Q3["Evaluate I = integral of |Psi(omega)|^2 / |omega| d(omega)"]
    Q3 --> Q4{"Is I finite and positive?"}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5["ACCEPT:\n Admissible Mother Wavelet\n C_psi = I"]
    Q4 -- No  --> R2["Reject:\n Admissibility Violated"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU:** The KTU valuation key frequently awards 2 marks for a "neatly labelled block diagram of the wavelet analysis–synthesis system" when admissibility-based questions appear. These flowcharts give you a ready-made, exam-hall-reproducible visual.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State and explain the admissibility condition for a function to be a mother wavelet. Why is the condition $C_\psi < \infty$ essential for the inverse CWT to exist?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The admissibility condition states that a function $\psi(t) \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$ is a valid mother wavelet if and only if:

$$C_\psi = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\vert \omega \vert} d\omega \;<\; \infty$$

where $\Psi(\omega)$ is the Fourier transform of $\psi(t)$. **(1 mark)**

The condition has two implicit consequences:
- $\Psi(0) = 0$, i.e., $\int \psi(t) dt = 0$ (zero-mean property). **(1 mark)**
- $\psi(t)$ must have finite energy (square-integrable), ensuring time-frequency localization. **(0.5 mark)**

The finiteness of $C_\psi$ is essential because the inverse CWT formula contains $\frac{1}{C_\psi}$ as a prefactor. If $C_\psi = 0$ or $C_\psi = \infty$, the reconstruction becomes undefined and no finite-energy signal can be recovered from its wavelet coefficients. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Prove that the admissibility condition implies that the mother wavelet must have a zero mean. Show the key steps.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Starting from:

$$C_\psi = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\vert \omega \vert} d\omega < \infty \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

We examine the integrand as $\omega \to 0$. The denominator $\vert \omega \vert \to 0$ but the integral must remain finite, so the numerator $\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2$ must also vanish. This forces:

$$\Psi(0) = 0 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

By the inverse Fourier transform evaluated at $\omega = 0$:

$$\Psi(0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t) \, dt \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

Hence:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t) \, dt = 0$$

This is the zero-mean property — a necessary condition for $\psi(t)$ to be admissible. **(0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**(a)** Derive the admissibility condition starting from the inverse continuous wavelet transform formula. Show clearly why $C_\psi$ must be finite and positive. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the **Morlet wavelet** defined (in its approximate form) as $\psi(t) = e^{j\omega_0 t} \, e^{-t^2/2}$, compute the admissibility constant $C_\psi$ and verify admissibility. Assume $\omega_0 = 5$ to keep the wavelet admissible. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

Start with the CWT pair:

$$W_f(a, b) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t) \, \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

and the reconstruction formula:

$$f(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_{0}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W_f(a, b) \, \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) \frac{da \, db}{a^2} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

Substituting $W_f$ into the right side and using Parseval's theorem to convert the inner product into the frequency domain:

$$f(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_{0}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F(\omega) \, \vert \Psi(a\omega) \vert^2 \, e^{j\omega t} \, \frac{d\omega \, da}{a} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

Apply the change of variable $\xi = a\omega$, so $d\xi = \omega \, da$:

$$f(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F(\omega) \, e^{j\omega t} \left[ \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\xi) \vert^2}{\xi} d\xi \right] d\omega \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

For the identity $f(t) = f(t)$ to hold, we require:

$$\frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\xi) \vert^2}{\xi} d\xi = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2\pi = 1$$

leading to:

$$C_\psi = 2 \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\omega} d\omega = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\vert \omega \vert} d\omega \quad \textbf{(1.5 mark)}$$

For the inverse to exist for all $f \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$, $C_\psi$ must satisfy $0 < C_\psi < \infty$. This is the admissibility condition. **(0.5 mark)**

#### Part (b) — Morlet Wavelet Computation (7 marks)

The Morlet wavelet is:

$$\psi(t) = e^{j\omega_0 t} \, e^{-t^2/2}$$

Its Fourier transform is:

$$\Psi(\omega) = \sqrt{2\pi} \, e^{-(\omega - \omega_0)^2 / 2} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

Substitute into the admissibility integral:

$$C_\psi = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{2\pi \, e^{-(\omega - \omega_0)^2}}{\vert \omega \vert} d\omega \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

For $\omega_0 = 5$, the Gaussian is centered at $\omega = 5$ and is essentially zero near $\omega = 0$. We approximate the integrand as not having any singularity. **Numerical integration (or analytical approximation using the centroid at $\omega = 5$):**

$$C_\psi \approx 2\pi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-(\omega - 5)^2}}{5} d\omega = \frac{2\pi}{5} \cdot \sqrt{\pi} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

Wait — correcting the calculation. Using $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-(\omega - \omega_0)^2} d\omega = \sqrt{\pi}$ and approximating $\vert \omega \vert \approx \omega_0 = 5$ within the Gaussian's effective support:

$$C_\psi \approx \frac{2\pi \sqrt{\pi}}{5} \approx \frac{2 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 1.7725}{5} \approx \frac{11.137}{5} \approx 2.227 \quad \textbf{(1.5 mark)}$$

Since $0 < 2.227 < \infty$, the Morlet wavelet is **admissible** with $C_\psi \approx 2.227$. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common error is forgetting that for the **exact** Morlet wavelet (with $\omega_0 = 0$), the function $\psi(t) = e^{-t^2/2}$ is NOT admissible because $\Psi(0) \neq 0$. The correction term $-\sqrt{2} \, e^{-\omega_0^2/2} \, \delta(\omega)$ must be subtracted, and $\omega_0 \geq 5$ is required to make the DC component negligible. Students who claim $C_\psi = \infty$ for any Morlet wavelet will lose **2 marks**.

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the role of the admissibility condition in the continuous wavelet transform analysis–synthesis system. Discuss what happens when $C_\psi \to 0$ and when $C_\psi \to \infty$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the **Haar wavelet** defined as:

$$\psi(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & 0 \leq t < 1/2 \\ -1, & 1/2 \leq t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

show that it satisfies the admissibility condition and compute $C_\psi$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Limiting Cases (7 marks)

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The CWT analysis–synthesis system consists of:

1. **Analysis stage:** Input $f(t)$ is correlated with scaled and translated versions $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$, producing the scalogram $W_f(a, b)$.
2. **Admissibility gate:** Before synthesis, the system verifies that the chosen $\psi(t)$ yields a finite, positive $C_\psi$.
3. **Synthesis stage:** The scalogram is re-weighted by $\frac{1}{C_\psi}$ and re-integrated using the inverse CWT formula to obtain $\hat{f}(t)$.

The admissibility condition is the **bridge** between the analysis scalogram and the synthesis reconstruction. Without it, the scalogram is "blind" — the coefficients cannot be unambiguously inverted back to a unique signal. **(1 mark)**

**Limiting case $C_\psi \to 0$ (1.5 marks):**
- The reconstruction formula $\hat{f}(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \iint \ldots$ **blows up** — the recovered signal amplitude becomes unbounded.
- This corresponds to wavelets whose spectrum has too much energy at low frequencies (e.g., a constant function, which has $\Psi(0) \neq 0$).

**Limiting case $C_\psi \to \infty$ (1.5 marks):**
- The reconstruction formula **vanishes** — the recovered signal becomes identically zero.
- This corresponds to wavelets with negligible energy in the passband, e.g., $\psi(t)$ supported only at $\omega = 0$ (a constant), which carries no oscillatory information.

#### Part (b) — Haar Wavelet Admissibility Verification (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Compute the Fourier transform of the Haar wavelet.

$$\Psi(\omega) = \int_{0}^{1/2} e^{-j\omega t} dt - \int_{1/2}^{1} e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

Evaluating:

$$\Psi(\omega) = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega/2}}{j\omega} - \frac{e^{-j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega}}{j\omega} = \frac{1 - 2e^{-j\omega/2} + e^{-j\omega}}{j\omega} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

Using the factorization $1 - 2e^{-j\omega/2} + e^{-j\omega} = (1 - e^{-j\omega/2})^2$:

$$\Psi(\omega) = \frac{(1 - e^{-j\omega/2})^2}{j\omega} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the magnitude squared.

$$\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{\vert 1 - e^{-j\omega/2} \vert^4}{\omega^2} = \frac{[2\sin(\omega/4)]^4}{\omega^2} = \frac{16 \sin^4(\omega/4)}{\omega^2} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the admissibility integral.

$$C_\psi = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{16 \sin^4(\omega/4)}{\omega^2 \cdot \vert \omega \vert} d\omega = 16 \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\sin^4(\omega/4)}{\vert \omega \vert^3} d\omega \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4:** Conclude convergence.

For large $\vert \omega \vert$, $\sin^4(\omega/4) \leq 1$, so the integrand decays as $\frac{1}{\vert \omega \vert^3}$, which is integrable. Near $\omega = 0$, $\sin^4(\omega/4) \sim (\omega/4)^4$, so the integrand behaves like $\frac{\omega^4}{\omega^3} = \omega \to 0$. Therefore the integral converges to a finite positive value. **(1 mark)**

The Haar wavelet is admissible. (Computing the exact numerical value of $C_\psi$ is non-trivial and is typically left as a lookup constant; the important conclusion is the **convergence** of the integral.) **(0.5 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Haar Wavelet):** Students frequently forget to verify the **zero-mean condition** explicitly. The Haar wavelet satisfies $\int_{0}^{1} \psi(t) dt = (+1)(1/2) + (-1)(1/2) = 0$, which **must** be stated to secure full marks. Also, the Haar wavelet is **discontinuous**, so any step claiming "smooth Gaussian decay" will be marked wrong — the convergence must be argued using asymptotic boundedness of sine.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Condition of Admissibility**

- **Core Equation to Memorize:**
$$C_\psi = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\vert \omega \vert} d\omega \;<\; \infty$$

- **Two Mandatory Implications (always state both):**
  - $\Psi(0) = 0$ — spectral null at DC.
  - $\int \psi(t) dt = 0$ — zero mean in time domain.

- **Square Integrability is a prerequisite:** $\psi(t) \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$, i.e., $\int \vert \psi(t) \vert^2 dt < \infty$.

- **Why $C_\psi$ must be STRICTLY positive (not zero):** If $C_\psi = 0$, the inverse CWT diverges. The condition is an *inequality* in both directions: $0 < C_\psi < \infty$.

- **Connection to Inversion:** The admissibility condition is the *mathematical justification* that the CWT is **invertible** (lossless analysis–synthesis).

- **Common Admissible Wavelets to Remember:**
  - **Morlet** (approximate, $\omega_0 \geq 5$): $C_\psi \approx 2\pi$
  - **Mexican Hat (Ricker):** $C_\psi = 2\pi$ (exact)
  - **Haar:** Admissible, with $C_\psi$ being a finite positive constant (no closed-form required for KTU)
  - **Daubechies family (db-N):** All admissible by construction
  - **Symlet, Coiflet families:** Admissible by construction

- **Frequent Mistake to Avoid:** Confusing the admissibility condition with the **regularity condition** or the **vanishing moments condition**. They are related but distinct:
  - Admissibility → necessary for CWT invertibility.
  - Vanishing moments → useful for sparse representation of polynomials.
  - Regularity → smoothness of $\psi(t)$ for stable reconstruction.

- **Valuation Tip:** In KTU exams, **always** present the admissibility condition in **both** the frequency-domain integral form AND the time-domain zero-mean form. Examiners are instructed to award 1 mark specifically for the dual-form presentation.

- **Numerical Sanity Check (Sanity Test):** If a candidate $\psi(t)$ has a non-zero average value, it is **immediately disqualified** — no need to evaluate the full integral.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Inverse Continuous Wavelet Transform (ICWT)** is the mathematical reconstruction operator that synthesizes a continuous-time signal $f(t)$ from its time-scale (time-frequency) representation $W_f(a,b)$ produced by the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT). Whereas the CWT performs an *analysis* (decomposition) of the signal into translated and dilated versions of a mother wavelet $\psi(t)$, the ICWT performs the corresponding *synthesis* (reconstruction), provided the mother wavelet satisfies the **admissibility condition**.

The reconstruction formula is given by:

$$f(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{0}^{\infty} W_f(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}}$$

where:
- $W_f(a,b) = \langle f, \psi_{a,b} \rangle$ are the wavelet coefficients.
- $C_{\psi}$ is the **admissibility constant** of the mother wavelet.
- $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$ is the scaled and translated daughter wavelet.
- $a \in \mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}$ is the **scale parameter**, and $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **translation parameter**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The ICWT is the dual of the CWT. For an *admissible* wavelet (i.e., $C_{\psi} < \infty$), the reconstruction is exact. For a *frame* wavelet, the reconstruction is stable but requires a *frame operator* — a nuance frequently asked in KTU 2024 Scheme module tests.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Acoustic Prism"

Imagine you are standing in a concert hall with a **prism that splits sound into its frequency components** (this is the *CWT* — it breaks the music into wavelets of different pitches at different times). The **Inverse Continuous Wavelet Transform** is like a **reverse prism** placed at the exit slit: it takes all those time-localized frequency pieces and reassembles them, weighted by their intensities, back into the original sound wave reaching your ear.

The "reverse prism" works only if the prism glass (the mother wavelet $\psi(t)$) is *physically valid* — meaning it has a zero DC component ($\Psi(0) = 0$) and finite energy. In wavelet terminology, this is the **admissibility condition**, mathematically expressed as $C_{\psi} < \infty$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (in bold):**
> - **Admissibility constant** ($C_{\psi}$): a positive, finite scalar that depends solely on the chosen mother wavelet $\psi(t)$. For the **Morlet wavelet**, $C_{\psi} \approx 0.868$ (after $\pi^{-1/4}$ normalization). For **Haar wavelet**, $C_{\psi} = 1$ (after orthonormal normalization). 
> - **Reconstruction measure**: the integration is performed over the **upper half-plane** $a > 0$ (positive scales only), with the measure $da\, db / a^{2}$, which is **scale-invariant** (often called the *natural measure* on the affine group).

## 1.3 Why Does the ICWT Exist? The Necessity of Inversion

The CWT maps a 1-D signal $f(t)$ into a 2-D function $W_f(a,b)$ — this is a **redundant representation** (since $(a,b) \in \mathbb{R}^{+} \times \mathbb{R}$ is a 2-D manifold while $f$ lives on $\mathbb{R}$). Because of redundancy, the ICWT is **not unique** in general. However, for *admissible* wavelets, there exists a **canonical (preferred) inverse** given by the formula above, which minimizes reconstruction error in the $L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$ sense.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The ICWT operates through the following structured pipeline:

- **Step 1 — Coefficient Availability:** Begin with the precomputed CWT map $W_f(a,b)$ over the half-plane $a > 0$, $b \in \mathbb{R}$. This is the input to the inverse.

- **Step 2 — Daughter Wavelet Generation:** For every $(a,b)$ pair, form the daughter wavelet $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$. This re-creates the time-domain atom corresponding to scale $a$ centered at time $b$.

- **Step 3 — Weighted Summation (Integration):** Multiply each daughter wavelet by its corresponding coefficient $W_f(a,b)$ and integrate over the entire time-scale plane. The integration uses the measure $da\, db / a^{2}$, which compensates for the fact that scales appear on a *logarithmic* axis (a uniform $da$ overemphasizes coarse scales).

- **Step 4 — Normalization by Admissibility Constant:** Divide the entire double integral by $C_{\psi}$ to compensate for the energy lost during the analysis stage. Without this normalization, the reconstructed signal would be scaled incorrectly.

- **Step 5 — Convergence Check:** Verify that the reconstructed $\hat{f}(t)$ satisfies the $L^{2}$ Parseval-type identity (Moyal's theorem) to confirm exact recovery in the energy sense.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the $da\, db / a^{2}$ Measure

The factor $1/a^{2}$ (not $1/a$, not $1$) arises from the *Haar measure* on the **affine group** $G = \mathbb{R}^{+} \times \mathbb{R}$ (the group of dilations and translations acting on $\mathbb{R}$). For the affine group, the left-invariant measure is $d\mu(a,b) = \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}}$. The CWT is essentially the *matrix coefficients of the regular representation* of this group, and group theory dictates that inversion must use the same invariant measure — hence the $1/a^{2}$ factor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing the ICWT as $f(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int\int W_f(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, da\, db$ (missing the $1/a^{2}$). This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula** | **Notes / Units** |
|:-----:|:-----------------------|:------------|:------------------|
| 1 | Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) | $W_f(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$ | Inner product form |
| 2 | Daughter Wavelet | $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$ | $a \in \mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}$, $b \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 3 | **Admissibility Condition** | $C_{\psi} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\vert \omega \vert}\, d\omega < \infty$ | Requires $\Psi(0) = 0$ |
| 4 | **Inverse CWT (ICWT)** | $f(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{0}^{\infty} W_f(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}}$ | Synthesis formula |
| 5 | Equivalent ICWT (integral form) | $f(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W_f(a,b)\, \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) db \right] \frac{da}{a^{3/2}}$ | Alternative form |
| 6 | **Energy Preservation (Moyal)** | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert f(t) \vert^{2} dt = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert W_f(a,b) \vert^{2}\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}}$ | Parseval for CWT |
| 7 | Moyal's Theorem (cross-version) | $\int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W_f(a,b)\, W_{g}^{*}(a,b)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}} = C_{\psi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\, g^{*}(t)\, dt$ | Generalizes #6 |
| 8 | Inner-product form (frame) | $f(t) = \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \langle f, \psi_{a,b} \rangle\, S^{-1}\psi_{a,b}(t)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}}$ | $S$ = frame operator |

> **Reminder on Table Notation:** In all formulas above, the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value (modulus), rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ICWT is the operational backbone in the following domains:

- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** Reconstructing ECG/EEG signals after denoising in the wavelet domain (removing high-frequency muscle artifacts).
- **Seismic Analysis:** Recovering subsurface velocity profiles from time-scale seismograms.
- **Audio Coding:** Sub-band coding (e.g., MP3, AAC) relies on perfect-reconstruction filter banks, which are discrete analogues of the ICWT.
- **Image Super-Resolution:** Multi-resolution fusion in satellite imaging depends on the ICWT's ability to combine detail and approximation coefficients without loss.
- **Communication Systems:** OFDM receivers use inverse transforms structurally similar to the ICWT for symbol recovery.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The ICWT Formula from First Principles

We derive the inverse formula starting from the definition of $W_f(a,b)$ as an inner product.

**Starting Point:** The CWT is defined as

$$W_f(a,b) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\, \psi_{a,b}^{*}(t)\, dt$$

**Goal:** Recover $f(t)$ from the 2-D map $W_f(a,b)$.

### Step 1: Express the ICWT Candidate

We *hypothesize* that the reconstruction takes the form of a double integral against daughter wavelets:

$$f(t) = \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W_f(a,b)\, \mathcal{K}(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, da\, db$$

where $\mathcal{K}(a,b)$ is an unknown kernel to be determined (in the standard formula, $\mathcal{K}(a,b) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}\, a^{2}}$).

### Step 2: Substitute the CWT Definition

Substituting the inner product expression for $W_f(a,b)$:

$$f(t) = \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(\tau)\, \psi_{a,b}^{*}(\tau)\, d\tau \right] \mathcal{K}(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, da\, db$$

### Step 3: Swap the Order of Integration

By Fubini's theorem (assuming $f \in L^{2}$ and $\psi$ admissible):

$$f(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(\tau) \left[ \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi_{a,b}^{*}(\tau)\, \mathcal{K}(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, da\, db \right] d\tau$$

### Step 4: Demand Reproducing-Kernel Behavior

For exact recovery, the bracketed kernel must equal $\delta(t - \tau)$ (the Dirac delta), so that the entire integral reduces to $f(t)$. This gives us the **reproducing kernel equation**:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mathcal{K}(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, \psi_{a,b}^{*}(\tau)\, da\, db = \delta(t-\tau)$$

### Step 5: Insert Explicit Form of $\psi_{a,b}$

Using $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$ and assuming $a > 0$:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mathcal{K}(a,b)\, \frac{1}{a}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{\tau-b}{a}\right) da\, db = \delta(t-\tau)$$

### Step 6: Change of Variables in $b$

Let $u = (t-b)/a$, so $b = t - au$ and $db = -a\, du$. The inner integral becomes:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\mathcal{K}(a,\, t-au)}{a} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(u)\, \psi^{*}(u + \tfrac{\tau-t}{a})\, du \right] da = \delta(t-\tau)$$

### Step 7: Recognize the Cross-Correlation in $u$

The inner integral is the *cross-correlation* $R_{\psi\psi}(\Delta)$ evaluated at $\Delta = (\tau-t)/a$. By the **Wiener–Khinchin theorem**, this equals the inverse Fourier transform of $\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}$:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(u)\, \psi^{*}(u+\Delta)\, du = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}\, e^{j\omega\Delta}\, d\omega$$

### Step 8: Change Variable to $\omega$

Setting $\omega = \xi/a$ (so $d\omega = -d\xi/a$):

$$\delta(t-\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \Psi(\xi) \vert^{2} \left[ \int_{0}^{\infty} \mathcal{K}(a,\, t-au)\, a \cdot e^{j\xi(\tau-t)/a}\, \frac{da}{a^{2}} \right] d\xi$$

### Step 9: Simplify and Identify the Kernel

The $a$-dependent inner integral must equal $\delta(\xi)$ *up to constants*, which yields:

$$\mathcal{K}(a,b) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}\, a^{2}}$$

where $C_{\psi}$ is the **admissibility constant**:

$$C_{\psi} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\vert \omega \vert}\, d\omega$$

### Step 10: Final ICWT Formula

Substituting $\mathcal{K}(a,b)$ back:

$$\boxed{\, f(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W_f(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}} \,}$$

This is the canonical **Inverse Continuous Wavelet Transform**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Moyal's Theorem (Energy Preservation)

**Claim:** For $f, g \in L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$,

$$\int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W_f(a,b)\, W_{g}^{*}(a,b)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}} = C_{\psi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\, g^{*}(t)\, dt$$

### Step 1: Expand the LHS

Substituting $W_f(a,b) = \int f(t)\, \psi_{a,b}^{*}(t)\, dt$:

$$\text{LHS} = \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int f(t)\, \psi_{a,b}^{*}(t)\, dt \right] \left[ \int g^{*}(\tau)\, \psi_{a,b}(\tau)\, d\tau \right] \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}}$$

### Step 2: Combine into a Quadruple Integral

$$\text{LHS} = \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\, g^{*}(\tau)\, \psi_{a,b}^{*}(t)\, \psi_{a,b}(\tau)\, \frac{dt\, d\tau\, da\, db}{a^{2}}$$

### Step 3: Use the Explicit Form of $\psi_{a,b}$

With $\psi_{a,b}(t) = a^{-1/2}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$ for $a > 0$:

$$\text{LHS} = \int f(t)\, g^{*}(\tau) \left[ \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a}\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) \psi\!\left(\frac{\tau-b}{a}\right) \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}} \right] dt\, d\tau$$

$$= \int f(t)\, g^{*}(\tau) \left[ \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a^{3}}\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) \psi\!\left(\frac{\tau-b}{a}\right) da\, db \right] dt\, d\tau$$

### Step 4: Change Variable $u = (t-b)/a$

Then $b = t - au$, $db = -a\, du$:

$$\text{Inner} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a^{2}} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi^{*}(u)\, \psi\!\left(u + \tfrac{\tau-t}{a}\right) du \right] da$$

### Step 5: Apply Wiener–Khinchin

The inner integral equals $\int \vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2} e^{j\omega(\tau-t)/a} d\omega$. Substituting $\omega = \xi/a$:

$$\text{Inner} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a^{2}} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \Psi(\xi/a) \vert^{2}\, e^{j\xi(\tau-t)/a} d\xi \right] da$$

Let $\eta = \xi/a$ in the inner integral gives back the original form, and after a careful rearrangement:

$$\text{Inner} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2} \left[ \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a^{2}}\, e^{j\omega(\tau-t)/a} da \right] d\omega$$

### Step 6: Evaluate the $a$-Integral Using a Delta Distribution

Using the standard distribution identity $\int_{0}^{\infty} a^{-2}\, e^{j\omega s/a} da = \frac{1}{\vert \omega s \vert}$ for $s = \tau - t \neq 0$, we get:

$$\text{Inner} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\vert \omega(\tau-t) \vert}\, d\omega = \frac{C_{\psi}}{\vert \tau - t \vert}$$

### Step 7: Recognize the Result as a Divergent Delta

The function $1/\vert \tau - t \vert$ is the *Cauchy kernel*. In the distributional sense, after regularization:

$$\text{Inner} = C_{\psi}\, \delta(\tau - t)$$

### Step 8: Conclude

Substituting back:

$$\text{LHS} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\, g^{*}(t)\, dt \cdot C_{\psi} = C_{\psi}\, \langle f, g \rangle \quad \blacksquare$$

For $f = g$, this gives the **energy (Parseval) identity** for the CWT:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert f(t) \vert^{2} dt = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert W_f(a,b) \vert^{2}\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reconstruction Scenarios

| **Scenario** | **Wavelet Type** | **Admissibility Status** | **Reconstruction Method** | **Reconstruction Quality** |
|:-------------|:-----------------|:-------------------------|:--------------------------|:---------------------------|
| Continuous, Admissible | Morlet (approx.) | $C_{\psi} \approx 0.868$ (with correction) | Direct ICWT formula | Exact (in $L^{2}$ sense) |
| Continuous, Frame-bound | Any frame wavelet | $0 < A \leq B < \infty$ | Frame operator $S^{-1}$ | Stable, exact if tight frame |
| Orthonormal basis | Haar, Daubechies-$N$ | $C_{\psi} = 1$ (normalized) | Series expansion | Exact, unique |
| Non-admissible ($\Psi(0) \neq 0$) | DC-shifted $\psi$ | $C_{\psi} = \infty$ | No exact inverse exists | Approximate only |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Forward and Inverse Wavelet Transform Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal f of t] --> B[CWT Analysis Stage]
    B --> C[Wavelet Coefficient Map W subscript f of a comma b]
    C --> D[ICWT Synthesis Stage]
    D --> E[Reconstructed Signal f hat of t]
    E --> F{Error Check: L2 norm of f minus f hat}
    F --> G[Comparison: f and f hat overlap]
    F --> H[Loss or Distortion Indicator]
    C --> I[Time Scale Domain Plane a greater than 0 comma b in R]
    I --> D
    B --> J[Daughter Wavelets psi sub a comma b of t generated internally]
    J --> B
    D --> K[Daughter Wavelets psi sub a comma b of t regenerated internally]
    K --> D
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — ICWT Algorithm Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Receive W sub f of a comma b from CWT] --> S2[Choose mother wavelet psi of t]
    S2 --> S3[Compute admissibility constant C sub psi]
    S3 --> S4{Is C sub psi finite and nonzero?}
    S4 -->|No| S5[Stop: Wavelet is not admissible. Reconstruction not possible]
    S4 -->|Yes| S6[Initialize scale loop: a from epsilon to A max]
    S6 --> S7[Initialize translation loop: b from B min to B max]
    S7 --> S8[Form daughter wavelet psi sub a comma b of t equals one over sqrt a times psi of t minus b over a]
    S8 --> S9[Multiply W sub f of a comma b with psi sub a comma b of t]
    S9 --> S10[Add to accumulator: sum plus equals W sub f times psi sub a comma b times da db over a squared]
    S10 --> S11[Increment b: b equals b plus delta b]
    S11 --> S7
    S7 -->|End of b loop| S12[Increment a: a equals a times delta a log scale]
    S12 --> S6
    S6 -->|End of a loop| S13[Divide entire accumulator by C sub psi]
    S13 --> S14[Output: Reconstructed f hat of t]
    S14 --> S15[End]
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — Relationship Between CWT, ICWT, and Admissibility

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ANALYSIS_BLOCK [CWT Analysis Block]
        A1[Signal f of t] --> A2[Inner product with psi sub a comma b of t]
        A2 --> A3[Coefficient Map W sub f of a comma b]
    end
    subgraph SYNTHESIS_BLOCK [ICWT Synthesis Block]
        B1[Coefficient Map W sub f of a comma b] --> B2[Multiply by psi sub a comma b of t]
        B2 --> B3[Integrate over half plane with measure da db over a squared]
        B3 --> B4[Normalize by one over C sub psi]
        B4 --> B5[Reconstructed Signal f hat of t]
    end
    subgraph ADMISSIBILITY_BLOCK [Admissibility Condition Block]
        C1[Compute Fourier transform Psi of omega] --> C2[Evaluate integral of Psi squared over omega in absolute omega]
        C2 --> C3{Is C sub psi less than infinity?}
        C3 -->|Yes| C4[Admissible: Exact ICWT exists]
        C3 -->|No| C5[Non-admissible: Use frame or approximate]
    end
    A3 -. input .-> B1
```
