# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Admissibility Condition [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q:** State and explain the **admissibility condition** for a mother wavelet $\psi(t)$. Why is it necessary for the existence of the Inverse Continuous Wavelet Transform? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **admissibility condition** requires that the mother wavelet $\psi(t) \in L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$ have a finite, positive **admissibility constant** $C_{\psi}$ defined as:

$$C_{\psi} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\vert \omega \vert}\, d\omega < \infty$$

where $\Psi(\omega)$ is the Fourier transform of $\psi(t)$. The integrand is well-defined at $\omega = 0$ only if $\Psi(0) = 0$, which means $\psi(t)$ must have **zero mean** (it must be an oscillating wave, not a DC pulse). This condition is necessary because the ICWT formula contains $1/C_{\psi}$ as a normalization factor; if $C_{\psi} = 0$ or $\infty$, the inverse either vanishes or diverges, making exact reconstruction impossible. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2: Daughter Wavelet [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q:** Write the explicit expression for the daughter wavelet $\psi_{a,b}(t)$ used in the ICWT. Explain the physical role of parameters $a$ and $b$. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The daughter wavelet is constructed from the mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ by scaling and translation:

$$\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right), \quad a \neq 0,\; b \in \mathbb{R}$$

- **Parameter $b$ (translation):** Controls the *time location* at which the wavelet is centered. It is a time-shift along the signal axis.
- **Parameter $a$ (scale):** Controls the *frequency content* of the wavelet. Small $a$ corresponds to *compressed* (high-frequency) wavelets, while large $a$ corresponds to *stretched* (low-frequency) wavelets.
- The factor $1/\sqrt{\vert a \vert}$ is a **normalization** that ensures $\Vert \psi_{a,b} \Vert_{2} = \Vert \psi \Vert_{2}$ (energy preservation across scales). **(3 Marks)**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A: Full Derivation + Application [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q(a):** Derive the **Inverse Continuous Wavelet Transform** formula from the inner-product definition of the CWT. Clearly state the admissibility condition and the natural measure $da\, db / a^{2}$ on the time-scale plane. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the CWT as an inner product:

$$W_f(a,b) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\, \psi_{a,b}^{*}(t)\, dt$$

Assume the ICWT takes the canonical form:

$$f(t) = \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W_f(a,b)\, \mathcal{K}(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, da\, db$$

Substituting the CWT expression and swapping the order of integration (Fubini's theorem for $f \in L^{2}$):

$$f(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(\tau) \left[ \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mathcal{K}(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, \psi_{a,b}^{*}(\tau)\, da\, db \right] d\tau$$

For the bracketed kernel to reproduce $\delta(t-\tau)$, we require:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mathcal{K}(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, \psi_{a,b}^{*}(\tau)\, da\, db = \delta(t-\tau)$$

Substituting the explicit form $\psi_{a,b}(t) = a^{-1/2}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$ and changing variables $u = (t-b)/a$ in the $b$-integral:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\mathcal{K}(a,\, t-au)}{a} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(u)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(u + \tfrac{\tau-t}{a}\right) du \right] da = \delta(t-\tau)$$

By the Wiener–Khinchin theorem, the inner integral equals $\int \vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2} e^{j\omega(\tau-t)/a} d\omega$. With $\omega = \xi/a$:

$$\delta(t-\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \Psi(\xi) \vert^{2} \left[ \int_{0}^{\infty} \mathcal{K}(a,\, t-au)\, a^{-1} e^{j\xi(\tau-t)/a} da \right] d\xi$$

Identifying the inner $a$-integral as the inverse of the admissible integral measure, we get:

$$\mathcal{K}(a,b) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}\, a^{2}}, \quad \text{where } C_{\psi} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\vert \omega \vert}\, d\omega$$

Therefore the ICWT is:

$$\boxed{\, f(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W_f(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}} \,} \quad \text{(7 Marks)}$$

> **Admissibility Condition:** $C_{\psi} < \infty$, requiring $\Psi(0) = 0$ (zero mean) and finite energy.
> **Natural Measure:** $d\mu(a,b) = da\, db / a^{2}$ is the left-invariant Haar measure on the affine group, ensuring scale-invariance of the reconstruction.

---

> **Q(b):** A signal $f(t)$ has a CWT given by $W_f(a,b) = e^{-a^{2}}\, e^{-b^{2}/(2a^{2})}$ with respect to a Mexican hat wavelet (admissibility constant $C_{\psi} = 1$). Using the ICWT, show the form of the reconstructed signal and comment on the role of the measure $da\, db / a^{2}$ in emphasizing small scales. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The Mexican hat wavelet (in time domain) is:

$$\psi(t) = (1 - t^{2})\, e^{-t^{2}/2}$$

The daughter wavelet is:

$$\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\, \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)^{2} \right] e^{-(t-b)^{2}/(2a^{2})}$$

The ICWT gives:

$$f(t) = \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-a^{2}}\, e^{-b^{2}/(2a^{2})}\, \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\, \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)^{2} \right] e^{-(t-b)^{2}/(2a^{2})}\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}}$$

**Combining the Gaussian terms:** The exponent becomes $-a^{2} - b^{2}/(2a^{2}) - (t-b)^{2}/(2a^{2})$. Completing the square in $b$:

$$-\frac{1}{2a^{2}} \left[ b^{2} + (t-b)^{2} \right] = -\frac{1}{2a^{2}} \left[ 2b^{2} - 2tb + t^{2} \right] = -\frac{1}{a^{2}} \left[ (b - t/2)^{2} - t^{2}/4 \right]$$

So the $b$-integral is:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-(b - t/2)^{2}/a^{2}}\, e^{t^{2}/(4a^{2})}\, db = a\sqrt{\pi}\, e^{t^{2}/(4a^{2})}$$

(using $\int e^{-x^{2}/\sigma^{2}} dx = \sigma\sqrt{\pi}$). Substituting back, with the $1/a^{2}$ measure, the leading $a$-dependence is:

$$f(t) \propto \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-a^{2}}\, e^{t^{2}/(4a^{2})}\, a^{1/2}\, e^{-t^{2}/(4a^{2})}\, da \cdot \text{(polynomial in } t/a\text{)}$$

The terms $e^{t^{2}/(4a^{2})}$ and $e^{-t^{2}/(4a^{2})}$ in the remaining factor partially cancel, leaving an integrand dominated by $a^{-3/2} e^{-a^{2}}$ at small $a$, which is integrable near zero.

**Role of $1/a^{2}$ measure:** The factor $1/a^{2}$ compensates for the *decreasing density* of scales at small $a$ when measured on a linear axis. Without it, coarse scales (large $a$) would dominate the integral because they occupy more linear measure. The $1/a^{2}$ makes the integration **logarithmic in scale**, treating each octave equally. This is why the CWT is naturally suited for multi-resolution analysis. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B: Moyal's Theorem + Reconstruction [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q(a):** State and prove **Moyal's theorem** for the Continuous Wavelet Transform. Use it to derive the energy-preservation (Parseval) identity. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** For $f, g \in L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$ and an admissible wavelet $\psi$,

$$\int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W_f(a,b)\, W_{g}^{*}(a,b)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}} = C_{\psi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\, g^{*}(t)\, dt$$

**Proof:**

*Step 1:* Expand the LHS using $W_f(a,b) = \int f(t)\, \psi_{a,b}^{*}(t)\, dt$ and similarly for $g$:

$$\text{LHS} = \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\, g^{*}(\tau)\, \psi_{a,b}^{*}(t)\, \psi_{a,b}(\tau)\, \frac{dt\, d\tau\, da\, db}{a^{2}}$$

*Step 2:* Swap integration order (Fubini), substitute $\psi_{a,b}(t) = a^{-1/2}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$:

$$= \int f(t)\, g^{*}(\tau) \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a^{3}}\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) \psi\!\left(\frac{\tau-b}{a}\right) da\, db\, dt\, d\tau$$

*Step 3:* Change variable $u = (t-b)/a$, so $b = t - au$, $db = -a\, du$:

$$\text{Inner} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a^{2}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi^{*}(u)\, \psi\!\left(u + \tfrac{\tau-t}{a}\right) du\, da$$

*Step 4:* By the Wiener–Khinchin theorem:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi^{*}(u)\, \psi(u + \Delta)\, du = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2} e^{j\omega\Delta}\, d\omega$$

with $\Delta = (\tau - t)/a$:

$$\text{Inner} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a^{2}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2} e^{j\omega(\tau-t)/a}\, d\omega\, da$$

*Step 5:* Change variable $\omega = \xi/a$ in the inner integral, $d\omega = -d\xi/a$:

$$= \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a^{3}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \Psi(\xi/a) \vert^{2} e^{j\xi(\tau-t)/a}\, d\xi\, da$$

*Step 6:* Change variable $\eta = \xi/a$ back to original, and recognize the standard distribution identity:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a^{2}}\, e^{j\omega(\tau-t)/a}\, da = \frac{\pi}{\vert \omega(\tau-t) \vert} \quad \text{(for } \tau \neq t\text{)}$$

(using $\int_{0}^{\infty} x^{-2} e^{ixy} dx$ evaluated as a distribution). Therefore:

$$\text{Inner} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\vert \omega(\tau-t) \vert}\, d\omega = \frac{1}{\vert \tau - t \vert} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\vert \omega \vert}\, d\omega = \frac{C_{\psi}}{\vert \tau - t \vert}$$

*Step 7:* The function $1/\vert \tau - t \vert$ is the *Cauchy principal value* kernel, which in the distributional limit equals $C_{\psi}\, \delta(\tau - t)$. Thus:

$$\text{LHS} = C_{\psi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\, g^{*}(t)\, dt \quad \blacksquare$$

**Parseval Identity:** Setting $f = g$:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert f(t) \vert^{2} dt = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert W_f(a,b) \vert^{2}\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}} \quad \text{(7 Marks)}$$

---

> **Q(b):** Consider the Gaussian signal $f(t) = e^{-t^{2}/2}$. The Morlet wavelet approximation (Gaussian-modulated complex exponential) has the form $\psi(t) = \pi^{-1/4}\, e^{j\omega_{0} t} e^{-t^{2}/2}$ with $\omega_{0} = 5$. Compute the admissibility constant $C_{\psi}$ approximately and explain why, for the *real* Morlet variant used in practice, the ICWT is applied with a small correction term. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Fourier transform of the (complex) Morlet wavelet:

$$\Psi(\omega) = \pi^{-1/4} \cdot \sqrt{2\pi} \cdot e^{-(\omega - \omega_{0})^{2}/2}$$

specifically: $\Psi(\omega) = \sqrt{2}\, \pi^{1/4}\, e^{-(\omega - \omega_{0})^{2}/2}$.

**Step 2:** Compute the admissibility constant:

$$C_{\psi} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\vert \omega \vert}\, d\omega = 2\sqrt{\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-(\omega - \omega_{0})^{2}}}{\vert \omega \vert}\, d\omega$$

**Step 3:** Numerical evaluation. For $\omega_{0} = 5$, the Gaussian $e^{-(\omega-5)^{2}}$ is essentially zero for $\omega \leq 0$. Hence $\vert \omega \vert \approx \omega$ in the integrand, and:

$$C_{\psi} \approx 2\sqrt{\pi} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-(\omega-5)^{2}}}{\omega}\, d\omega$$

Substitute $u = \omega - 5$:

$$C_{\psi} \approx 2\sqrt{\pi}\, e^{0} \int_{-5}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-u^{2}}}{u + 5}\, du \approx 2\sqrt{\pi} \cdot (0.204) \approx 0.720$$

More precisely, numerical integration yields $C_{\psi} \approx 0.868$ for the standardly normalized Morlet.

**Step 4:** The *ideal* Morlet wavelet (Gabor wavelet) is $\psi(t) = e^{j\omega_{0} t} e^{-t^{2}/2}$, which does NOT satisfy $\Psi(0) = 0$ because $\Psi(0) = e^{-\omega_{0}^{2}/2} \neq 0$. Hence the *ideal* Morlet is **not strictly admissible**; $C_{\psi}$ formally diverges (the $1/\vert \omega \vert$ singularity at $\omega = 0$).

**Step 5:** The **practical Morlet wavelet** adds a small correction term:

$$\psi(t) = \pi^{-1/4} \left( e^{j\omega_{0} t} - e^{-\omega_{0}^{2}/2} \right) e^{-t^{2}/2}$$

The subtracted term $e^{-\omega_{0}^{2}/2}$ is chosen so that $\Psi(0) = 0$ exactly. For $\omega_{0} = 5$, $e^{-12.5} \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-6}$, which is negligible for analysis but mathematically restores admissibility.

**Step 6:** Consequently, in ICWT applications, practitioners use:

- $C_{\psi} \approx 0.868$ for normalized Morlet (with correction).
- The reconstruction may include an additional term to compensate for the small DC residual if the *uncorrected* Morlet is used.

**Conclusion:** The Morlet wavelet is an excellent choice for *analysis* due to its Gaussian envelope and good time-frequency localization, but for *exact* ICWT reconstruction, the correction term is theoretically necessary. In practice, the approximation $C_{\psi} \approx 0.868$ is used and reconstruction is *near-exact* (error $\sim 10^{-6}$). **(7 Marks)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on ICWT Questions:**
>
> 1. **Missing the $1/a^{2}$ measure** (–2 marks): Forgetting this factor is the single most common error. Always write the measure explicitly.
> 2. **Wrong admissibility constant formula** (–1 mark): Confusing $C_{\psi} = \int \vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2} d\omega$ (Parseval for Fourier) with the *correct* $C_{\psi} = \int \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\vert \omega \vert} d\omega$.
> 3. **Integration over $\mathbb{R}$ instead of $\mathbb{R}^{+}$** (–1 mark): The ICWT integrates over positive scales $a > 0$, not the full real line.
> 4. **Forgetting the $1/\sqrt{\vert a \vert}$ normalization in $\psi_{a,b}$** (–1 mark): This factor ensures energy preservation and must appear.
> 5. **Confusing inner-product notation**: Using $\langle \psi, f \rangle$ vs $\langle f, \psi \rangle$ inconsistently. KTU examiners are strict about conjugate-linear vs. linear arguments.
> 6. **Not stating $\Psi(0) = 0$ explicitly**: This is a required condition derived from admissibility; omitting it costs a mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Inverse Continuous Wavelet Transform:**

- ⭐ The **ICWT** reconstructs a signal $f(t)$ from its 2-D CWT coefficient map $W_f(a,b)$.
- ⭐ The reconstruction formula is $f(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W_f(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}}$.
- ⭐ The **admissibility constant** $C_{\psi} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\vert \omega \vert}\, d\omega$ must be finite and positive.
- ⭐ Admissibility requires $\Psi(0) = 0$ (zero mean / DC-free).
- ⭐ The integration measure $da\, db / a^{2}$ is the **left-invariant Haar measure** on the affine group.
- ⭐ **Daughter wavelet:** $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$.
- ⭐ **Moyal's theorem:** $\int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W_f(a,b)\, W_{g}^{*}(a,b)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}} = C_{\psi} \langle f, g \rangle$.
- ⭐ **Parseval identity:** $\Vert f \Vert_{2}^{2} = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert W_f(a,b) \vert^{2}\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}}$.
- ⭐ For **non-admissible** wavelets, use the **frame operator** $S^{-1}$ for stable (but not exact) reconstruction.
- ⭐ CWT → ICWT is the **analysis–synthesis** duality; the ICWT exists uniquely only in the $L^{2}$ sense (almost everywhere).
- ⭐ In KTU exam answers, always **state admissibility first**, then the formula — this is a "process marker" that examiners look for.
- ⭐ **Real-world uses:** ECG/EEG denoising, seismic inversion, audio coding, image super-resolution, OFDM receivers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of CWT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Properties of CWT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)** of a continuous-time, square-integrable signal $x(t) \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$ with respect to a mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ is defined as:

$$
CWT_x(a, b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^*\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt
$$

where:
- $a \in \mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}$ is the **scale parameter** (inverse of frequency)
- $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **translation (shift) parameter**
- $\psi^*\left(\cdot\right)$ denotes the complex conjugate of the analyzing wavelet
- The factor $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}$ is the **energy normalization constant** ensuring that the energy of $\psi_{a,b}(t)$ remains the same as that of the mother wavelet at every scale.

The analyzing function $\psi_{a,b}(t)$ generated from the mother wavelet is:

$$
\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \, \psi\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Under the admissibility condition, the CWT must be invertible (i.e., the original signal $x(t)$ can be reconstructed from $CWT_x(a,b)$) only if the **admissibility constant** $C_\psi$ satisfies $0 < C_\psi < \infty$. This is a high-yield 7-mark concept in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Tuning Fork and the Microscope":**
> Imagine you are a doctor listening to a patient's heartbeat. The standard **stethoscope (Fourier Transform)** tells you *what frequencies* exist but loses the *time* information of when they occurred. Now, replace the stethoscope with a **variable-power microscope (CWT)**. You can zoom in (small scale = high frequency) to see fine details, or zoom out (large scale = low frequency) to see the overall trend. As you shift the microscope left/right (translation parameter $b$), you can analyze different portions of the signal. Each zoom-level and each position gives a "coefficient" — together they form a 2-D time-scale map of the signal.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Admissibility Constant:** $C_\psi = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\omega} d\omega < \infty$
- **Unit Energy Condition:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \psi(t) \vert^2 dt = 1$
- **Zero Mean Condition:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t) \, dt = 0$
- **Time-Bandwidth Product:** Heisenberg uncertainty gives $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$ for any wavelet.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** CWT properties are foundational in **seismic signal processing** (oil exploration), **ECG/EEG biomedical analysis**, **fault detection in rotating machinery**, and **speech recognition systems** — all part of the Industry 4.0 toolchain expected of KTU 2024 graduates.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Properties of CWT — Structured Logic

The CWT inherits its structural properties from the inner product between the signal and the scaled-translated wavelets. Below is a systematic breakdown:

#### (a) Linearity (Superposition Principle)
If $x(t) = \alpha x_1(t) + \beta x_2(t)$, then:
$$
CWT_x(a,b) = \alpha \, CWT_{x_1}(a,b) + \beta \, CWT_{x_2}(a,b)
$$
**Why it matters:** Allows the CWT to be applied component-wise to decomposed signals (e.g., in sub-band coding).

#### (b) Translation / Time-Shifting Property
If $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$, then:
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = CWT_x(a, b - t_0)
$$
**Interpretation:** A time-shift in the input causes an **equal time-shift in the $b$-axis** of the CWT plane.

#### (c) Scaling / Dilation Property
If $y(t) = x(\lambda t)$ with $\lambda > 0$, then:
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}} \, CWT_x(\lambda a, \lambda b)
$$
**Interpretation:** A compression/expansion in time produces an inverse scaling in the $(a, b)$ plane.

#### (d) Energy Conservation (Parseval-like Identity)
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert CWT_x(a,b) \vert^2}{a^2} \, da \, db = C_\psi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt
$$
**Why it matters:** Forms the basis of the **scelogram** (energy distribution in the time-scale plane).

#### (e) Translation Invariance
Unlike the **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)**, the CWT exhibits **translation covariance**:
$$
CWT\{x(t - t_0)\}(a,b) = CWT\{x(t)\}(a, b - t_0)
$$

#### (f) Scale Invariance
$$
CWT\{x(t/s)\}(a,b) = \sqrt{s} \, CWT\{x(t)\}(sa, sb)
$$

#### (g) Inner Product Interpretation
$$
CWT_x(a,b) = \langle x(t), \psi_{a,b}(t) \rangle = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi_{a,b}^*(t) \, dt
$$

#### (h) Redundancy
The CWT is **highly redundant** because $(a,b)$ is a continuous 2-D parameter space mapping to a 1-D signal — typically infinite coefficients describe one signal.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Property** | **Mathematical Form** | **Physical Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int x(t) \psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$ | Inner product of signal with scaled-translated wavelet |
| Admissibility Condition | $C_\psi = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\omega} d\omega < \infty$ | Necessary and sufficient for inversion |
| Linearity | $CWT\{\alpha x_1 + \beta x_2\} = \alpha CWT\{x_1\} + \beta CWT\{x_2\}$ | Superposition holds |
| Time Shift | $CWT\{x(t-t_0)\} = CWT_x(a, b-t_0)$ | Shift in $b$-axis only |
| Dilation of Input | $CWT\{x(\lambda t)\} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}} CWT_x(\lambda a, \lambda b)$ | Both axes rescale |
| Energy Conservation | $\iint \frac{\vert CWT_x(a,b) \vert^2}{a^2} da\, db = C_\psi E_x$ | Scelogram energy |
| Inversion Formula | $x(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_0^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} CWT_x(a,b) \, \psi_{a,b}(t) \, \frac{da\, db}{a^2}$ | Reconstruct original signal |
| Resolution Cell Area | $\Delta a \cdot \Delta b = a^2 \Delta a_0 \cdot \Delta b_0$ | Heisenberg-like uncertainty in scale |
| Mother Wavelet Energy | $\int \vert \psi(t) \vert^2 dt = 1$ | Unit energy normalization |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Power Systems:** Detecting transient disturbances (sags, swells) in voltage signals.
> - **Biomedical:** Identifying QRS complexes in ECG, spike detection in EEG.
> - **Geophysics:** Locating oil reservoirs via seismic CWT scalograms.
> - **Communications:** Multi-resolution analysis of OFDM signals.
> - **Mechanical Vibration:** Bearing fault diagnosis in rotating machinery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Translation Property of CWT

**Statement:** If $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$, prove that $CWT_y(a,b) = CWT_x(a, b - t_0)$.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of CWT applied to $y(t)$:**
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y(t) \, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$:**
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t - t_0) \, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt
$$

**Step 3 — Apply a change of variable.** Let $\tau = t - t_0 \implies t = \tau + t_0$, $dt = d\tau$. When $t \to -\infty$, $\tau \to -\infty$; when $t \to +\infty$, $\tau \to +\infty$.

$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{\tau + t_0 - b}{a}\right) d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange the argument of $\psi$:**
$$
\frac{\tau + t_0 - b}{a} = \frac{\tau - (b - t_0)}{a}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into the integral:**
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{\tau - (b - t_0)}{a}\right) d\tau
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the right-hand side as $CWT_x(a, b - t_0)$ by definition:**
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = CWT_x(a, b - t_0) \qquad \blacksquare
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Dilation (Scaling) Property of CWT

**Statement:** If $y(t) = x(\lambda t)$ with $\lambda > 0$, prove that $CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}} \, CWT_x(\lambda a, \lambda b)$.

**Step 1 — Apply CWT definition to $y(t)$:**
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\lambda t) \, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt
$$

**Step 2 — Change of variable.** Let $\tau = \lambda t \implies t = \tau/\lambda$, $dt = d\tau/\lambda$.

$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{\tau/\lambda - b}{a}\right) \frac{d\tau}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the argument of $\psi$:**
$$
\frac{\tau/\lambda - b}{a} = \frac{\tau - \lambda b}{\lambda a}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\lambda \sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{\tau - \lambda b}{\lambda a}\right) d\tau
$$

**Step 5 — Pull $\sqrt{\lambda a}/\sqrt{\lambda a}$ inside the integral (carefully):**
$$
\frac{1}{\lambda \sqrt{\vert a \vert}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda} \cdot \sqrt{\vert \lambda a \vert}}
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the form of CWT with new parameters $(a' = \lambda a, b' = \lambda b)$:**
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert \lambda a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{\tau - \lambda b}{\lambda a}\right) d\tau
$$

**Step 7 — Conclude:**
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}} \, CWT_x(\lambda a, \lambda b) \qquad \blacksquare
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Energy Conservation (Parseval's Theorem for CWT)

**Step 1 — Begin with the inverse CWT formula:**
$$
x(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_0^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} CWT_x(a,b) \, \psi_{a,b}(t) \, \frac{da\, db}{a^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$:**
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) x^*(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_0^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{CWT_x(a,b)}{a^2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^*(t) \psi_{a,b}(t) \, dt \, da\, db
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the inner integral as $CWT_{x^*}(a,b) = CWT_x^*(a,b)$:**
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_0^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert CWT_x(a,b) \vert^2}{a^2} da\, db \qquad \blacksquare
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Admissibility Condition from Fourier Domain

**Step 1 — Write Parseval's identity for the CWT inner product:**
$$
CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int x(t) \psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt = \sqrt{\vert a \vert} \int X(\omega) \Psi^*(a\omega) e^{j\omega b} d\omega
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the energy conservation theorem (Cauchy-Schwarz):**
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert CWT_x(a,b) \vert^2 db = \vert a \vert \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 \vert \Psi(a\omega) \vert^2 d\omega
$$

**Step 3 — Change variable $\omega' = a\omega \implies d\omega = d\omega'/a$:**
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert CWT_x(a,b) \vert^2 db = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(\omega') \vert^2 \vert \Psi(\omega') \vert^2 \frac{d\omega'}{\vert a \vert}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over $a$ and use the admissibility condition** (requires $\vert \Psi(0) \vert^2 = 0$ for the integral over $a$ to converge):
$$
\iint \frac{\vert CWT_x(a,b) \vert^2}{a^2} da\, db = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 \vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2 \left[\int_0^{\infty} \frac{da}{a^3} \cdot \frac{1}{\vert a \vert}\right] d\omega
$$

After careful reduction, this yields the constraint:
$$
C_\psi = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\omega} d\omega < \infty
$$

**Conclusion:** For inversion to be possible, $\Psi(0) = 0$ (zero mean / zero DC) is **mandatory**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Property

| **Problem Statement Clue** | **Property to Apply** | **Expected Answer Form** |
|---|---|---|
| "Signal delayed by $t_0$" | Translation | CWT shifts in $b$ |
| "Signal compressed/expanded" | Dilation | Both $a$ and $b$ scale by $\lambda$ |
| "Sum of two signals" | Linearity | Sum of individual CWTs |
| "Energy of signal" | Parseval | Integral of $\vert CWT\vert^2 / a^2$ |
| "Reconstruction" | Admissibility + Inversion | Use $C_\psi$ |
| "Frequency of wavelet is zero?" | Admissibility | $\Psi(0) = 0$ required |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: CWT Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal x of t] --> B[Choose Mother Wavelet psi of t]
    B --> C[Select Scale Parameter a]
    C --> D[Select Translation Parameter b]
    D --> E[Generate Daughter Wavelet psi a b of t]
    E --> F[Compute Inner Product: Integral x of t times psi a b conjugate dt]
    F --> G[Output CWT Coefficient CWT x a b]
    G --> H{Admissibility C psi Finite?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Store in 2D Time-Scale Plane]
    H -- No --> J[Reject Wavelet: Not Admissible]
    I --> K[Apply Inverse CWT]
    K --> L[Reconstructed Signal x hat of t]
    L --> M[Compare with x of t for Error]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Property Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Linearity] --> P2[Translation]
    P1 --> P3[Dilation]
    P1 --> P4[Parseval Energy]
    P2 --> P5[Translation Invariance]
    P3 --> P6[Scale Invariance]
    P4 --> P7[Scelogram]
    P5 --> P8[Time-Frequency Localization]
    P6 --> P8
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Multi-Resolution Analysis]
    Q[Admissibility Condition] --> P4
    Q --> K1[Inversion Formula]
    K1 --> P9
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Resolution Cell Geometry (Heisenberg Box)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Time-Frequency Plane] --> S1[Small Scale a small]
    R --> S2[Large Scale a large]
    S1 --> T1[Good Time Resolution]
    S1 --> F1[Poor Frequency Resolution]
    S2 --> T2[Poor Time Resolution]
    S2 --> F2[Good Frequency Resolution]
    T1 --> H1[Adaptive Resolution Cell: Small Area at High f]
    F2 --> H2[Adaptive Resolution Cell: Large Area at Low f]
    H1 --> HC[Constant Q Factor Property]
    H2 --> HC
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: CWT Property Verification Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Original Signal x of t] --> X1[Apply Time Shift: x of t minus t0]
    X --> X2[Apply Dilation: x of lambda t]
    X --> X3[Apply Linear Combination: alpha x1 plus beta x2]
    X1 --> CWTA[Compute CWT]
    X2 --> CWTB[Compute CWT]
    X3 --> CWTC[Compute CWT]
    X --> CWTD[Compute CWT of x]
    CWTA --> CMP1[Compare with CWT of x shifted in b]
    CWTB --> CMP2[Compare with scaled CWT]
    CWTC --> CMP3[Compare with linear combo of CWTs]
    CWTD --> CMP1
    CWTD --> CMP2
    CWTD --> CMP3
    CMP1 --> RES[Verify Properties]
    CMP2 --> RES
    CMP3 --> RES
```

> [!TIP]
> **Constant-Q Property:** The CWT's most celebrated feature — the **quality factor** $Q = \frac{f_0}{\Delta f}$ remains constant across all scales. This is what makes CWT superior to STFT for analyzing signals with broadband frequency content.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and prove the translation property of Continuous Wavelet Transform.**

**Model Answer:**
The translation property states that if $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$, then $CWT_y(a,b) = CWT_x(a, b - t_0)$.

**Proof:** Starting from the CWT definition of $y(t)$:
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t - t_0) \, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt
$$
Using substitution $\tau = t - t_0$, we get $dt = d\tau$ and $t = \tau + t_0$, hence:
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{\tau - (b - t_0)}{a}\right) d\tau = CWT_x(a, b - t_0)
$$
A time shift in the input produces an equivalent shift in the translation parameter $b$, leaving the scale $a$ unchanged. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the admissibility condition for a function $\psi(t)$ to be a wavelet? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**
The admissibility condition requires:
$$
C_\psi = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\omega} d\omega < \infty
$$
where $\Psi(\omega)$ is the Fourier transform of $\psi(t)$.

This condition has two direct consequences:
1. **Zero DC constraint:** $\Psi(0) = 0$, which implies $\int \psi(t) dt = 0$ (wavelets must oscillate).
2. **Invertibility:** Only when $C_\psi$ is finite and nonzero can the original signal $x(t)$ be reconstructed from $CWT_x(a,b)$ using the inverse formula.

It is necessary because it ensures that the wavelet transform is **invertible** and that the energy in the time-scale plane is conserved (Parseval's identity). **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**OR** *(Choice 1)*

**(a)** Derive in detail the **dilation (scaling) property** of the Continuous Wavelet Transform. Mention its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and prove the **energy conservation theorem** for CWT. Explain how it leads to the concept of a scelogram. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

**Statement:** If $y(t) = x(\lambda t)$ where $\lambda > 0$, then $CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}} CWT_x(\lambda a, \lambda b)$.

**Derivation:**
Starting from the CWT definition:
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\lambda t) \, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt
$$
Substitute $\tau = \lambda t$, so $dt = d\tau/\lambda$ and $t = \tau/\lambda$:
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\lambda \sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{\tau/\lambda - b}{a}\right) d\tau
$$
Simplify the wavelet argument:
$$
\frac{\tau/\lambda - b}{a} = \frac{\tau - \lambda b}{\lambda a}
$$
Thus:
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\lambda \sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{\tau - \lambda b}{\lambda a}\right) d\tau
$$
Note that $\frac{1}{\lambda \sqrt{\vert a \vert}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda} \cdot \sqrt{\vert \lambda a \vert}}$. Recognizing the integral as $CWT_x(\lambda a, \lambda b)$:
$$
CWT_y(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}} \, CWT_x(\lambda a, \lambda b)
$$
**Physical Significance:** A time-domain dilation of the signal by factor $\lambda$ causes the CWT to compress in the $a$-axis by $1/\lambda$ (frequency stretched) and in the $b$-axis by $1/\lambda$ (time compressed). This confirms the **self-similar multi-resolution nature** of the CWT. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (b):

**Statement (Energy Conservation):**
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert CWT_x(a,b) \vert^2}{a^2} da\, db = C_\psi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt
$$

**Proof:**
Using the inversion formula:
$$
x(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_0^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} CWT_x(a,b) \, \psi_{a,b}(t) \, \frac{da\, db}{a^2}
$$
Computing the energy:
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_0^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{CWT_x(a,b)}{a^2} \left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^*(t) \psi_{a,b}(t) dt\right] da\, db
$$
The bracketed term equals $CWT_x^*(a,b)$:
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_0^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\vert CWT_x(a,b) \vert^2}{a^2} da\, db
$$
**Scelogram:** A **scelogram** is defined as $S_x(a,b) = \frac{\vert CWT_x(a,b) \vert^2}{C_\psi \, a^2}$. From the theorem, $\iint S_x(a,b) da\, db = E_x$ (signal energy). It represents the **energy density distribution** of the signal in the time-scale plane — analogous to the spectrogram in STFT. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**OR** *(Choice 2)*

**(a)** Explain the **admissibility condition** of a wavelet. Show that any function satisfying it must have zero mean. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With necessary derivations, prove the **linearity property** of the CWT and explain the **redundancy** in CWT representation. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

The admissibility condition states:
$$
C_\psi = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\omega} d\omega < \infty
$$
For this integral to be finite, the integrand must not diverge at $\omega = 0$. This requires:
$$
\lim_{\omega \to 0} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\omega} < \infty
$$
Since the numerator is non-negative, we need $\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2 = O(\omega)$ as $\omega \to 0$, meaning $\Psi(0) = 0$.

By the Fourier transform property, $\Psi(0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t) dt$. Therefore:
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t) \, dt = 0
$$
**Physical Meaning:** A wavelet must have **zero mean**, i.e., it must be oscillatory (positive and negative parts cancel). This is why wavelets are "small waves." Functions like $\sin(\omega_0 t)$ satisfy this, but a simple rectangular pulse does not. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (b):

**Linearity Proof:**
Let $x(t) = \alpha x_1(t) + \beta x_2(t)$. By definition:
$$
CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} [\alpha x_1(t) + \beta x_2(t)] \, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt
$$
Distributing the integral:
$$
CWT_x(a,b) = \alpha \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int x_1(t) \psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt\right] + \beta \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int x_2(t) \psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt\right]
$$
$$
CWT_x(a,b) = \alpha \, CWT_{x_1}(a,b) + \beta \, CWT_{x_2}(a,b)
$$
**Redundancy:** The CWT maps a 1-D signal $x(t)$ to a 2-D function $CWT_x(a,b)$. Since $(a, b) \in \mathbb{R}^2$ is a continuous space, **infinitely many coefficients** represent a single signal. This makes the CWT **highly redundant**, in contrast to the DWT which uses a discrete, orthogonal basis. The redundancy is advantageous for:
- Noise robustness
- Feature extraction
- Pattern recognition

However, it requires more storage and computation. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}$ normalization factor** in derivations — costs 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $a$ and $1/a$:** In CWT, **large $a$ = low frequency** (stretched wavelet), **small $a$ = high frequency** (compressed wavelet). Reversing this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Missing the $\frac{1}{a^2}$ Jacobian term** in the inversion formula and Parseval's theorem — this is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Writing the admissibility integral as $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}$ instead of $\int_0^{\infty}$:** Wavelets use only positive scales; full-line integration is **wrong**.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention that wavelets are bandpass:** State explicitly that $\Psi(0) = 0$ implies $\psi(t)$ has no DC component.
> 6. **Conflating CWT properties with DWT properties:** Translation invariance holds in CWT but **not in orthonormal DWT** (unless using stationary/à trous DWT). Mixing them is a common KTU evaluation trap.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Properties of CWT**
>
> - **Definition:** $CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int x(t) \psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$
> - **Two parameters:** Scale $a$ (inverse of frequency) and translation $b$ (time location).
> - **Admissibility Condition:** $C_\psi = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\omega} d\omega < \infty$ — must hold for invertibility.
> - **Zero Mean:** $\int \psi(t) dt = 0$ (consequence of admissibility).
> - **Linearity:** CWT is linear — superposition holds.
> - **Translation Property:** $CWT\{x(t-t_0)\} = CWT_x(a, b-t_0)$ — only $b$ shifts.
> - **Dilation Property:** $CWT\{x(\lambda t)\} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}} CWT_x(\lambda a, \lambda b)$ — both axes scale.
> - **Energy Conservation:** $\iint \frac{\vert CWT_x(a,b) \vert^2}{a^2} da\, db = C_\psi \int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$
> - **Inversion Formula:** $x(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_0^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} CWT_x(a,b) \psi_{a,b}(t) \frac{da\, db}{a^2}$
> - **Redundancy:** CWT is over-complete (infinite coefficients for one signal).
> - **Constant-Q:** Quality factor $Q = f_0/\Delta f$ is scale-independent.
> - **Scelogram:** $S(a,b) = \frac{\vert CWT_x(a,b) \vert^2}{C_\psi a^2}$ — energy density in time-scale plane.
> - **Heisenberg Cell:** $\Delta b \cdot \Delta \omega \geq 1/2$, area preserved across scales (adaptive resolution).
> - **Vs STFT:** CWT has adaptive resolution; STFT has fixed window resolution.
> - **Key Engineering Applications:** Seismic analysis, ECG/EEG, fault detection, speech processing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concept of Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** is the discrete-time, discrete-scale counterpart of the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT). It is defined by discretizing the scaling parameter $a$ and translation parameter $b$ of the CWT onto a **dyadic grid** such that $a = 2^j$ and $b = k \cdot 2^j$ for integers $j, k \in \mathbb{Z}$.

$$
W_x(j,k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^j}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^*\left( \frac{t - k \, 2^j}{2^j} \right) dt
$$

Equivalently, in inner-product notation:

$$
W_x(j,k) = \langle x(t), \, \psi_{j,k}(t) \rangle
$$

where the **discrete wavelet basis functions** are:

$$
\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{-j/2} \, \psi\left( 2^{-j} t - k \right)
$$

The index $j$ represents the **scale (octave) level**, while $k$ represents the **discrete time-shift** index.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** DWT is the **engine of every practical wavelet application** (JPEG2000, denoising, ECG compression, fault detection). Unlike the CWT, the DWT produces a **non-redundant, orthogonal (or bi-orthogonal) representation** that can be inverted **without loss of information**, making it a *Parseval-tight frame* when the chosen $\psi$ is orthogonal.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are examining a **painting of a forest under a microscope**:

* The **CWT** is like moving the magnifying glass to *every* possible position and *every* possible magnification — you get a beautifully detailed but **highly redundant** image, and your notebook overflows with overlapping notes.
* The **DWT** is a *smart inspector*: at each pass, the inspector looks at the painting at **doubling magnifications** ($1\times$, $2\times$, $4\times$, $8\times$…) and at positions that are scaled *with* the magnification. The notes are **compact**, **non-overlapping**, and yet the painting can be **perfectly reconstructed** from them.

**Why "doubling"?** Because doubling the scale at every step ($a = 2^j$) halves the bandwidth required at that level, which is mathematically *natural* for digital systems where bandwidths and sample rates are powers of two.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** DWT chops a signal into a coarse approximation (low-frequency content) plus a stack of fine details (high-frequency content) at successively halved resolutions — exactly mirroring how the human eye processes an image: first the broad shapes, then the edges, then the textures.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Property |
|----------|--------|--------------------------|
| Dyadic scale base | $a_0$ | **2** (octave sampling) |
| Translation step | $b_0$ | **1** (integer sample grid) |
| Admissibility constant | $C_\psi$ | **finite, positive** |
| Energy of $\psi$ | $\Vert\psi\Vert^2$ | **1** (normalised) |
| Mean value | $\int \psi(t)\,dt$ | **0** (band-pass property) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The admissibility constant $C_\psi$ **must be finite and non-zero** for the DWT to be invertible. This forces $\Psi(0) = 0$, i.e. the wavelet has **no DC component**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" — Limitations of the CWT that Motivate the DWT

1. **Redundancy:** CWT coefficients at adjacent $(a,b)$ are *highly correlated* — information is repeated.
2. **Computational cost:** Continuous integral evaluation for every $(a,b)$ is intractable for real-time DSP.
3. **Non-invertibility by orthogonal projection:** CWT frames may be over-complete, so simple projection is ambiguous.
4. **No fast algorithm:** Without a discrete lattice, no $O(N \log N)$ transform exists.

The DWT fixes all four by working on a **dyadic lattice** that aligns perfectly with the **scaling equation** of multiresolution analysis.

---

### 2.2 The "How" — Logical Derivation Flow of DWT

**Step 1 — Discretise the CWT pair $(a,b)$:**
Choose $a = 2^j$ and $b = k \cdot 2^j$ (dyadic sampling). Substituting:

$$
W_x(j,k) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, 2^{-j/2} \, \psi^*\left( 2^{-j} t - k \right) dt
$$

**Step 2 — Define the basis functions:**
The set $\{\psi_{j,k}(t)\}_{j,k \in \mathbb{Z}}$ must form an **orthonormal basis of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$**:

$$
\int \psi_{j,k}(t) \, \psi_{j',k'}^*(t) \, dt = \delta_{j,j'} \, \delta_{k,k'}
$$

**Step 3 — Express any $x(t)$ as a wavelet series:**

$$
x(t) = \sum_{j=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} W_x(j,k) \, \psi_{j,k}(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Implement via filter banks (Mallat, 1989):**
Because the $\psi_{j,k}$ satisfy a *two-scale relation* with an associated scaling function $\phi(t)$, the DWT becomes a cascade of **low-pass / high-pass filters followed by 2:1 down-sampling** — a perfectly realisable digital structure.

**Step 5 — Reconstruction (Inverse DWT):**
Up-sample by 2, filter with synthesis filters $g_0, g_1$, and add the two branches. Perfect reconstruction is achieved when the filter bank satisfies the **alias cancellation** and **perfect reconstruction** conditions.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly examinable** under the KTU 2024 ESE pattern for Module 3.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | DWT coefficient | $W_x(j,k) = \int x(t) \, 2^{-j/2} \, \psi^*(2^{-j} t - k) \, dt$ | $j,k \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 2 | DWT basis | $\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{-j/2} \, \psi(2^{-j} t - k)$ | Orthonormal |
| 3 | Inverse DWT | $x(t) = \sum_{j} \sum_{k} W_x(j,k) \, \psi_{j,k}(t)$ | Parseval tight |
| 4 | Admissibility | $C_\psi = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\omega} d\omega < \infty$ | Invertibility condition |
| 5 | Mean-zero property | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t) \, dt = 0$ | Band-pass behaviour |
| 6 | Energy conservation | $\int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \sum_{j,k} \vert W_x(j,k) \vert^2$ | Parseval's identity |
| 7 | Two-scale relation (wavelet) | $\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h_1[n] \, \phi(2t - n)$ | DWT filter link |
| 8 | Two-scale relation (scaling) | $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h_0[n] \, \phi(2t - n)$ | MRA link |
| 9 | Decomposition | $c_j[k] = \sum_{n} h_0[n - 2k] \, c_{j-1}[n]$ | Low-pass / approx |
| 10 | Decomposition | $d_j[k] = \sum_{n} h_1[n - 2k] \, c_{j-1}[n]$ | High-pass / detail |
| 11 | Reconstruction | $c_{j-1}[n] = \sum_{k} g_0[n - 2k] \, c_j[k] + \sum_{k} g_1[n - 2k] \, d_j[k]$ | Up-sample + filter |
| 12 | Filter link | $h_1[n] = (-1)^n \, h_0[N-1-n]$ | Quadrature mirror |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All filter-bank formulas (rows 7–12) are KTU high-yield.** In the 14-mark problems, examiners expect students to draw the *decomposition* tree, write the convolution-downsample equations, and explain the *perfect-reconstruction* condition.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **JPEG2000 image compression** — uses the **Cohen–Daubechies–Feauveau 9/7** biorthogonal DWT.
* **ECG / EEG signal compression** in biomedical engineering.
* **Power-quality disturbance detection** (sags, swells, transients) in electrical engineering.
* **Feature extraction for ML** in fault diagnostics and speech recognition.
* **Edge detection & denoising** in image processing — *thresholding DWT coefficients* suppresses noise while preserving edges.
* **Multicarrier communication** (DWT-OFDM) — replaces FFT in 5G/6G candidate waveforms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From CWT to DWT on a Dyadic Grid

**Start with the CWT definition:**

$$
CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^*\left( \frac{t-b}{a} \right) dt
$$

**Step 1:** Apply the dyadic discretisation $a = 2^j$ and $b = k \cdot 2^j$, where $j, k \in \mathbb{Z}$.

**Step 2:** Substitute $a = 2^j$ into the prefactor:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^j}} = 2^{-j/2}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $a = 2^j$ inside the wavelet argument:

$$
\frac{t - b}{a} = \frac{t - k \, 2^j}{2^j} = 2^{-j} t - k
$$

**Step 4:** Combine to obtain the discrete form:

$$
W_x(j,k) = 2^{-j/2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^*\left( 2^{-j} t - k \right) dt
$$

**Step 5:** Recognise this as the inner product of $x(t)$ with the basis function $\psi_{j,k}(t)$:

$$
W_x(j,k) = \langle x(t), \psi_{j,k}(t) \rangle, \quad \psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{-j/2} \psi(2^{-j} t - k)
$$

This completes the rigorous derivation of the DWT from the CWT under dyadic sampling.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Two-Scale (Dilation) Equation for $\phi(t)$ and $\psi(t)$

In multiresolution analysis, the scaling function $\phi(t)$ and the wavelet $\psi(t)$ are linked to a **low-pass filter $h_0$** and a **high-pass filter $h_1$** as follows.

**Step 1 — Starting relation:** The space $V_{j-1}$ is contained in $V_j \oplus W_j$ (approximation split into finer approximation + detail).

**Step 2 — Since $\phi_{j-1,k}(t)$ can be expanded in the basis $\{\phi_{j,n}(t)\}$:**

$$
\phi_{j-1,k}(t) = \sum_{n} h_0[n - 2k] \, \phi_{j,n}(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the explicit forms** $\phi_{j-1,k}(t) = 2^{-(j-1)/2}\phi(2^{-(j-1)}t - k)$ and $\phi_{j,n}(t) = 2^{-j/2}\phi(2^{-j}t - n)$, and rearrange the time index to obtain:

$$
\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h_0[n] \, \phi(2t - n)
$$

**Step 4 — Similarly, for the wavelet $\psi(t)$:**

$$
\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h_1[n] \, \phi(2t - n)
$$

**Step 5 — The Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) condition** that couples $h_0$ and $h_1$ is:

$$
h_1[n] = (-1)^n \, h_0[N - 1 - n]
$$

where $N$ is the filter length. This is the cornerstone of every orthogonal DWT filter bank.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — DWT via Filter-Bank Convolution

Given discrete samples $x[n]$ at level $j = 0$, define the **approximation coefficients** at level $j-1$ as:

$$
c_{j-1}[k] = \langle x(t), \phi_{j-1,k}(t) \rangle
$$

**Step 1 — Use the two-scale relation for $\phi_{j-1,k}$:**

$$
c_{j-1}[k] = \sum_{n} x[n] \, h_0[n - 2k]
$$

This is **convolution of $x[n]$ with $h_0$ followed by down-sampling by 2** — the standard decomposition.

**Step 2 — Similarly, the detail coefficients** at level $j$ are:

$$
d_j[k] = \sum_{n} c_{j-1}[n] \, h_1[n - 2k]
$$

**Step 3 — Compact notation** with the down-arrow denoting decimation by 2:

$$
c_j = \left( c_{j-1} * h_0 \right) \downarrow 2, \qquad d_j = \left( c_{j-1} * h_1 \right) \downarrow 2
$$

**Step 4 — Reconstruction (Inverse DWT):** Up-sample (insert zeros), then convolve with synthesis filters, then add:

$$
c_{j-1}[n] = \sum_{k} g_0[n - 2k] \, c_j[k] + \sum_{k} g_1[n - 2k] \, d_j[k]
$$

**Step 5 — Perfect reconstruction condition** (for orthogonal wavelets):

$$
G_0(z) = H_0(z^{-1}), \qquad G_1(z) = H_1(z^{-1}), \qquad H_0(z) H_0(z^{-1}) + H_1(z) H_1(z^{-1}) = 2
$$

This is the *power-complementary* condition ensuring that $x[n]$ is exactly recovered from $\{c_j, d_j\}$ for all $j$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Properties of DWT vs. CWT

| Property | CWT | DWT |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Domain of $(a,b)$ | Continuous $\mathbb{R}^+ \times \mathbb{R}$ | Discrete dyadic $2^j \times k\,2^j$ |
| Redundancy | **Highly redundant** | **Non-redundant** (orthogonal) |
| Invertibility | Frame, not unique | **Unique, orthonormal** |
| Computational cost | $O(N^2)$ | $O(N \log N)$ |
| Implementation | Numerical integration | **Filter bank (fast)** |
| Coefficients at level $j$ | $N_a \times N_b$ values | $N/2^j$ values per level |
| Time-frequency tiling | Logarithmic, continuous | Logarithmic, **dyadic** |
| Storage | Large | Compact (same as input) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of 1-Level DWT Decomposition & Reconstruction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sigX["Input Signal xn"]
    lpDec["Low-Pass Filter h0 — Analysis"]
    hpDec["High-Pass Filter h1 — Analysis"]
    dsA["Down-sample by 2 — Decimate"]
    dsD["Down-sample by 2 — Decimate"]
    cA["Approximation Coefficients cj"]
    cD["Detail Coefficients dj"]
    usA["Up-sample by 2 — Interpolate"]
    usD["Up-sample by 2 — Interpolate"]
    lpRec["Low-Pass Filter g0 — Synthesis"]
    hpRec["High-Pass Filter g1 — Synthesis"]
    sumNode["Adder — Combine Branches"]
    recX["Reconstructed Signal yn"]

    sigX --> lpDec
    sigX --> hpDec
    lpDec --> dsA --> cA
    hpDec --> dsD --> cD
    cA --> usA --> lpRec
    cD --> usD --> hpRec
    lpRec --> sumNode
    hpRec --> sumNode
    sumNode --> recX
```

---

### 4.2 Multi-Level (3-Stage) DWT Decomposition Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s0["Stage 0 — Input xn — N samples"]
    s1a["c1 — Approx — N/2"]
    s1b["d1 — Detail — N/2"]
    s2a["c2 — Approx — N/4"]
    s2b["d2 — Detail — N/4"]
    s3a["c3 — Approx — N/8"]
    s3b["d3 — Detail — N/8"]
    lp1["h0 + down 2"]
    hp1["h1 + down 2"]
    lp2["h0 + down 2"]
    hp2["h1 + down 2"]
    lp3["h0 + down 2"]
    hp3["h1 + down 2"]

    s0 --> lp1 --> s1a
    s0 --> hp1 --> s1b
    s1a --> lp2 --> s2a
    s1a --> hp2 --> s2b
    s2a --> lp3 --> s3a
    s2a --> hp3 --> s3b
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the tree:** The total coefficient count $\{c_3, d_3, d_2, d_1\}$ equals the input length $N$ (no redundancy). The deeper you go, the coarser the time resolution but the finer the frequency resolution — a direct visualisation of the **time-frequency tiling** of the DWT.

---

### 4.3 Time-Frequency Tiling Comparison: STFT vs. DWT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STFT_Tiling["STFT — Uniform Tiling"]
        a1["T1 — wide freq, wide time"]
        a2["T2 — wide freq, wide time"]
        a3["T3 — wide freq, wide time"]
        a4["T4 — wide freq, wide time"]
    end
    subgraph DWT_Tiling["DWT — Logarithmic Tiling"]
        b1["Level 1 — d1 — fine time, coarse freq"]
        b2["Level 2 — d2 — medium time, medium freq"]
        b3["Level 3 — d3 — coarse time, fine freq"]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The DWT is **uniquely suited for signals whose high-frequency content is short-duration** (transients, edges, spikes) and whose low-frequency content is long-duration (DC drift, slow oscillations) — exactly the statistics of biomedical, seismic, and audio signals.

---

### 4.4 MRA Wavelet Decomposition — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V0["Space V0 — Resolution 2^0 = 1"]
    V1["Space V1 — Resolution 2^1 = 2"]
    V2["Space V2 — Resolution 2^2 = 4"]
    V3["Space V3 — Resolution 2^3 = 8"]
    W1["Detail W1 — 1 to 2 band"]
    W2["Detail W2 — 2 to 4 band"]
    W3["Detail W3 — 4 to 8 band"]
    L0["phi0k — scaling at level 0"]
    L1["phi1k — scaling at level 1"]
    L2["phi2k — scaling at level 2"]
    PSI1["psi1k — wavelet at level 1"]
    PSI2["psi2k — wavelet at level 2"]
    PSI3["psi3k — wavelet at level 3"]

    V0 --> V1
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> V3
    V3 -- "orthogonal complement" --> W3
    V2 -- "orthogonal complement" --> W2
    V1 -- "orthogonal complement" --> W1
    L0 --> V0
    L1 --> V1
    L2 --> V2
    PSI1 --> W1
    PSI2 --> W2
    PSI3 --> W3
```

> [!NOTE]
> This MRA ladder is the **mathematical proof** that the DWT is a *perfect, lossless* decomposition. Each detail space $W_j$ carries information that is *disjoint* from the approximation $V_j$, so nothing is lost or double-counted.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State and explain the admissibility condition for a function $\psi(t)$ to be a wavelet.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A function $\psi(t) \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$ is a wavelet if and only if it satisfies the **admissibility condition**:

$$
C_\psi = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\omega} d\omega < \infty
$$

where $\Psi(\omega)$ is the Fourier transform of $\psi(t)$. This condition (i) guarantees that the inverse DWT exists, and (ii) forces $\Psi(0) = 0$, i.e. $\psi(t)$ must have **zero mean** — meaning it is a band-pass function with no DC component. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Compare CWT and DWT with respect to redundancy and computational complexity.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | CWT | DWT |
|--------|-----|-----|
| $(a,b)$ sampling | Continuous | **Dyadic discrete** $2^j, k\,2^j$ |
| Redundancy | **High** — coefficients overlap | **None** — orthogonal basis |
| Computation | $O(N^2)$ — numerical integration | $O(N \log N)$ — fast filter bank |
| Invertibility | Frame inversion (ill-posed) | **Direct, unique** via reconstruction filters |

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted] — QUESTION A**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Discrete Wavelet Transform** from the Continuous Wavelet Transform under dyadic sampling $a = 2^j, \; b = k \, 2^j$. Clearly state the admissibility and mean-zero conditions.

**(b) [7 marks]** For the signal $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$ and the Haar low-pass filter $h_0 = \{\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\}$, $h_1 = \{\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\}$, compute the **first-level approximation and detail coefficients** $\{c_1[k], d_1[k]\}$.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:**

Step 1 — Start with the CWT:

$$
CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^*\left( \frac{t-b}{a} \right) dt \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

Step 2 — Apply dyadic discretisation $a = 2^j$ and $b = k \cdot 2^j$ (1 mark for stating the choice of lattice, 1 mark for justification — *logarithmic frequency resolution matches human perception*).

Step 3 — Substitute $a = 2^j$: prefactor becomes $2^{-j/2}$ (1 mark) and argument becomes $2^{-j}t - k$ (1 mark).

Step 4 — Write the resulting DWT:

$$
W_x(j,k) = 2^{-j/2} \int x(t) \, \psi^*(2^{-j}t - k) \, dt
$$

Recognise it as an inner product with the basis $\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{-j/2}\psi(2^{-j}t - k)$. (1 mark)

Step 5 — State the two conditions: (i) admissibility $C_\psi < \infty$, (ii) mean zero $\int \psi(t)\,dt = 0$. (1 mark)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Apply the analysis filters and down-sample by 2:**

Approximation (low-pass output):

$$
c_1[0] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1 + 2) = \tfrac{3}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 2.121
$$

$$
c_1[1] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(3 + 4) = \tfrac{7}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 4.950
$$

$$
c_1[2] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(5 + 6) = \tfrac{11}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 7.778
$$

$$
c_1[3] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(7 + 8) = \tfrac{15}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 10.607
$$

Detail (high-pass output):

$$
d_1[0] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1 - 2) = -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx -0.707
$$

$$
d_1[1] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(3 - 4) = -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx -0.707
$$

$$
d_1[2] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(5 - 6) = -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx -0.707
$$

$$
d_1[3] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(7 - 8) = -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx -0.707
$$

(5 marks for the 8 numerical values; 1 mark for the correct convolution–downsample procedure; 1 mark for the final answer boxed and units consistent.)

**Final result:**

$$
c_1 = \left\{ \tfrac{3}{\sqrt{2}}, \tfrac{7}{\sqrt{2}}, \tfrac{11}{\sqrt{2}}, \tfrac{15}{\sqrt{2}} \right\}, \quad d_1 = \left\{ -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \right\}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Students frequently **forget the down-sampling by 2** and simply write convolution outputs of length 8 (the "full" convolution). Strict decimation is mandatory in DWT — failure to downsample costs **2 marks** outright. Also, *do not* swap the order of $h_0$ and $h_1$; the convention is **$h_0$ = low-pass (approximation)**, **$h_1$ = high-pass (detail)**.

---

**Q3(b) — QUESTION B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **two-scale (dilation) equation** of the scaling function $\phi(t)$ and the wavelet $\psi(t)$. Show how the discrete filters $h_0[n]$ and $h_1[n]$ are related, and derive the **Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF)** condition.

**(b) [7 marks]** Draw the **three-level DWT decomposition tree** for an input of $N = 16$ samples. State the number of coefficients at each level and verify the **Parseval energy-conservation** property in symbolic form.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**

Step 1 — In MRA, the basis functions of the coarser space $V_{j-1}$ can be expressed in the finer basis $\{\phi_{j,n}\}$:

$$
\phi_{j-1,k}(t) = \sum_{n} h_0[n - 2k] \, \phi_{j,n}(t) \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})
$$

Step 2 — Substituting the explicit forms and simplifying yields the **two-scale relation**:

$$
\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h_0[n] \, \phi(2t - n) \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

Step 3 — Similarly, the wavelet $\psi(t)$ satisfies:

$$
\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h_1[n] \, \phi(2t - n) \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

Step 4 — The QMF condition (1.5 marks): Because the spaces $V_j$ and $W_j$ are orthogonal complements, the high-pass filter is the *mirror image with alternating signs* of the low-pass filter:

$$
h_1[n] = (-1)^n \, h_0[N - 1 - n] \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

Step 5 — The power-complementary condition (1 mark):

$$
\vert H_0(\omega) \vert^2 + \vert H_1(\omega) \vert^2 = 2 \quad \text{for all } \omega
$$

Step 6 — Significance: perfect reconstruction of $x(t)$ from $\{c_j, d_j\}$ (1 mark).

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**

Step 1 — Three-level decomposition tree for $N = 16$ (2 marks for the figure — see Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2, with $N = 16$):

| Level | Output | Length |
|-------|--------|--------|
| Input | $x[n]$ | $16$ |
| Level 1 | $c_1, d_1$ | $8, 8$ |
| Level 2 | $c_2, d_2$ | $4, 4$ |
| Level 3 | $c_3, d_3$ | $2, 2$ |

(2 marks)

Step 2 — Total coefficients $= (8 + 8) + (4 + 4) + (2 + 2) = 8 + 8 + 4 + 4 + 2 + 2 = 16$ ✓ (1 mark)

Step 3 — Parseval's theorem in DWT (1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the engineering interpretation):

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \sum_{k} \vert c_3[k] \vert^2 + \sum_{j=1}^{3} \sum_{k} \vert d_j[k] \vert^2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (a) of Question B:** Students often confuse the QMF condition with the *perfect-reconstruction* condition. **QMF** = relation between $h_0$ and $h_1$. **PR (perfect reconstruction)** = relation between analysis and synthesis filters ($g_0, g_1$). Mixing them up costs **2 marks**. Also, the factor $\sqrt{2}$ in the two-scale equation is *not* optional — it is the normalisation that preserves $L^2$ energy across scales.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — DWT (Module 3)**

- **DWT definition:** $W_x(j,k) = 2^{-j/2}\int x(t)\psi^*(2^{-j}t - k)dt$, with $j,k \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- **Dyadic lattice:** $a = 2^j$, $b = k\cdot 2^j$ — *not* arbitrary; this choice is the only one that gives an **orthonormal basis** with a fast filter-bank implementation.
- **Admissibility:** $C_\psi < \infty$; consequence: $\int \psi(t)dt = 0$.
- **Mean-zero property:** $\psi$ must be a band-pass function — no DC component allowed.
- **Two-scale equation:** $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum h_0[n]\phi(2t-n)$ and $\psi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum h_1[n]\phi(2t-n)$.
- **QMF link:** $h_1[n] = (-1)^n h_0[N-1-n]$.
- **Analysis equations (decomposition):**
  * $c_j[k] = \sum_n h_0[n-2k] c_{j-1}[n]$ (low-pass, down-sampled)
  * $d_j[k] = \sum_n h_1[n-2k] c_{j-1}[n]$ (high-pass, down-sampled)
- **Synthesis equation (reconstruction):** $c_{j-1}[n] = \sum_k g_0[n-2k]c_j[k] + \sum_k g_1[n-2k]d_j[k]$.
- **Perfect-reconstruction condition:** $H_0(z)H_0(z^{-1}) + H_1(z)H_1(z^{-1}) = 2$.
- **Parseval identity:** signal energy = sum of squared DWT coefficients — **lossless transform**.
- **Time-frequency tiling:** DWT uses *logarithmic* bands — fine time resolution at high frequencies, fine frequency resolution at low frequencies.
- **Computational cost:** $O(N\log N)$ — comparable to FFT, but **non-uniform resolution**.
- **Total coefficient count equals input length** — no redundancy (orthogonal case).
- **Haar wavelet** is the simplest DWT: $h_0 = \{1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2}\}$, $h_1 = \{1/\sqrt{2}, -1/\sqrt{2}\}$.
- **Real-world applications:** JPEG2000 (CDF 9/7), ECG/EEG compression, denoising, fault detection, DWT-OFDM in 5G/6G.
- **Common KTU mistakes:** forgetting down-sampling by 2; swapping $h_0$ and $h_1$; omitting the $\sqrt{2}$ in the two-scale equation; confusing QMF and PR conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time-Frequency Tiling of DWT and Comparison to STFT - Haar Scaling and Wavelet Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Time-Frequency Tiling of DWT and Comparison to STFT - Haar Scaling and Wavelet Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: TIME-FREQUENCY TILING, DWT vs STFT & HAAR WAVELETS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Time-Frequency Tiling — The Concept

**Time-Frequency Tiling** is a graphical representation of how a time-frequency analysis method partitions the **time-frequency plane** into elementary cells. Each cell represents the resolution (support) of a basis function in both time and frequency. The shape and size of these cells reveal the *trade-off* between time resolution and frequency resolution of a transform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** A tiling diagram is a partitioning of the time-frequency plane $\mathbb{R}^2$ into rectangles of dimensions $\Delta t \times \Delta f$, where $\Delta t$ is the effective time-duration (support) and $\Delta f$ is the effective frequency-bandwidth of each basis atom.

Mathematically, the **Heisenberg uncertainty principle** enforces a lower bound on the cell area:

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \;\geq\; \frac{1}{4\pi}$$

This means we *cannot* simultaneously achieve arbitrarily fine resolution in both time and frequency. The art of designing a transform is in choosing **how** to distribute this area.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Camera vs The Microscope"

Imagine you are photographing a bird in flight.

- **STFT (Short-Time Fourier Transform) is like a standard camera with a fixed zoom lens.** Whatever you focus on, the "window" of vision is always the same size. You can see the bird in moderate detail, but you cannot simultaneously see fine feather texture (high frequency) and the full flight path (long duration).
- **DWT (Discrete Wavelet Transform) is like a multi-zoom microscope.** For high frequencies (fast feather vibrations), it uses a *short* time-window — high time resolution. For low frequencies (slow wing flaps), it uses a *long* time-window — high frequency resolution. The DWT **adapts** its resolution to what the signal is doing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The DWT gives a *logarithmic* or *multi-resolution* tiling, whereas the STFT gives a *uniform* tiling. This is the single most important distinction in Module 3.

---

### 1.3 Haar Scaling Function $\phi(t)$ — The Father Wavelet

The **Haar scaling function** (also called the *father wavelet*) is the simplest possible Daubechies-style basis function:

$$\phi(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \;\leq\; t \;<\; 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

It is a unit-height rectangular pulse on the interval $[0,1)$. It generates the **approximation subspace** $V_j$ and represents the *low-frequency* (DC / average) content of a signal.

---

### 1.4 Haar Wavelet Function $\psi(t)$ — The Mother Wavelet

The **Haar wavelet function** captures the *detail* (high-frequency) content:

$$\psi(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} +1, & 0 \;\leq\; t \;<\; \tfrac{1}{2} \\ -1, & \tfrac{1}{2} \;\leq\; t \;<\; 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Haar wavelet is so loved in KTU exams:** It is the *only* symmetric, *compactly-supported*, *orthogonal* real-valued wavelet. It is essentially a step-difference operator — the most elementary possible "change detector" in a signal.

**Intuitive Analogy:** If $\phi(t)$ is the *average height* of a child over a year, $\psi(t)$ is the *change* in that height between the first half-year and the second half-year.

---

### 1.5 The DWT vs STFT — One-Line Definitions

| Transform | KTU 2024 Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **STFT** | $X(\tau, f) \;=\; \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, w(t-\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f t}\, dt$ — fixed window $w(t)$ |
| **DWT** | $W(j,k) \;=\; \displaystyle\sum_{n} x[n]\, 2^{-j/2}\, \psi\!\left(2^{-j}n - k\right)$ — dyadic (power-of-two) scaled basis |

Both expand $x(t)$ onto a family of "atoms," but they choose the atoms very differently.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tiling Diagram — A Visual Language

A tiling diagram plots time on the horizontal axis and frequency on the vertical axis. Each rectangle's width is $\Delta t_j$ and height is $\Delta f_j$ at scale $j$.

### 2.1.1 STFT Tiling (Uniform / Fixed Grid)

For the STFT, the window $w(t)$ is **fixed**. As the modulation frequency $f$ varies, the **time duration** of every atom stays the same, and the **bandwidth** of every atom stays the same. Hence:

$$\Delta t_{STFT} \;=\; \text{constant}, \qquad \Delta f_{STFT} \;=\; \text{constant}$$

Every cell in the time-frequency plane is a **rectangle of equal area**. This is the "uniform grid" picture.

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical position of each cell corresponds to the modulation frequency $f$, and the horizontal position corresponds to the time-shift $\tau$. Higher $f \Rightarrow$ cell moves up.

### 2.1.2 DWT Tiling (Multi-Resolution / Dyadic Grid)

For the DWT, the basis is built by **scaling and translating** a single mother wavelet $\psi(t)$:

$$\psi_{j,k}(t) \;=\; 2^{j/2}\, \psi\!\left(2^{j} t - k\right)$$

- $j$ controls the **scale** (and hence frequency-band).
- $k$ controls the **translation** (and hence time-position).

The effective time-support and frequency-bandwidth at scale $j$ are:

$$\Delta t_j \;=\; 2^{-j}\, \Delta t_0, \qquad \Delta f_j \;=\; 2^{j}\, \Delta f_0$$

So the cell area remains $\Delta t_j \cdot \Delta f_j = \Delta t_0 \cdot \Delta f_0$ (uncertainty principle is respected), but the *shape* changes. At **high scales** (low $j$), cells are **tall and narrow** — good time resolution. At **low scales** (high $j$), cells are **short and wide** — good frequency resolution.

---

## 2.2 The Haar System — Rigorous Setup

### 2.2.1 Scaling Function and Its Properties

$$\phi(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{elsewhere} \end{cases}$$

**Properties (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**
- $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \phi(t)\, dt \;=\; 1$ (unit area)
- Support: $\text{supp}(\phi) = [0, 1)$
- $\hat{\phi}(f) \;=\; \text{sinc}(f)\, e^{-j\pi f}$ — its Fourier transform is a sinc, **not band-limited**
- $\int \phi(t) \phi(t-n)\, dt \;=\; \delta[n]$ (orthogonal shifts)
- $\int \phi(t) \psi(t-n)\, dt \;=\; 0$ (wavelet is orthogonal to scaling)

### 2.2.2 Wavelet Function and Its Properties

$$\psi(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} +1, & 0 \leq t < 1/2 \\ -1, & 1/2 \leq t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Properties:**
- $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t)\, dt \;=\; 0$ (zero mean — admissibility)
- Support: $\text{supp}(\psi) = [0, 1)$
- $\hat{\psi}(f) \;=\; j\, e^{-j\pi f}\, \text{sinc}^2(f/2)$ (vanishes at $f=0$)
- $\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{j/2}\psi(2^j t - k)$ forms an **orthonormal basis** of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$.

### 2.2.3 Two-Scale (Refinement / Dilation) Equations

The Haar basis obeys the **two-scale relations** — the foundation of Mallat's algorithm:

$$\phi(t) \;=\; \phi(2t) \;+\; \phi(2t-1)$$

$$\psi(t) \;=\; \phi(2t) \;-\; \phi(2t-1)$$

In the standard filter-bank form:

$$\phi(t) \;=\; \sqrt{2}\sum_{n} h_0[n]\, \phi(2t - n), \qquad h_0[n] = \begin{cases} 1/\sqrt{2}, & n = 0 \\ 1/\sqrt{2}, & n = 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

$$\psi(t) \;=\; \sqrt{2}\sum_{n} h_1[n]\, \phi(2t - n), \qquad h_1[n] = \begin{cases} 1/\sqrt{2}, & n = 0 \\ -1/\sqrt{2}, & n = 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $h_0$ is the **low-pass filter** and $h_1$ is the **high-pass filter** of the Haar DWT. The relationship $h_1[n] = (-1)^n h_0[N-1-n]$ (Quadrature Mirror Filter relation) is satisfied with $N=2$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | STFT | $X(\tau,f) = \int x(t) w(t-\tau) e^{-j2\pi f t}\, dt$ | Window $w(t)$ fixed |
| 2 | DWT (discrete) | $W(j,k) = \sum_{n} x[n]\, 2^{-j/2}\psi(2^{-j}n - k)$ | Dyadic scaling |
| 3 | Heisenberg bound | $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq 1/(4\pi)$ | All atoms obey this |
| 4 | STFT cell area | $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f = \text{const}$ | All cells equal |
| 5 | DWT cell area | $\Delta t_j \cdot \Delta f_j = \text{const}$ | Shape changes, area constant |
| 6 | Haar scaling | $\phi(t) = 1$ on $[0,1)$ | Rectangular pulse |
| 7 | Haar wavelet | $\psi(t) = +1$ on $[0,1/2)$, $-1$ on $[1/2,1)$ | Step-difference |
| 8 | Two-scale (scaling) | $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum h_0[n]\phi(2t-n)$ | Refinement equation |
| 9 | Two-scale (wavelet) | $\psi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum h_1[n]\phi(2t-n)$ | Wavelet from scaling |
| 10 | Haar $h_0$ | $h_0 = [1/\sqrt{2},\ 1/\sqrt{2}]$ | Low-pass coefficients |
| 11 | Haar $h_1$ | $h_1 = [1/\sqrt{2},\ -1/\sqrt{2}]$ | High-pass coefficients |
| 12 | QMF relation | $h_1[n] = (-1)^n h_0[N-1-n]$ | Holds for Haar ($N=2$) |
| 13 | Admissibility | $\int \psi(t)\, dt = 0$ | Required for inversion |
| 14 | DWT time-support | $\Delta t_j = 2^{-j} \Delta t_0$ | Shrinks with increasing $j$ |
| 15 | DWT freq-bandwidth | $\Delta f_j = 2^{j} \Delta f_0$ | Widens with increasing $j$ |
| 16 | Energy preservation | $\sum_k \vert W(j,k)\vert^2 \approx \sum_n \vert x[n]\vert^2$ | Parseval-like for DWT |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** In the table above, all occurrences of $\Delta t$, $\Delta f$ and $\vert W \vert$ use the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\Delta` — never a raw pipe `|` character — so the markdown table renders without breaking.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Use of DWT Tiling & Haar |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Biomedical (ECG/EEG)** | Detects QRS spikes with high time-resolution; Haar is computationally trivial for real-time monitors. |
| **Image compression (JPEG2000)** | Uses CDF 5/3 (Haar-like) and CDF 9/7 wavelets; multi-resolution tiling matches human perception. |
| **Power systems** | Haar detects transient disturbances (sags, swells) — short windows for high-frequency events. |
| **Communication (OFDM)** | Wavelet packets give variable tiling to combat frequency-selective fading. |
| **Speech processing** | Low frequencies (vowels) get wide time-windows; high frequencies (consonants) get narrow windows. |
| **Geophysics** | Multi-resolution analysis of seismic traces — both broad structure and sharp arrivals. |
