# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proving the Two-Scale Relation for $\phi(t)$

**Goal:** Show that $\phi(t) = \phi(2t) + \phi(2t-1)$.

**Step 1 — Recall the definition.**

$$\phi(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{elsewhere} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $2t$ into the definition.**

$$\phi(2t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq 2t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{elsewhere} \end{cases} = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t < 1/2 \\ 0, & \text{elsewhere} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $2t-1$.**

$$\phi(2t-1) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq 2t-1 < 1 \\ 0, & \text{elsewhere} \end{cases} = \begin{cases} 1, & 1/2 \leq t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{elsewhere} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Add the two.**

On $[0, 1/2)$: only $\phi(2t) = 1$, so sum = 1.
On $[1/2, 1)$: only $\phi(2t-1) = 1$, so sum = 1.
Elsewhere: both are 0, so sum = 0.

**Step 5 — Compare with the original $\phi(t)$.**

The sum equals 1 on $[0,1)$ and 0 elsewhere — *exactly* $\phi(t)$. Q.E.D.

$$\phi(t) \;=\; \phi(2t) \;+\; \phi(2t-1) \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Two-Scale Relation for $\psi(t)$

**Goal:** Show that $\psi(t) = \phi(2t) - \phi(2t-1)$.

**Step 1 — Recall $\psi(t)$.**

$$\psi(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & 0 \leq t < 1/2 \\ -1, & 1/2 \leq t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{elsewhere} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Use the previously derived results for $\phi(2t)$ and $\phi(2t-1)$.**

From Derivation 1:
- $\phi(2t) = 1$ on $[0, 1/2)$, else 0.
- $\phi(2t-1) = 1$ on $[1/2, 1)$, else 0.

**Step 3 — Subtract.**

On $[0, 1/2)$: $\phi(2t) - \phi(2t-1) = 1 - 0 = +1$. ✓
On $[1/2, 1)$: $\phi(2t) - \phi(2t-1) = 0 - 1 = -1$. ✓
Elsewhere: $0 - 0 = 0$. ✓

**Step 4 — Conclude.**

$$\psi(t) \;=\; \phi(2t) \;-\; \phi(2t-1) \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Extraction of Filter Coefficients

**Goal:** Express $\phi(t)$ in the form $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum h_0[n]\phi(2t - n)$ and find $h_0$.

**Step 1 — Start from the integer form.**

The two-scale relation we proved is:

$$\phi(t) = \phi(2t) + \phi(2t-1)$$

This can be written as a sum over integer $n$:

$$\phi(t) = \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} c_n\, \phi(2t - n), \qquad c_0 = 1,\ c_1 = 1,\ \text{others} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Convert to orthonormal form.**

In the standard DWT notation, the scaling function expansion uses the **normalised** coefficients $h_0[n] = c_n / \sqrt{2}$, because the inner-product structure requires:

$$\langle \phi_{0,0},\ \phi_{0,0}\rangle = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_{n} \vert h_0[n]\vert^2 = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum \vert c_n \vert^2 = 2$$

**Step 3 — Solve.**

We have $\sum c_n^2 = 1^2 + 1^2 = 2$. So the normalisation $\sum h_0^2 = 1$ is consistent.

$$h_0[0] = \frac{c_0}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \qquad h_0[1] = \frac{c_1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Repeat for $h_1$.**

From $\psi(t) = \phi(2t) - \phi(2t-1)$:

$$h_1[0] = \frac{+1}{\sqrt{2}}, \qquad h_1[1] = \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Verify QMF relation.**

$$h_1[n] = (-1)^n h_0[N-1-n] \quad \text{with } N=2$$

For $n=0$: $h_1[0] = (+1) h_0[1] = (1/\sqrt{2})$. ✓
For $n=1$: $h_1[1] = (-1) h_0[0] = -(1/\sqrt{2})$. ✓

$$\boxed{h_0 = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}},\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right], \qquad h_1 = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}},\ -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy (Parseval) Identity for DWT

**Goal:** Show that the DWT preserves energy: $\sum_{j,k} \vert W(j,k)\vert^2 = \sum_{n} \vert x[n]\vert^2$.

**Step 1 — Express $x[n]$ via inverse DWT.**

$$x[n] = \sum_{j,k} W(j,k)\, \psi_{j,k}[n]$$

where $\psi_{j,k}[n] = 2^{j/2} \psi(2^j n - k)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the energy of $x$.**

$$E_x = \sum_n \vert x[n]\vert^2 = \sum_n x[n]\, x^*[n]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the inverse expansion and use orthonormality.**

Because $\{\psi_{j,k}\}$ is an orthonormal basis of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$:

$$\sum_n \psi_{j,k}[n]\, \psi^*_{j',k'}[n] = \delta[j-j']\, \delta[k-k']$$

This is the *unitarity* property of the wavelet basis.

**Step 4 — Conclude.**

After substitution, every cross-term vanishes and we obtain:

$$\sum_n \vert x[n]\vert^2 = \sum_{j,k} \vert W(j,k)\vert^2 \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Parseval-like identity** for the DWT. It guarantees that the Haar DWT is a *lossless, orthonormal* transform — vital for compression.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — STFT vs DWT

| Property | STFT | DWT (Haar / General) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Tiling shape** | Uniform rectangles (constant $\Delta t$, $\Delta f$) | Logarithmic (varying $\Delta t_j$, $\Delta f_j$) |
| **Time resolution at high $f$** | Fixed (sub-optimal) | Good (narrow windows) |
| **Freq resolution at low $f$** | Fixed (sub-optimal) | Good (wide windows) |
| **Basis type** | Windowed complex exponentials $w(t-\tau)e^{j2\pi ft}$ | Scaled & translated wavelets $\psi_{j,k}(t)$ |
| **Redundancy** | High (overlapping windows) | None (orthonormal) — for standard DWT |
| **Computational cost** | $O(N \log N)$ via FFT | $O(N)$ via Mallat's filter bank |
| **Best suited for** | Stationary / quasi-stationary signals | Non-stationary, transient signals |
| **Inverse** | Windowed overlap-add | Direct from filter bank |
| **Choice of basis** | Only window $w(t)$ | Wide family: Haar, Daubechies, Symlet, Coiflet |
| **Number of coefficients** | $N \times M$ (over-complete) | $N$ (critically sampled) |
| **Localized in** | Both time and frequency (but uniformly) | Both time and frequency (adaptively) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**"Compare the time-frequency tiling of the STFT and the DWT. Which one is more suitable for analysing non-stationary signals and why?" (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The STFT partitions the time-frequency plane into a **uniform grid** of rectangles — the window $w(t)$ is fixed, so the time-resolution $\Delta t$ and frequency-resolution $\Delta f$ are *constant* across all frequencies. This is sub-optimal because high-frequency transients need short windows and low-frequency components need long windows.

The DWT partitions the plane into a **logarithmic / dyadic grid** where cells *change shape* with scale. At scale $j$, the time-support is $\Delta t_j = 2^{-j} \Delta t_0$ and the frequency-bandwidth is $\Delta f_j = 2^{j} \Delta f_0$, but the cell area $\Delta t_j \cdot \Delta f_j$ is preserved (Heisenberg bound). For non-stationary signals, the DWT is superior because it **adapts** its resolution — narrow windows for high frequencies and wide windows for low frequencies. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**"Define the Haar scaling function $\phi(t)$ and Haar wavelet function $\psi(t)$. State any two properties of each." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Haar scaling function:**

$$\phi(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Properties:** (i) $\int \phi(t)\, dt = 1$ (unit area); (ii) Its integer shifts $\{\phi(t-n)\}$ are mutually orthogonal: $\int \phi(t)\phi(t-n)\, dt = \delta[n]$. **(1.5 Marks)**

**Haar wavelet function:**

$$\psi(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & 0 \leq t < 1/2 \\ -1, & 1/2 \leq t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Properties:** (i) Zero mean, $\int \psi(t)\, dt = 0$ (admissibility condition); (ii) Orthogonal to all scaling-function shifts, $\int \psi(t)\phi(t-n)\, dt = 0$. **(1.5 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the time-frequency tiling diagrams of the STFT and the DWT. Highlight the key differences in the resolution achieved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the definitions of the Haar scaling and wavelet functions, derive the two-scale (refinement) relations and extract the filter coefficients $h_0[n]$ and $h_1[n]$. Show that the QMF relation $h_1[n] = (-1)^n h_0[N-1-n]$ holds. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to A(a):**

**STFT tiling:** Plot time $t$ on the horizontal axis and frequency $f$ on the vertical. For a fixed window $w(t)$ of duration $T_w$, the time-resolution is $\Delta t = T_w$ for *all* frequencies. The frequency resolution is $\Delta f = 1/T_w$ for *all* times. Each cell in the time-frequency plane is therefore a **rectangle of fixed dimensions** $T_w \times (1/T_w)$.

**DWT tiling:** For the DWT, the basis atoms $\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{j/2}\psi(2^j t - k)$ have time-support $\Delta t_j = 2^{-j} T_0$ and bandwidth $\Delta f_j = 2^{j} B_0$. The cells form a **logarithmic / dyadic** pattern: at the *top* of the plane (high frequencies, small $j$), cells are *narrow* in time and *wide* in frequency — good for transients. At the *bottom* (low frequencies, large $j$), cells are *wide* in time and *narrow* in frequency — good for slow trends.

**Key differences:**
1. STFT cells are *uniform*; DWT cells are *logarithmically spaced*.
2. STFT has a fixed resolution trade-off; DWT adapts the trade-off to frequency.
3. DWT matches the non-stationary nature of most real-world signals (speech, ECG, music).
4. Both satisfy the Heisenberg bound $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq 1/(4\pi)$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1 — Two-scale relation for $\phi(t)$.**

Substitute $2t$ in the definition: $\phi(2t) = 1$ on $[0, 1/2)$, else $0$.
Substitute $2t-1$ in the definition: $\phi(2t-1) = 1$ on $[1/2, 1)$, else $0$.

Add the two: the sum is $1$ on $[0, 1)$ and $0$ elsewhere — exactly $\phi(t)$.

$$\phi(t) = \phi(2t) + \phi(2t-1) \quad \text{...(i)} \qquad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Two-scale relation for $\psi(t)$.**

Similarly subtract: on $[0, 1/2)$, $\phi(2t) - \phi(2t-1) = 1 - 0 = +1$; on $[1/2, 1)$, $0 - 1 = -1$. This is exactly $\psi(t)$.

$$\psi(t) = \phi(2t) - \phi(2t-1) \quad \text{...(ii)} \qquad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Extract filter coefficients.**

Equation (i) can be written as $\phi(t) = \sum_{n} c_n \phi(2t - n)$ with $c_0 = c_1 = 1$, others zero. Normalising by $\sqrt{2}$ to satisfy $\sum h_0^2[n] = 1$:

$$h_0[0] = h_0[1] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \qquad \text{(1.5 Marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Extract $h_1$.**

From (ii): $c_0 = +1, c_1 = -1$, so:

$$h_1[0] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \qquad h_1[1] = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \qquad \text{(1.5 Marks)}$$

**Step 5 — Verify QMF relation.**

For $N=2$: $h_1[n] = (-1)^n h_0[1-n]$.

- $n=0$: $h_1[0] = (+1) \cdot h_0[1] = 1/\sqrt{2}$. ✓
- $n=1$: $h_1[1] = (-1) \cdot h_0[0] = -1/\sqrt{2}$. ✓

Hence the QMF relation holds. **(2 Marks)**

---

### **Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the construction of the Haar wavelet basis. Show that $\{\psi_{j,k}(t)\}$ forms an orthonormal basis of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ and state the admissibility condition. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the signal $x[n] = [4,\ 2,\ 6,\ 4,\ 8,\ 6,\ 10,\ 8]$, perform a *single-level* Haar DWT and verify the Parseval energy identity $\sum_n \vert x[n]\vert^2 = \sum_k \vert a_1[k]\vert^2 + \sum_k \vert d_1[k]\vert^2$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to B(a):**

**Construction:** Start with the Haar scaling function $\phi(t) = 1$ on $[0, 1)$ and the Haar wavelet $\psi(t) = +1$ on $[0, 1/2)$ and $-1$ on $[1/2, 1)$. For integers $j$ (scale) and $k$ (translation), define:

$$\phi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{j/2}\, \phi(2^j t - k), \qquad \psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{j/2}\, \psi(2^j t - k)$$

**Orthonormality — three conditions:**

(i) $\int \phi_{j,k}(t) \phi_{j,k'}(t) dt = \delta[k - k']$ — *time-shifts* of $\phi$ at the same scale are orthogonal. (1 Mark)
(ii) $\int \psi_{j,k}(t) \psi_{j',k'}(t) dt = \delta[j-j'] \delta[k-k']$ — wavelets at *different* scales and shifts are orthogonal. (2 Marks)
(iii) $\int \phi_{j,k}(t) \psi_{j',k'}(t) dt = 0$ — scaling and wavelet subspaces are orthogonal. (1 Mark)

Together, the collection $\{\psi_{j,k}\}_{j,k\in\mathbb{Z}}$ spans $L^2(\mathbb{R})$, so it is an **orthonormal basis**. (1 Mark)

**Admissibility condition:**

For the inverse DWT to exist, the wavelet must satisfy:

$$C_\psi = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \hat{\psi}(f)\vert^2}{f}\, df \;<\; \infty$$

A sufficient condition is $\int \psi(t)\, dt = 0$, i.e., the wavelet has **zero mean** (no DC component). The Haar wavelet clearly satisfies this since its positive and negative halves cancel. **(2 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to B(b):**

**Given:** $x[n] = [4,\ 2,\ 6,\ 4,\ 8,\ 6,\ 10,\ 8]$, length 8.

**Haar filters:** $h_0 = [1/\sqrt{2},\ 1/\sqrt{2}]$, $h_1 = [1/\sqrt{2},\ -1/\sqrt{2}]$.

**Step 1 — Compute approximation coefficients $a_1[k]$ (low-pass + downsample by 2):**

$$a_1[0] = \frac{x[0] + x[1]}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4 + 2}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{6}{\sqrt{2}} = 3\sqrt{2}$$

$$a_1[1] = \frac{x[2] + x[3]}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{6 + 4}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{2}} = 5\sqrt{2}$$

$$a_1[2] = \frac{x[4] + x[5]}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{8 + 6}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{14}{\sqrt{2}} = 7\sqrt{2}$$

$$a_1[3] = \frac{x[6] + x[7]}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{10 + 8}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{18}{\sqrt{2}} = 9\sqrt{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute detail coefficients $d_1[k]$ (high-pass + downsample by 2):**

$$d_1[0] = \frac{x[0] - x[1]}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4 - 2}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2}$$

$$d_1[1] = \frac{x[2] - x[3]}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{6 - 4}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2}$$

$$d_1[2] = \frac{x[4] - x[5]}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{8 - 6}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2}$$

$$d_1[3] = \frac{x[6] - x[7]}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{10 - 8}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2}$$

**(4 Marks for steps 1 and 2)**

**Step 3 — Verify Parseval identity.**

Energy of $x$:

$$E_x = 4^2 + 2^2 + 6^2 + 4^2 + 8^2 + 6^2 + 10^2 + 8^2 = 16+4+36+16+64+36+100+64 = 336$$

Energy of approximation:

$$E_a = (3\sqrt{2})^2 + (5\sqrt{2})^2 + (7\sqrt{2})^2 + (9\sqrt{2})^2 = 18 + 50 + 98 + 162 = 328$$

Energy of detail:

$$E_d = (\sqrt{2})^2 + (\sqrt{2})^2 + (\sqrt{2})^2 + (\sqrt{2})^2 = 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 8$$

Total: $E_a + E_d = 328 + 8 = 336 = E_x$. ✓ **(3 Marks)**

The Parseval identity is verified — the Haar DWT is energy-preserving (orthonormal transform).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT** forget the $1/\sqrt{2}$ factor in $h_0$ and $h_1$. A common mistake is to use $h_0 = [0.5, 0.5]$ (ortho-normalised form) — the KTU 2024 scheme expects the **orthonormal** $1/\sqrt{2}$ version because the question asks for an orthonormal DWT.
> 2. **Do NOT** draw the DWT tiling with *equal* cells — examiners *immediately* deduct 2 marks if cells are drawn as uniform rectangles instead of dyadically varying ones.
> 3. **Do NOT** confuse the *Haar scaling function* with the *Haar wavelet*. The scaling function is *always* non-negative; the wavelet has a *sign change*. Many students swap them and lose 1 mark instantly.
> 4. In the Parseval verification, **show all arithmetic** — partial arithmetic without intermediate sums loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 Sub-Topic**

- [ ] **STFT tiling** = uniform rectangles in the time-frequency plane (fixed $\Delta t$, fixed $\Delta f$).
- [ ] **DWT tiling** = logarithmic / dyadic rectangles (varying $\Delta t_j$, varying $\Delta f_j$, constant area).
- [ ] **Heisenberg bound** $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq 1/(4\pi)$ — no transform can beat this.
- [ ] **Haar scaling function** $\phi(t) = 1$ on $[0, 1)$ — represents low-frequency / average content.
- [ ] **Haar wavelet function** $\psi(t) = +1$ on $[0, 1/2)$, $-1$ on $[1/2, 1)$ — represents detail / change.
- [ ] **Two-scale relations:** $\phi(t) = \phi(2t) + \phi(2t-1)$ and $\psi(t) = \phi(2t) - \phi(2t-1)$.
- [ ] **Haar filters:** $h_0 = [1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2}]$, $h_1 = [1/\sqrt{2}, -1/\sqrt{2}]$.
- [ ] **QMF relation** $h_1[n] = (-1)^n h_0[N-1-n]$ holds for Haar with $N=2$.
- [ ] **Admissibility:** $\int \psi(t)\, dt = 0$ (zero mean, no DC).
- [ ] **Orthonormality:** $\{\psi_{j,k}\}$ is an orthonormal basis of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$.
- [ ] **Parseval energy preservation:** $\sum_n \vert x[n]\vert^2 = \sum_{j,k} \vert W(j,k)\vert^2$.
- [ ] **DWT for non-stationary signals > STFT** because it adapts window size to frequency.
- [ ] **Mallat's algorithm** = iterative low-pass / high-pass filtering + decimation by 2.
- [ ] **At scale $j$:** time-support $\Delta t_j = 2^{-j} T_0$, bandwidth $\Delta f_j = 2^{j} B_0$.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 favourite question pattern:** "Compare DWT and STFT tiling" + "Derive Haar two-scale relation" appear every semester — master these for full marks.
- [ ] **Symmetry bonus:** Haar is the *only* real, symmetric, compactly-supported, orthogonal wavelet — quote this in viva.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Function Spaces
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Function Spaces

# 📘 KTU PREMIUM NOTES — Function Spaces

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Function Spaces** are collections (sets) of functions that share a common mathematical structure — usually a **norm**, **inner product**, or **metric** — which allows us to measure the "size", "energy", or "smoothness" of functions and compare them in a meaningful way.

### 1.1 Formal Definition

Let $\mathcal{F}$ denote a family of functions defined on a domain $\mathcal{D} \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ (or $\mathbb{R}^n$). A **function space** is a vector space $\mathcal{F}$ equipped with one or more of the following structures:

- A **norm** $\Vert \cdot \Vert : \mathcal{F} \to [0, \infty)$
- An **inner product** $\langle \cdot , \cdot \rangle : \mathcal{F} \times \mathcal{F} \to \mathbb{C}$
- A **metric** $d : \mathcal{F} \times \mathcal{F} \to [0, \infty)$

In the context of **wavelets and multirate analysis**, the most relevant function spaces are:
1. **Lebesgue spaces** $L^p(\mathbb{R})$ — particularly $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ (the natural habitat of wavelets)
2. **Sequence spaces** $\ell^p(\mathbb{Z})$ — for discrete filter coefficients
3. **Sobolev spaces** $H^s(\mathbb{R})$ — for measuring function regularity
4. **Besov spaces** $B^s_{p,q}(\mathbb{R})$ — the *unconditional basis* territory of wavelets

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of function spaces as "neighborhoods" of functions with a common character.**

Imagine a city where each "resident" is a function. We can group residents by:
- **How much energy they have** → $L^2$ space (finite-energy functions, like audio signals with bounded power)
- **How bounded they are** → $L^\infty$ space (functions that never exceed a ceiling, like a clipped signal)
- **How smooth they are** → Sobolev spaces (functions that don't wiggle too violently, like a smooth curve)
- **How detailed they are at different scales** → Besov spaces (functions described by scale-by-scale detail)

Just as a **hotel** classifies guests by amenities, a function space classifies functions by measurable properties. The wavelets $\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{j/2}\psi(2^j t - k)$ and scaling functions $\phi_{j,k}(t)$ form **bases** for these spaces, allowing any function in the space to be expressed as an (infinite) linear combination of wavelets.

### 1.3 Why Function Spaces Matter in Wavelet Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Wavelets are not just a tool — they are a **coordinate system for function spaces**. The deep theorem of Daubechies, Meyer, and Mallat states:
>
> *A wavelet basis gives an unconditional basis for certain Besov spaces, and the wavelet coefficients themselves live in sequence spaces $\ell^p$.*

This means **function spaces ↔ sequence spaces** under wavelet transforms, which is the engine behind compression, denoising, and feature extraction in modern signal processing.

### 1.4 Key Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Hölder exponent** $s$ — measures smoothness, with **critical value** $s = n/2 - 1/p$ for embedding theorems
- **Norm constants** — $C_p$ in $\Vert f \Vert_p$ definitions, with $C_2 = 1$ (Parseval's identity)
- **Basis constant** $B$ — the smallest constant such that $B^{-1}\Vert f \Vert \le \sum \vert c_k \vert \le B \Vert f \Vert$ for a basis expansion
- **Sampling rate** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ — Nyquist in $L^2$ context

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Lebesgue Space $L^p(\mathbb{R})$

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** $L^p(\mathbb{R}) = \left\{ f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{C} \;\Big|\; \int_{\mathbb{R}} \vert f(x) \vert^p \, dx < \infty \right\}$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. We want to measure the "size" of a function $f$ over $\mathbb{R}$.
2. Raising $\vert f \vert$ to the $p$-th power ensures **non-negativity**.
3. The integral forces the "tail" of $f$ to decay sufficiently — large $f$ over an infinite domain must be offset by rapid decay.
4. The **norm** quantifies the size:

$$\Vert f \Vert_p = \left( \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert f(x) \vert^p \, dx \right)^{1/p}$$

5. Two functions are identified if they are equal **almost everywhere** (a.e.) — this avoids the "set of measure zero" pathology.

**Special cases (KTU favourites):**

- **$p = 1$**: $\Vert f \Vert_1 = \int \vert f \vert \, dx$ — total area under the magnitude
- **$p = 2$**: $\Vert f \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\int \vert f \vert^2 \, dx}$ — **energy** of the signal; this is the **Hilbert space** with inner product $\langle f, g \rangle = \int f \overline{g} \, dx$
- **$p = \infty$**: $\Vert f \Vert_\infty = \text{ess sup} \vert f(x) \vert$ — peak magnitude

### 2.2 The Sequence Space $\ell^p(\mathbb{Z})$

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** $\ell^p(\mathbb{Z}) = \left\{ (a_k)_{k \in \mathbb{Z}} \;\Big|\; \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert a_k \vert^p < \infty \right\}$

**Why it matters:** When we expand a function in a wavelet basis,

$$f(t) = \sum_{j,k} c_{j,k} \, \psi_{j,k}(t)$$

the **coefficients** $c_{j,k}$ form a doubly-indexed sequence. For $f \in L^2$, the orthonormality of $\psi_{j,k}$ gives $c_{j,k} = \langle f, \psi_{j,k} \rangle$, and the energy identity is:

$$\Vert f \Vert_2^2 = \sum_{j,k} \vert c_{j,k} \vert^2 \quad \text{(Parseval's frame equality)}$$

So **$L^2$ ↔ $\ell^2$** under a wavelet basis — a deep and beautiful result.

### 2.3 Sobolev Space $H^s(\mathbb{R})$ — Regularity Space

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The Sobolev space of order $s \in \mathbb{R}$ is

$$H^s(\mathbb{R}) = \left\{ f \in L^2(\mathbb{R}) \;\Big|\; \int_{\mathbb{R}} (1 + \vert \omega \vert^2)^s \, \vert \hat{f}(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega < \infty \right\}$$

with the Sobolev norm:

$$\Vert f \Vert_{H^s} = \left( \int_{\mathbb{R}} (1 + \omega^2)^s \, \vert \hat{f}(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega \right)^{1/2}$$

**The "Why":**
- The weight $(1 + \omega^2)^s$ **penalizes high frequencies** when $s > 0$ — so $H^s$ functions are smoother.
- For $s > 1/2$, functions are continuous; for $s > 1$, they are continuously differentiable.
- The **mother wavelet** $\psi$ itself must belong to some $H^s$ to ensure the basis inherits the regularity of the space.

### 2.4 Besov Space $B^s_{p,q}(\mathbb{R})$ — The Wavelet's Natural Home

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (via wavelet coefficients):** A function $f$ belongs to the Besov space $B^s_{p,q}(\mathbb{R})$ if

$$\Vert f \Vert_{B^s_{p,q}} = \left( \sum_{j=-\infty}^{\infty} 2^{j(s + 1/2 - 1/p)q} \left( \sum_{k \in \mathbb{Z}} \vert c_{j,k} \vert^p \right)^{q/p} \right)^{1/q} < \infty$$

where $c_{j,k} = \langle f, \psi_{j,k} \rangle$ are the wavelet coefficients.

**The magic:** This definition directly uses wavelets! It says a function is in $B^s_{p,q}$ if its **detail coefficients decay at scale $j$ with rate $2^{j(s+1/2-1/p)}$**. This is why wavelets are called "atomic decompositions" of Besov spaces.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Space** | **Norm / Metric** | **Inner Product (if any)** | **Completeness** | **Wavelet Role** |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------------|------------------|------------------|
| $L^1(\mathbb{R})$ | $\Vert f \Vert_1 = \int \vert f \vert \, dx$ | — | Banach | Integrability of scaling function |
| $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ | $\Vert f \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\int \vert f \vert^2 \, dx}$ | $\langle f,g \rangle = \int f \bar{g} \, dx$ | **Hilbert** | Native space of orthonormal wavelets |
| $L^\infty(\mathbb{R})$ | $\Vert f \Vert_\infty = \text{ess sup}\vert f \vert$ | — | Banach | Bound on $\phi, \psi$ for stability |
| $\ell^1(\mathbb{Z})$ | $\Vert a \Vert_1 = \sum \vert a_k \vert$ | — | Banach | Sum-absolute filter coefficients |
| $\ell^2(\mathbb{Z})$ | $\Vert a \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum \vert a_k \vert^2}$ | $\langle a,b \rangle = \sum a_k \bar{b}_k$ | **Hilbert** | Energy of wavelet coefficients |
| $\ell^\infty(\mathbb{Z})$ | $\Vert a \Vert_\infty = \sup \vert a_k \vert$ | — | Banach | BIBO stability of filters |
| $H^s(\mathbb{R})$ | $\Vert f \Vert_{H^s} = \Vert (1+\omega^2)^{s/2} \hat{f} \Vert_2$ | Same as $L^2$ on weighted $\hat{f}$ | Hilbert | Regularity of $\psi$ (vanishing moments) |
| $B^s_{p,q}(\mathbb{R})$ | $\Vert f \Vert_{B^s_{p,q}}$ (see §2.4) | — | Banach | Native characterisation space for wavelets |

| **Key Identity / Theorem** | **Statement** |
|----------------------------|---------------|
| Parseval's Theorem | $\Vert f \Vert_2^2 = \sum_{j,k} \vert \langle f, \psi_{j,k} \rangle \vert^2$ |
| Hölder's Inequality | $\int \vert fg \vert \le \Vert f \Vert_p \, \Vert g \Vert_q$, $\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$ |
| Minkowski's Inequality | $\Vert f + g \Vert_p \le \Vert f \Vert_p + \Vert g \Vert_p$ |
| Cauchy–Schwarz | $\vert \langle f,g \rangle \vert \le \Vert f \Vert_2 \, \Vert g \Vert_2$ |
| Fourier $L^2$ Isometry | $\Vert \hat{f} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{2\pi} \, \Vert f \Vert_2$ |
| Besov Embedding | $B^s_{p,q} \hookrightarrow L^{p^*}$ where $\frac{1}{p^*} = \frac{1}{p} - \frac{s}{n}$ (if $s > n/p$) |
| Sobolev Embedding | $H^s \hookrightarrow L^{p^*}$ for $s > n/2$ |
| Riesz Basis Criterion | $\{\psi_{j,k}\}$ is a Riesz basis of $L^2$ iff $\exists A,B>0 : A \Vert f \Vert_2^2 \le \sum \vert c_{j,k} \vert^2 \le B \Vert f \Vert_2^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** $L^2$ is the universe of finite-energy signals (audio, images). $H^s$ controls the **smoothness** of reconstructed signals (e.g., Daubechies-DB4 has $s \approx 1.5$). $B^s_{p,q}$ is the natural space for **sparse representations** — JPEG2000 and denoising operate in Besov-type spaces.

### 2.6 Hierarchical Relationships (inclusion map)

$$C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R}) \subset L^p(\mathbb{R}) \text{ for all } 1 \le p \le \infty$$

$$H^s(\mathbb{R}) \subset L^2(\mathbb{R}) \quad \forall s \ge 0$$

$$B^s_{2,2}(\mathbb{R}) = H^s(\mathbb{R}) \quad \text{(Besov-Sobolev coincidence!)}$$

This last identity is **golden** for KTU — when $p = q = 2$, the Besov space collapses to the Sobolev space. This is why wavelets diagonalize the Sobolev norm.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Verification that $L^2$ is an Inner Product Space

**Claim:** $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ with $\langle f, g \rangle = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \overline{g(x)} \, dx$ is an inner product space.

**Step 1 — Conjugate symmetry.** For any $f, g \in L^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle f, g \rangle &= \int f \overline{g} \, dx \\
&= \overline{\int \overline{f} g \, dx} \\
&= \overline{\langle g, f \rangle}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Conjugation commutes with the integral (finite for $L^2$ functions by Cauchy–Schwarz), and $\overline{\overline{f}g} = f\bar{g}$.

**Step 2 — Linearity in the first argument.** For scalars $\alpha, \beta$ and $f_1, f_2, g \in L^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle \alpha f_1 + \beta f_2, g \rangle &= \int (\alpha f_1 + \beta f_2)\overline{g}\,dx \\
&= \alpha \int f_1 \bar{g}\,dx + \beta \int f_2 \bar{g}\,dx \\
&= \alpha \langle f_1, g \rangle + \beta \langle f_2, g \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Integral is linear over the sum, and constants factor out.

**Step 3 — Positive definiteness.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle f, f \rangle &= \int f(x)\overline{f(x)}\,dx = \int \vert f(x) \vert^2 \, dx = \Vert f \Vert_2^2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

with equality **iff** $f = 0$ almost everywhere. ∎

> [!NOTE]
> The induced norm is $\Vert f \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\langle f, f \rangle}$, validating the Hilbert space structure. Wavelet orthonormality is precisely $\langle \psi_{j,k}, \psi_{j',k'} \rangle = \delta_{j,j'} \delta_{k,k'}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Parseval's Identity for an Orthonormal Wavelet Basis

**Claim:** If $\{\psi_{j,k}\}_{j,k \in \mathbb{Z}}$ is an orthonormal basis of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ and $f \in L^2$, then

$$\Vert f \Vert_2^2 = \sum_{j=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert c_{j,k} \vert^2, \quad c_{j,k} = \langle f, \psi_{j,k} \rangle$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1.** Since $\{\psi_{j,k}\}$ is complete in $L^2$, $f$ can be expanded as:

$$f(t) = \sum_{j,k} c_{j,k} \, \psi_{j,k}(t)$$

convergence being in $L^2$ norm.

**Step 2.** Take the inner product of both sides with $f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle f, f \rangle &= \left\langle \sum_{j,k} c_{j,k} \psi_{j,k},\; f \right\rangle \\
&= \sum_{j,k} c_{j,k} \langle \psi_{j,k}, f \rangle \quad \text{(continuity of inner product)} \\
&= \sum_{j,k} c_{j,k} \overline{c_{j,k}} \quad \text{(by definition of } c_{j,k}) \\
&= \sum_{j,k} \vert c_{j,k} \vert^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Recognising $\langle f, f \rangle = \Vert f \Vert_2^2$ completes the proof. ∎

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This identity is the **theoretical backbone** of wavelet-based compression: the energy of the original signal equals the energy of the coefficient sequence, and since many coefficients are tiny, thresholding them loses negligible energy.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: The Besov-Sobolev Coincidence $B^s_{2,2} = H^s$

**Claim:** For any $s \in \mathbb{R}$, $B^s_{2,2}(\mathbb{R}) = H^s(\mathbb{R})$ with equivalent norms.

**Step 1.** Take the wavelet-coefficient definition of the Besov norm and substitute $p = q = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert f \Vert_{B^s_{2,2}} &= \left( \sum_{j} 2^{j(s + 1/2 - 1/2)\cdot 2} \left( \sum_k \vert c_{j,k} \vert^2 \right)^{2/2} \right)^{1/2} \\
&= \left( \sum_j 2^{2js} \sum_k \vert c_{j,k} \vert^2 \right)^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Recall that for an orthonormal wavelet basis, by Parseval applied to the **detail space** $W_j$ (the span of $\{\psi_{j,k}\}_k$):

$$\sum_k \vert c_{j,k} \vert^2 = \int_{\mathbb{R}} \vert \langle f, \psi_{j,k} \rangle \vert^2 dk = \int_{I_j} \vert \hat{f}(\omega) \vert^2 \, \frac{d\omega}{2\pi}$$

where $I_j = [2^j \pi, 2^{j+1}\pi] \cup [-2^{j+1}\pi, -2^j \pi]$ is the frequency band covered by scale $j$.

**Step 3.** Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert f \Vert_{B^s_{2,2}}^2 &= \sum_{j=-\infty}^{\infty} 2^{2js} \int_{I_j} \vert \hat{f}(\omega) \vert^2 \, \frac{d\omega}{2\pi} \\
&\asymp \int_{\mathbb{R}} (1 + \omega^2)^s \vert \hat{f}(\omega) \vert^2 \, \frac{d\omega}{2\pi} \\
&= \Vert f \Vert_{H^s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The "$\asymp$" denotes equivalence up to constants, since on $I_j$, $\vert \omega \vert \asymp 2^j$, so $(1+\omega^2)^s \asymp 2^{2js}$. ∎

> [!NOTE]
> This is a celebrated result in wavelet theory. It tells us that **wavelets diagonalize the Sobolev norm** — a fact exploited in numerical solvers for PDEs (wavelet-Galerkin methods).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Computing the $L^2$ Norm of a Scaling Function

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** Verify that the Haar scaling function $\phi(t) = \mathbf{1}_{[0,1]}(t)$ belongs to $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ and compute its norm.

**Step 1.** The Haar scaling function is the indicator of $[0,1]$:

$$\phi(t) = \begin{cases} 1 & 0 \le t \le 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2.** Compute the $L^2$ norm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert \phi \Vert_2^2 &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \phi(t) \vert^2 \, dt \\
&= \int_0^1 1 \, dt \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Therefore $\Vert \phi \Vert_2 = 1$, confirming that the Haar scaling function is **normalized in $L^2$**. Its dyadic translates $\phi(t-k)$ for $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ are orthonormal on the integers. ∎

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Membership of $f(t) = e^{-t^2}$ in $L^p$ Spaces

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** Show that the Gaussian $f(t) = e^{-t^2}$ belongs to $L^p(\mathbb{R})$ for all $1 \le p < \infty$ but is unbounded in $L^\infty$.

**Step 1.** For $1 \le p < \infty$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert e^{-t^2} \vert^p \, dt &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-pt^2} \, dt \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-(\sqrt{p}\,t)^2} \, dt \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{p}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-u^2} \, du \quad (u = \sqrt{p}\,t)\\
&= \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{\sqrt{p}} < \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

So $f \in L^p$ for all finite $p$, with $\Vert f \Vert_p = (\pi/p)^{1/(2p)}$.

**Step 2.** For $L^\infty$:

$$\text{ess sup } \vert e^{-t^2} \vert = e^0 = 1$$

So $f \in L^\infty$ as well (the Gaussian is bounded, not unbounded — the problem statement is slightly imprecise; **the Gaussian is in fact in $L^\infty$** with norm 1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction:** The Gaussian is in $L^\infty$ with $\Vert f \Vert_\infty = 1$ since $e^{-t^2} \le 1$ for all $t$. It is, however, **not** in $L^1$ if we use a non-decaying variant — always check decay vs. growth. The Gaussian's *integrability* is what makes it special.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Space** | **Containing?** | **Favoured by wavelets with…** | **Sample basis element** |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
| $L^1$ | $\phi, \psi$ (integral = 0 conditions) | Compact support, finite integral | Haar $\psi$ |
| $L^2$ | Every orthonormal wavelet | Vanishing moments $N$ | Daubechies DB$N$ |
| $H^s$ | Sufficiently smooth $\psi$ | Higher vanishing moments | Meyer wavelet |
| $B^s_{p,q}$ | Sparse coefficient decays | $N$ vanishing moments + smoothness | Battle–Lemarié spline wavelet |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Function Space Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["C_c_infty R - Smooth Compact Support"] --> B["L^1 R - Integrable"]
    A --> C["L^p R for p in 1 infinity - Finite p-norm"]
    A --> D["L_infty R - Essentially Bounded"]
    C --> E["L^2 R - Square Integrable - Hilbert Space"]
    E --> F["H^s R - Sobolev - s derivative in L^2"]
    F --> G["B^s_2_2 R - Besov with p q equals 2 2"]
    C --> H["B^s_p_q R - General Besov - p q arbitrary"]
    E --> I["l^2 Z - Sequence of Wavelet Coefficients"]
    E --> J["Wavelet Basis - psi_jk - Orthonormal"]
    J --> I
    J --> F
    J --> H
    I --> K["DWT Block - Filter Bank - Multirate"]
    K --> L["C_q Decomposition Tree - Subband Coding"]
    L --> M["JPEG2000 / Compression / Denoising"]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style E fill:#ffe1e1
    style F fill:#fff4e1
    style H fill:#e1ffe1
    style J fill:#f5e1ff
    style M fill:#e1e1ff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Smooth test functions $C_c^\infty$ are the "seed" of all $L^p$ spaces. $L^2$ sits centrally as the Hilbert space, and Sobolev/Besov spaces refine it by adding regularity. The wavelet basis acts as a **bridge** from the continuous function space to the discrete sequence space, enabling digital signal processing.

---

### 4.2 Wavelet Decomposition: From $L^2$ Function to $\ell^2$ Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F["f t in L^2 R"] -->|Inner Product| PHI["c_phi k equals f phi_0k"]
    F -->|Inner Product| PSI["c_psi jk equals f psi_jk"]
    PHI --> A["Approximation - Coarse Scale"]
    PSI --> D["Detail - Fine Scales"]
    A --> SEQ["Sequence c_phi k and c_psi jk in l^2"]
    D --> SEQ
    SEQ -->|Thresholding| COMP["Compressed Representation"]
    SEQ -->|Inverse DWT| REC["Reconstructed f tilde in L^2"]
    style F fill:#ffe1e1
    style SEQ fill:#e1ffe1
    style COMP fill:#e1e1ff
    style REC fill:#fff4e1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The flow chart shows how a function $f \in L^2$ is converted by wavelet analysis into a sequence of coefficients in $\ell^2$, processed (compressed, thresholded), and then mapped back to $L^2$ via synthesis filters. The function spaces provide the **mathematical guarantees** that each step is well-defined and stable.

---

### 4.3 Besov Space Tri-Parameter Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Smoothness s - regularity index"] --> B["B^s_p_q R"]
    P["Integrability p - L^p of coefficients at each scale"] --> B
    Q["Fine-tuning q - L^q across scales"] --> B
    B --> E1["s large - very smooth functions - Sobolev-like"]
    B --> E2["p small - sparser coefficients - sparsity friendly"]
    B --> E3["q small - emphasizes isolated large coefficients"]
    B --> E4["q equals 2 - recovers Sobolev H^s"]
    B --> APP["Application: Denoising if s gt 0 and q small"]
    B --> APP2["Application: Compression if p small"]
    style B fill:#f5e1ff
    style E4 fill:#e1ffe1
    style APP fill:#ffe1e1
    style APP2 fill:#e1e1ff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three parameters $(s, p, q)$ of the Besov space allow **fine-grained classification** of functions. For wavelets:
> - $s$ controls **approximation rate** (how fast coefficients shrink)
> - $p$ controls **sparsity** (concentration of energy in few coefficients)
> - $q$ controls **inter-scale averaging** behaviour

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📚 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the function space $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ and state its inner product. Why is it important in wavelet theory?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$L^2(\mathbb{R})$ is the set of all (Lebesgue-measurable) complex-valued functions $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{C}$ such that
$$\Vert f \Vert_2^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert f(x) \vert^2 \, dx < \infty$$
It is equipped with the inner product
$$\langle f, g \rangle = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \overline{g(x)} \, dx$$
$L^2(\mathbb{R})$ is a **complete inner product space** (a Hilbert space), and it is the natural habitat of wavelets because:
1. It admits **orthonormal wavelet bases** $\{\psi_{j,k}\}_{j,k \in \mathbb{Z}}$.
2. Parseval's identity holds, preserving energy between the function and its coefficient sequence.
3. All **finite-energy signals** in engineering (audio, images, communication signals) lie in $L^2$.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for inner product, 1 mark for importance.)*

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Sobolev space $H^s(\mathbb{R})$? State its norm and explain the role of the parameter $s$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Sobolev space of order $s \in \mathbb{R}$ is defined as
$$H^s(\mathbb{R}) = \left\{ f \in L^2(\mathbb{R}) : \int_{\mathbb{R}} (1 + \omega^2)^s \vert \hat{f}(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega < \infty \right\}$$
with norm
$$\Vert f \Vert_{H^s} = \left( \int_{\mathbb{R}} (1 + \omega^2)^s \vert \hat{f}(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega \right)^{1/2}$$
The parameter $s$ is the **smoothness index**:
- $s = 0$ recovers $L^2$.
- $s > 0$ enforces higher regularity — penalising high-frequency content.
- For $s > 1/2$, every $f \in H^s$ is continuous; for $s > 1$, $f$ is continuously differentiable.
- The **mother wavelet** $\psi$ must belong to $H^s$ for the wavelet basis to be stable and for the associated multiresolution analysis to inherit regularity.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark norm, 1 mark role of $s$.)*

---

### 📚 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define the function spaces $L^p(\mathbb{R})$ for $1 \le p \le \infty$. State and prove Hölder's inequality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the Besov space $B^s_{p,q}(\mathbb{R})$ via wavelet coefficients. Show that $B^s_{2,2}(\mathbb{R}) = H^s(\mathbb{R})$. State two applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Definition of $L^p$:** For $1 \le p < \infty$,
$$L^p(\mathbb{R}) = \left\{ f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{C} \text{ measurable} : \int_{\mathbb{R}} \vert f(x) \vert^p \, dx < \infty \right\}$$
with norm $\Vert f \Vert_p = \left( \int \vert f \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$. For $p = \infty$, $\Vert f \Vert_\infty = \text{ess sup} \vert f \vert$. *(1 mark)*

**Hölder's Inequality:** If $f \in L^p$ and $g \in L^q$ with $1/p + 1/q = 1$, then $fg \in L^1$ and
$$\int \vert fg \vert \, dx \le \Vert f \Vert_p \, \Vert g \Vert_q$$

**Proof:**

*Step 1.* We first prove the **Young inequality for products**: For non-negative $a, b$ and $1/p + 1/q = 1$,
$$ab \le \frac{a^p}{p} + \frac{b^q}{q}$$
This follows from the concavity of $\log$ applied to $\log(ab) = \log(a^p/p) + \log(b^q/q)$ and Jensen's inequality. *(2 marks)*

*Step 2.* Apply Young's inequality to $a = \vert f(x) \vert / \Vert f \Vert_p$ and $b = \vert g(x) \vert / \Vert g \Vert_q$ (assuming the norms are nonzero; trivial case otherwise):
$$\frac{\vert f(x) g(x) \vert}{\Vert f \Vert_p \Vert g \Vert_q} \le \frac{\vert f(x) \vert^p}{p \Vert f \Vert_p^p} + \frac{\vert g(x) \vert^q}{q \Vert g \Vert_q^q}$$ *(2 marks)*

*Step 3.* Integrate over $\mathbb{R}$:
$$\frac{1}{\Vert f \Vert_p \Vert g \Vert_q} \int \vert fg \vert \, dx \le \frac{1}{p} \cdot \frac{\int \vert f \vert^p \, dx}{\Vert f \Vert_p^p} + \frac{1}{q} \cdot \frac{\int \vert g \vert^q \, dx}{\Vert g \Vert_q^q} = \frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$$
Multiplying both sides by $\Vert f \Vert_p \Vert g \Vert_q$ yields Hölder's inequality. *(2 marks)* ∎

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Besov Space Definition:** A function $f \in L^p(\mathbb{R})$ belongs to $B^s_{p,q}(\mathbb{R})$ if its wavelet coefficients $c_{j,k} = \langle f, \psi_{j,k} \rangle$ satisfy
$$\Vert f \Vert_{B^s_{p,q}} = \left( \sum_{j=-\infty}^{\infty} 2^{j\left(s + \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{p}\right)q} \left( \sum_{k} \vert c_{j,k} \vert^p \right)^{q/p} \right)^{1/q} < \infty$$ *(2 marks)*

**Proof of $B^s_{2,2} = H^s$:**

*Step 1.* Substitute $p = q = 2$ in the Besov norm:
$$\Vert f \Vert_{B^s_{2,2}} = \left( \sum_j 2^{2js} \sum_k \vert c_{j,k} \vert^2 \right)^{1/2}$$ *(1 mark)*

*Step 2.* Using Parseval's identity on the detail subspace $W_j$:
$$\sum_k \vert c_{j,k} \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{I_j} \vert \hat{f}(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega$$
where $I_j$ is the dyadic frequency band $[2^j\pi, 2^{j+1}\pi] \cup$ its negative. *(1 mark)*

*Step 3.* On $I_j$, $\vert \omega \vert \asymp 2^j$, so $(1+\omega^2)^s \asymp 2^{2js}$. Therefore:
$$\sum_j 2^{2js} \sum_k \vert c_{j,k} \vert^2 \asymp \sum_j \int_{I_j} (1+\omega^2)^s \vert \hat{f}(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega = \int_{\mathbb{R}} (1+\omega^2)^s \vert \hat{f}(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega$$
which is exactly $\Vert f \Vert_{H^s}^2$. ∎ *(2 marks)*

**Applications:** *(1 mark)*
1. **Wavelet denoising:** Signals with $f \in B^s_{p,q}$ for $s > 0, p \le 1$ are sparsely represented, and thresholding in the wavelet domain removes noise effectively.
2. **JPEG2000 compression:** The standard exploits the embedding of piecewise-smooth images in Besov spaces, using wavelet coefficient quantization.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ and the sequence space $\ell^2(\mathbb{Z})$. Prove Parseval's identity for an orthonormal wavelet basis and discuss its significance in wavelet-based compression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define Sobolev spaces $H^s(\mathbb{R})$ and state the Sobolev embedding theorem. Use the Fourier transform to show that $H^s$ controls the regularity of $f$ — the larger $s$, the smoother $f$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**$L^2(\mathbb{R})$:** Functions $f$ with $\int \vert f \vert^2 < \infty$, norm $\Vert f \Vert_2 = (\int \vert f \vert^2)^{1/2}$, inner product $\langle f, g \rangle = \int f \bar{g}$. *(1 mark)*

**$\ell^2(\mathbb{Z})$:** Bivariate sequences $a = (a_k)_{k \in \mathbb{Z}}$ with $\sum_k \vert a_k \vert^2 < \infty$, norm $\Vert a \Vert_2 = (\sum \vert a_k \vert^2)^{1/2}$. *(1 mark)*

**Parseval's Identity Proof:**

*Step 1.* Let $\{\psi_{j,k}\}$ be an orthonormal basis. Then any $f \in L^2$ admits expansion $f = \sum_{j,k} c_{j,k} \psi_{j,k}$ with $c_{j,k} = \langle f, \psi_{j,k} \rangle$. *(1 mark)*

*Step 2.* Compute $\Vert f \Vert_2^2 = \langle f, f \rangle = \langle \sum_{j,k} c_{j,k} \psi_{j,k}, f \rangle$. By continuity and linearity of the inner product:
$$= \sum_{j,k} c_{j,k} \overline{\langle f, \psi_{j,k} \rangle} = \sum_{j,k} \vert c_{j,k} \vert^2$$ *(2 marks)*

*Step 3.* Identifying the right side as the squared $\ell^2$ norm of the coefficient sequence $(c_{j,k})$, we get $\Vert f \Vert_2 = \Vert c \Vert_{\ell^2}$. *(1 mark)*

**Significance in compression:** *(1 mark)*
Parseval's identity guarantees that **energy is preserved** under the wavelet transform. Therefore, discarding small coefficients (thresholding) only reduces total energy by a small, controlled amount. This is the mathematical basis for lossy compression standards (JPEG2000, MP3-style audio codecs).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Definition of $H^s$:** Already given. *(1 mark)*

**Sobolev Embedding Theorem:** If $s > n/2$ (in dimension $n$), then $H^s(\mathbb{R}^n) \hookrightarrow C^0(\mathbb{R}^n)$ (continuous functions), and in general $H^s \hookrightarrow L^{p^*}$ for $p^* = np/(n-sp)$ when $s < n/p$. *(2 marks)*

**Fourier-based regularity proof:**

*Step 1.* The Fourier transform $\hat{f}(\omega) = \int f(t) e^{-i\omega t} dt$ is an isometry on $L^2$:
$$\int \vert f \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int \vert \hat{f}(\omega) \vert^2 d\omega$$ *(1 mark)*

*Step 2.* Differentiation in time corresponds to multiplication by $i\omega$ in frequency. Thus the $k$-th derivative satisfies $\widehat{f^{(k)}}(\omega) = (i\omega)^k \hat{f}(\omega)$, and $\Vert f^{(k)} \Vert_2^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int \omega^{2k} \vert \hat{f}(\omega) \vert^2 d\omega$. *(1 mark)*

*Step 3.* The Sobolev norm $\Vert f \Vert_{H^s}^2 = \int (1+\omega^2)^s \vert \hat{f} \vert^2 d\omega$ combines all derivatives up to order $s$ (fractionally). If $f \in H^s$ for large $s$, the integrand decays rapidly with $\omega$, forcing $\hat{f}$ to be **concentrated at low frequencies** — equivalently, $f$ has no sharp features and is **smooth**. *(1 mark)*

*Step 4.* Conversely, if $f$ has a jump discontinuity (e.g., a step function), $\hat{f}$ decays only as $1/\omega$, and the integral $\int (1+\omega^2)^s / \omega^2 \, d\omega$ diverges for any $s > 1/2$, so such $f \notin H^s$ for large $s$. This confirms that $s$ is a **smoothness measure**. *(1 mark)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on Function Spaces:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $L^p$ norm with $\ell^p$ norm.** The integral $\int \vert f \vert^p$ (continuous) is NOT the same as the sum $\sum \vert a_k \vert^p$ (discrete). One mark is often lost when students write the same expression for both.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "almost everywhere" (a.e.) qualifier.** Two functions that differ on a set of measure zero are considered equal in $L^p$. Omitting this loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mis-stating Parseval's identity.** Some students write $\Vert f \Vert_2^2 = \sum \vert c_{j,k} \vert$ (linear) instead of $\sum \vert c_{j,k} \vert^2$. This is a critical error.
>
> 4. **Wrong embedding direction.** Always check: $H^s \subset L^2$ for $s \ge 0$ (more regular → smaller space). The reverse inclusion is FALSE.
>
> 5. **Skipping the constant $1/2\pi$ in Fourier identities.** KTU examiners deduct half a mark for missing Fourier normalization constants.
>
> 6. **Treating Besov and Sobolev as identical.** They are equal only at $p = q = 2$; otherwise they are different spaces.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Function Spaces (Module 3)**

- [x] **$L^p(\mathbb{R})$** — set of functions with $\int \vert f \vert^p < \infty$; norm $\Vert f \Vert_p = (\int \vert f \vert^p)^{1/p}$.
- [x] **$L^2$ is a Hilbert space** with inner product $\langle f, g \rangle = \int f \bar{g}$.
- [x] **$\ell^p(\mathbb{Z})$** — set of sequences with $\sum \vert a_k \vert^p < \infty$.
- [x] **Wavelet transform** maps $L^2$ bijectively (with Riesz basis) to $\ell^2$.
- [x] **Parseval's identity:** $\Vert f \Vert_2^2 = \sum_{j,k} \vert c_{j,k} \vert^2$ — energy conservation.
- [x] **Sobolev space $H^s$:** $\Vert f \Vert_{H^s}^2 = \int (1+\omega^2)^s \vert \hat{f} \vert^2 d\omega$; smoothness index $s$.
- [x] **Besov space $B^s_{p,q}$:** defined by wavelet coefficient decay $\sim 2^{j(s+1/2-1/p)}$.
- [x] **$B^s_{2,2} = H^s$** — the key coincidence making wavelets diagonalize Sobolev norms.
- [x] **Hölder's inequality:** $\int \vert fg \vert \le \Vert f \Vert_p \Vert g \Vert_q$ for $1/p + 1/q = 1$.
- [x] **Cauchy–Schwarz:** $\vert \langle f, g \rangle \vert \le \Vert f \Vert_2 \Vert g \Vert_2$.
- [x] **Sobolev embedding:** $H^s \hookrightarrow L^{p^*}$ for $s > n/p$ with $1/p^* = 1/p - s/n$.
- [x] **Mother wavelet regularity:** $\psi \in H^s$ ensures vanishing moments and stable basis.
- [x] **Engineering link:** Finite-energy signals → $L^2$; smooth signals → $H^s$; sparse signals → $B^s_{p,q}$ with $p$ small.
- [x] **Compression rationale:** Parseval + sparsity in Besov space → thresholding → near-perfect reconstruction.

> 🎓 **Final Exam Tip:** When asked "in which space does this wavelet live?", the answer is almost always $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ with regularity $H^s$ for some $s$ depending on the number of vanishing moments. The Daubechies DB$N$ wavelet has $s \approx N - 0.5$ (approximate Sobolev regularity).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refinement Relation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Refinement Relation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the theory of **Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA)** and the construction of compactly supported wavelets, the **Refinement Relation** (also called the **Two-Scale Relation**, **Dilation Equation**, or **Scaling Equation**) is the fundamental functional equation that any scaling function $\phi(t)$ must satisfy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard Notation):**
> A function $\phi(t) \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$ is called a **scaling function** (or *father wavelet*) if it satisfies the refinement relation:
>
> $$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h[n]\, \phi(2t - n)$$
>
> where $\{h[n]\}_{n \in \mathbb{Z}}$ is a finite or absolutely summable sequence called the **refinement mask** (or **scaling filter coefficients**).

An equivalent form frequently seen in literature (without the $\sqrt{2}$ factor, absorbed into $h[n]$) is:

$$\phi(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n]\, \phi(2t - n)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The refinement relation is the **mathematical heart** of the fast wavelet transform. Every orthogonal wavelet basis (Haar, Daubechies, Symlet, Coiflet) is built upon a scaling function that satisfies this equation. The sequence $h[n]$ is the discrete-time **low-pass analysis filter** of the associated conjugate mirror filter bank.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the refinement relation as a **"fractal self-similarity rule"** — like a Russian nesting doll (Matryoshka).

> **Plain English Analogy — "The Photocopier Puzzle":**
> Imagine you have a shape $\phi(t)$ drawn on a piece of paper. Now apply a *magic photocopier* that:
> 1. **Zooms in by 2×** (this is the dilation: $t \mapsto 2t$)
> 2. **Shifts the copy by integer amounts** (this is the translation: $n$)
> 3. **Adds all copies together with weights $h[n]$**
>
> If the result of this process reproduces **the exact same original shape**, then $\phi(t)$ is a valid scaling function and the rule you used is its refinement relation.

For the **Haar scaling function** (a simple box pulse from 0 to 1), the rule is:
- Zoom in by 2, then place **two unit-height copies** of the original box — one at position 0 and one at position 1.
- The two copies overlap, but their sum at any point equals exactly **1** (the original height). Thus, the Haar function is *self-reproducing* under this rule.

This self-reproduction property is precisely what makes **nested approximation spaces** possible in MRA: a function approximated at a coarse resolution can be exactly reconstructed at the next finer resolution simply by passing the coarse samples through the filter $h[n]$.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Number of Taps ($N$):** The number of non-zero coefficients in $h[n]$. For Daubechies-$\mathcal{N}$ wavelets, $N = 2\mathcal{N}$.
- **Vanishing Moments ($p$):** The largest $p$ such that $\sum_n n^k h[n] = 0$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, p-1$.
- **Sum of Coefficients:** For a valid scaling filter, $\sum_n h[n] = \sqrt{2}$ (in the $L^2$-normalized form) — this preserves the DC gain so that $\phi(t)$ integrates to a non-zero constant.
- **Support Length:** A finite $h[n]$ with $h[n] \neq 0$ for $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$ gives $\phi(t)$ a compact support of length $N-1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Pillars of the Refinement Relation

The refinement relation is not arbitrary; it is **forced upon us** by the axioms of Multi-Resolution Analysis. Let us derive *why* it must exist.

**Pillar 1 — The Inclusion Property of MRA:**
A standard MRA defines a nested sequence of closed subspaces:
$$\{0\} \subset \dots \subset V_{-1} \subset V_0 \subset V_1 \subset V_2 \subset \dots \subset L^2(\mathbb{R})$$
where $V_{j+1}$ is a *finer* approximation space than $V_j$.

**Pillar 2 — Self-Similarity Under Dyadic Scaling:**
By definition, for any $j \in \mathbb{Z}$:
$$f(t) \in V_j \iff f(2t) \in V_{j+1}$$

**Pillar 3 — The Scaling Function Spans $V_0$:**
The space $V_0$ is spanned by integer translates of $\phi(t)$:
$$V_0 = \overline{\text{span}\{\phi(t-n) \mid n \in \mathbb{Z}\}}$$

**Pillar 4 — Bridging $V_0$ to $V_1$:**
Since $V_0 \subset V_1$, the basis function $\phi(t)$ of $V_0$ must be expressible as a linear combination of the basis functions of $V_1$. The basis of $V_1$ is $\{\sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-n)\}_{n \in \mathbb{Z}}$ (the $\sqrt{2}$ maintains $L^2$ normalization). Hence:

$$\phi(t) = \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} \langle \phi(t), \sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-n) \rangle \cdot \sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-n)$$

Defining the projection coefficient:
$$h[n] = \langle \phi(t), \phi(2t-n) \rangle = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \phi(t)\,\phi(2t-n)\,dt$$

yields **exactly** the refinement relation $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n]\,\phi(2t-n)$.

**Pillar 5 — The Partition of Unity (DC Preservation):**
A scaling function must satisfy $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \phi(t)\,dt = 1$ (or some non-zero constant). This forces the **sum condition**:
$$\sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h[n] = \sqrt{2}$$

**Pillar 6 — The Orthogonality Constraint:**
For the associated wavelet to be orthogonal, the sequence $h[n]$ must satisfy the **no-DC-in-quadrature-mirror** condition:
$$\sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h[n]\,h[n-2k] = \delta[k]$$

where $\delta[k]$ is the Kronecker delta. This is the well-known **Smith-Barnwell condition** for perfect reconstruction.

**Pillar 7 — The Fourier Domain Equivalent:**
Taking the Fourier transform of the refinement relation (using the property that $\mathcal{F}\{\phi(2t)\} = \frac{1}{2}\hat{\phi}(\omega/2)$) gives the **Fourier-domain refinement equation**:

$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\, H\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) \hat{\phi}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

where $H(\omega) = \sum_n h[n] e^{-j\omega n}$ is the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform** of the refinement mask. This is the cornerstone of the **cascade algorithm** for computing $\phi(t)$.

> [!NOTE]
> The iterative product form $\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \prod_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} H(\omega/2^k)$ is the foundation of the **cascade algorithm** used in MATLAB's `wfilters` and `wavefun` to numerically plot every Daubechies, Symlet, and Coiflet scaling function.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|
| Refinement Relation (Time) | $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n]\,\phi(2t-n)$ | Defines all scaling functions |
| Refinement Relation (Fourier) | $\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} H(\omega/2)\, \hat{\phi}(\omega/2)$ | Enables cascade algorithm |
| Infinite Product Form | $\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \prod_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} H(\omega/2^k)$ | Numerical synthesis of $\phi(t)$ |
| Refinement Mask (FIR Filter) | $H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h[n]\, z^{-n}$ | Low-pass filter in DWT |
| Sum Condition (DC) | $\sum_n h[n] = \sqrt{2}$ | Ensures $\int \phi(t) dt \neq 0$ |
| Orthogonality Condition | $\sum_n h[n]\,h[n-2k] = \delta[k]$ | Gives orthogonal wavelets |
| Vanishing Moment Order $p$ | $\sum_n n^k h[n] = 0,\; k=1,\dots,p-1$ | Polynomial reproduction |
| Quadrature Mirror Filter | $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$ | High-pass wavelet filter |
| Support of $\phi(t)$ | $\text{supp}(\phi) = [0,\, N-1]$ | Finite support for Daubechies |
| Haar Coefficients | $h[0] = h[1] = 1/\sqrt{2}$ | Simplest orthogonal case |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **Role of Refinement Relation** |
|---|---|
| **JPEG 2000 Image Compression** | The Cohen-Daubechies-Feauveau (CDF) 9/7 biorthogonal filters used in JPEG 2000 are derived from refinement masks. |
| **ECG / EEG Signal Denoising** | Daubechies-4 / Symlet-6 scaling coefficients are used to detect QRS complexes. |
| **Edge Detection in Images** | The high-pass filter $g[n]$ is mathematically defined by the refinement mask $h[n]$. |
| **FEM / Isogeometric Analysis** | B-spline scaling functions satisfy the refinement relation; this is what enables hierarchical refinement of CAD models. |
| **Numerical PDE Solvers** | Multi-grid methods exploit the self-similar refinement structure for fast $O(N)$ solvers. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Refinement Relation from MRA Axioms

**Given:** MRA axioms: (i) $V_j \subset V_{j+1}$, (ii) $f(t) \in V_j \iff f(2t) \in V_{j+1}$, (iii) $V_0 = \overline{\text{span}\{\phi(t-n)\}}$, (iv) $\cap_j V_j = \{0\}$, (v) $\overline{\cup_j V_j} = L^2(\mathbb{R})$.

**To Prove:** $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n]\,\phi(2t-n)$.

**Step 1:** Since $V_0 \subset V_1$, every function in $V_0$ can be written as a linear combination of the $V_1$ basis. The orthonormal basis for $V_1$ is $\{\phi_{1,n}(t)\} = \{\sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-n)\}_{n \in \mathbb{Z}}$.

**Step 2:** The basis for $V_0$ is $\{\phi_{0,n}(t)\} = \{\phi(t-n)\}_{n \in \mathbb{Z}}$. In particular, $\phi(t) = \phi_{0,0}(t) \in V_0$.

**Step 3:** Project $\phi(t)$ onto the $V_1$ orthonormal basis to find the coefficients:

$$c_n = \langle \phi(t),\, \sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-n) \rangle = \sqrt{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \phi(t)\,\phi(2t-n)\,dt$$

**Step 4:** Define $h[n] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} c_n = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \phi(t)\,\phi(2t-n)\,dt$. Then $c_n = \sqrt{2}\,h[n]$.

**Step 5:** Reconstruct $\phi(t)$ from its $V_1$ expansion:

$$\phi(t) = \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} c_n \cdot \sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-n) = \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} \sqrt{2}\,h[n] \cdot \sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-n)$$

**Step 6:** Simplify:

$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} \sqrt{2}\,h[n]\,\phi(2t-n)$$

Wait — we double-counted the $\sqrt{2}$. Let us re-derive correctly. The $V_1$ basis is already $\sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-n)$ (it is normalized), so the projection gives:

$$\phi(t) = \sum_n \langle \phi(t), \sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-n)\rangle \cdot \sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-n) = \sum_n 2\,h[n]\,\phi(2t-n)$$

So the proper refinement relation, depending on convention, is one of:

$$\boxed{\;\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n]\,\phi(2t-n) \quad \text{or} \quad \phi(t) = \sum_n \tilde{h}[n]\,\phi(2t-n)\;}$$

where $\tilde{h}[n] = 2h[n]$ absorbs the $\sqrt{2}$ factor. Both conventions appear in textbooks; the KTU standard uses the **$\sqrt{2}$ form** for $L^2$-normalization.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Haar Scaling Function

**Given:** The Haar scaling function is defined as:

$$\phi_{\text{Haar}}(t) = \begin{cases} 1 & 0 \le t < 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Claim:** $\phi_{\text{Haar}}$ satisfies the refinement relation with $h[0] = h[1] = 1/\sqrt{2}$.

**Proof (Direct Verification):**

Compute the right-hand side:

$$\text{RHS} = \sqrt{2}\,h[0]\,\phi(2t) + \sqrt{2}\,h[1]\,\phi(2t-1)$$

$$= \sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,\phi(2t) + \sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,\phi(2t-1)$$

$$= \phi(2t) + \phi(2t-1)$$

Now, $\phi(2t)$ is the indicator of $[0, 1/2)$ and $\phi(2t-1)$ is the indicator of $[1/2, 1)$. Their sum is the indicator of $[0, 1)$, which is exactly $\phi(t)$. $\blacksquare$

**Verification of Constraints:**

- Sum condition: $h[0] + h[1] = 1/\sqrt{2} + 1/\sqrt{2} = \sqrt{2}$. ✓
- Orthogonality: $\sum_n h[n]h[n-2k] = (1/\sqrt{2})^2 = 1/2$ for $k=0$, and $0$ for $k \neq 0$ (since the mask has only 2 taps). This is $\delta[k]/2$ ... wait, we need to check normalization more carefully. With $L^2$-normalized mask, the condition is $\sum_n h[n]h[n-2k] = \delta[k]$ only if there is an additional $1/\sqrt{2}$ scaling. The exact condition for $\sum h[n] = \sqrt{2}$ is $\sum h[n]h[n-2k] = \delta[k]$, but for Haar we have $1/2$, indicating a different convention is being used. **The Haar refinement mask in the $L^2$-orthonormal convention is $h[n] = 1/2$** (giving sum $= 1$ in the alternate form), or **$h[n] = 1/\sqrt{2}$** when the $\sqrt{2}$ factor is included externally.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Linear B-Spline Refinement

**Given:** The linear B-spline scaling function is the triangular pulse:
$$\phi(t) = \Lambda(t) = \max(0, 1 - \vert t \vert)$$

**Claim:** $\Lambda(t) = \frac{1}{2}\Lambda(2t) + \Lambda(2t-1) + \frac{1}{2}\Lambda(2t-2)$.

**Proof (Sketch):** Each $\Lambda(2t-k)$ is a triangle of width 1 and height 1 centered at $k/2$. At $t=1/2$, all three contribute. The sum of these three shifted, scaled triangles reproduces the original triangle of width 2 and height 1. This is a standard result from B-spline theory (the B-spline of order $N$ satisfies a refinement relation with mask coefficients given by binomial weights $h[n] = \binom{N}{n}/2^N$).

### 3.4 Fourier-Domain Derivation (Cascade Algorithm Foundation)

**Step 1:** Take the Fourier transform of $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n]\phi(2t-n)$:

$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n] \int \phi(2t-n) e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $u = 2t - n$, so $t = (u+n)/2$, $dt = du/2$, and $e^{-j\omega t} = e^{-j\omega u/2} e^{-j\omega n/2}$:

$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n] \cdot \frac{1}{2} e^{-j\omega n/2} \hat{\phi}(\omega/2)$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $\hat{\phi}(\omega/2)$ and recognize the DTFT:

$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{\phi}(\omega/2) \cdot \sum_n h[n] e^{-j\omega n/2}$$

$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} H(\omega/2) \cdot \hat{\phi}(\omega/2)$$

where $H(\omega) = \sum_n h[n] e^{-j\omega n}$ is the DTFT of $h[n]$.

**Step 4:** Iterate $K$ times to obtain the **finite cascade approximation**:

$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} \frac{H(\omega/2^k)}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \hat{\phi}(\omega/2^K)$$

**Step 5:** As $K \to \infty$, if $H(0) = \sqrt{2}$ and $\phi$ is normalized so $\int \phi = 1$, then $\hat{\phi}(\omega/2^K) \to \hat{\phi}(0) = 1$, giving the **infinite product**:

$$\boxed{\;\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \prod_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} H(\omega/2^k)\;}$$

This is the theoretical foundation of the **cascade algorithm** used in MATLAB's `wavefun` to plot $\phi(t)$ for any Daubechies family wavelet.

### 3.5 Derivation of the Orthogonality Condition (Smith-Barnwell)

**Step 1:** For an orthogonal MRA, the basis $\{\phi(t-n)\}$ of $V_0$ must be orthonormal, i.e., $\langle \phi(t-m), \phi(t-n) \rangle = \delta[m-n]$.

**Step 2:** Substitute the refinement relation into $\phi(t-n)$:

$$\phi(t-n) = \sqrt{2}\sum_k h[k]\,\phi(2(t-n)-k) = \sqrt{2}\sum_k h[k]\,\phi(2t-2n-k)$$

**Step 3:** Take the inner product with $\phi(t-m)$:

$$\delta[m-n] = \langle \phi(t-m), \phi(t-n) \rangle = 2 \sum_{k,\ell} h[k] h[\ell] \langle \phi(2t-2m-k), \phi(2t-2n-\ell) \rangle$$

**Step 4:** By orthonormality in the dilated space (with $\sqrt{2}$ normalization of the $V_1$ basis), the inner product equals $\delta[2n-2m+k-\ell]/2$.

**Step 5:** After careful manipulation, this reduces to:

$$\sum_{k \in \mathbb{Z}} h[k] h[k-2(n-m)] = \delta[n-m]$$

Letting $p = n - m$:

$$\boxed{\;\sum_{k \in \mathbb{Z}} h[k] h[k-2p] = \delta[p]\;}$$

This is the **Smith-Barnwell condition** for orthogonality of the scaling basis.
