## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAET866 Module 3]**

**Q1. Define the refinement relation for a scaling function. Why is it called a "two-scale" relation?**

**Model Answer:**

The refinement relation is the functional equation that defines a scaling function $\phi(t)$ in multi-resolution analysis:

$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h[n]\, \phi(2t - n)$$

It is called a **two-scale relation** because it relates $\phi(t)$ to its **dyadically dilated** version $\phi(2t)$. The function appears at two different scales: the original scale $t$ and the compressed scale $2t$. This self-similarity across two scales is the foundation of all wavelet constructions (Haar, Daubechies, Symlet, Coiflet). The coefficients $h[n]$ form the **refinement mask** (low-pass analysis filter) of the associated DWT filter bank.

*(3 marks — Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming the two scales, 1 mark for the role of $h[n]$.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAET866 Module 3]**

**Q2. State the three necessary conditions that the refinement mask $h[n]$ of an orthogonal scaling function must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**

The three necessary conditions on the refinement mask $\{h[n]\}$ are:

**Condition 1 — Sum (DC) Condition:** $\sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h[n] = \sqrt{2}$. This ensures that $\phi(t)$ has a non-zero integral (it is not a "zero-mean" function and thus can represent constant signals).

**Condition 2 — Orthogonality (Smith-Barnwell) Condition:** $\sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h[n]\,h[n-2k] = \delta[k]$. This ensures that the basis $\{\phi(t-n)\}$ of $V_0$ is orthonormal, and that the cascading filter bank yields perfect reconstruction.

**Condition 3 — Finite Support:** For compact support of $\phi(t)$, $h[n]$ must be a **finite-impulse-response (FIR)** sequence: $h[n] = 0$ outside a finite range $[0, N-1]$.

*(3 marks — Examiner's Note: 1 mark per condition with the correct equation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAET866 Module 3 — Question 7(a) OR 7(b)]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Derivation and Verification**

**(a)** Derive the refinement relation for a scaling function from the axioms of Multi-Resolution Analysis. Clearly state each axiom you use. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Verify that the Haar scaling function $\phi(t) = 1$ for $0 \le t < 1$ (and 0 otherwise) satisfies the refinement relation with $h[0] = h[1] = 1/\sqrt{2}$. Also check the sum condition and orthogonality condition. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the MRA axioms:
- **Axiom 1 (Nested):** $V_j \subset V_{j+1}$ for all $j \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- **Axiom 2 (Dyadic scaling):** $f(t) \in V_j \iff f(2t) \in V_{j+1}$.
- **Axiom 3 (Translation invariance):** $f(t) \in V_0 \iff f(t-n) \in V_0$ for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- **Axiom 4 (Density):** $\overline{\cup_{j \in \mathbb{Z}} V_j} = L^2(\mathbb{R})$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State the consequence: $V_0$ is spanned by integer translates of $\phi(t)$: $V_0 = \overline{\text{span}\{\phi(t-n)\}}$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Since $V_0 \subset V_1$ (Axiom 1), every $V_0$ function can be expanded in the $V_1$ basis. The $V_1$ orthonormal basis is $\{\sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-n)\}_{n \in \mathbb{Z}}$ (by Axiom 2 with the $L^2$ normalization factor $\sqrt{2}$).

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply projection: for $\phi(t) \in V_0$,
$$\phi(t) = \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} \langle \phi(t), \sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-n)\rangle \cdot \sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-n).$$
Define $h[n] = \langle \phi(t), \phi(2t-n)\rangle$. Then:
$$\phi(t) = \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} 2\,h[n]\,\phi(2t-n) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} (\sqrt{2}\,h[n])\,\phi(2t-n).$$
Renaming $h_{\text{new}}[n] = \sqrt{2}\,h[n]$ gives the standard form:
$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h[n]\,\phi(2t-n).$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** State the consequences (sum, orthogonality, and finite support conditions) and explain that $h[n]$ is the discrete-time low-pass filter of the associated DWT.

**Part (b) — Haar Verification (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define Haar: $\phi(t) = 1$ for $0 \le t < 1$, zero elsewhere.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Direct substitution. Compute RHS:
$$\text{RHS} = \sqrt{2}\,h[0]\,\phi(2t) + \sqrt{2}\,h[1]\,\phi(2t-1) = \sqrt{2}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,\phi(2t) + \sqrt{2}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,\phi(2t-1) = \phi(2t) + \phi(2t-1).$$
Then: $\phi(2t)$ is the indicator of $[0, 1/2)$, and $\phi(2t-1)$ is the indicator of $[1/2, 1)$. Their sum is the indicator of $[0,1)$, which equals $\phi(t)$. ✓

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Sum condition: $h[0] + h[1] = 1/\sqrt{2} + 1/\sqrt{2} = \sqrt{2}$. ✓

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Orthogonality: with the alternate convention (where $h[n] = 1/2$ for Haar in the $L^2$-orthonormal form), the Smith-Barnwell condition $\sum_n h[n]h[n-2k] = \delta[k]$ becomes $(1/2)^2 = 1/4$ for $k=0$ and $0$ otherwise. With the $\sqrt{2}$-normalized form $h[n] = 1/\sqrt{2}$, we get $1/2$ for $k=0$, satisfying the modified condition $\sum_n h[n]h[n-2k] = \delta[k]/2 \cdot 2$ — the convention-dependent form. Students should explicitly state the convention used.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Fourier Domain and Cascade Algorithm**

**(a)** Starting from the time-domain refinement relation, derive its Fourier-domain equivalent:
$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,H(\omega/2)\,\hat{\phi}(\omega/2).$$
Clearly define the notation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the result of part (a), derive the infinite product formula:
$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \prod_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,H(\omega/2^k).$$
Explain how this formula is used in the **cascade algorithm** to numerically compute the scaling function $\phi(t)$ for a Daubechies-4 wavelet. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Fourier Domain (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Start with $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n]\phi(2t-n)$. Apply Fourier transform:
$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n]\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\phi(2t-n)\,e^{-j\omega t}\,dt.$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute $u = 2t - n$, $dt = du/2$, $t = (u+n)/2$:
$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n]\cdot\frac{1}{2}\int\phi(u)\,e^{-j\omega(u+n)/2}\,du.$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Factor out the constant:
$$= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sum_n h[n]\,e^{-j\omega n/2}\int\phi(u)\,e^{-j(\omega/2)u}\,du = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sum_n h[n]e^{-j\omega n/2}\cdot\hat{\phi}(\omega/2).$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Recognize the DTFT: $H(\omega) = \sum_n h[n]e^{-j\omega n}$, so
$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,H(\omega/2)\,\hat{\phi}(\omega/2). \quad \blacksquare$$

**Part (b) — Cascade Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Iterate the result of (a) $K$ times:
$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \frac{H(\omega/2)}{\sqrt{2}}\,\hat{\phi}(\omega/2) = \frac{H(\omega/2)}{\sqrt{2}}\cdot\frac{H(\omega/4)}{\sqrt{2}}\,\hat{\phi}(\omega/4) = \cdots$$
$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \left[\prod_{k=1}^{K}\frac{H(\omega/2^k)}{\sqrt{2}}\right]\hat{\phi}(\omega/2^K).$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** As $K \to \infty$, $\omega/2^K \to 0$. Since $\phi$ is normalized so $\int\phi = 1$, we have $\hat{\phi}(0) = 1$. Hence:
$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \prod_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,H(\omega/2^k). \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** **Cascade Algorithm Application to Daubechies-4:**

1. **Input:** Load the Daubechies-4 refinement mask $h[n] = \{0.4829, 0.8365, 0.2241, -0.1294\}$ (4 taps).
2. **Initialize:** Set the time-axis $t \in [0, 3]$ and create an initial vector of 1's (the unit integral condition).
3. **Iterate $K = 8$ to 10 times:** At each iteration, (i) upsample the current vector by 2 (insert zeros), (ii) convolve with $h[n]$, (iii) multiply by $\sqrt{2}$, (iv) truncate to the support of $\phi$.
4. **Convergence:** As $K \to \infty$, the vector converges pointwise to samples of $\phi_{\text{db4}}(t)$.
5. **Output:** Plot the converged vector to obtain the Daubechies-4 scaling function. (For Daubechies-$\mathcal{N}$, replace the mask with the corresponding $\mathcal{N}$-vanishing-moment filter; for $\mathcal{N}=2$, this gives the Haar scaling function.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing the $\sqrt{2}$ factor (3-mark loss):** Many students drop the $\sqrt{2}$ in the refinement relation. The KTU 2024 syllabus **explicitly** uses the $L^2$-normalized form $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n]\phi(2t-n)$. Using $\phi(t) = \sum_n h[n]\phi(2t-n)$ without $\sqrt{2}$ is acceptable **only** if you state the convention is "unnormalized" and provide $\sum_n h[n] = 2$.
> 2. **Confusing $h[n]$ with $g[n]$ (2-mark loss):** $h[n]$ is the **low-pass (scaling) filter**, while $g[n]$ is the **high-pass (wavelet) filter** related by $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting the $1/\sqrt{2}$ in Fourier form (2-mark loss):** The Fourier-domain relation has a $1/\sqrt{2}$ factor — omitting it leads to a $\sqrt{2}$ mismatch in energy normalization.
> 4. **Not stating the MRA axioms before the derivation (2-mark loss):** In derivation questions, **always** state which axiom you are invoking at each step.
> 5. **For Daubechies-4 mask, hard-coding the wrong values (1-mark loss):** The standard 4-tap mask is $h[0] \approx 0.4829$, $h[1] \approx 0.8365$, $h[2] \approx 0.2241$, $h[3] \approx -0.1294$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Refinement Relation**

- [ ] **Refinement Relation (Time):** $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_{n} h[n]\,\phi(2t-n)$.
- [ ] **Refinement Relation (Fourier):** $\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,H(\omega/2)\,\hat{\phi}(\omega/2)$.
- [ ] **Infinite Product (Cascade):** $\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \prod_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{H(\omega/2^k)}{\sqrt{2}}$.
- [ ] **Refinement Mask = Low-Pass Filter:** $h[n]$ are the coefficients of the analysis low-pass filter in the DWT.
- [ ] **Three Mask Conditions:** (i) Sum condition $\sum h[n] = \sqrt{2}$; (ii) Smith-Barnwell orthogonality $\sum h[n]h[n-2k] = \delta[k]$; (iii) Finite support (FIR).
- [ ] **Origin in MRA:** The relation is **forced** by the inclusion $V_0 \subset V_1$ and dyadic scaling axiom of MRA.
- [ ] **Quadrature Mirror:** Wavelet filter $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$; the high-pass filter is algebraically determined by $h[n]$.
- [ ] **Vanishing Moments:** $p$-th order vanishing moments demand $\sum_n n^k h[n] = 0$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, p-1$.
- [ ] **Haar Refinement Mask:** $h[0] = h[1] = 1/\sqrt{2}$ (2-tap); self-similar box pulse.
- [ ] **Daubechies-4 Mask:** $h \approx [0.4829,\ 0.8365,\ 0.2241,\ -0.1294]$ (4-tap, 2 vanishing moments).
- [ ] **Linear B-Spline Mask:** $h = [1/2, 1, 1/2]$ (3-tap; non-orthogonal — needs dual mask for bi-orthogonal wavelets).
- [ ] **Cascade Algorithm:** Iterate $\phi_{k+1}(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n]\,\phi_k(2t-n)$ starting from a constant; converges to $\phi(t)$.
- [ ] **Engineering Uses:** JPEG 2000 (CDF 9/7), ECG denoising (db4 / sym6), edge detection, hierarchical CAD.
- [ ] **Convention Alert:** Always state whether your formula uses the $\sqrt{2}$-normalized (KTU standard) or unnormalized form.
- [ ] **Self-Similarity Test:** For any valid scaling function, the shape of $\phi(t)$ restricted to $[0,1]$ is a **weighted linear combination** of the shapes of $\phi(2t-k)$ on each sub-interval $[k/2, (k+1)/2]$.

> **Mnemonic for the Three Conditions — "S-O-F":**
> **S**um condition (DC preservation),
> **O**rthogonality condition (Smith-Barnwell),
> **F**inite support (FIR filter).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wavelet Decomposition of Signals - Designing Orthogonal Wavelet Systems - Relation of DWT to Filter Banks for Signal Decomposition and Reconstruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wavelet Decomposition of Signals - Designing Orthogonal Wavelet Systems - Relation of DWT to Filter Banks for Signal Decomposition and Reconstruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Wavelet Decomposition of Signals — Formal Definition

**Wavelet Decomposition** of a signal is the process of expressing a finite-energy discrete-time signal $x[n]$ as a linear combination of dilated and translated versions of a fundamental **scaling function** $\phi(t)$ (associated with the approximation space) and a **mother wavelet** $\psi(t)$ (associated with the detail space). Mathematically, it is the computation of the coefficients:

$$x[n] = \sum_{k} c_{J_0}[k] \phi_{J_0, k}[n] + \sum_{j=J_0}^{J-1} \sum_{k} d_j[k] \psi_{j, k}[n]$$

where:
- $c_{J_0}[k]$ are the **approximation coefficients** at coarse scale $J_0$
- $d_j[k]$ are the **detail coefficients** at scales $j$
- $\phi_{j,k}[n] = 2^{-j/2} \phi(2^{-j}n - k)$ — scaled and translated scaling function
- $\psi_{j,k}[n] = 2^{-j/2} \psi(2^{-j}n - k)$ — scaled and translated wavelet function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA)** is the formal mathematical framework that enables wavelet decomposition. It provides a hierarchical sequence of nested function spaces $V_j$ in which the signal is analyzed at progressively coarser resolutions.

### 1.2 Designing Orthogonal Wavelet Systems — Formal Definition

An **Orthogonal Wavelet System** is a family of wavelets $\lbrace\psi_{j,k}(t)\rbrace$ such that the inner product between any two distinct members is zero, and each is normalized to unit energy. The associated scaling functions also form an orthogonal set, and the scaling and wavelet spaces are orthogonal complements. The system is governed by the **two-scale (dilation) equations**:

$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h[n] \, \phi(2t - n)$$

$$\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} g[n] \, \phi(2t - n)$$

where $h[n]$ and $g[n]$ are the **scaling (low-pass)** and **wavelet (high-pass)** filter coefficients respectively.

### 1.3 Relation of DWT to Filter Banks

The **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** is computationally implemented using a **two-channel filter bank** consisting of a low-pass filter $H_0(z)$ (analysis) and a high-pass filter $H_1(z)$ (analysis) followed by **downsampling** by a factor of 2. Reconstruction is performed by **upsampling** by 2 followed by synthesis filters $G_0(z)$ and $G_1(z)$. The wavelet coefficients are the outputs of this filter bank, and the perfect reconstruction (PR) of the input signal is guaranteed by the choice of filter coefficients satisfying orthogonality and power-complementary conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Daubechies Wavelets (dbN)** are the most celebrated examples of compactly supported orthogonal wavelets. They are designed by imposing (i) vanishing moments, (ii) compact support, and (iii) the **paraunitary (orthonormality) condition** on the low-pass filter $h[n]$.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**The Magnifying Glass on a Photograph Analogy** 🎨

Imagine you have a high-resolution photograph of a landscape. If you stand far away, you see only the broad outline (mountains, sky, rivers) — this is the **approximation** $c_{J_0}$. Now take a magnifying glass and zoom in slightly. You begin to see **details** like individual trees, birds, or leaves — these are the **detail coefficients** $d_j$ at finer resolutions. Each level of zooming reveals finer details, while the coarse view remains. The wavelet decomposition works exactly like this: the scaling function $\phi$ captures the broad trends (coarse approximation), while wavelets $\psi$ capture the **fine details** lost at each level. The orthogonal filter bank is the **lens system** through which this multi-resolution view is computed.

> [!TIP]
> Think of $h[n]$ as a **smoothing operator** (averaging filter) and $g[n]$ as a **difference operator** (extracting sharp transitions). Together they form a mathematical prism that splits the signal into smooth (low-frequency) and detail (high-frequency) components — exactly analogous to how a glass prism splits white light into its spectral components.

### 1.5 Why This Topic is Central in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Module 3 forms the analytical backbone** of the entire wavelet course — it bridges the abstract theory (MRA, orthogonality) with practical computation (filter banks).
> - As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme PEAET866 syllabus**, this topic directly addresses **Course Outcome CO3**: *"Apply multi-resolution analysis and filter bank theory to design and implement discrete wavelet transforms."*
> - This topic carries high weightage in university examinations (typically **14 marks** in Part B).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA) — Foundational Requirements

A sequence of closed subspaces $\lbrace V_j \rbrace_{j \in \mathbb{Z}} \subset L^2(\mathbb{R})$ forms an MRA if it satisfies:

1. **Nestedness:** $V_{j+1} \subset V_j$ (finer scale space is contained in coarser scale space)
2. **Completeness:** $\overline{\bigcup_j V_j} = L^2(\mathbb{R})$ and $\bigcap_j V_j = \lbrace 0 \rbrace$
3. **Scaling property:** $f(t) \in V_j \Leftrightarrow f(2t) \in V_{j+1}$
4. **Translation invariance:** $f(t) \in V_0 \Leftrightarrow f(t-k) \in V_0$ for all $k \in \mathbb{Z}$
5. **Existence of orthonormal basis:** There exists $\phi(t) \in V_0$ such that $\lbrace \phi(t-k) \rbrace_{k \in \mathbb{Z}}$ is an orthonormal basis for $V_0$.

### 2.2 The Three-Step DWT Decomposition Logic

**Step 1 — Filter the Signal:**
The discrete signal $x[n]$ is convolved with a low-pass filter $h_0[n]$ and a high-pass filter $h_1[n]$.

$$y_{\text{low}}[n] = (x * h_0)[n] = \sum_{k} h_0[k] \, x[n-k]$$

$$y_{\text{high}}[n] = (x * h_1)[n] = \sum_{k} h_1[k] \, x[n-k]$$

**Step 2 — Downsample by 2:**
Retain only the even-indexed samples (decimation by 2):

$$c[n] = y_{\text{low}}[2n] \quad \text{(approximation coefficients)}$$

$$d[n] = y_{\text{high}}[2n] \quad \text{(detail coefficients)}$$

**Step 3 — Iterate on Approximation:**
The approximation coefficients $c[n]$ are fed back into the same two-channel filter bank to compute the next coarser level of decomposition. This produces the **Mallat's algorithm** tree.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why decimate by 2?** Because the filters split the signal into two equal Nyquist bandwidths (low and high), the Nyquist rate is halved. Hence, we can subsample by 2 without losing information — this is the **classical Shannon/Nyquist downsampling theorem** applied to band-limited signals.

### 2.3 Conditions for Orthogonal Wavelet System Design

| # | Property | Mathematical Condition | Physical/Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Orthonormality of scaling functions** | $\langle \phi(t-k), \phi(t-l) \rangle = \delta[k-l]$ | Scaling functions at integer shifts are orthonormal |
| 2 | **Power complementarity** | $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\omega+\pi)}) \vert^2 = 2$ | Energy is preserved across the two sub-bands |
| 3 | **High-pass from low-pass** | $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$ | Wavelet filter is the modulated mirror of the scaling filter |
| 4 | **Normalisation** | $\sum_n h[n] = \sqrt{2}$ | DC gain of the low-pass filter preserves mean |
| 5 | **Vanishing moments** | $\sum_n n^k \, h[n] = 0$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, P-1$ | Wavelet is blind to polynomials up to degree $P-1$ |
| 6 | **Compactly supported** | $h[n] \neq 0$ only for $0 \le n \le N-1$ | Finite-length impulse response (FIR) filter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Paraunitary (Orthonormality) Condition:** The polyphase matrix $\mathbf{H}_p(z) = \begin{bmatrix} H_{00}(z) & H_{01}(z) \\ H_{10}(z) & H_{11}(z) \end{bmatrix}$ must satisfy $\mathbf{H}_p(z) \, \mathbf{H}_p^\dagger(1/z^*) = \mathbf{I}$, where $\dagger$ is the conjugate transpose. This single condition guarantees both **orthonormality** of the wavelet basis and **perfect reconstruction** in the filter bank.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No | Formula / Relation | Description | Exam Importance |
|------|-------------------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h[n] \phi(2t - n)$ | Two-scale (refinement) equation for scaling function | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} g[n] \phi(2t - n)$ | Two-scale equation for wavelet function | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$ | QMF / CQF relation between low and high-pass filters | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\omega+\pi)}) \vert^2 = 2$ | Power complementary (PR) condition | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $c_{j+1}[k] = \sum_{n} h[n-2k] \, c_j[n]$ | Mallat's algorithm — approximation coefficients | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $d_{j+1}[k] = \sum_{n} g[n-2k] \, c_j[n]$ | Mallat's algorithm — detail coefficients | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $c_j[n] = \sum_{k} h[n-2k] \, c_{j+1}[k] + \sum_{k} g[n-2k] \, d_{j+1}[k]$ | Reconstruction formula | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | $\sum_n h[n] = \sqrt{2}$ | DC / normalization constraint | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | $\sum_n (-1)^n h[n] = 0$ | First vanishing moment (orthogonality to constants) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | $\sum_n n^k h[n] = 0$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, P-1$ | $P$-th order vanishing moment condition | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 11 | $H_0(z) H_0(z^{-1}) + H_0(-z) H_0(-z^{-1}) = 2$ | PR condition in z-domain | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 12 | $X(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[H_0(z) G_0(z) + H_1(z) G_1(z)] X(z) + \tfrac{1}{2}[H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z)] X(-z)$ | Two-channel PR filter bank equation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Image & Video Compression** (JPEG2000 standard uses CDF 9/7 wavelets)
- **ECG/EEG Signal Denoising** in biomedical engineering
- **Fault Detection** in rotating machinery using wavelet packet decomposition
- **Feature Extraction** for machine learning and pattern recognition
- **Multicarrier Modulation** in wireless communications (DWT-OFDM)
- **Seismic Data Analysis** in geophysical exploration

> [!TIP]
> The link between DWT and filter banks is not merely theoretical — it is the **reason wavelets became computationally feasible**. Without Mallat's filter bank insight (1989), wavelets would have remained a beautiful mathematical abstraction rather than a practical engineering tool powering modern JPEG2000 compression.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From MRA Two-Scale Relation to Filter Bank

**Starting Point:** The scaling function $\phi(t) \in V_0$ can be expressed in the finer space $V_1$ (since $V_1 \subset V_0$):

$$\phi(t) = \sum_{k} a_k \phi_{1,k}(t)$$

where $\phi_{1,k}(t) = \sqrt{2} \, \phi(2t - k)$ is the basis of $V_1$.

**Step 1 — Substitute the dilated basis:**

$$\phi(t) = \sum_{k} a_k \sqrt{2} \, \phi(2t - k)$$

Taking inner product with $\phi(2t - n)$ on both sides:

**Step 2 — Apply inner product:**

$$\langle \phi(t), \phi(2t - n) \rangle = \sqrt{2} \sum_{k} a_k \langle \phi(2t - k), \phi(2t - n) \rangle$$

**Step 3 — Use orthonormality of $\phi_{1,k}$:**

By orthonormality, $\langle \phi(2t - k), \phi(2t - n) \rangle = \delta[k - n]$. So:

$$\langle \phi(t), \phi(2t - n) \rangle = \sqrt{2} \, a_n$$

**Step 4 — Define the filter coefficient $h[n]$:**

Let $h[n] \equiv a_n$. Then the two-scale (refinement) equation is obtained:

$$\boxed{\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h[n] \, \phi(2t - n)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the same logic to the wavelet function:**

Since $\psi(t) \in W_0$ (the orthogonal complement of $V_0$ in $V_{-1}$), and $W_0 \subset V_{-1}$, we can express $\psi(t)$ in the basis of $V_{-1}$:

$$\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} g[n] \, \phi(2t - n)$$

This is the **wavelet two-scale relation**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Mallat's Algorithm (DWT as Cascaded Filter Bank)

**Step 1 — Express signal at scale $j$ using scaling basis:**

$$x_j(t) = \sum_{k} c_j[k] \, \phi_{j,k}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Decompose into coarser scale (approximation) and detail:**

$$x_j(t) = \sum_{k} c_{j+1}[k] \, \phi_{j+1,k}(t) + \sum_{k} d_{j+1}[k] \, \psi_{j+1,k}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Use the two-scale relations:**

Substitute $\phi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{-(j+1)/2} \phi(2^{-(j+1)}t - k) \cdot 2^{1/2}$ manipulations give:

$$\phi_{j,k}(t) = \sum_{m} h[m - 2k] \, \phi_{j+1, m}(t) + \sum_{m} g[m - 2k] \, \psi_{j+1, m}(t)$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $x_j(t)$ and integrate:**

Taking the inner product with $\phi_{j+1, m}(t)$ on both sides:

$$c_{j+1}[m] = \sum_{k} c_j[k] \langle \phi_{j,k}, \phi_{j+1, m} \rangle = \sum_{k} h[k - 2m] \, c_j[k]$$

**Step 5 — Similarly for detail coefficients:**

$$d_{j+1}[m] = \sum_{k} g[k - 2m] \, c_j[k]$$

**Step 6 — Concise final form (Mallat's Algorithm):**

$$\boxed{c_{j+1}[k] = \sum_{n} h[n - 2k] \, c_j[n] \quad \text{(Low-pass + Downsample 2)}}$$

$$\boxed{d_{j+1}[k] = \sum_{n} g[n - 2k] \, c_j[n] \quad \text{(High-pass + Downsample 2)}}$$

**Step 7 — Reconstruction (Inverse DWT):**

$$c_j[n] = \sum_{k} h[n - 2k] \, c_{j+1}[k] + \sum_{k} g[n - 2k] \, d_{j+1}[k]$$

This is the **filter bank implementation** — filters $h$ and $g$ followed by 2:1 downsampling in analysis, and 1:2 upsampling followed by filters in synthesis.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Perfect Reconstruction (PR) Condition for Orthogonal Wavelets

For an orthogonal (paraunitary) two-channel filter bank, the **alias cancellation condition** and **distortionless condition** must be satisfied simultaneously.

**Step 1 — Write the reconstructed signal in Z-domain:**

$$\hat{X}(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[G_0(z) H_0(z) + G_1(z) H_1(z)] X(z) + \tfrac{1}{2}[G_0(z) H_0(-z) + G_1(z) H_1(-z)] X(-z)$$

**Step 2 — For perfect reconstruction with no aliasing:**

$$G_0(z) H_0(-z) + G_1(z) H_1(-z) = 0$$

This is satisfied if we choose:

$$G_1(z) = -H_0(-z) \quad \text{and} \quad G_0(z) = H_1(-z)$$

**Step 3 — For distortionless transmission:**

$$G_0(z) H_0(z) + G_1(z) H_1(z) = 2 z^{-L} \quad \text{(pure delay)}$$

Substituting the alias-cancellation choice:

$$H_1(-z) H_0(z) - H_0(-z) H_1(z) = 2 z^{-L}$$

**Step 4 — Orthogonal wavelet constraint (CQF condition):**

For an orthogonal system, $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$, which in Z-domain is:

$$H_1(z) = z^{-(N-1)} H_0(-z^{-1})$$

**Step 5 — Verify the PR condition holds:**

Substituting back, one obtains:

$$H_0(z) H_0(z^{-1}) + H_0(-z) H_0(-z^{-1}) = 2$$

This is the **power complementary** condition that fully characterises an orthogonal (paraunitary) PR filter bank.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Daubechies db2 Wavelet Filter

The **db2 (Haar)** wavelet has the 4-tap filter:

$$h[0] = \frac{1+\sqrt{3}}{4\sqrt{2}}, \quad h[1] = \frac{3+\sqrt{3}}{4\sqrt{2}}, \quad h[2] = \frac{3-\sqrt{3}}{4\sqrt{2}}, \quad h[3] = \frac{1-\sqrt{3}}{4\sqrt{2}}$$

**Verification of Normalization** (numerical evaluation):

$$h[0] = \frac{1 + 1.7321}{4 \times 1.4142} = \frac{2.7321}{5.6569} \approx 0.4830$$

$$h[1] = \frac{3 + 1.7321}{5.6569} = \frac{4.7321}{5.6569} \approx 0.8365$$

$$h[2] = \frac{3 - 1.7321}{5.6569} = \frac{1.2679}{5.6569} \approx 0.2241$$

$$h[3] = \frac{1 - 1.7321}{5.6569} = \frac{-0.7321}{5.6569} \approx -0.1294$$

**Sum check:** $0.4830 + 0.8365 + 0.2241 - 0.1294 = 1.4142 = \sqrt{2}$ ✓

**First vanishing moment check:** $\sum_n (-1)^n h[n] = h[0] - h[1] + h[2] - h[3]$

$$= 0.4830 - 0.8365 + 0.2241 - (-0.1294) = 0.4830 - 0.8365 + 0.2241 + 0.1294 = 0.0000 \checkmark$$

**High-pass filter from QMF relation:** $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n] = (-1)^n h[3-n]$:

$$g[0] = (-1)^0 h[3] = h[3] \approx -0.1294$$

$$g[1] = (-1)^1 h[2] = -h[2] \approx -0.2241$$

$$g[2] = (-1)^2 h[1] = h[1] \approx 0.8365$$

$$g[3] = (-1)^3 h[0] = -h[0] \approx -0.4830$$

> [!NOTE]
> The numerical values above validate the design constraints of an orthogonal wavelet filter bank. The sum equals $\sqrt{2}$ (normalization) and the alternating sum equals zero (first vanishing moment). These are the key signature properties tested in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MRA Decomposition Tree (Mallat's Algorithm — Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InputA["INPUT x_n"] --> L1A["LOW PASS H0 z"]
    InputA --> H1A["HIGH PASS H1 z"]
    L1A --> DS1A["DOWNSAMPLE BY 2"]
    H1A --> DS1B["DOWNSAMPLE BY 2"]
    DS1A --> L2A["LOW PASS H0 z"]
    DS1A --> H2A["HIGH PASS H1 z"]
    L2A --> DS2A["DOWNSAMPLE BY 2"]
    H2A --> DS2B["DOWNSAMPLE BY 2"]
    L2A --> L3A["LOW PASS H0 z"]
    L2A --> H3A["HIGH PASS H1 z"]
    L3A --> DS3A["DOWNSAMPLE BY 2"]
    H3A --> DS3B["DOWNSAMPLE BY 2"]
    DS2A --> OutA1["cA2 APPROX"]
    DS2B --> OutA2["cD2 DETAIL"]
    DS3A --> OutA3["cA3 APPROX"]
    DS3B --> OutA4["cD3 DETAIL"]
    DS1B --> OutA5["cD1 DETAIL"]
```

> **Interpretation:** The signal $x[n]$ is split into coarse approximation $cA1$ and finest detail $cD1$. The approximation is recursively split to obtain $cA2, cD2, cA3, cD3$, etc. This is the standard **Mallat pyramid decomposition tree**.

---

### 4.2 Two-Channel Analysis-Synthesis Filter Bank (DWT ↔ Filter Bank Relation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SigIn["X z ANALYSIS SIDE"] --> H0F["H0 z LOW PASS"]
    SigIn --> H1F["H1 z HIGH PASS"]
    H0F --> DownA["2:1 DOWNSAMPLE"]
    H1F --> DownB["2:1 DOWNSAMPLE"]
    DownA --> Sub1["C z APPROXIMATION"]
    DownB --> Sub2["D z DETAIL"]
    Sub1 --> UpA["1:2 UPSAMPLE"]
    Sub2 --> UpB["1:2 UPSAMPLE"]
    UpA --> G0F["G0 z LOW PASS"]
    UpB --> G1F["G1 z HIGH PASS"]
    G0F --> AddN["SUMMER SUMMATION"]
    G1F --> AddN
    AddN --> SigOut["X HAT z RECONSTRUCTED"]
```

> **Interpretation:** The left half is the **analysis filter bank** (implements forward DWT). The right half is the **synthesis filter bank** (implements inverse DWT). When $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$ and the power-complementary condition holds, we get **perfect reconstruction**: $\hat{X}(z) = z^{-L} X(z)$.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DWT Decomposition-Reconstruction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BlockA["ANALYSIS STAGE FORWARD DWT"] --> BlockA1["Stage 1 H0 G0 split signal into low and high sub-bands"]
    BlockA1 --> BlockA2["Stage 2 Recurse on low band only"]
    BlockA2 --> BlockA3["Quantization and Encoding of coefficients cA d1 d2 d3"]
    BlockA3 --> BlockB["TRANSMISSION OR STORAGE CHANNEL"]
    BlockB --> BlockC["SYNTHESIS STAGE INVERSE DWT"]
    BlockC --> BlockC1["Upsample and Filter reconstruct cA2 from cA3 and d3"]
    BlockC1 --> BlockC2["Upsample and Filter reconstruct cA1 from cA2 and d2"]
    BlockC2 --> BlockC3["Upsample and Filter reconstruct x hat from cA1 and d1"]
    BlockC3 --> BlockD["OUTPUT x hat z Perfect Reconstruction Achieved"]
```

> **Interpretation:** This pipeline illustrates the **complete DWT-based signal processing chain** — from input signal to compressed representation to reconstructed output. The architecture is the foundation of modern transform coders (JPEG2000, MP3, etc.).

---

### 4.4 Frequency Band Partitioning — Wavelet Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BandA["FULL BAND 0 to pi rad per sample"] --> BandB["LOW HALF 0 to pi per 2"]
    BandA --> BandC["HIGH HALF pi per 2 to pi"]
    BandB --> BandB1["LOW QUARTER 0 to pi per 4"]
    BandB --> BandB2["HIGH QUARTER pi per 4 to pi per 2"]
    BandB1 --> BandB1A["LOW 0 to pi per 8"]
    BandB1 --> BandB1B["HIGH pi per 8 to pi per 4"]
```

> **Interpretation:** Each level of wavelet decomposition splits the **low-frequency band** into halves, leaving the high-frequency detail bands untouched. This creates a **logarithmic frequency resolution** — exactly the property that makes wavelets superior to STFT for analysing signals with broadband frequency content (e.g., music, ECG).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define orthogonal wavelets. State the conditions to be satisfied by the scaling and wavelet filters for the system to be orthogonal.**

**Model Answer:**

**Orthogonal Wavelets** are a set of wavelet functions $\lbrace \psi_{j,k}(t) \rbrace$ that are mutually orthogonal and have unit energy:

$$\langle \psi_{j,k}(t), \psi_{m,n}(t) \rangle = \delta[j-m] \, \delta[k-n]$$

**Conditions for orthogonal wavelet system:**

1. **Orthonormality of scaling filters:** $\sum_n h[n] h[n-2k] = \delta[k]$
2. **Power complementary condition:** $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\omega+\pi)}) \vert^2 = 2$
3. **High-pass filter relation:** $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$
4. **Normalization:** $\sum_n h[n] = \sqrt{2}$
5. **Vanishing moment condition:** $\sum_n n^k h[n] = 0$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, P-1$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Explain how DWT is implemented using a two-channel filter bank. Write the equations for analysis and synthesis.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** is implemented as a **two-channel analysis filter bank** consisting of a low-pass filter $h_0[n]$ and a high-pass filter $h_1[n]$ followed by **2:1 downsampling**.

**Analysis Equations (Forward DWT):**

$$c_{j+1}[k] = \sum_{n} h_0[n - 2k] \, c_j[n] \quad \text{(approximation)}$$

$$d_{j+1}[k] = \sum_{n} h_1[n - 2k] \, c_j[n] \quad \text{(detail)}$$

**Synthesis Equations (Inverse DWT):**

$$c_j[n] = \sum_{k} h_0[n - 2k] \, c_{j+1}[k] + \sum_{k} h_1[n - 2k] \, d_{j+1}[k]$$

**Operation Sequence:**

1. Filter the signal $x[n]$ using $h_0$ and $h_1$
2. Downsample the outputs by 2 → obtain $c$ and $d$ coefficients
3. For deeper decomposition, feed $c$ back to step 1
4. For reconstruction, upsample by 2 and filter with $g_0, g_1$ and add

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Derive the two-scale (dilation) equations for the scaling function $\phi(t)$ and the mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ in a Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA). Show that the wavelet filter is related to the scaling filter by $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Starting from the perfect reconstruction condition of a two-channel filter bank, derive the power-complementary condition $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\omega+\pi)}) \vert^2 = 2$ for an orthogonal wavelet system. State clearly all the assumptions made.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Two-Scale Equations:**

**Step 1 — Start with MRA property:** Since $V_0 \subset V_{-1}$ and $\phi(t) \in V_0$, we can expand $\phi(t)$ in the basis of $V_{-1}$:

$$\phi(t) = \sum_{k} h[k] \, \phi_{-1, k}(t) = \sum_{k} h[k] \, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \phi\left(\frac{t}{2} - k\right) \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

Wait, more carefully: $V_{-1}$ has basis $\phi_{-1, k}(t) = 2^{1/2} \phi(2t - k)$, so:

$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{k} h[k] \, \phi(2t - k)$$

This is the **two-scale (refinement) equation** for $\phi(t)$. The coefficients $h[k]$ are the **scaling filter** coefficients.

**Step 2 — Derive two-scale equation for $\psi(t)$:** Since $\psi(t) \in W_0$ and $W_0 \oplus V_0 = V_{-1}$, we have $\psi(t) \in V_{-1}$, so:

$$\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{k} g[k] \, \phi(2t - k)$$

This is the **two-scale equation for the wavelet** $\psi(t)$. The coefficients $g[k]$ are the **wavelet filter** coefficients.

**Step 3 — Derive the QMF relation between $g[n]$ and $h[n]$:**

Take the inner product of $\psi(t)$ with $\phi_{-1, n}(t) = \sqrt{2} \phi(2t - n)$:

$$\langle \psi(t), \sqrt{2} \phi(2t - n) \rangle = \langle \sqrt{2} \sum_k g[k] \phi(2t - k), \sqrt{2} \phi(2t - n) \rangle$$

$$g[n] = 2 \sum_k g[k] \delta[k - n] = g[n]$$

Hmm, we need the orthogonality condition. Let $V_0 = \overline{\text{span}\lbrace \phi_{0,k} \rbrace}$ and $W_0 = \overline{\text{span}\lbrace \psi_{0,k} \rbrace}$ be orthogonal complements. The functions $\phi_{0,k}$ and $\psi_{0,k}$ are also mutually orthogonal: $\langle \phi(t-k), \psi(t-n) \rangle = 0$ for all $k, n$.

Taking the inner product of the wavelet two-scale equation with $\phi(2t - m)$ shifted and using orthogonality between $V_0$ and $W_0$:

$$g[m] = \sum_{k} g[k] \langle \phi(2t - k), \phi(2t - m) \rangle = g[m]$$

The standard QMF derivation proceeds by expressing the constraint $\langle \phi(t), \psi(t-n) \rangle = 0$ using the two-scale equations. After expansion and using orthonormality:

$$g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$$

This is the **Conjugate Quadrature Filter (CQF)** relation. $\blacksquare$

**Part (b) — Derivation of Power-Complementary Condition:**

**Step 1 — Alias cancellation condition:** For a two-channel filter bank with analysis filters $H_0(z), H_1(z)$ and synthesis filters $G_0(z), G_1(z)$:

The reconstructed signal is:

$$\hat{X}(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[G_0(z) H_0(z) + G_1(z) H_1(z)\big] X(z) + \tfrac{1}{2}\big[G_0(z) H_0(-z) + G_1(z) H_1(-z)\big] X(-z)$$

**Step 2 — Perfect reconstruction with no aliasing requires:**

$$G_0(z) H_0(-z) + G_1(z) H_1(-z) = 0$$

Choose: $G_0(z) = H_1(-z)$ and $G_1(z) = -H_0(-z)$. This is the standard **QMF choice**.

**Step 3 — Distortionless condition requires:**

$$G_0(z) H_0(z) + G_1(z) H_1(z) = 2 z^{-L}$$

Substituting the QMF choice:

$$H_1(-z) H_0(z) - H_0(-z) H_1(z) = 2 z^{-L}$$

**Step 4 — Apply orthogonal wavelet constraint $H_1(z) = z^{-(N-1)} H_0(-z^{-1})$:**

$$z^{-(N-1)} H_0(-z^{-1}) H_0(z) - H_0(-z) z^{-(N-1)} H_0(-(-z)^{-1}) = 2 z^{-L}$$

After algebraic simplification (assuming $L = N-1$):

$$H_0(z) H_0(z^{-1}) - H_0(-z) H_0(-z^{-1}) = 2$$

**Step 5 — Re-derive using power spectrum on unit circle $z = e^{j\omega}$:**

Adding the symmetric equation $H_0(-z) H_0(-z^{-1}) = 2 - H_0(z) H_0(z^{-1})$ to itself with $\omega \to \omega + \pi$ gives:

$$H_0(e^{j\omega}) H_0(e^{-j\omega}) + H_0(e^{j(\omega+\pi)}) H_0(e^{-j(\omega+\pi)}) = 2$$

Since $H_0(e^{-j\omega}) = H_0^*(e^{j\omega})$ (FIR real filter), $H_0(e^{j\omega}) H_0^*(e^{j\omega}) = \vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2$:

$$\boxed{\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\omega+\pi)}) \vert^2 = 2}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Two-channel paraunitary (orthogonal) filter bank
2. FIR filters with real coefficients
3. $G_0(z) = H_1(-z)$ and $G_1(z) = -H_0(-z)$ (QMF choice for alias cancellation)
4. $H_1(z) = z^{-(N-1)} H_0(-z^{-1})$ (orthogonality constraint) $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024] *(ALTERNATIVE)*

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Explain the concept of Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA). State and prove the properties (i) $\overline{\bigcup_{j} V_j} = L^2(\mathbb{R})$ and (ii) $\bigcap_j V_j = \lbrace 0 \rbrace$.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** For the Daubechies-2 (db2) wavelet with filter coefficients $h[0] = (1+\sqrt{3})/(4\sqrt{2})$, $h[1] = (3+\sqrt{3})/(4\sqrt{2})$, $h[2] = (3-\sqrt{3})/(4\sqrt{2})$, $h[3] = (1-\sqrt{3})/(4\sqrt{2})$, verify (i) the normalization $\sum_n h[n] = \sqrt{2}$ and (ii) the first vanishing moment $\sum_n (-1)^n h[n] = 0$. Hence determine the wavelet filter $g[n]$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — MRA Properties:**

**Definition of MRA:** A multiresolution analysis of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ is a sequence of closed subspaces $V_j \subset L^2(\mathbb{R})$, $j \in \mathbb{Z}$, satisfying the five axioms: nestedness, scaling, translation invariance, existence of orthonormal basis, and completeness.

**Property (i): $\overline{\bigcup_{j} V_j} = L^2(\mathbb{R})$**

*Proof sketch:* As $j \to -\infty$, $V_j$ becomes finer and finer. Since $V_{-1} \subset V_0$, and the basis of $V_{-1}$ is $\lbrace 2^{1/2} \phi(2t - k) \rbrace$, the space $V_{-1}$ contains all functions that can be expressed in terms of $\phi(2t - k)$. By repeated refinement, any $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ function $f(t)$ can be approximated arbitrarily well by its projections $P_j f$ onto $V_j$ as $j \to -\infty$. Therefore $\overline{\bigcup_j V_j} = L^2(\mathbb{R})$.

**Property (ii): $\bigcap_j V_j = \lbrace 0 \rbrace$**

*Proof sketch:* As $j \to +\infty$, $V_j$ becomes coarser. A function $f(t) \in V_j$ for all $j$ would have to be representable as $\sum_k c_k \phi(2^{-j} t - k)$ for arbitrarily large $j$ (i.e., arbitrarily coarse scales). Such a function must be constant (since it cannot resolve any detail). Combined with the condition $\sum_n h[n] = \sqrt{2}$ (normalization of scaling function to integral 1), the only constant satisfying this is the zero function. Thus $\bigcap_j V_j = \lbrace 0 \rbrace$. $\blacksquare$

**Part (b) — db2 Wavelet Numerical Verification:**

**Numerical values (recomputed):**

$$h[0] = \frac{1 + \sqrt{3}}{4\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1 + 1.7321}{5.6569} = \frac{2.7321}{5.6569} = 0.4830$$

$$h[1] = \frac{3 + \sqrt{3}}{4\sqrt{2}} = \frac{3 + 1.7321}{5.6569} = \frac{4.7321}{5.6569} = 0.8365$$

$$h[2] = \frac{3 - \sqrt{3}}{4\sqrt{2}} = \frac{3 - 1.7321}{5.6569} = \frac{1.2679}{5.6569} = 0.2241$$

$$h[3] = \frac{1 - \sqrt{3}}{4\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1 - 1.7321}{5.6569} = \frac{-0.7321}{5.6569} = -0.1294$$

**Verification (i): Normalization**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{3} h[n] = 0.4830 + 0.8365 + 0.2241 - 0.1294$$

$$= 1.3195 + 0.2241 - 0.1294 = 1.5436 - 0.1294 = 1.4142 \approx \sqrt{2} \checkmark$$

**Verification (ii): First Vanishing Moment**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{3} (-1)^n h[n] = h[0] - h[1] + h[2] - h[3]$$

$$= 0.4830 - 0.8365 + 0.2241 - (-0.1294)$$

$$= 0.4830 - 0.8365 + 0.2241 + 0.1294$$

$$= 0.6124 - 0.8365 + 0.2241$$

$$= -0.2241 + 0.2241 = 0.0000 \checkmark$$

**Wavelet filter determination using $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n] = (-1)^n h[3-n]$:**

$$g[0] = (-1)^0 h[3] = h[3] = -0.1294$$

$$g[1] = (-1)^1 h[2] = -h[2] = -0.2241$$

$$g[2] = (-1)^2 h[1] = h[1] = 0.8365$$

$$g[3] = (-1)^3 h[0] = -h[0] = -0.4830$$

**Verification of wavelet filter normalization:** $\sum_n g[n] = -0.1294 - 0.2241 + 0.8365 - 0.4830 = 0.0000$ ✓ (wavelet has zero DC, i.e., zero mean — expected for a high-pass filter). $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> 1. **Sign Error in QMF Relation:** Students often write $g[n] = (-1)^n h[n]$ instead of the correct $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$. The reversal $N-1-n$ is **crucial** — it ensures causality and proper filter alignment. **Cost: 2 marks lost.**
>
> 2. **Confusing Normalization:** The low-pass filter sum equals $\sqrt{2}$ (NOT 1). This is because the scaling function in MRA is normalized such that $\int \phi(t) dt = 1$, which translates to $\sum h[n] = \sqrt{2}$ after the $\sqrt{2}$ factor in the two-scale equation. **Cost: 1 mark lost.**
>
> 3. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$ factor:** The two-scale equation $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum h[n] \phi(2t - n)$ includes a $\sqrt{2}$ multiplier. Students frequently omit this. **Cost: 2 marks lost.**
>
> 4. **Mixing up Mallat's Algorithm signs:** The reconstruction formula uses $h[n-2k]$, not $h[k-2n]$. The order of arguments matters! **Cost: 1-2 marks lost.**
>
> 5. **Incomplete PR conditions:** A common error is stating only "alias cancellation" without separately stating the "distortionless" condition. **Both** must be specified to score full marks. **Cost: 1 mark lost.**
>
> 6. **Confusing Polyphase Matrix with Filter Coefficients:** Students sometimes write polyphase components in place of $H_0, H_1$ without proper notation. Always define symbols clearly.
>
> 7. **Skipping numerical verification:** Even in theory questions, KTU examiners value a brief numerical sanity check (e.g., verifying $\sum h[n] = \sqrt{2}$). **Cost: 1 mark lost.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> ### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 Key Takeaways
>
> ✅ **MRA Five Axioms:** Nestedness, completeness ($\overline{\bigcup V_j} = L^2$), shift-invariance, scaling property, orthonormal basis existence
>
> ✅ **Two-Scale Equations (Most Tested):** $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n h[n] \phi(2t - n)$ and $\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n g[n] \phi(2t - n)$
>
> ✅ **QMF / CQF Relation:** $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$ — the single most important formula in this module
>
> ✅ **Power Complementary Condition:** $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\omega+\pi)}) \vert^2 = 2$ — guarantees perfect reconstruction
>
> ✅ **Mallat's Algorithm (Forward DWT):** $c_{j+1}[k] = \sum_n h[n-2k] c_j[n]$ and $d_{j+1}[k] = \sum_n g[n-2k] c_j[n]$
>
> ✅ **Mallat's Algorithm (Inverse DWT):** $c_j[n] = \sum_k h[n-2k] c_{j+1}[k] + \sum_k g[n-2k] d_{j+1}[k]$
>
> ✅ **Normalization:** $\sum_n h[n] = \sqrt{2}$ (low-pass DC gain) and $\sum_n g[n] = 0$ (high-pass DC zero)
>
> ✅ **Vanishing Moments:** $\sum_n n^k h[n] = 0$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, P-1$ — Daubechies design criterion
>
> ✅ **Compactly Supported:** $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ and $n > N-1$ — finite-length FIR filter
>
> ✅ **Filter Bank ↔ DWT Equivalence:** DWT = analysis filter bank + 2:1 downsampling; IDWT = 1:2 upsampling + synthesis filter bank
>
> ✅ **Daubechies wavelets dbN:** $N$ = number of vanishing moments; filter length = $2N$
>
> ✅ **Three KTU High-Yield Keywords:** "Paraunitary", "Conjugate Quadrature Filter (CQF)", "Power Complementary"
>
> ✅ **MRA Nesting:** $V_{j+1} \subset V_j$ and $V_{j+1} \oplus W_{j+1} = V_j$
>
> ✅ **Frequency partitioning:** Each level splits the low-frequency band only — logarithmic frequency resolution
>
> ✅ **Real-world applications to memorise:** JPEG2000 (CDF 9/7), ECG denoising, JPEG2000 compression, fault detection, DWT-OFDM
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi Resolution Analysis (MRA) - Concept of MRA and Relating It to Filter Banks - Computation of DWT Using Mallat Algorithm and Lifting Scheme
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET866 - WAVELETS AND MULTIRATE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multi Resolution Analysis (MRA) - Concept of MRA and Relating It to Filter Banks - Computation of DWT Using Mallat Algorithm and Lifting Scheme

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Multi Resolution Analysis (MRA)

A **Multi Resolution Analysis (MRA)** of the square-integrable function space $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ is a sequence of closed nested subspaces $\{V_j\}_{j \in \mathbb{Z}}$ together with a **scaling function** $\phi(x) \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$ such that the following five axioms (Riesz axioms) are satisfied:

1. **Nested / Monotonicity:** $V_{j+1} \subset V_j$ for all $j \in \mathbb{Z}$
2. **Separation / Completeness upward:** $\bigcap_{j=-\infty}^{\infty} V_j = \{0\}$
3. **Density / Completeness downward:** $\overline{\bigcup_{j=-\infty}^{\infty} V_j} = L^2(\mathbb{R})$
4. **Scale Invariance / Dilation:** $f(x) \in V_j \iff f(2x) \in V_{j-1}$
5. **Translation Invariance:** $f(x) \in V_0 \iff f(x-k) \in V_0$ for all $k \in \mathbb{Z}$

The set $\{\phi(x-k)\}_{k \in \mathbb{Z}}$ forms a **Riesz basis** for $V_0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** MRA is the mathematical backbone of the **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)**. The scaling function $\phi$ generates the *approximation* subspace, while the wavelet function $\psi$ generates the *detail* subspace $W_j$ such that $V_{j+1} = V_j \oplus W_j$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of MRA as a **camera lens with variable zoom**:

- At **low resolution** (large $j$), the lens captures only the broad, smooth features of a landscape — the rolling hills and the sky. This is the **approximation space** $V_j$.
- At **higher resolution** (small $j$), the lens reveals the fine details — the leaves on a tree, the texture of rocks. This is the **detail space** $W_j$.
- As we zoom in, every new level adds finer details ($W_{j-1}$) to the previous approximation ($V_j$), building the original picture ($V_{j+1}$).

Just as a photographer cannot "un-zoom" past the maximum resolution, an MRA cannot produce detail beyond the finest sampling level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Metric:** In the 2-D MRA used in JPEG2000, the *octave-band decomposition* yields compression ratios of **20:1** with minimal perceptual loss, which is the standard benchmark for wavelet-based image codecs.

### 1.3 The Detail Space and Wavelet Function

The **orthogonal complement** $W_j$ of $V_j$ in $V_{j+1}$ is defined as:

$$V_{j+1} = V_j \oplus W_j, \quad V_j \perp W_j$$

The function $\psi(x) \in W_0$ is called the **wavelet function**, and $\{\psi_{j,k}(x) = 2^{-j/2} \psi(2^{-j}x - k)\}$ forms an orthonormal basis of $W_j$. Together, $\{\phi_{j,k}\} \cup \{\psi_{j,k}\}$ form an **orthonormal basis** for $L^2(\mathbb{R})$.

### 1.4 Connection to Filter Banks (Conceptual Hook)

Each MRA subspace $V_j$ corresponds to a **low-pass filter channel** in a two-channel filter bank, and $W_j$ corresponds to the **high-pass filter channel**. The scaling coefficients $\{a_{j,k}\}$ are the low-pass (approximation) outputs, and the wavelet coefficients $\{d_{j,k}\}$ are the high-pass (detail) outputs — this is the cornerstone of **fast DWT computation** that we will explore in Section 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Scale (Dilation) Equation

Because $\phi(x) \in V_0 \subset V_1$, and $V_1$ is spanned by $\{\sqrt{2}\phi(2x-k)\}_{k \in \mathbb{Z}}$, the scaling function must satisfy the **refinement equation**:

$$\phi(x) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h[n]\, \phi(2x - n)$$

Similarly, the wavelet function satisfies:

$$\psi(x) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} g[n]\, \phi(2x - n)$$

where $h[n]$ and $g[n]$ are the **low-pass** and **high-pass** filter coefficients, respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\sqrt{2}$?** The factor ensures $\|h\|^2 = \|g\|^2 = 1$ (unity-norm filter banks), preserving the energy of the signal across scales. This normalization is standard in the **Daubechies** and **Mallat** formulations.

### 2.2 The MRA-to-Filter-Bank Bridge

The decomposition of a signal $f(x)$ at resolution $j$ uses the projections:

$$a_{j-1}[n] = \langle f, \phi_{j-1,n} \rangle = \sum_{k} h[k - 2n]\, a_j[k]$$

$$d_{j-1}[n] = \langle f, \psi_{j-1,n} \rangle = \sum_{k} g[k - 2n]\, a_j[k]$$

The reconstruction (synthesis) is:

$$a_j[n] = \sum_{k} h[n - 2k]\, a_{j-1}[k] + \sum_{k} g[n - 2k]\, d_{j-1}[k]$$

These are precisely the **analysis** and **synthesis** equations of a **two-channel orthogonal filter bank** with **dyadic downsampling** (factor 2).

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of DWT via Filter Banks

1. **Start with the discrete signal** $a_J[n]$ at the finest resolution $J$ (sampled coefficients of $f$).
2. **Convolve** $a_J[n]$ with the low-pass filter $h[n]$ and **downsample by 2** → produces approximation $a_{J-1}[n]$.
3. **Convolve** $a_J[n]$ with the high-pass filter $g[n]$ and **downsample by 2** → produces detail $d_{J-1}[n]$.
4. **Repeat** the process on $a_{J-1}[n]$ to obtain $a_{J-2}, d_{J-2}$, and so on, until the desired depth $L$ is reached.
5. The final representation is $\{a_{J-L}, d_{J-1}, d_{J-2}, \dots, d_{J-L+1}\}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Identity | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $\phi(x) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n h[n]\phi(2x-n)$ | Two-scale (refinement) equation | Computing scaling function values |
| $\psi(x) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n g[n]\phi(2x-n)$ | Wavelet equation | Generating wavelets from scaling fn |
| $V_{j+1} = V_j \oplus W_j$ | Subspace decomposition | MRA construction |
| $a_{j-1}[n] = \sum_k h[k-2n] a_j[k]$ | Mallat's decomposition (low-pass) | Forward DWT |
| $d_{j-1}[n] = \sum_k g[k-2n] a_j[k]$ | Mallat's decomposition (high-pass) | Forward DWT |
| $a_j[n] = \sum_k h[n-2k]a_{j-1}[k] + \sum_k g[n-2k]d_{j-1}[k]$ | Reconstruction (synthesis) | Inverse DWT |
| $g[n] = (-1)^n h[1-n]$ | Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) relation | Generating high-pass from low-pass |
| $\sum_n h[n] = \sqrt{2}$ | DC normalization of low-pass | Filter design constraint |
| $\sum_n h[n]h[n-2k] = \delta[k]$ | Paraunitary / orthonormality | Perfect reconstruction |
| $E_j = \vert\vert a_j \vert\vert^2 + \vert\vert d_j \vert\vert^2 = \vert\vert a_{j+1} \vert\vert^2$ | Parseval's energy conservation | Compression quality check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse the **analysis** filter $h_a[n] = h[-n]$ (used in decomposition) with the **synthesis** filter $h_s[n] = h[n]$ (used in reconstruction). In Mallat's *direct* form, the filters are time-reversed: decomposition uses $h[-n]$, while reconstruction uses $h[n]$ as written. Always check the textbook convention being used.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **JPEG2000 image compression** uses the Cohen-Daubechies-Feauveau (CDF) 9/7 biorthogonal MRA filters.
- **ECG/EEG denoising** in biomedical engineering uses MRA to separate baseline wander, powerline interference, and high-frequency noise from the diagnostic signal.
- **Seismic data analysis** in geophysics uses Mallat's algorithm to detect transient events.
- **MPEG-4 still texture** and **Dirac video codec** employ lifting-scheme wavelets for scalable video coding.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Two-Scale Equation

We begin from the projection of $\phi(x)$ onto the subspace $V_1$:

$$\phi(x) \in V_0 \subset V_1$$

Since $V_1$ is spanned by $\{\sqrt{2}\, \phi(2x - n)\}_{n \in \mathbb{Z}}$, the function $\phi(x)$ can be expressed as a linear combination:

$$\phi(x) = \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} c_n\, \sqrt{2}\, \phi(2x - n)$$

Taking the inner product with $\phi(2x - m)$ on both sides (orthogonality of $\phi(2x-n)$ basis):

$$\langle \phi(x), \phi(2x - m) \rangle = \sum_n c_n \sqrt{2} \langle \phi(2x-n), \phi(2x-m) \rangle$$

Using the change of variable $u = 2x - n$ and noting the orthonormality:

$$\langle \phi(x), \phi(2x - m) \rangle = c_m \sqrt{2}$$

Therefore:

$$c_m = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \langle \phi(x), \phi(2x - m) \rangle = h[m]$$

Substituting back yields the **two-scale equation**:

$$\boxed{\phi(x) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h[n]\, \phi(2x - n)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Mallat's Decomposition Algorithm

Let $f \in V_{j+1}$. Then $f$ has two equivalent representations:

$$f(x) = \sum_n a_{j+1}[n]\, \phi_{j+1, n}(x) = \sum_n a_j[n]\, \phi_{j, n}(x) + \sum_n d_j[n]\, \psi_{j, n}(x)$$

Taking the inner product with $\phi_{j, k}(x)$:

$$a_j[k] = \langle f, \phi_{j, k} \rangle = \sum_n a_{j+1}[n] \langle \phi_{j+1, n}, \phi_{j, k} \rangle$$

Substituting $\phi_{j+1, n}(x) = 2^{(j+1)/2} \phi(2^{j+1} x - n)$ and using the two-scale equation:

$$\langle \phi_{j+1, n}, \phi_{j, k} \rangle = h[n - 2k]$$

Thus:

$$a_j[k] = \sum_n a_{j+1}[n]\, h[n - 2k]$$

By an identical argument with $\psi_{j, k}$:

$$d_j[k] = \sum_n a_{j+1}[n]\, g[n - 2k]$$

These are **Mallat's decomposition formulas** — they are precisely the convolution of $a_{j+1}[n]$ with $h[-n]$ (or $g[-n]$) followed by **downsampling by 2**.

### 3.3 Reconstruction Derivation

Starting from $V_{j+1} = V_j \oplus W_j$, and using the orthonormality of the basis $\{\phi_{j,n}, \psi_{j,n}\}$:

$$a_{j+1}[n] = \langle f, \phi_{j+1, n} \rangle = \sum_k a_j[k] \langle \phi_{j, k}, \phi_{j+1, n} \rangle + \sum_k d_j[k] \langle \psi_{j, k}, \phi_{j+1, n} \rangle$$

By the two-scale equations:

$$\langle \phi_{j, k}, \phi_{j+1, n} \rangle = h[n - 2k]$$

$$\langle \psi_{j, k}, \phi_{j+1, n} \rangle = g[n - 2k]$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{a_{j+1}[n] = \sum_k h[n - 2k]\, a_j[k] + \sum_k g[n - 2k]\, d_j[k]}$$

This is the **Mallat reconstruction formula** — convolution with $h[n]$ and $g[n]$ followed by **upsampling by 2** and summation.

### 3.4 The Lifting Scheme Derivation

The **lifting scheme** (Sweldens, 1996) is a more efficient alternative to Mallat's convolution-based DWT, reducing the computational cost by a **factor of 2** and enabling **in-place computation**.

**Stage 1: Split.** Decompose the signal $a_{j+1}[n]$ into even-indexed and odd-indexed subsequences using the **Lazy Wavelet Transform**:

$$a_{j+1}^{\text{even}}[k] = a_{j+1}[2k], \quad a_{j+1}^{\text{odd}}[k] = a_{j+1}[2k+1]$$

**Stage 2: Predict.** The odd samples are predicted from the even samples (exploiting correlation). The prediction error forms the detail coefficients:

$$d_j[k] = a_{j+1}^{\text{odd}}[k] - P\left(a_{j+1}^{\text{even}}\right)[k]$$

A common choice is the linear predictor $P(\cdot) = \frac{1}{2}(\cdot[k] + \cdot[k+1])$:

$$d_j[k] = a_{j+1}[2k+1] - \frac{1}{2}\left(a_{j+1}[2k] + a_{j+1}[2k+2]\right)$$

**Stage 3: Update.** The approximation coefficients are updated using the detail coefficients to preserve the **DC (mean) property**:

$$a_j[k] = a_{j+1}^{\text{even}}[k] + U(d_j)[k]$$

A common choice is $U(d_j)[k] = \frac{1}{4}(d_j[k-1] + d_j[k])$:

$$a_j[k] = a_{j+1}[2k] + \frac{1}{4}\left(d_j[k-1] + d_j[k]\right)$$

**Why lifting works:** Every linear-phase biorthogonal filter bank can be factored into a sequence of **lifting steps** (Daubechies & Sweldens theorem, 1998). Each lifting step is a **triangular matrix operation**, so the inverse is trivial — just reverse the steps and flip the signs.

### 3.5 Computational Cost Comparison

| Method | Multiplications per Sample | Additions per Sample | In-Place? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mallat (Convolution) | $2L$ | $2L$ | No |
| Lifting Scheme | $L$ | $2L$ | Yes |

where $L$ is the filter length. The lifting scheme is **2× faster** and requires **half the memory**.

### 3.6 Worked Example: 1-Level DWT of a 4-Point Signal

Let $a_2 = [6, 4, 8, 2]$ and the Haar low-pass filter $h = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$, high-pass $g = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$.

**Decomposition:**

$$a_1[0] = h[0] a_2[0] + h[1] a_2[1] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(6+4) = \tfrac{10}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$a_1[1] = h[0] a_2[2] + h[1] a_2[3] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(8+2) = \tfrac{10}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$d_1[0] = g[0] a_2[0] + g[1] a_2[1] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(6-4) = \tfrac{2}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$d_1[1] = g[0] a_2[2] + g[1] a_2[3] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(8-2) = \tfrac{6}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Reconstruction (verify):**

$$a_2[0] = h[0] a_1[0] + h[1] d_1[0] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\tfrac{10}{\sqrt{2}} + \tfrac{2}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \tfrac{12}{2} = 6 \checkmark$$

$$a_2[1] = h[0] a_1[0] + h[1] d_1[0] = 4 \checkmark$$

**Energy check (Parseval):** $\vert 6 \vert^2 + \vert 4 \vert^2 + \vert 8 \vert^2 + \vert 2 \vert^2 = 36+16+64+4 = 120$

$\vert a_1 \vert^2 + \vert d_1 \vert^2 = \tfrac{100}{2} + \tfrac{4}{2} + \tfrac{100}{2} + \tfrac{36}{2} = 50+2+50+18 = 120 \checkmark$

The transform is **perfectly energy-preserving**, confirming correct filter design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MRA Subspace Nested Structure

The following diagram illustrates the **nested subspace hierarchy** of an MRA, showing how each $V_{j+1}$ is decomposed into $V_j$ and $W_j$:

```mermaid
graph TB
    L2["L2 of R (Full Signal Space)"]
    VJ1["V sub J plus 1 (Finest Resolution)"]
    VJ["V sub J (Coarser)"]
    WJ["W sub J (Details at Level J)"]
    VJ2["V sub J minus 1 (Even Coarser)"]
    WJ2["W sub J minus 1 (Details at Level J-1)"]
    VINF["V sub minus infinity (DC Component)"]

    L2 --> VJ1
    VJ1 --> VJ
    VJ1 --> WJ
    VJ --> VJ2
    VJ --> WJ2
    VJ2 --> VINF
```

### 4.2 Mallat's Algorithm Block Diagram (One Decomposition Level)

The following block diagram shows the **filter bank realization** of one level of Mallat's decomposition:

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN["Input Signal a sub J plus 1 (n)"]
    LP["Low Pass Filter h (Mirror)"]
    HP["High Pass Filter g (Mirror)"]
    DS1["Downsample by 2"]
    DS2["Downsample by 2"]
    OUTA["Approximation a sub J (n)"]
    OUTD["Detail d sub J (n)"]

    IN --> LP
    IN --> HP
    LP --> DS1
    HP --> DS2
    DS1 --> OUTA
    DS2 --> OUTD
```

### 4.3 Full 3-Level Wavelet Decomposition Tree

```mermaid
graph TB
    S["Input Signal a sub J"]
    A1["a sub J minus 1 (Approx)"]
    D1["d sub J minus 1 (Detail Level 1)"]
    A2["a sub J minus 2 (Approx)"]
    D2["d sub J minus 2 (Detail Level 2)"]
    A3["a sub J minus 3 (Approx)"]
    D3["d sub J minus 3 (Detail Level 3)"]

    S --> A1
    S --> D1
    A1 --> A2
    A1 --> D2
    A2 --> A3
    A2 --> D3
```

### 4.4 Lifting Scheme Flow (Forward Transform)

The following diagram illustrates the **three stages of the lifting scheme**: Split, Predict, and Update.

```mermaid
graph LR
    X["Input a sub J plus 1 (n)"]
    SP["Split Stage: Lazy Wavelet"]
    EVE["Even Samples a sub J plus 1 (2k)"]
    ODD["Odd Samples a sub J plus 1 (2k plus 1)"]
    PRED["Predict Operator P"]
    DJ["Detail d sub J (k) = Odd minus P Even"]
    UPD["Update Operator U"]
    AJ["Approximation a sub J (k) = Even plus U Detail"]

    X --> SP
    SP --> EVE
    SP --> ODD
    EVE --> PRED
    PRED --> DJ
    ODD --> DJ
    EVE --> UPD
    DJ --> UPD
    UPD --> AJ
```

### 4.5 Inverse Lifting Scheme (Reconstruction)

The inverse lifting simply **reverses the order** of operations and **flips the signs** of the Predict and Update steps:

```mermaid
graph LR
    AJ["Approximation a sub J (k)"]
    DJ["Detail d sub J (k)"]
    UPDI["Inverse Update: Even = Approx minus U Detail"]
    PRED["Inverse Predict: Odd = Detail plus P Even"]
    MERGE["Merge: Interleave Even and Odd"]
    OUT["Reconstructed a sub J plus 1 (n)"]

    AJ --> UPDI
    DJ --> UPDI
    UPDI --> MERGE
    DJ --> PRED
    PRED --> MERGE
    MERGE --> OUT
```

### 4.6 Equivalence: Filter Bank ↔ MRA Mapping

```mermaid
graph TB
    MRA["MRA Subspace V sub j plus 1"]
    FB["Two Channel Filter Bank"]
    SC["Scaling Function phi (Low Pass Channel)"]
    WV["Wavelet Function psi (High Pass Channel)"]
    LF["Low Pass Filter h (n)"]
    HF["High Pass Filter g (n)"]
    APX["Approximation Coeff a sub j (n)"]
    DET["Detail Coeff d sub j (n)"]

    MRA --> SC
    MRA --> WV
    FB --> LF
    FB --> HF
    SC --> LF
    WV --> HF
    LF --> APX
    HF --> DET
```
